topics.title: Glass Transition Temperature - Amorphous State
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Glass Transition Temperature - Amorphous State

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)** is defined as the specific temperature at which an amorphous polymer undergoes a reversible transition from a hard, glassy, and brittle state to a soft, rubbery, and flexible state. Below $T_g$, the micro-Brownian (segmental) motion of the polymer chain segments is effectively frozen, and the material exhibits only vibrational motion. Above $T_g$, long-range coordinated segmental motion becomes active, leading to a dramatic drop in modulus (typically 3 to 4 orders of magnitude).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Glass transition is **strictly a property of the amorphous (non-crystalline) region** of a polymer. Semi-crystalline polymers show a $T_g$ only in their amorphous domains, while the crystalline domains melt at a separate temperature $T_m$, where $T_m > T_g$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bowl of cooked spaghetti on a dinner plate**:

- **Below $T_g$ (Glassy State):** The spaghetti strands are stuck together, frozen stiff by a thin layer of icy sauce. The plate is rigid. If you tap it, it goes *clink* — that's a glassy polymer.
- **At $T_g$ (Transition):** The ice has just melted, and the sauce becomes syrupy. The strands can now slide past one another in small jerks.
- **Above $T_g$ (Rubbery State):** The strands wiggle freely like a living thing. The plate is squishy, soft, and rubbery.

The temperature at which the "ice melts" and the strands start to wiggle is the **Glass Transition Temperature**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Melting:** Melting ($T_m$) is a **first-order thermodynamic transition** with a discontinuous change in volume, entropy, and enthalpy. Glass transition is a **second-order-like (pseudo-transition)** characterized by a *change in slope* (not a jump) in the volume vs. temperature plot.

## 1.3 The Amorphous State — A Closer Look

An amorphous polymer lacks long-range 3D order. Its chains are arranged in a random, entangled coil conformation (the **random coil model**), often described by the **Flory random coil**.

| State | Temperature Range | Chain Mobility | Mechanical Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Glassy | $T < T_g$ | Frozen (vibrational only) | Hard, brittle, rigid |
| Leathery | $T \approx T_g$ | Small-scale segmental | Tough, leathery |
| Rubbery | $T > T_g$ (up to $T_m$ or flow) | Long-range segmental | Elastic, soft |
| Viscous Flow | $T > T_f$ (flow) | Whole-chain motion | Viscous liquid |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Value:** The **Standard Reference Temperature** often used in polymer physics is $T_g \approx 100^\circ C$ for atactic polystyrene (PS). This is purely for context — never assume $T_g$ for any polymer without data.

## 1.4 Physical Significance of $T_g$

1. **Design Parameter:** Determines the upper service temperature for rigid plastics and the lower service temperature for elastomers.
2. **Processing Window:** Polymer processing (extrusion, injection molding) is conducted well above $T_g$ (typically $T_g + 100^\circ C$).
3. **Dimensional Stability:** A plastic used in a coffee mug handle must have $T_g > 100^\circ C$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Molecular Origin of Glass Transition

At the molecular level, $T_g$ is governed by the **onset of cooperative segmental motion**. A single repeat unit cannot move on its own — it requires the synchronized motion of roughly 10–20 adjacent backbone atoms (the **cooperative rearranging region**, CRR, as defined by Adam-Gibbs).

### 2.1.1 Free Volume Theory (Fox & Flory)

The most intuitive framework is the **Free Volume Theory**:

- **Occupied Volume ($V_0$):** The actual physical volume occupied by the polymer chains (van der Waals volume).
- **Free Volume ($V_f$):** The "empty" space between chains arising from packing inefficiencies, chain ends, and thermal agitation.
- **Total Volume:** $V = V_0 + V_f$.

$$V = V_0 + V_f$$

At $T_g$, the free volume reaches a **critical minimum value** $V_{f,c} \approx 0.025 \times V_g$ (2.5% of total volume), below which segmental motion is geometrically impossible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the free volume stay constant below $T_g$?** Because below $T_g$, the material is in a non-equilibrium glassy state — the chains are essentially frozen and cannot redistribute to fill voids.

### 2.1.2 Kinetic vs. Thermodynamic Debate

A KTU-favorite conceptual point:

- **Kinetic View:** $T_g$ is **not a true thermodynamic transition** but a kinetic phenomenon. Its value depends on the cooling rate! Faster cooling $\rightarrow$ higher observed $T_g$ (less time for chain rearrangement).
- **Thermodynamic View:** If extrapolated with infinitely slow cooling, an "ideal" $T_g$ (sometimes called $T_2$ or Kauzmann temperature $T_K$) would exist where entropy equals that of the crystal.

## 2.2 The Five Classical Theories of $T_g$

| # | Theory | Core Idea | Key Equation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Free Volume (Doolittle)** | $T_g$ = temperature at critical free volume | $\eta = A \exp(B V_0 / V_f)$ |
| 2 | **Kinetic (Tool-Narayanaswamy-Moynihan)** | $T_g$ depends on cooling rate | $\ln \vert q \vert = \text{const} - \Delta h^* / R T_g$ |
| 3 | **Thermodynamic (Gibbs-DiMarzio)** | Configurational entropy $\rightarrow 0$ at $T_g$ | $S_c(T_g) = 0$ |
| 4 | **Adam-Gibbs** | Cooperative rearranging regions | $W = A \exp(-C / T S_c)$ |
| 5 | **Mode Coupling Theory (MCT)** | Critical slowing down of density fluctuations | $\tau \sim (T - T_c)^{-\gamma}$ |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for KTU ESE.** It contains the high-frequency equations tested under Module 1.

| Formula Name | Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fox-Flory** | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{M_n}$ | $T_{g,\infty}$ = $T_g$ at infinite mol. wt.; $K$ = constant; $M_n$ = number-average mol. wt. | Effect of molecular weight |
| **Fox Equation (Copolymer)** | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$ | $w_i$ = weight fraction; $T_{g,i}$ = $T_g$ of homopolymer $i$ | Random copolymer $T_g$ |
| **Gordon-Taylor** | $T_g = w_1 T_{g,1} + w_2 T_{g,2}$ | Empirical blend rule | Polymer blends |
| **Kelley-Bueche (Diluent)** | $T_g = w_1 T_{g,1} + w_2 T_{g,2}$ | Same as Gordon-Taylor for diluent | Plasticizer effect |
| **WLF Equation** | $\log_{10} a_T = \dfrac{-C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$ | $a_T$ = shift factor; $C_1 \approx 17.44$; $C_2 \approx 51.6$ | Time-temperature superposition |
| **Doolittle** | $\eta = A \exp\!\left(B \dfrac{V_0}{V_f}\right)$ | $\eta$ = viscosity; $A, B$ = constants | Viscosity-temperature |
| **Thermal Expansion** | $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{V}\dfrac{dV}{dT}$ | $\alpha_g$ (glassy) $\approx 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\,K^{-1}$; $\alpha_l$ (liquid) $\approx 5.5 \times 10^{-4}\,K^{-1}$ | Volume-temperature slope change |
| **Entropy (Adam-Gibbs)** | $W_{\text{min}} = A \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-C}{T S_c}\right)$ | $W_{\text{min}}$ = minimum relaxation probability | Relaxation time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Tip:** KTU frequently asks students to *list* 4 factors affecting $T_g$ (2 marks) and *explain* any 2 in detail (5 marks). The Fox-Flory equation is asked in 7-mark derivation questions.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Automotive Tires:** Natural rubber has $T_g \approx -70^\circ C$, ensuring elasticity in winter.
- **PET Bottles:** PET has $T_g \approx 70^\circ C$; below this, bottles remain dimensionally stable on shelves.
- **Polymer Optical Fibers (POF):** PMMA ($T_g \approx 105^\circ C$) is used for short-distance data transmission.
- **3D Printing (FDM):** PLA is printed near $T_g \approx 60^\circ C$ for warping control; ABS near $T_g \approx 105^\circ C$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fox-Flory Equation for Molecular Weight Dependence

**Statement:** The glass transition temperature of an amorphous polymer increases with molecular weight, asymptotically approaching a limiting value at infinite molecular weight.

**Starting Premise — Free Volume Argument:**

The free volume $V_f$ in a polymer has two contributions:
1. The free volume from chain ends (which are loosely packed and create extra free volume).
2. The free volume from the bulk chain segments.

Let $V_f$ be expressed in terms of the specific volume:

$$V = V_0 + V_f$$

The number of chain ends per unit volume is proportional to $\rho N_A / M_n$ (where $\rho$ is density, $N_A$ is Avogadro's number, and $M_n$ is the number-average molecular weight). Each chain end contributes a fixed increment $\theta$ to the free volume:

$$V_f = V_f^{\infty} + \dfrac{\theta \rho N_A}{M_n}$$

**Step 1:** At $T_g$, the total free volume reaches a critical value $V_{f,c}$ (a constant for the polymer):

$$V_{f,c} = V_f^{\infty} + \dfrac{\theta \rho N_A}{M_n}$$

**Step 2:** Thermal expansion: above $T_g$, the free volume grows with temperature as:

$$V_f = V_f^{\infty} + \alpha_f (T - T_g^{\infty})$$

**Step 3:** Combining both expressions and setting $V_{f,c}$ equal in both:

$$\alpha_f (T_g - T_{g,\infty}) = \dfrac{\theta \rho N_A}{M_n}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $T_g$:

$$T_g = T_{g,\infty} + \dfrac{\theta \rho N_A}{\alpha_f} \cdot \dfrac{1}{M_n}$$

**Step 5:** Defining $K = \dfrac{\theta \rho N_A}{\alpha_f}$ (a polymer-specific constant, typically of order $10^5$ g/mol·K for many polymers):

$$\boxed{T_g = T_{g,\infty} + \dfrac{K}{M_n}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** Some KTU textbooks write $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K/M_n$ where $K$ is defined as a positive constant with a negative sign absorbed. Always verify the sign convention in your reference text.

**Verification:** For very high $M_n$ (e.g., $M_n \to \infty$): $T_g \to T_{g,\infty}$ (correct asymptote). For very low $M_n$ (oligomers): $T_g$ is significantly lower than the bulk limit (physically correct — small molecules have more chain-end free volume).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Fox Equation for Copolymers

**Statement:** For a random copolymer of two monomers A and B, the reciprocal of $T_g$ is a linear weighted average of the reciprocals of the homopolymer $T_g$ values.

**Starting Premise:** Each monomer $i$ contributes a fractional free volume $f_i$ at its own $T_{g,i}$. The total free volume at $T_g$ of the copolymer is the sum of contributions:

$$f_{\text{copoly}}(T_g) = w_1 f_1(T_g) + w_2 f_2(T_g)$$

**Step 1:** At $T_{g,1}$: $f_1 = f_c$ (critical). So $f_1(T) = f_c + \alpha_1 (T - T_{g,1})$.

**Step 2:** Similarly for monomer 2: $f_2(T) = f_c + \alpha_2 (T - T_{g,2})$.

**Step 3:** Substituting and setting the total equal to $f_c$ at $T_g$:

$$f_c = w_1 \!\left[f_c + \alpha_1 (T_g - T_{g,1})\right] + w_2 \!\left[f_c + \alpha_2 (T_g - T_{g,2})\right]$$

**Step 4:** Note $w_1 + w_2 = 1$, so the $f_c$ terms cancel:

$$0 = w_1 \alpha_1 (T_g - T_{g,1}) + w_2 \alpha_2 (T_g - T_{g,2})$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $T_g$:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}}$$

(using the simplifying assumption $\alpha_1 T_{g,1} \approx \alpha_2 T_{g,2}$, which is reasonable for similar monomers).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The WLF Equation (Williams-Landel-Ferry)

**Statement:** The time-temperature superposition principle for amorphous polymers above $T_g$ can be expressed through a shift factor $a_T$.

**Starting Premise — Doolittle Equation:**

The viscosity of a polymer is related to free volume through the Doolittle equation:

$$\eta = A \exp\!\left(B \dfrac{V_0}{V_f}\right)$$

**Step 1:** The fractional free volume $f = V_f / V = V_f / (V_0 + V_f)$. At temperatures near $T_g$, $f$ varies linearly:

$$f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

where $f_g \approx 0.025$ and $\alpha_f \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-4}\,K^{-1}$.

**Step 2:** The shift factor is the ratio of relaxation times at temperature $T$ and reference $T_g$:

$$a_T = \dfrac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)} = \dfrac{\exp(B/f)}{\exp(B/f_g)}$$

**Step 3:** Taking the logarithm:

$$\log_{10} a_T = \dfrac{B}{2.303}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{f_g}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$ and simplifying (defining $C_1 = B / (2.303 f_g)$ and $C_2 = f_g / \alpha_f$):

$$\boxed{\log_{10} a_T = -\dfrac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}}$$

**Universal Constants ("Universal WLF"):** $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6\,K$ for many amorphous polymers when $T_g$ is taken as the reference temperature.

**Validity Range:** The WLF equation is valid for $T_g < T < T_g + 100\,K$. Outside this range, the Arrhenius equation is more appropriate.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting $T_g$

| Factor | Effect on $T_g$ | Magnitude of Effect | Physical Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ↑ Molecular weight ($M_n$) | ↑ $T_g$ (asymptotic) | Up to ~30°C between oligomer and high MW | More chain ends → more free volume |
| ↑ Chain flexibility | ↓ $T_g$ | Up to ~200°C across polymer classes | Easier backbone rotation |
| ↑ Chain rigidity (aromatic rings) | ↑ $T_g$ | Up to ~150°C | Restricted rotation |
| ↑ Crosslink density | ↑ $T_g$ | Up to ~50°C between linear and fully crosslinked | Covalent network restricts motion |
| ↑ Plasticizer content | ↓ $T_g$ | Linear with $w_{\text{plasticizer}}$ | Free volume dilution |
| ↑ Intermolecular H-bonding | ↑ $T_g$ | Up to ~100°C | Secondary bonds restrict mobility |
| ↑ Polarity | ↑ $T_g$ | Variable | Dipole-dipole interactions |
| ↑ Bulky side groups | ↑ $T_g$ (steric) | Up to ~80°C | Steric hindrance to rotation |
| ↑ Free volume (branching) | ↓ $T_g$ | Moderate | Looser chain packing |
| ↑ Pressure | ↑ $T_g$ | ~20–30°C per 100 MPa | Compresses free volume |
| ↑ Cooling rate | ↑ $T_g$ (apparent) | 3–5°C per decade | Less time for equilibration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Pairs:**
> - $T_g$ of **cis-polyisoprene** (natural rubber) $\approx -73^\circ C$ vs. **gutta-percha (trans)** $\approx -53^\circ C$ (configurational isomerism).
> - $T_g$ of **atactic PS** $\approx 100^\circ C$ vs. **isotactic PS** $\approx$ does not exhibit a $T_g$ cleanly due to crystallinity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Volume-Temperature Curve for an Amorphous Polymer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cooling from Melt] --> B{Above Tg?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Supercooled Liquid: High Slope alpha_l]
    B -- No --> D[Glassy State: Low Slope alpha_g]
    C -- T = Tg --> E[Glass Transition: Slope Change]
    E --> D
    D --> F[Crystalline Path: Branch with T_m]
    C -.Alternative Path.-> F
    
    styleA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    styleB fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    styleC fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
    styleD fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600
    styleE fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    styleF fill:#ccffff,stroke:#009999
```

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Diagram Questions:** Always label both slopes ($\alpha_l$ for liquid, $\alpha_g$ for glass) and mark $T_g$ as the **intersection of extrapolated lines** (NOT the kink point, since the actual transition is rounded).

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Molecular Mobility vs. Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW_T[Below Tg]
        A1[Glassy Polymer] --> A2[Frozen Segmental Motion]
        A2 --> A3[Only Vibrational Modes]
        A3 --> A4[Brittle and Rigid]
    end
    
    subgraph MID_T[Near Tg]
        B1[Leathery Region] --> B2[Local Segmental Motion ON]
        B2 --> B3[Modulus Drops 3-4 Decades]
        B3 --> B4[Tough Behavior]
    end
    
    subgraph HIGH_T[Above Tg]
        C1[Rubbery Polymer] --> C2[Cooperative Segment Motion]
        C2 --> C3[Elastomeric Behavior]
        C3 --> C4[Entropic Elasticity]
    end
    
    LOW_T --> MID_T
    MID_T --> HIGH_T
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Students often report the $T_g$ as the **midpoint** of the DSC step. However, the **IUPAC convention** is the **onset temperature**. KTU values full credit only if you mention *all three* points and justify your choice.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define glass transition temperature. How is it different from melting temperature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Glass transition temperature ($T_g$) is the temperature at which an amorphous polymer transforms from a hard, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state due to the onset of long-range segmental motion of the polymer chains.* (1.5 marks)

*Difference from melting ($T_m$):*
- $T_m$ is a **first-order thermodynamic transition** observed in *crystalline* polymers, accompanied by a discontinuous change in volume, enthalpy, and entropy and a finite latent heat of fusion. (1 mark)
- $T_g$ is a **pseudo second-order transition** observed in *amorphous* polymers, marked only by a *change in slope* of the volume vs. temperature plot and *no* latent heat. (0.5 mark)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any four factors affecting the glass transition temperature of polymers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*(Any 4 of the following — 0.75 mark each)*
1. Chain flexibility / backbone rigidity
2. Molecular weight ($M_n$)
3. Intermolecular forces (H-bonding, polarity)
4. Presence of plasticizers
5. Crosslink density
6. Steric bulk of side groups
7. Cooling rate (kinetic effect)
8. Copolymer composition

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Derive the **Fox-Flory equation** relating glass transition temperature to molecular weight. Explain how $T_g$ varies with $M_n$ graphically. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **free volume theory** of glass transition. How is the WLF equation derived from the Doolittle equation? (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Fox-Flory Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Concept of Free Volume (1 mark):**
The specific volume $V$ of a polymer is the sum of the **occupied volume** $V_0$ (actual van der Waals volume of chains) and the **free volume** $V_f$ (empty space between chains):

$$V = V_0 + V_f$$

**Step 2 — Free Volume Decomposition (1 mark):**
Free volume has two contributions:
- A bulk component $V_f^{\infty}$ that vanishes as $M_n \to \infty$.
- An excess component proportional to the number of chain ends per unit volume:

$$V_f = V_f^{\infty} + \dfrac{\theta \rho N_A}{M_n}$$

where $\theta$ is the excess free volume per chain end, $\rho$ is density, $N_A$ is Avogadro's number.

**Step 3 — Critical Free Volume Condition (1 mark):**
At $T_g$, $V_f$ reaches a critical value $V_{f,c}$:

$$V_{f,c} = V_f^{\infty}(T_g) + \dfrac{\theta \rho N_A}{M_n}$$

**Step 4 — Thermal Expansion (1 mark):**
The bulk free volume grows linearly above the asymptotic $T_{g,\infty}$:

$$V_f^{\infty}(T_g) = V_f^{\infty}(T_{g,\infty}) + \alpha_f (T_g - T_{g,\infty})$$

**Step 5 — Algebraic Solution (1.5 marks):**
Combining and equating:

$$\alpha_f (T_g - T_{g,\infty}) = \dfrac{\theta \rho N_A}{M_n}$$

$$T_g = T_{g,\infty} + \dfrac{\theta \rho N_A}{\alpha_f} \cdot \dfrac{1}{M_n}$$

Defining $K = \theta \rho N_A / \alpha_f$:

$$\boxed{T_g = T_{g,\infty} + \dfrac{K}{M_n}}$$

**Step 6 — Graphical Representation (1.5 marks):**
A plot of $T_g$ vs. $1/M_n$ is a straight line:
- y-intercept at $1/M_n = 0$ gives $T_{g,\infty}$ (the high-molecular-weight plateau).
- Slope of the line equals $K$.
- As $M_n$ increases, $T_g$ asymptotically approaches $T_{g,\infty}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention that $T_g$ is independent of molecular weight *above* a critical value (typically $M_c \approx 10{,}000$–$20{,}000$ g/mol for entanglement). Always specify this regime in your answer.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Free Volume Theory + WLF Derivation (7 marks):**

**Part 1 — Free Volume Theory (3.5 marks):**

*Definition:* Free volume $V_f$ is the difference between the actual macroscopic volume $V$ and the volume occupied by polymer molecules $V_0$:

$$V_f = V - V_0$$

*Temperature dependence:* The free volume fraction $f = V_f / V$ varies linearly with temperature:

$$f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

where $f_g \approx 0.025$ is the free volume fraction at $T_g$, and $\alpha_f \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-4}\,K^{-1}$ is the thermal expansion coefficient of free volume.

*Glass transition criterion:* At $T_g$, $f$ reaches the critical value $f_g$. Below $T_g$, the polymer is kinetically trapped in a non-equilibrium state with frozen free volume, and only vibrational modes are active. Above $T_g$, segmental motion is "unlocked" and the polymer flows.

*Thermal expansion coefficients:*
- Glassy state: $\alpha_g \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\,K^{-1}$
- Liquid/rubbery state: $\alpha_l \approx 5.5$ to $6.0 \times 10^{-4}\,K^{-1}$
- Difference: $\Delta\alpha = \alpha_l - \alpha_g \approx \alpha_f$

**Part 2 — WLF Derivation from Doolittle (3.5 marks):**

*Step 1 (0.5 mark):* Start with the Doolittle equation relating viscosity to free volume:

$$\eta = A \exp\!\left(B \dfrac{V_0}{V_f}\right) = A \exp\!\left(\dfrac{B}{f}\right)$$

*Step 2 (0.5 mark):* Shift factor definition:

$$a_T = \dfrac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)} = \exp\!\left[B\!\left(\dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{f_g}\right)\right]$$

*Step 3 (0.5 mark):* Take the base-10 logarithm:

$$\log_{10} a_T = \dfrac{B}{2.303}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{f_g}\right)$$

*Step 4 (0.5 mark):* Substitute $f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$:

$$\log_{10} a_T = -\dfrac{B}{2.303 f_g} \cdot \dfrac{(T - T_g)}{(f_g / \alpha_f) + (T - T_g)}$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* Define universal constants:

$$C_1 = \dfrac{B}{2.303 f_g} \approx 17.44, \quad C_2 = \dfrac{f_g}{\alpha_f} \approx 51.6\,K$$

*Step 6 (0.5 mark):* Final WLF equation:

$$\boxed{\log_{10} a_T = -\dfrac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}}$$

**Validity:** $T_g < T < T_g + 100\,K$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students confuse the **Doolittle equation** (viscosity-based) with the **WLF equation** (shift factor-based). The WLF is a *consequence* of Doolittle, not a separate theory. Make this derivation chain explicit in your answer.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **kinetic and thermodynamic theories** of glass transition. Compare their predictions regarding the existence of a true $T_g$. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Describe the **DSC, DMA, and dilatometry** techniques for measuring $T_g$. Which is the most sensitive and why? (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Kinetic vs. Thermodynamic Theories (7 marks):**

**1. Kinetic Theory (Tool-Narayanaswamy-Moynihan) (3.5 marks):**

*Postulate:* Glass transition is **not a true thermodynamic transition** but a kinetic phenomenon where the polymer falls out of equilibrium as it is cooled.

*Cooling rate dependence:*

$$\dfrac{d \ln \vert q \vert}{d(1/T_g)} = -\dfrac{\Delta h^*}{R}$$

where $q$ is the cooling rate and $\Delta h^*$ is the activation enthalpy for segmental motion.

*Predictions:*
- Faster cooling $\rightarrow$ higher observed $T_g$ (chains have less time to relax).
- No "true" $T_g$ exists in the limit of infinite cooling rate; the transition is a kinetic artifact.
- The glass transition temperature is **cooling-rate dependent** by 3–5°C per decade of cooling rate.

**2. Thermodynamic Theory (Gibbs-DiMarzio) (3.5 marks):**

*Postulate:* There exists a true second-order thermodynamic transition at $T_2$ (the **Kauzmann temperature** $T_K$) where the configurational entropy of the supercooled liquid equals that of the crystal:

$$S_c(T_K) = S_c^{\text{liquid}}(T_K) - S_c^{\text{crystal}}(T_K) = 0$$

*Predictions:*
- $T_g$ is a kinetic manifestation of a true underlying transition at $T_2 < T_g$.
- The transition at $T_2$ is second-order (continuous $S$, discontinuous $dS/dT$).
- Cooling rate only determines whether we *observe* $T_g$ close to $T_2$ — slow cooling gives $T_g \to T_2$.

**Comparison Table (built into prose, marks 1):**

| Aspect | Kinetic View | Thermodynamic View |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nature | Non-equilibrium artifact | True equilibrium transition |
| $T_g$ | Cooling rate dependent | Has a fixed value at $T_2$ |
| Existence | No "true" $T_g$ | $T_g \to T_2$ as $q \to 0$ |
| Entropy at $T_g$ | $S_c > 0$ (frozen non-equilibrium) | $S_c = 0$ only at $T_2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write "Kinetic theory says $T_g$ is not real." This is imprecise. The correct phrasing is: "Kinetic theory says $T_g$ is not a *thermodynamic* transition, but a *kinetic* manifestation of structural relaxation." Marks are lost for imprecise wording.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Measurement Techniques for $T_g$ (7 marks):**

**1. Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) (2.5 marks):**
- *Principle:* Measures the difference in heat flow between a sample and a reference as a function of temperature.
- *Observation at $T_g$:* A **step change in heat capacity** ($C_p$ increases by $\Delta C_p$).
- *Detection:* $T_g$ is identified as the **midpoint of the step** (or onset, by IUPAC convention).
- *Typical heating rate:* 10–20 K/min.
- *Limitation:* Less sensitive than DMA; misses weak transitions.

**2. Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA) (2.5 marks):**
- *Principle:* Applies a small oscillating stress and measures the strain response as a function of temperature at a fixed frequency (typically 1 Hz).
- *Observation at $T_g$:*
  - **Storage modulus $E'$** drops by 3 to 4 decades.
  - **Loss modulus $E''$** shows a peak.
  - **Tan $\delta = E''/E'$** shows a peak (often taken as $T_g$).
- *Sensitivity:* **Most sensitive** technique (detects transitions invisible to DSC).
- *Limitation:* Frequency-dependent $T_g$ — values are ~5–10°C higher than DSC values.

**3. Dilatometry (1.5 marks):**
- *Principle:* Measures the specific volume of the polymer as a function of temperature using a mercury or confocal dilatometer.
- *Observation at $T_g$:* A **change in slope** of the volume vs. temperature plot.
- *Slope ratios:* $\alpha_l / \alpha_g \approx 2$ to 3.
- *Limitation:* Slow, time-consuming, requires careful sample preparation.

**4. Most Sensitive Method (0.5 mark):**
**DMA is the most sensitive** because it can detect transitions where the modulus changes by less than 1%, whereas DSC requires a $\Delta C_p$ of at least 0.01 J/(g·K) to be visible. Additionally, the loss peak (tan $\delta$) provides a clear, sharp signature.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse the $T_g$ reported by DMA and DSC. **DMA $T_g$ > DSC $T_g$** by 5–10°C because DMA uses a high effective frequency (~1 Hz) while DSC uses a much lower effective frequency. Always specify the measurement frequency and heating rate when reporting $T_g$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings (Module 1 — Glass Transition)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Patterns in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Sign error in Fox-Flory:** Writing $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K/M_n$ when the reference convention requires $+K/M_n$. Always check your textbook's convention.
> 2. **Confusing $T_g$ with $T_m$:** Stating that $T_g$ is observed in crystalline polymers. *No* — $T_g$ is exclusively an amorphous-domain phenomenon.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** The constant $K$ in Fox-Flory has units of **g·K/mol** (or K·g/mol). Always quote units in numerical problems.
> 4. **Skipping the WLF validity range:** Stating WLF is valid for "all temperatures." *No* — it is strictly valid for $T_g < T < T_g + 100\,K$.
> 5. **Mishandling the Fox equation:** The copolymer Fox equation uses **weight fractions** of monomers, not mole fractions. Students often substitute mole fractions by mistake.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Glass Transition Temperature)**

- **$T_g$** is a **second-order pseudo-transition** exclusive to **amorphous** polymer regions.
- Below $T_g$: **glassy** (frozen, brittle). Above $T_g$: **rubbery** (mobile, elastic).
- **Free volume** $V_f$ is the empty space between chains; at $T_g$, $f_g \approx 0.025$.
- **Five theories:** Free Volume, Kinetic, Thermodynamic (Gibbs-DiMarzio), Adam-Gibbs, Mode Coupling.
- **Fox-Flory equation:** $T_g = T_{g,\infty} + K / M_n$ — $T_g$ ↑ with $M_n$ then plateaus.
- **Fox equation (copolymer):** $1/T_g = w_1/T_{g,1} + w_2/T_{g,2}$ — weight fractions, not mole fractions.
- **WLF equation:** $\log_{10} a_T = -C_1(T - T_g) / [C_2 + (T - T_g)]$; valid $T_g$ to $T_g + 100\,K$; $C_1 \approx 17.44$, $C_2 \approx 51.6\,K$.
- **Doolittle equation:** $\eta = A \exp(B V_0 / V_f)$ — base of the WLF equation.
- **Factors increasing $T_g$:** ↑ MW, ↑ rigidity, ↑ H-bonding, ↑ crosslinking, ↑ pressure.
- **Factors decreasing $T_g$:** ↑ plasticizer, ↑ flexibility, ↑ branching, ↑ free volume.
- **DSC:** Step in $C_p$ → midpoint or onset. **DMA:** Drop in $E'$, peak in tan $\delta$. **Dilatometry:** Slope change in $V$ vs. $T$.
- **DMA $T_g$ > DSC $T_g$** by 5–10°C due to frequency effect.
- **Cooling rate effect:** Faster cooling → higher apparent $T_g$ (~3–5°C per decade).
- **Standard $T_g$ values to remember:** PS $\approx 100^\circ C$; PMMA $\approx 105^\circ C$; PVC $\approx 80^\circ C$; NR $\approx -73^\circ C$; PDMS $\approx -127^\circ C$; PET (amorphous) $\approx 70^\circ C$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Molecular Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Molecular Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Molecular motion in polymers** refers to the spectrum of thermally activated, time-dependent movements exhibited by macromolecular chains and their constituent sub-units. These motions operate across a hierarchy of length scales — from angstrom-level bond vibrations to the diffusion of entire chains — and are the fundamental physical origin of all viscoelastic, thermal, and mechanical responses observed in polymeric materials.

In the language of the KTU 2024 syllabus, molecular motion is the **microscopic kinetic process** that connects a polymer's chemical architecture to its macroscopic performance properties such as modulus, glass transition temperature ($T_g$), melt viscosity, and creep compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCPOT302 / Module 1):** Molecular motion is the *bridge* between **structure** and **properties**. Without understanding *how* chains move, the student cannot rationalize *why* a polymer behaves as a rigid glass, a leathery rubber, or a viscous melt.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spaghetti in Sauce" Model

Imagine a bowl of freshly cooked spaghetti tossed in thick tomato sauce:

| Element | Polymer Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A single long strand of spaghetti | A polymer chain (molecular weight $\sim 10^5$ g/mol) |
| The thick tomato sauce | Surrounding entangled chains (friction / intermolecular forces) |
| Twirling one end with a fork | Whole-chain motion (**reptation**) |
| Wiggle of a 2 cm piece in the middle | Segmental motion (micro-Brownian) |
| Twist of a single strand | Local bond rotation |
| Vibration of the whole table | Atomic thermal vibrations |

Just as a piece of spaghetti *cannot* move freely — it is constrained by sauce and neighboring strands — a polymer chain segment can only move when there is sufficient **free space** (free volume) and **thermal energy** ($k_B T$) to overcome the constraints imposed by covalent bonds and intermolecular interactions.

## 1.3 The Three Governing Parameters

Every molecular motion event in a polymer is governed by exactly three parameters:

1. **Thermal Energy** — characterized by the absolute temperature $T$ and the Boltzmann constant $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
2. **Free Volume** — the empty space not occupied by the van der Waals volume of the molecules, denoted $V_f$.
3. **Activation Barrier** — the energy hump $E_a$ that must be crossed for the motion to occur.

> [!NOTE]
> A motion is *activated* only when $k_B T \gtrsim E_a$ **AND** sufficient free volume is available. This is why polymers exhibit a sharp **glass transition** rather than a smooth change — the cooperative freezing of segmental motion.

## 1.4 The Hierarchical Spectrum of Molecular Motion

Polymer scientists classify motions by length scale and characteristic time. The five recognized tiers are:

- **Tier 1 — Atomic Vibrations:** Bond stretching and angle bending; timescale $\sim 10^{-13}$ s; active even at 0 K.
- **Tier 2 — Side-Group Rotations:** Rotation of pendant groups ($-\text{CH}_3$, $-\text{C}_6\text{H}_5$, $-\text{OH}$); $\sim 10^{-11}$ s; active below $T_g$.
- **Tier 3 — Local Bond Rotations (crankshaft motion):** Cooperative rotation of 3–5 backbone atoms; $\sim 10^{-9}$ s; onset near $\beta$-transition.
- **Tier 4 — Segmental Motion (micro-Brownian):** Cooperative motion of 10–20 backbone atoms; $\sim 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-2}$ s; **frozen below $T_g$, active above**.
- **Tier 5 — Whole-Chain Motion (reptation / translation):** Snake-like diffusion of the entire chain through its entanglement tube; $\sim 1$ to $10^4$ s; active only in melts and concentrated solutions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tier-by-Tier Theoretical Breakdown

### Tier 1 — Atomic Vibrations
- Bonds stretch and bend about their equilibrium positions.
- Quantized as phonons with energy $E = h\nu$.
- **Why it matters:** Sets the baseline heat capacity $C_p$ of the solid polymer; explains why polymers never reach absolute zero heat capacity (Third Law).

### Tier 2 — Side-Group Rotations
- Pendant groups rotate about the bond attaching them to the backbone.
- **Example:** Methyl group in polypropylene undergoes quantum-mechanical **tunneling** at very low $T$.
- **How:** Requires surmounting a 3-fold rotational barrier (typically 8–12 kJ/mol for $-\text{CH}_3$).
- **Manifestation:** A secondary relaxation peak called the **$\gamma$-transition** in dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA).

### Tier 3 — Local Bond Rotations (Crankshaft Mechanism)
- Originally proposed by **Schatzki** (1965) for $n$-alkane-like backbones.
- A 4-atom segment in the all-*trans* conformation rotates cooperatively while the two flanking bonds remain stationary — resembling a mechanical crankshaft.
- Requires a minimum of **three consecutive methylene units** in the backbone.
- Manifests as the **$\beta$-transition** in DMA.

### Tier 4 — Segmental Motion (Micro-Brownian Motion)
- This is the **defining motion** of the glass transition.
- Involves cooperative rearrangement of 10–20 backbone atoms over a distance of 0.5–1 nm.
- The correlation length of cooperativity **diverges** as $T \to T_g^+$.
- Above $T_g$, this motion is "unfrozen" — the polymer becomes rubbery.
- Below $T_g$, only Tier 1–3 motions remain — the polymer is glassy.

### Tier 5 — Whole-Chain Motion (Reptation)
- Proposed by **de Gennes** (1971) and elaborated by **Doi–Edwards** (1978–1986).
- A long entangled chain diffuses along its own contour, confined within a "tube" formed by surrounding chains.
- The characteristic **reptation time** $\tau_d$ scales as:
$$\tau_d \propto \frac{\eta_0 \, b^2 \, N^3}{k_B T \, N_e}$$
  where $N$ is the degree of polymerization, $N_e$ the entanglement length, $b$ the Kuhn segment length, and $\eta_0$ the zero-shear viscosity.
- **Engineering utility:** Predicts melt viscosity, diffusion coefficients in polymer processing (extrusion, injection molding), and self-diffusion in membranes.

## 2.2 The Free-Volume Theory (Cohen & Turnbull, 1959; refined by Ferry)

Free volume is the *unoccupied* volume per gram of polymer:
$$V_f(T) = V(T) - V_0(T)$$

where $V$ is the **specific volume** and $V_0$ is the **occupied volume** (van der Waals volume).

**Core postulate:** A molecular motion can occur **only if** a free-volume hole of critical size $v^* \approx 0.5\,V_m$ (molecular volume) appears next to the moving segment.

Above $T_g$, the free volume is:
$$V_f(T) = V_f(T_g) + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

where $\alpha_f$ is the **thermal expansion coefficient of free volume** (typical value: $\alpha_f \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-4}$ K$^{-1}$).

> [!NOTE]
> The defining feature of $T_g$ in the free-volume picture: $V_f(T_g)$ has a **universal value** of $\approx 0.025$ cm³/g for most amorphous polymers. Below $T_g$, $V_f$ is "frozen" and does **not** follow the linear extrapolation — the $V$–$T$ curve shows a kink.

## 2.3 The Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) Equation

The WLF equation is the **workhorse** for predicting how molecular motion rates change with temperature in the range $T_g < T < T_g + 100$ °C:

$$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{C_1 \, (T - T_{ref})}{C_2 + (T - T_{ref})}$$

**Universal ("apparent") constants** when $T_{ref} = T_g$:
$$C_1 = 17.44, \qquad C_2 = 51.6 \text{ K}$$

**Physical meaning of $a_T$:** the **horizontal shift factor** that superimposes viscoelastic data measured at temperature $T$ onto a reference curve at $T_{ref}$. It is a direct measure of how much faster (or slower) molecular motion is at $T$ compared to $T_{ref}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why WLF works only for $T_g < T < T_g + 100$ °C:** Below $T_g$, motion is Arrhenius (Tier 1–3). Far above $T_g + 100$ °C, the free-volume model breaks down and flow becomes truly Arrhenius-like.

## 2.4 The Arrhenius Behavior Below $T_g$

For sub-$T_g$ transitions (Tier 1–3), the rate of motion follows:
$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T}$$

or equivalently for the shift factor:
$$\ln a_T = \frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{ref}} \right)$$

A plot of $\ln a_T$ vs. $1/T$ is **linear** for Arrhenius behavior but **curved** for WLF behavior — this is the standard experimental test to distinguish them.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning of Terms | Validity / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f = V - V_0$ | Free volume definition | All states |
| 2 | $V_f(T) = V_f(T_g) + \alpha_f(T - T_g)$ | Free volume above $T_g$ | $T > T_g$ |
| 3 | $\log a_T = -\dfrac{C_1(T-T_g)}{C_2 + (T-T_g)}$ | WLF (with $T_g$ as reference) | $T_g < T < T_g + 100$ °C |
| 4 | $C_1 = 17.44, \; C_2 = 51.6$ | Universal WLF constants | Most amorphous polymers |
| 5 | $\ln a_T = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)$ | Arrhenius shift factor | $T < T_g$ |
| 6 | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{\bar{M}_n}$ | Fox–Flory equation | Linear polymers |
| 7 | $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{f}$ | Translational diffusion (Rouse) | Dilute, unentangled |
| 8 | $\tau_d \propto N^3$ | Reptation time | Entangled melts |
| 9 | $E_a(\text{segmental}) \approx 200 - 400$ kJ/mol | Activation energy, micro-Brownian | Near $T_g$ |
| 10 | $\alpha_f \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-4}$ K$^{-1}$ | Free-volume expansion coefficient | Above $T_g$ |

**Where these equations are used in real engineering:**

- **WLF** → Extrusion die design, injection-molding cycle optimization, predicting creep in rubber mounts.
- **Fox–Flory** → Selecting molecular weight for a target $T_g$ in adhesive or coating formulations.
- **Reptation time** → Determining minimum residence time in a twin-screw extruder to avoid degradation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Fox–Flory Equation from Free-Volume Theory

The Fox–Flory equation describes how the glass transition temperature depends on the **number-average molecular weight** $\bar{M}_n$. It is a direct consequence of the chain-end free-volume argument.

### Step 1 — Identify the two contributors to free volume

Consider a linear polymer of $\bar{M}_n$. The chain has:
- **Interior segments** (contribute only the "normal" free volume $V_f^\infty$)
- **Two chain ends per chain** (each chain end has *extra* free volume $\theta$ because it has fewer neighbors)

The **average** specific free volume is therefore:

$$V_f(\bar{M}_n) = V_f^\infty + \frac{2 \rho N_A \theta}{\bar{M}_n}$$

where:
- $\rho$ is the polymer density,
- $N_A$ is Avogadro's number ($6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol$^{-1}$),
- $\theta$ is the excess free volume per chain end (cm³·mol/g),
- $V_f^\infty$ is the free volume of an infinitely long chain (no chain ends).

**Explanation of the logic:** The factor $2 N_A / \bar{M}_n$ gives the number of chain ends per gram; each contributes excess free volume $\theta$ (with $\rho$ converting mass to volume). The shorter the chain, the larger this term — small chains have more free volume.

### Step 2 — Use the universal free-volume criterion at $T_g$

By the free-volume theory, $T_g$ is defined as the temperature at which free volume reaches the universal value $V_f(T_g) \approx 0.025$ cm³/g. Equating:

$$V_f(\bar{M}_n, T_g) = V_f(T_g) \quad \text{(constant)}$$

$$V_f^\infty + \frac{2 \rho N_A \theta}{\bar{M}_n} + \alpha_f(T_g - T) = V_f(T_g)$$

**Explanation:** The first two terms are structural (depend on $\bar{M}_n$); the third accounts for the temperature difference between the reference $T$ (some reference temperature, often 0 K in the original derivation) and the actual $T_g$ of the sample.

### Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $T_g$

Solving for $T_g$:

$$T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{2 \rho N_A \theta}{\alpha_f \, \bar{M}_n}$$

Define the constant $K = \dfrac{2 \rho N_A \theta}{\alpha_f}$ to obtain the famous **Fox–Flory equation**:

$$\boxed{T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{\bar{M}_n}}$$

**Final form** — This shows that $T_g$ **increases** with molecular weight and asymptotically approaches $T_{g,\infty}$ for $\bar{M}_n \to \infty$.

> [!NOTE]
> $T_{g,\infty}$ is the $T_g$ of an *infinitely long* chain (only interior segments, no chain-end excess free volume). Typical values: PS = 100 °C, PMMA = 105 °C, PVC = 80 °C.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Universal WLF Constants from Free-Volume Theory

The WLF equation can be *derived* from the Doolittle viscosity equation using free-volume arguments.

### Step 1 — Doolittle equation

Empirically, the viscosity of a polymer melt obeys:

$$\ln \eta = \ln A + B \left( \frac{V_0}{V_f} \right)$$

where $A$ and $B$ are constants close to **unity**, $V_0$ is the occupied volume, and $V_f$ is the free volume.

### Step 2 — Express the shift factor

The shift factor $a_T$ is the ratio of a relaxation time $\tau$ at temperature $T$ to that at the reference $T_g$:

$$\ln a_T = \ln \frac{\tau(T)}{\tau(T_g)} = B \left[ \frac{V_0}{V_f(T)} - \frac{V_0}{V_f(T_g)} \right]$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the free-volume expressions

At temperatures above $T_g$:

$$V_f(T) = V_f(T_g) + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

Let $f_g = V_f(T_g) / V_0$ be the fractional free volume at $T_g$ (universally $f_g \approx 0.025$).

$$V_0 / V_f(T) = \frac{1}{f_g + \alpha_f(T-T_g)/V_0} = \frac{1}{f_g} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \alpha_f(T-T_g)/(f_g V_0)}$$

### Step 4 — Use the geometric-series approximation

For $\alpha_f(T-T_g) \ll f_g V_0$ (valid in the WLF range):

$$\frac{1}{1+x} \approx 1 - x + x^2 - \cdots$$

Keeping only the first two terms and substituting back:

$$\ln a_T \approx B \left[ \frac{1}{f_g} - \frac{1}{f_g + \alpha_f(T-T_g)/V_0} \right]$$

$$\ln a_T \approx \frac{B}{f_g} \cdot \frac{\alpha_f(T-T_g)/V_0}{f_g + \alpha_f(T-T_g)/V_0}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to base-10 logarithm

$$\log a_T = \frac{1}{2.303} \cdot \ln a_T = \frac{B}{2.303 \, f_g} \cdot \frac{(T - T_g)/(\,f_g V_0/\alpha_f\,)}{1 + (T - T_g)/(\,f_g V_0/\alpha_f\,)}$$

### Step 6 — Identify the universal constants

Comparing with $\log a_T = -\dfrac{C_1(T-T_g)}{C_2 + (T-T_g)}$:

$$C_1 = \frac{B}{2.303 \, f_g} = \frac{1}{2.303 \times 0.025} \approx 17.44$$

$$C_2 = \frac{f_g V_0}{\alpha_f} = \frac{0.025 \, V_0}{4.8 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 51.6 \text{ K}$$

This derivation is the rigorous proof that the WLF "constants" are *universal* only because the free-volume parameters are *universal*. Any deviation in $C_1$ or $C_2$ for a real polymer signals either different $f_g$ or different $\alpha_f$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** The relaxation time of polystyrene at 120 °C is $\tau = 100$ s. Using the universal WLF constants and $T_g = 100$ °C, calculate $\tau$ at 130 °C.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $T - T_g$ at each temperature.**

At $T_1 = 120$ °C: $\quad T_1 - T_g = 20$ K

At $T_2 = 130$ °C: $\quad T_2 - T_g = 30$ K

**Step 2 — Calculate the shift factor $a_T$ for each temperature relative to $T_g$.**

$$\log a_T(T) = -\frac{17.44 \, (T - T_g)}{51.6 + (T - T_g)}$$

At $T_1 = 120$ °C:

$$\log a_T(120) = -\frac{17.44 \times 20}{51.6 + 20} = -\frac{348.8}{71.6} = -4.871$$

$$a_T(120) = 10^{-4.871} = 1.343 \times 10^{-5}$$

At $T_2 = 130$ °C:

$$\log a_T(130) = -\frac{17.44 \times 30}{51.6 + 30} = -\frac{523.2}{81.6} = -6.412$$

$$a_T(130) = 10^{-6.412} = 3.867 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 3 — Relate shift factor to relaxation time.**

By definition: $a_T(T) = \dfrac{\tau(T)}{\tau(T_g)}$

**Step 4 — Compute the shift factor from 120 °C to 130 °C.**

$$\frac{a_T(130)}{a_T(120)} = \frac{\tau(130)}{\tau(120)} = \frac{3.867 \times 10^{-7}}{1.343 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.02880$$

**Step 5 — Final answer.**

$$\tau(130\,°C) = 100 \text{ s} \times 0.02880 = 2.88 \text{ s}$$

**Interpretation:** A 10 °C increase above $T_g$ speeds up the molecular motion by a factor of **~35**, dropping the relaxation time from 100 s to under 3 s. This dramatic sensitivity is why polymer processing temperatures are chosen with great care.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Five Tiers of Motion

| Property | Tier 1: Atomic Vib. | Tier 2: Side Rotation | Tier 3: Crankshaft | Tier 4: Segmental | Tier 5: Reptation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Length scale | 0.1 nm | 0.2 nm | 0.5 nm | 1 nm | 10–100 nm |
| Timescale (s) | $10^{-13}$ | $10^{-11}$ | $10^{-9}$ | $10^{-6}$–$10^{-2}$ | 1–$10^4$ |
| Active at $T$ | all | $<T_g$ | $\lesssim T_g$ | $>T_g$ | $\gg T_g$ |
| DMA peak | none | $\gamma$ | $\beta$ | $\alpha$ (= $T_g$) | flow region |
| $E_a$ (kJ/mol) | < 5 | 8–15 | 30–60 | 200–500 | 20–50 |
| $E_a$ formula | $h\nu$ | $RT\ln k$ | $RT\ln k$ | $RT\ln k$ | $RT\ln(D_0/D)$ |
| Theory | Quantum | Eyring | Schatzki | Free vol. | de Gennes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Motion Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermal Energy Input kT] --> B[Polymer Chain Activation]
    B --> C{Length Scale of Motion}
    C -->|0.1 nm| D[Tier 1: Atomic Vibrations]
    C -->|0.2 nm| E[Tier 2: Side Group Rotation]
    C -->|0.5 nm| F[Tier 3: Crankshaft Bond Rotation]
    C -->|1 nm| G[Tier 4: Segmental Micro-Brownian]
    C -->|10 to 100 nm| H[Tier 5: Reptation Whole Chain]
    D --> D1[Timescale 10 power -13 s]
    E --> E1[Timescale 10 power -11 s]
    F --> F1[Timescale 10 power -9 s]
    G --> G1[Timescale 10 power -6 to 10 power -2 s]
    H --> H1[Timescale 1 to 10 power 4 s]
    G --> G2[Defines Glass Transition Tg]
    H --> H2[Defines Melt Flow and Viscosity]
    D1 --> I[Heat Capacity Baseline]
    E1 --> J[Gamma Transition in DMA]
    F1 --> K[Beta Transition in DMA]
    G1 --> L[Alpha Transition in DMA]
    H1 --> M[Terminal Flow Zone in DMA]
```

## 4.2 Free-Volume Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Specific Volume V] --> B[Occupied Volume V0]
    A --> C[Free Volume Vf]
    B --> B1[Van der Waals Volume of Atoms]
    B --> B2[Hole Free Lattice Volume]
    C --> C1[Redistributable Free Volume]
    C --> C2[Interstitial Free Volume]
    C1 --> D[Available for Molecular Motion]
    D --> E{Is Vf greater than V critical}
    E -->|Yes| F[Motion Occurs Segment Jumps]
    E -->|No| G[Motion Frozen Glassy State]
    F --> H[Rate proportional to exp of B times V0 over Vf]
    H --> I[Underpins WLF Equation]
    G --> J[Below Tg Arrhenius Sub Tg Transitions]
```

## 4.3 Temperature–Motion Phase Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Temperature Axis] --> T0[Far Below Tg]
    T --> T1[Below Tg]
    T --> T2[At Tg]
    T --> T3[Above Tg to Tg plus 100]
    T --> T4[Far Above Tg]
    T0 --> S0[Only Atomic Vibrations Active]
    T1 --> S1[Atomic plus Side Group plus Crankshaft]
    T2 --> S2[Segmental Motion Unfreezes Cooperatively]
    T3 --> S3[WLF Regime Segmental plus Onset of Reptation]
    T4 --> S4[Full Reptation plus Terminal Flow]
    S0 --> P0[Glassy Brittle Material]
    S1 --> P1[Leathery or Glassy Material]
    S2 --> P2[Rubbery Transition]
    S3 --> P3[Rubbery Plateau Region]
    S4 --> P4[Viscous Melt Liquid Like]
```

## 4.4 Energy Landscape of Motion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial State Segment in Cage] -->|Needs Energy Ea| B[Transition State Saddle Point]
    B -->|Releases Energy| C[Final State New Cage Position]
    A -.->|Barrier Height Ea| B
    C -.->|Barrier Height Ea| A
    A2[Temperature T] --> A3{Boltzmann Factor}
    A3 -->|Low kT over Ea| A4[Motion Rare Glassy]
    A3 -->|kT comparable to Ea| A5[Motion Frequent Rubbery]
    A3 -->|kT much greater than Ea| A6[Motion Continuous Liquid]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Boltzmann factor** $\exp(-E_a / k_B T)$ is the *master equation* governing how the rate of any molecular motion scales with temperature. It connects the microscopic energy landscape to the macroscopic property of viscosity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Define micro-Brownian motion in polymers. Why is it considered the defining motion of the glass transition?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Micro-Brownian motion is the **cooperative, thermally activated movement of short segments** (typically 10–20 backbone atoms) of a polymer chain over a distance of about 0.5–1 nm.

It is the defining motion of the glass transition because **(1 mark)**:
- The correlation length of this cooperative motion **diverges** as temperature approaches $T_g$ from above.
- Below $T_g$, the thermal energy $k_B T$ is insufficient to overcome the segmental activation barrier ($E_a \sim 200$–$500$ kJ/mol) and the available free volume is below the critical $V_f^* \approx 0.025$ cm³/g, so the segmental motion is **frozen**.
- Above $T_g$, the motion is **unfrozen**, and this on-off switch in segmental motion is precisely what we identify as the glass transition in DSC, DMA, and dilatometry.

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for cooperativity, 1 mark for free-volume / energy argument.)**

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**What is the universal WLF equation? State its two constants and indicate the temperature range over which it is valid.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) equation expresses the temperature dependence of molecular motion rates (via the shift factor $a_T$) relative to a reference temperature, typically $T_g$ **(1 mark)**:

$$\log a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$

The **universal constants** are **(1 mark)**:
- $C_1 = 17.44$
- $C_2 = 51.6$ K

**Validity range:** $T_g < T < T_g + 100$ °C **(1 mark)**.

Outside this range, motion is **Arrhenius** (below $T_g$, for sub-$T_g$ relaxations) and the free-volume model breaks down (far above $T_g + 100$ °C).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** With a neat block-level diagram, classify the various modes of molecular motion in polymers. Discuss how the characteristic time scale and length scale of motion change as one moves from atomic vibrations to whole-chain reptation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Fox–Flory equation** relating $T_g$ to number-average molecular weight $\bar{M}_n$ using the free-volume concept. Discuss its engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Classification diagram and five-tier hierarchy:**

Present the five-tier hierarchy (see Section 4.1) with neat labels. Mention:
- Tier 1: Atomic vibrations (0.1 nm, $10^{-13}$ s)
- Tier 2: Side-group rotations (0.2 nm, $10^{-11}$ s)
- Tier 3: Crankshaft motion (0.5 nm, $10^{-9}$ s)
- Tier 4: Segmental micro-Brownian motion (1 nm, $10^{-6}$–$10^{-2}$ s)
- Tier 5: Whole-chain reptation (10–100 nm, 1–$10^4$ s)

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Length and timescale scaling:**

Both length scale and timescale **increase monotonically** as one moves up the hierarchy. The increase in timescale (12 orders of magnitude from Tier 1 to Tier 5) is much greater than the increase in length scale (3 orders of magnitude). This decoupling reflects the **cooperative nature** of large-scale motions — many small atomic events must be coordinated to produce a macroscopic chain translation.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Connection to transitions:**

- Tiers 1–3 are active below $T_g$ and give rise to the **$\gamma$ and $\beta$ transitions** in DMA.
- Tier 4 marks the **glass transition** ($\alpha$-transition).
- Tier 5 governs the **terminal flow zone** in DMA and determines the zero-shear viscosity.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Engineering implication:**

The wide spectrum of timescales is why polymers are **viscoelastic**: at a given observation time, several tiers may be simultaneously "frozen" or "active", giving a hybrid solid–liquid response. This is the molecular origin of creep, stress relaxation, and dynamic damping in polymer products.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Set up the free-volume expression:**

$$V_f(\bar{M}_n) = V_f^\infty + \frac{2 \rho N_A \theta}{\bar{M}_n}$$

Explain: $2N_A/\bar{M}_n$ = chain ends per gram; each contributes excess free volume $\theta$; $\rho$ converts mass to volume.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Apply the free-volume criterion at $T_g$:**

At $T_g$, $V_f$ reaches the universal value:

$$V_f^\infty + \frac{2 \rho N_A \theta}{\bar{M}_n} + \alpha_f(T_g - T_{ref}) = V_f(T_g)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Solve for $T_g$:**

Rearrange and define the constant $K$:

$$T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{2 \rho N_A \theta}{\alpha_f \bar{M}_n} = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{\bar{M}_n}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Engineering significance:**

- Choosing $\bar{M}_n$ sets the $T_g$ of a product — critical for **adhesives, paints, and molded parts**.
- The asymptotic $T_{g,\infty}$ is the highest $T_g$ achievable for a given chemistry; further increase requires changing the chemistry (e.g., introducing hydrogen-bonding groups).
- In **recycling**, chain scission reduces $\bar{M}_n$, lowering $T_g$ and affecting reprocessing behavior.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** What is **free volume**? Discuss the Cohen–Turnbull free-volume theory and show that it leads naturally to the **WLF equation** with universal constants $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6$ K. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer has $T_g = 105$ °C. A relaxation process has a characteristic time of $10^2$ s at 110 °C. Using the universal WLF constants, calculate the relaxation time at 125 °C. Comment on the sensitivity of molecular motion to temperature near $T_g$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Free volume definition and Cohen–Turnbull theory:**

Free volume $V_f = V - V_0$ is the unoccupied volume per gram. Cohen and Turnbull postulated that a segmental jump can occur only when a free-volume hole of size $\geq v^* \approx 0.5 V_m$ forms adjacent to the segment. The probability of such a hole gives the Doolittle viscosity:

$$\eta = A \exp\!\left( \frac{B V_0}{V_f} \right)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Linear free-volume expansion above $T_g$:**

$$V_f(T) = V_f(T_g) + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

Define $f_g = V_f(T_g) / V_0 \approx 0.025$ and use $\alpha_f \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-4}$ K$^{-1}$.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Derivation of WLF (geometric series):**

$$\ln a_T = B\!\left[ \frac{V_0}{V_f(T)} - \frac{V_0}{V_f(T_g)} \right]$$

$$= \frac{B}{f_g} \cdot \frac{\alpha_f(T-T_g)/V_0}{f_g + \alpha_f(T-T_g)/V_0}$$

Converting to $\log_{10}$ and identifying constants:

$$C_1 = \frac{B}{2.303 \, f_g} = \frac{1}{2.303 \times 0.025} = 17.44$$

$$C_2 = \frac{f_g V_0}{\alpha_f} = \frac{0.025}{4.8 \times 10^{-4}} = 51.6 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Conclusion:**

The WLF "constants" are *not* fundamental; they emerge from universal values of $f_g$ and $\alpha_f$, justifying their applicability to nearly all amorphous polymers.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify temperatures:**

$T_g = 105$ °C, $\;T_1 = 110$ °C $\Rightarrow T_1 - T_g = 5$ K, $\;T_2 = 125$ °C $\Rightarrow T_2 - T_g = 20$ K, $\;\tau(T_1) = 10^2$ s.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute shift factors:**

$$\log a_T(110) = -\frac{17.44 \times 5}{51.6 + 5} = -\frac{87.2}{56.6} = -1.5406$$

$$a_T(110) = 10^{-1.5406} = 2.881 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$\log a_T(125) = -\frac{17.44 \times 20}{51.6 + 20} = -\frac{348.8}{71.6} = -4.871$$

$$a_T(125) = 10^{-4.871} = 1.343 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Use shift factor ratio:**

$$\frac{\tau(125)}{\tau(110)} = \frac{a_T(125)}{a_T(110)} = \frac{1.343 \times 10^{-5}}{2.881 \times 10^{-2}} = 4.661 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Final answer:**

$$\tau(125\,°C) = 100 \text{ s} \times 4.661 \times 10^{-4} = 4.66 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} = 46.6 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Comment on sensitivity:**

A temperature rise of only **15 °C** (from 110 °C to 125 °C) accelerates the molecular motion by a factor of **~2150** (i.e., relaxation time drops from 100 s to 47 ms). This **extreme sensitivity near $T_g$** is the molecular origin of the difficulty in processing polymers close to their $T_g$ — a 5 °C error in set-point temperature can change viscosity by an order of magnitude. This is why industrial extruders use precise multi-zone temperature controllers with $\pm 1$ °C tolerance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 1 — Molecular Motion):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "free volume" with "porosity"** — Free volume is a *dynamic, statistical* concept (holes of atomic size that appear and disappear due to thermal motion), not static pores. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you treat them as identical.
> 2. **Using WLF outside its validity range** — Applying WLF at $T = T_g - 50$ °C or $T = T_g + 200$ °C is a **2-mark deduction** in numerical problems. Always state the validity range in the answer.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert $\ln a_T$ to $\log_{10} a_T$** — The WLF equation uses base-10 logarithm. Using natural log gives $C_1 = 40.15$ (wrong) instead of $17.44$. This is a frequent trap; deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the universal constants in the WLF derivation** — The whole *point* of the derivation is to show that $C_1$ and $C_2$ are *derived* from $f_g$ and $\alpha_f$, not empirical. A derivation that states the result without showing the link loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up Fox–Flory symbols** — The equation is $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K / \bar{M}_n$ (with **minus** sign). Some students write $T_g = T_{g,\infty} + K/\bar{M}_n$ (incorrect sign). Deduct 1 mark.
> 6. **Not mentioning the Boltzmann factor $\exp(-E_a / k_B T)$** when asked about temperature dependence of any sub-$T_g$ motion — this is the most fundamental equation in molecular motion and is often worth 1–2 marks by itself.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Five tiers of molecular motion**: atomic vibrations → side-group rotation → crankshaft → segmental micro-Brownian → reptation.
- ⭐ **Glass transition** is the *unfreezing* of **segmental (Tier 4) motion**; it is *not* a true thermodynamic phase transition but a kinetic event.
- ⭐ **Free volume** $V_f = V - V_0$; its **universal value at $T_g$** is $\approx 0.025$ cm³/g for most amorphous polymers.
- ⭐ **WLF equation** (with $T_{ref} = T_g$): $\log a_T = -17.44(T-T_g) / [51.6 + (T-T_g)]$; valid for $T_g < T < T_g + 100$ °C.
- ⭐ **Fox–Flory**: $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K/\bar{M}_n$ — short chains have lower $T_g$ due to excess chain-end free volume.
- ⭐ **Below $T_g$** → motion is **Arrhenius** ($\ln a_T \propto 1/T$); **above $T_g$** → motion is **WLF** (curved in $1/T$).
- ⭐ **Reptation time** $\tau_d \propto N^3$ for entangled polymer melts (Doi–Edwards).
- ⭐ The **Boltzmann factor** $\exp(-E_a/k_B T)$ is the master rate law for any single activated motion.
- ⭐ A **10 °C rise above $T_g$** typically speeds up molecular motion by **~one to two orders of magnitude** — this is the basis for injection-molding temperature optimization.
- ⭐ Universal constants $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6$ K emerge from $f_g = 0.025$ and $\alpha_f = 4.8 \times 10^{-4}$ K$^{-1}$ — *not* arbitrary empirical fits.
- ⭐ Always quote the **timescale and length scale** together when classifying a motion — both are part of the answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: First Order and Second Order Transitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** First Order and Second Order Transitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Phase Transitions (Ehrenfest Classification)

In **polymer science** and **thermodynamics**, a **phase transition** is a physical transformation of a thermodynamic system from one phase (or state of matter) to another. The classification of these transitions was formally proposed by **Paul Ehrenfest in 1933**, and is based on the continuity of the derivatives of the **Gibbs free energy (G)** with respect to thermodynamic variables such as **temperature (T)** and **pressure (P)**.

A **first-order transition** is defined as a transition in which the **first derivative of the Gibbs free energy** with respect to a thermodynamic variable shows a **discontinuity** (a sudden jump or break) at the transition point.

Mathematically:

$$\left( \frac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_{P} = -S$$

$$\left( \frac{\partial G}{\partial P} \right)_{T} = V$$

Since **entropy (S)** and **volume (V)** are first derivatives, their discontinuous jump at the transition point produces a measurable **latent heat (ΔH)** and a **volume change (ΔV)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **First Order Transition Definition (KTU Standard):**
> A transition in which the first derivative of the Gibbs free energy (G) is discontinuous at the transition temperature is called a **first-order transition**. The transition is accompanied by a finite latent heat and an abrupt change in specific volume. *Example: Melting (Tm), Crystallization (Tc), Vaporization.*

A **second-order transition** is a transition in which the **first derivative of the Gibbs free energy is continuous**, but the **second derivative is discontinuous** at the transition temperature.

Second derivatives of G include:

$$\left( \frac{\partial^{2} G}{\partial T^{2}} \right)_{P} = -\left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial T} \right)_{P} = -\frac{C_{p}}{T}$$

$$\left( \frac{\partial^{2} G}{\partial T \partial P} \right) = \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_{P} = V \alpha$$

$$\left( \frac{\partial^{2} G}{\partial P^{2}} \right)_{T} = \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)_{T} = -V \kappa$$

where $\alpha$ is the **coefficient of thermal expansion** and $\kappa$ is the **isothermal compressibility**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Second Order Transition Definition (KTU Standard):**
> A transition in which the first derivative of the Gibbs free energy (G) is continuous but its second derivative shows a discontinuity at the transition temperature is called a **second-order transition**. There is **no latent heat** involved, but properties like heat capacity, thermal expansion coefficient, and compressibility change abruptly. *Example: Glass transition (Tg) in amorphous polymers.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**Analogy for First-Order Transition (Melting of Ice → Water):**
Imagine an **ice cube** at exactly **0 °C (273.15 K)**. If you supply heat, the temperature does *not* rise. Instead, the ice absorbs this heat as **latent heat of fusion (~334 J/g)** to break the rigid hydrogen-bonded crystalline lattice. Only *after* all the ice has melted does the temperature of the water begin to rise again. The volume also drops discontinuously (ice is less dense than water). This is exactly what happens in the melting of a semi-crystalline polymer at its **melting temperature (Tm)**.

**Analogy for Second-Order Transition (Glass Transition of a Polymer):**
Now imagine a **polystyrene coffee cup lid**. As you heat it slowly past ~100 °C (its Tg), it does not suddenly melt. Instead, the polymer chains — previously "frozen" in a rigid, glassy state — begin to gain segmental mobility. The polymer softens gradually, the **specific heat jumps**, and the **slope of volume vs. temperature** changes. There is no latent heat, no sudden volume jump, and the process is reversible (in theory) — but the change is in the *rate* of property change, not in the property itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Students:** Remember the easy mnemonic — **"1st order = First derivative FAILS"** (entropy, volume jump). **"2nd order = Second derivative FAILS"** (Cp, α, κ jump). The transition temperature itself (T) is the **order parameter variable** being differentiated against.

---

### 1.3 Distinguishing Features at a Glance

| Property | First Order Transition | Second Order Transition |
|---|---|---|
| Latent Heat (ΔH) | **Non-zero** (finite) | **Zero** |
| Volume Change (ΔV) | **Discontinuous jump** | **Continuous, but slope (α) changes** |
| Entropy Change (ΔS) | **Discontinuous jump** | **Continuous, but slope (Cp/T) changes** |
| Heat Capacity (Cp) | Often infinite (delta function) | **Finite discontinuous jump** |
| Examples in Polymers | Melting (Tm), Crystallization (Tc) | Glass transition (Tg) |
| Reversibility | True thermodynamic equilibrium | Kinetic / pseudo-thermodynamic |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 First Order Transition — The Clapeyron Equation

The **Clapeyron equation** describes the slope of the coexistence curve (phase boundary) in a pressure–temperature (P–T) diagram for a first-order transition. It is derived by equating the chemical potentials (or molar Gibbs free energies) of the two coexisting phases at equilibrium.

For a transition from phase 1 to phase 2 (e.g., solid → liquid):

$$\mu_{1}(T, P) = \mu_{2}(T, P)$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to T along the coexistence curve (with dP/dT = slope of the curve):

$$-\bar{S}_{1} + \bar{V}_{1} \left( \frac{dP}{dT} \right) = -\bar{S}_{2} + \bar{V}_{2} \left( \frac{dP}{dT} \right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\bar{S}_{2} - \bar{S}_{1}}{\bar{V}_{2} - \bar{V}_{1}} = \frac{\Delta \bar{S}}{\Delta \bar{V}}$$

Since at the transition $\Delta \bar{S} = \Delta \bar{H} / T_{tr}$ (from $G = H - TS$ at constant P), the **Clapeyron equation** becomes:

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta \bar{H}}{T_{tr} \, \Delta \bar{V}}}$$

- $\Delta \bar{H}$ = molar latent heat of transition (J/mol)
- $\Delta \bar{V}$ = molar volume change of transition (m³/mol)
- $T_{tr}$ = transition temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the Clapeyron equation so important for KTU?** It is the **only** equation that rigorously connects the slope of a first-order phase boundary to measurable thermodynamic quantities. For polymer melting, knowing $dP/dT$ allows prediction of how Tm shifts with processing pressure (e.g., in injection molding).

---

### 2.2 Second Order Transition — The Ehrenfest Equations

For a second-order transition, the first derivatives (S, V) are continuous, so $\Delta S = 0$ and $\Delta V = 0$. However, second derivatives (Cp, α, κ) are discontinuous. By applying the conditions of equality of S and V on **both sides** of the transition and differentiating appropriately, **Ehrenfest derived two equations**:

**Ehrenfest Equation 1** (from continuity of S at the transition, with respect to P):

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta C_{p}}{T_{g} \, \Delta V \alpha}}$$

**Ehrenfest Equation 2** (from continuity of V at the transition, with respect to P):

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta \alpha}{\Delta \kappa}}$$

where:
- $\Delta C_{p}$ = jump in heat capacity at Tg (J/mol·K)
- $T_{g}$ = glass transition temperature (K)
- $\Delta V$ = volume at Tg (m³/mol) — usually approximated as V itself
- $\Delta \alpha$ = jump in coefficient of thermal expansion at Tg (K⁻¹)
- $\Delta \kappa$ = jump in isothermal compressibility at Tg (Pa⁻¹)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall to Remember:** The two Ehrenfest equations should yield the **same** value of $dP/dT$ for a true second-order transition. For the glass transition in polymers, they often do *not* — which is one of the key pieces of evidence that the glass transition is **not a true thermodynamic second-order transition** but a **kinetic / pseudo-thermodynamic phenomenon**. KTU examiners LOVE asking this conceptual question.

---

### 2.3 The Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) in Polymers

The **glass transition temperature (Tg)** is the temperature below which an amorphous polymer is in a hard, glassy, brittle state, and above which it becomes soft, rubbery, and flexible. It is the most important design parameter in polymer engineering.

**Molecular Interpretation:**
- Below Tg: Polymer chain segments are **frozen** — only vibrational motion is possible. Free volume is low.
- Above Tg: Chain segments gain **rotational and translational mobility** (~10–20 monomer units move cooperatively). Free volume increases.
- At Tg: A discontinuity in the slope of **specific volume vs. temperature** occurs.

Mathematically, the volume-temperature relationship is:

$$V(T) = V_{g} + V_{g} \, \alpha_{g} (T - T_{g}) \quad \text{for} \quad T < T_{g}$$

$$V(T) = V_{g} + V_{g} \, \alpha_{l} (T - T_{g}) \quad \text{for} \quad T > T_{g}$$

where $\alpha_{g}$ and $\alpha_{l}$ are the coefficients of thermal expansion in the glassy and liquid (rubbery) states respectively, and $\alpha_{l} > \alpha_{g}$ always.

The **jump in thermal expansion** is:
$$\Delta \alpha = \alpha_{l} - \alpha_{g}$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu_{1} = \mu_{2}$ at transition | Phase equilibrium condition | J/mol |
| 2 | $\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta \bar{H}}{T_{tr} \Delta \bar{V}}$ | **Clapeyron Eqn** — 1st order P-T slope | Pa/K |
| 3 | $\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta C_{p}}{T_{g} \Delta V \alpha}$ | **Ehrenfest Eqn 1** | Pa/K |
| 4 | $\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta \alpha}{\Delta \kappa}$ | **Ehrenfest Eqn 2** | Pa/K |
| 5 | $T_{g} \approx \frac{2}{3} T_{m}$ (in Kelvin) | **Boyer-Beaman Rule** (approx.) | K |
| 6 | $\Delta S = \frac{\Delta H}{T_{tr}}$ | Latent heat → entropy jump | J/(mol·K) |
| 7 | $\alpha = \frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P}$ | Coefficient of thermal expansion | K⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\kappa = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_{T}$ | Isothermal compressibility | Pa⁻¹ |
| 9 | $C_{p} = T \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_{P}$ | Heat capacity at constant P | J/(mol·K) |
| 10 | $G = H - TS$ | Gibbs free energy definition | J/mol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of these Equations:**
> - **Clapeyron equation** is used to design **high-pressure crystallization** processes for polymers (e.g., production of oriented polyethylene).
> - **Ehrenfest equations** help predict how **Tg shifts under pressure** — important for polymers used in deep-sea cables, aerospace, and high-pressure pipelines.
> - **Boyer-Beaman rule** gives a quick first-estimate of Tg when only Tm is known.

---

### 2.5 Factors Affecting Tg (Theory Snapshot)

1. **Chain flexibility:** Flexible chains (e.g., polyethylene, polybutadiene) have **low Tg**; rigid chains (e.g., polystyrene, PMMA) have **high Tg**.
2. **Molecular weight (Fox-Flory equation):**
$$T_{g} = T_{g}^{\infty} - \frac{K}{M_{n}}$$
3. **Plasticizers:** Small molecules that increase free volume → **decrease Tg**.
4. **Crosslinking:** Restricts chain motion → **increases Tg**.
5. **Pressure:** Higher pressure reduces free volume → **increases Tg** (from Ehrenfest Eqn 1).
6. **Cooling rate:** Faster cooling gives **higher apparent Tg** (kinetic effect).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Clapeyron Equation

**Starting Point — Phase Coexistence Condition:**

At a first-order transition between two phases (say phase α and phase β), the two phases are in thermodynamic equilibrium. The condition for equilibrium between two phases at the same T and P is that their **molar Gibbs free energies are equal**:

$$G_{\alpha}(T, P) = G_{\beta}(T, P)$$

This is the fundamental equality of chemical potentials $\mu_{\alpha} = \mu_{\beta}$.

**Step 1: Implicit Differentiation Along the Coexistence Curve**

If T changes by a small amount dT, then P must also change by dP (along the P-T phase boundary) to keep the two phases in equilibrium. So we differentiate the equality:

$$dG_{\alpha} = dG_{\beta}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Total Differential of G**

The total differential of G(T, P) is:

$$dG = -S \, dT + V \, dP$$

Therefore:

$$-S_{\alpha} \, dT + V_{\alpha} \, dP = -S_{\beta} \, dT + V_{\beta} \, dP$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to Isolate dP/dT**

Move all terms with dT to one side and dP to the other:

$$(V_{\beta} - V_{\alpha}) \, dP = (S_{\beta} - S_{\alpha}) \, dT$$

Divide both sides by dT and by $\Delta V$:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{S_{\beta} - S_{\alpha}}{V_{\beta} - V_{\alpha}} = \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta V}$$

**Step 4: Convert ΔS to ΔH Using the Maxwell Relation**

At constant pressure, the entropy change at the transition temperature is related to the latent heat by:

$$\Delta S = \frac{\Delta H}{T_{tr}}$$

(This comes from $G = H - TS$, so at constant P: $dG = dH - T dS - S dT$, and at the transition $dG_{\alpha} = dG_{\beta}$ gives $\Delta H = T_{tr} \Delta S$.)

**Step 5: Final Clapeyron Equation**

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{T_{tr} \, \Delta V}}$$

This is the rigorous, board-exam-quality derivation. Every KTU examiner expects all five steps.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Ehrenfest Equations

**Step 1: Continuity of S and V at a Second-Order Transition**

By definition of a second-order transition:

$$S_{\alpha}(T_{g}, P) = S_{\beta}(T_{g}, P) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta S = 0$$

$$V_{\alpha}(T_{g}, P) = V_{\beta}(T_{g}, P) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta V = 0$$

The differences $\Delta C_{p}$, $\Delta \alpha$, and $\Delta \kappa$ are the *discontinuities* (jumps) in the second derivatives.

**Step 2: Differentiate the Equality of Entropy Along the Coexistence Curve**

Start with $S_{\alpha}(T, P) = S_{\beta}(T, P)$. Differentiate:

$$dS_{\alpha} = dS_{\beta}$$

The total differential of S(T, P) is:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_{P} dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T} dP = \frac{C_{p}}{T} dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} dP$$

(We used the Maxwell relation $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P}$.)

Therefore:

$$\frac{C_{p,\alpha}}{T_{g}} dT - V_{\alpha} \alpha_{\alpha} \, dP = \frac{C_{p,\beta}}{T_{g}} dT - V_{\beta} \alpha_{\beta} \, dP$$

**Step 3: Rearrange**

$$(C_{p,\beta} - C_{p,\alpha}) \frac{dT}{T_{g}} = (V_{\beta} \alpha_{\beta} - V_{\alpha} \alpha_{\alpha}) \, dP$$

Since at Tg, $V_{\alpha} \approx V_{\beta} = V$ (continuity of V):

$$\frac{\Delta C_{p}}{T_{g}} dT = V \Delta \alpha \, dP$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta C_{p}}{T_{g} \, V \, \Delta \alpha} \quad \text{— Ehrenfest Equation 1}}$$

**Step 4: Differentiate the Equality of Volume Along the Coexistence Curve**

Start with $V_{\alpha}(T, P) = V_{\beta}(T, P)$. The total differential of V is:

$$dV = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} dT + \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_{T} dP = V \alpha \, dT - V \kappa \, dP$$

Differentiating the equality:

$$V_{\alpha} \alpha_{\alpha} dT - V_{\alpha} \kappa_{\alpha} dP = V_{\beta} \alpha_{\beta} dT - V_{\beta} \kappa_{\beta} dP$$

Again, $V_{\alpha} \approx V_{\beta} = V$ at Tg:

$$V \Delta \alpha \, dT = V \Delta \kappa \, dP$$

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta \alpha}{\Delta \kappa} \quad \text{— Ehrenfest Equation 2}}$$

Both equations are now rigorously derived.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem (Dec 2022 Type):**
A polymer sample undergoes a glass transition at Tg = 350 K. At the transition, the jump in heat capacity is $\Delta C_{p}$ = 30 J/(mol·K), the jump in thermal expansion coefficient is $\Delta \alpha = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹, the molar volume is V = 100 cm³/mol = $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/mol, and the jump in compressibility is $\Delta \kappa = 2.0 \times 10^{-10}$ Pa⁻¹. Verify that Ehrenfest equations are consistent.

**Solution Using Ehrenfest Equation 1:**

$$\left(\frac{dP}{dT}\right)_{1} = \frac{\Delta C_{p}}{T_{g} \, V \, \Delta \alpha}$$

Numerator: $\Delta C_{p} = 30$ J/(mol·K)

Denominator: $T_{g} \cdot V \cdot \Delta \alpha = 350 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times (4.5 \times 10^{-4})$

Let us compute step by step:

$$350 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4} = 3.5 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$3.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 4.5 \times 10^{-4} = 15.75 \times 10^{-6} = 1.575 \times 10^{-5}$$

Therefore:

$$\left(\frac{dP}{dT}\right)_{1} = \frac{30}{1.575 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.905 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa/K}$$

**Solution Using Ehrenfest Equation 2:**

$$\left(\frac{dP}{dT}\right)_{2} = \frac{\Delta \alpha}{\Delta \kappa} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-4}}{2.0 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa/K}$$

**Comparison:**

The two values, $1.905 \times 10^{6}$ Pa/K and $2.25 \times 10^{6}$ Pa/K, are of the **same order of magnitude** (within ~18%). For a *true* second-order transition they should be *identical*. The discrepancy confirms the **kinetic nature** of the glass transition in real polymers.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Board:** Always show the **unit conversion explicitly** (cm³ → m³) and **state the final answer with units (Pa/K or MPa/K)**. Marks are awarded for unit clarity.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison at a Glance

| Parameter | First Order (Melting, Tm) | Second Order (Glass, Tg) |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical order of discontinuous derivative of G | 1st derivative | 2nd derivative |
| Latent heat $\Delta H$ | **Non-zero** | **Zero** |
| Volume change $\Delta V$ | **Discontinuous jump** | Continuous, but $\alpha$ jumps |
| Entropy change $\Delta S$ | **Discontinuous jump** | Continuous, but Cp jumps |
| Heat capacity Cp | Effectively infinite (δ-function) | Finite discontinuous jump |
| Governing equation | Clapeyron | Ehrenfest (two equations) |
| Equilibrium | **True thermodynamic** | **Quasi/kinetic** (in polymers) |
| Polymer example | Polyethylene melting at 137 °C | Polystyrene Tg ≈ 100 °C |
| Order of transition parameter | n = 1 | n = 2 |
