### 4.2 Specific Volume vs Temperature Diagram (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GL["Glassy Region T less than Tg"]
        G1["Slope = αg low"]
    end
    subgraph TG["At Tg"]
        T1["Slope CHANGES abruptly"]
        T2["Δα = αl minus αg jump"]
    end
    subgraph RU["Rubbery Region T greater than Tg"]
        R1["Slope = αl higher"]
    end
    GL --> TG --> RU
    T1 --> C1["Second-Order Signature"]
    T2 --> C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** In the volume-temperature (V vs T) plot, the **first-order melting transition** appears as a **sudden vertical jump** (true ΔV). The **second-order glass transition** appears as a **kink** (change in slope, Δα) — *no vertical jump*. This is the single most distinguishing visual feature.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Tg & Tm in a Semi-Crystalline Polymer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P["Semi-Crystalline Polymer Sample"] --> H["Heating from Low T"]
    H --> T1["Region 1 Glassy Amorphous Phase rigid T less than Tg"]
    H --> T2["Region 2 Crystalline Phase stable T less than Tm"]
    T1 --> M1["Crossing Tg Softening of amorphous regions"]
    T2 --> M2["Crystalline regions intact until Tm"]
    M1 --> RUB["Region 3 Rubbery Amorphous mobile chain segments"]
    M2 --> RUB
    RUB --> MELT["Crossing Tm Melting of crystallites"]
    MELT --> LIQ["Region 4 Fully Molten Polymer flow regime"]
    RUB --> CP1["Cp jumps at Tg second order signature"]
    MELT --> CP2["ΔH latent heat absorbed at Tm first order signature"]
    LIQ --> END["Processing window injection molding extrusion"]
```

### 4.4 Cp vs T Diagram — Heat Capacity Signature

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["T low"] --> B["Cp glassy = Cp g low baseline"]
    B --> C["Crossing Tg"]
    C --> D["Cp jumps to Cp l high rubbery"]
    D --> E["T high continuous curve"]
    C --> F["ΔCp = Cp l minus Cp g measurable jump"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** Always remember — in DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry) traces, **first-order transitions** show as **sharp endothermic or exothermic peaks** (melting, crystallization). **Second-order transitions** show as a **step change in baseline** (glass transition). Examiners frequently show a DSC plot and ask students to identify which transition is which.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Differentiate between a first-order and a second-order phase transition with one suitable polymer example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | First Order | Second Order |
|---|---|---|
| Discontinuity | In **first derivative** of G (S, V) | In **second derivative** of G (Cp, α, κ) |
| Latent heat | **Present** (ΔH ≠ 0) | **Absent** (ΔH = 0) |
| Equation | Clapeyron equation | Ehrenfest equations |
| Polymer example | **Melting of polyethylene at ~137 °C** | **Glass transition of polystyrene at ~100 °C** |

In a first-order transition, properties like entropy and volume show a sudden, discontinuous jump at the transition temperature and a finite amount of heat (latent heat) is absorbed or released. In a second-order transition, the first derivatives of Gibbs free energy remain continuous, but second derivatives (heat capacity, thermal expansion coefficient) show a step change. **3 marks** — 1 mark each for difference rows + 0.5 mark each for examples.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is the significance of the Clapeyron equation in polymer science?**

**Model Answer:**

The Clapeyron equation, $\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{T_{tr} \Delta V}$, gives the **slope of the pressure–temperature phase boundary** for a first-order transition such as polymer melting or crystallization.

**Significance:**
1. It predicts **how the melting temperature (Tm) of a polymer changes with applied pressure** — important for processing techniques like injection molding, extrusion, and high-pressure crystallization.
2. It connects measurable thermodynamic quantities (latent heat ΔH, volume change ΔV) to the P–T behavior of the polymer.
3. For most polymers $\Delta V > 0$ on melting and $\Delta H > 0$, so $dP/dT > 0$ — meaning Tm **increases** with pressure.
4. It is used to determine the **equilibrium melting temperature** of a polymer by extrapolating $dP/dT$ data. **(3 marks)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Clapeyron equation** for a first-order phase transition. Explain each step of the derivation clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The melting of a polymer crystal occurs at Tm = 450 K at atmospheric pressure. The latent heat of fusion is ΔH = 8.5 kJ/mol and the molar volume change is ΔV = $6.2 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/mol. Calculate the slope $dP/dT$ of the melting curve and estimate the new melting temperature at a pressure of 200 atm (1 atm = 101325 Pa).

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The Clapeyron equation is derived as follows:

**Step 1:** At a first-order transition, the two phases α and β are in equilibrium, so their molar Gibbs free energies are equal:

$$G_{\alpha}(T, P) = G_{\beta}(T, P)$$

**Step 2:** Taking the total differential of both sides along the coexistence curve:

$$dG_{\alpha} = dG_{\beta}$$

**Step 3:** Using $dG = -S \, dT + V \, dP$:

$$-S_{\alpha} dT + V_{\alpha} dP = -S_{\beta} dT + V_{\beta} dP$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging:

$$(V_{\beta} - V_{\alpha}) dP = (S_{\beta} - S_{\alpha}) dT$$

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta V}$$

**Step 5:** At the transition, $\Delta S = \frac{\Delta H}{T_{tr}}$:

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{T_{tr} \, \Delta V}}$$

This is the **Clapeyron equation**. **(7 marks: 1.5 for each step + 1 for final boxed equation + 0.5 for physical interpretation.)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $T_m = 450$ K
- $\Delta H = 8.5 \times 10^{3}$ J/mol
- $\Delta V = 6.2 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/mol
- $\Delta P = 200 \text{ atm} - 1 \text{ atm} \approx 199 \text{ atm} = 199 \times 101325 = 2.0164 \times 10^{7}$ Pa (we can use 200 atm for simplicity: $2.0265 \times 10^{7}$ Pa)

**Step 1: Calculate dP/dT**

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{T_{m} \Delta V} = \frac{8.5 \times 10^{3}}{450 \times 6.2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

Denominator: $450 \times 6.2 \times 10^{-6} = 2790 \times 10^{-6} = 2.79 \times 10^{-3}$

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{8500}{2.79 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.046 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa/K}$$

**Step 2: Use this to find ΔT for ΔP**

Assuming $dP/dT$ is approximately constant over the small pressure range:

$$\Delta T = \frac{\Delta P}{dP/dT} = \frac{2.0265 \times 10^{7}}{3.046 \times 10^{6}} \approx 6.65 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3: New Melting Temperature**

$$T_{m}' = T_{m} + \Delta T = 450 + 6.65 \approx 456.7 \text{ K}$$

**Final Answer:** The slope is $3.05 \times 10^{6}$ Pa/K, and the new melting temperature is approximately **456.7 K** (or **183.5 °C**). **(7 marks: 2 for calculation, 2 for unit handling, 1.5 for final answer, 1.5 for physical interpretation.)**

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **glass transition (Tg)** in amorphous polymers. Explain the change in (i) specific volume, (ii) heat capacity, and (iii) entropy at Tg with suitable sketches.

**(b) [7 Marks]** State and derive the **two Ehrenfest equations** for a second-order transition. Why does the glass transition in real polymers deviate from ideal Ehrenfest behavior?

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The **glass transition temperature (Tg)** is the temperature at which an amorphous polymer transitions from a hard, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state. It is **not** a true thermodynamic phase transition but a kinetic transition related to the onset of long-range segmental motion of polymer chains.

**Changes at Tg:**

**(i) Specific Volume:**
Below Tg, the polymer is in a glassy state with a low coefficient of thermal expansion ($\alpha_g$). Above Tg, the polymer is in a rubbery state with a higher expansion coefficient ($\alpha_l$). The volume vs. temperature plot shows a **kink** (slope change) at Tg, not a jump. This indicates $\Delta V = 0$ but $\Delta \alpha \neq 0$.

**(ii) Heat Capacity (Cp):**
The heat capacity shows a **step increase** at Tg as chain segments gain mobility and can absorb more thermal energy. $\Delta C_p$ is finite and measurable by DSC.

**(iii) Entropy (S):**
The entropy is continuous through Tg, but its derivative ($C_p/T$) shows a step change. Hence $\Delta S = 0$ at Tg (no latent heat), but the rate of entropy change differs above and below Tg.

These three behaviors are **second-order signatures** — first derivatives continuous, second derivatives discontinuous. **(7 marks: 2 for definition, 1.5 for each property + 1 for sketch description.)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Ehrenfest Equations:**

For a second-order transition, $S$ and $V$ are continuous, but their temperature and pressure derivatives (second derivatives of G) are discontinuous.

By differentiating $S_{\alpha}(T, P) = S_{\beta}(T, P)$ along the coexistence curve and using Maxwell relations, we get:

**Ehrenfest Equation 1:**

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta C_{p}}{T_{g} \, V \, \Delta \alpha}}$$

**Ehrenfest Equation 2:**

By differentiating $V_{\alpha}(T, P) = V_{\beta}(T, P)$:

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta \alpha}{\Delta \kappa}}$$

**Deviation from Ideal Behavior:**

For a *true* second-order transition, both Ehrenfest equations must yield the *same* $dP/dT$. However, for the glass transition in real polymers:

1. The two equations predict **different** values of $dP/dT$.
2. The observed Tg is **cooling-rate dependent** — faster cooling gives a higher apparent Tg.
3. The transition shows **hysteresis** between heating and cooling cycles.

These observations indicate that the **glass transition is not a true thermodynamic second-order transition** but a **kinetic phenomenon** governed by the relaxation time of polymer chain segments becoming comparable to the experimental timescale. **(7 marks: 2 for each equation + 1.5 for deviation + 1.5 for kinetic interpretation.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Clapeyron/Ehrenfest equations *require SI units* (Pa, m³, J, K). Marks are deducted for using mixed units like cm³ with Pa. **Always convert cm³ → m³ by dividing by 10⁶.**
> 2. **Confusing Tg with Tm** — Tg is a **second-order** (glass transition) and Tm is a **first-order** (melting). Mixing them up in a derivation results in **loss of 3–4 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the Maxwell relation step** in deriving Ehrenfest equations — Examiners explicitly check whether students used $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$. This is a **favourite 2-mark question** in KTU boards.
> 4. **Claiming the glass transition is a true second-order transition** — This is **scientifically incorrect** for polymers. The correct statement is: "The glass transition exhibits second-order *signatures* (Cp jump, α change) but is **kinetic in nature** and not a true Ehrenfest second-order transition." Losing 1–2 marks for this is common.
> 5. **Forgetting to write the Clapeyron equation in its boxed/final form** — Always end with a clearly boxed equation to signal completion.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **First-order transition:** Discontinuous first derivative of G → latent heat (ΔH ≠ 0) and volume jump (ΔV ≠ 0). Governed by **Clapeyron equation**: $\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{T_{tr} \Delta V}$. Example: **Polymer melting (Tm)**.
- [ ] **Second-order transition:** Continuous first derivative of G, discontinuous second derivative. No latent heat. Governed by **two Ehrenfest equations**. Example: **Glass transition (Tg)** in amorphous polymers.
- [ ] **Glass transition (Tg)** is **kinetic, not true thermodynamic** — it shows second-order *signatures* (Cp jump, α change) but deviates from ideal Ehrenfest behavior.
- [ ] **Clapeyron equation** gives the slope of the P–T phase boundary for first-order transitions. Derived from equality of Gibbs free energies.
- [ ] **Ehrenfest Equation 1:** $\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta C_{p}}{T_{g} V \Delta \alpha}$. **Ehrenfest Equation 2:** $\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta \alpha}{\Delta \kappa}$.
- [ ] **Boyer-Beaman Rule:** $T_{g} \approx \frac{2}{3} T_{m}$ (both in Kelvin) — useful for **quick estimates**.
- [ ] **Fox-Flory Equation:** $T_{g} = T_{g}^{\infty} - \frac{K}{M_{n}}$ — Tg **increases** with molecular weight up to a limit.
- [ ] **DSC traces:** First-order = **peaks**; Second-order = **baseline steps**.
- [ ] **Factors increasing Tg:** Rigid chains, crosslinking, high pressure, high molecular weight, polar groups, hydrogen bonding.
- [ ] **Factors decreasing Tg:** Plasticizers, flexible chains, low molecular weight, free volume enhancers.
- [ ] **Always use SI units** in Clapeyron and Ehrenfest calculations: Pa for pressure, m³ for volume, J for energy, K for temperature.
- [ ] **Glass transition temperatures (typical values to memorize):**
  - Polyethylene (PE): ~ -120 °C
  - Polystyrene (PS): ~ 100 °C
  - Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA): ~ 105 °C
  - Poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC): ~ 80 °C
  - Polycarbonate (PC): ~ 150 °C
- [ ] **Ehrenfest classification is incomplete** for the glass transition — modern theories describe Tg using the **Adam-Gibbs entropy model** and **free volume theory** (Vogel-Fulcher-Tammann equation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secondary Transitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Secondary Transitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Secondary Transitions (Sub-Tg Transitions / Secondary Relaxations):**
> Secondary transitions are reversible, temperature-dependent molecular motions occurring **below the glass transition temperature (Tg)** of a polymer. They involve the localized motion of small chain segments, side groups, pendant groups, or atoms, without requiring the cooperative long-range segmental mobility that characterizes the primary glass transition. By convention, transitions are labelled in order of decreasing temperature using Greek letters: **α (primary), β, γ, δ, ε ...**

In the KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature, secondary transitions are critical because they govern **low-temperature mechanical behaviour, impact strength, damping, and long-term performance** of polymeric components — properties that directly affect service life in engineering applications.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a long railway train (the polymer backbone) parked in a freezing station. As the temperature rises:

- At very low temperatures (**γ-transition**): only the bolts and nuts inside individual wheels start vibrating — a *single component* moves.
- A little warmer (**β-transition**): entire carriage doors swing on their hinges — *pendant/side group motion*.
- Even warmer (**α-transition = Tg**): the entire train cars start shuffling and shifting in concert — *cooperative segmental motion*.

> [!NOTE]
> Secondary transitions are therefore **localized, non-cooperative molecular events**, while the primary glass transition (Tg) is a **cooperative** event involving ~5–10 monomer units moving in synchrony.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Significance |
|-----------|--------|---------------|--------------|
| Glass Transition | Tg (α) | 100 – 400 K | Cooperative segmental motion |
| β-transition | Tβ | Tg − 20 to − 100 K | Side group / short segment motion |
| γ-transition | Tγ | Tg − 100 to − 200 K | Methyl/methylene crankshaft motion |
| δ-transition | Tδ | Below 100 K | Phenyl ring flip / end group |
| Activation Energy | Ea | 20 – 100 kJ/mol (β) | Energy barrier for motion |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Transitions

Polymer transitions follow a strict thermodynamic hierarchy:

**Step 1 — Primary Transition (α):**
- The **glass transition (Tg)** for amorphous polymers and the **melting (Tm)** for crystalline polymers.
- Involves cooperative motion of 5–10 backbone repeat units.
- High activation energy: **300–1000 kJ/mol**.
- Governed by the **Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF)** equation above Tg.

**Step 2 — Secondary Transitions (β, γ, δ):**
- Localized motions; non-cooperative.
- Activation energy: **20–100 kJ/mol**.
- Governed by the **Arrhenius equation**.

**Step 3 — Why do secondary transitions exist?**
- Polymers are not rigid below Tg; small-scale motions persist because:
  - Side groups have rotational freedom.
  - Short (4–6 carbon) backbone segments can execute *crankshaft* motions.
  - Pendant rings can flip (180°) without disturbing the backbone.

### 2.2 Molecular Origins of Common Secondary Transitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Molecular Motions:**

| Transition | Molecular Origin | Example Polymer |
|------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **β** | Rotation of side groups (–COOCH3, –C6H5) | PMMA, PS |
| **γ** | Crankshaft motion of (CH2)n (n ≥ 4) | Polyethylene, Nylon |
| **δ** | Methyl (–CH3) group rotation | Polypropylene |
| **ε** | Phenyl ring 180° flip | Polystyrene, PC |

### 2.3 The Arrhenius Framework

Secondary transitions obey Arrhenius kinetics because they involve a single, well-defined energy barrier.

$$\tau(T) = \tau_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

Where:
- $\tau(T)$ = relaxation time at temperature $T$ (seconds)
- $\tau_0$ = pre-exponential factor (~10⁻¹² to 10⁻¹⁴ s)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Contrast:** Above Tg → WLF (free-volume controlled). Below Tg → **Arrhenius** (thermally activated barrier crossing).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation Table for Secondary Transitions (KTU 2024 Module 1)**

| # | Equation | Purpose | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\tau = \tau_0 \exp(E_a / RT)$ | Relaxation time | s |
| 2 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$ | Peak frequency in DMA | Hz |
| 3 | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{E''}{E'}$ | Damping factor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $E^{*} = E' + iE''$ | Complex modulus | Pa |
| 5 | $\ln f_{max} = \ln f_0 - \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\cdot\!\dfrac{1}{T}$ | Arrhenius plot slope | — |
| 6 | $E_a = -R \cdot \dfrac{d(\ln f_{max})}{d(1/T)}$ | Activation energy | J/mol |
| 7 | $\Delta E = E'_{glass} - E'_{rubbery}$ | Modulus drop across Tg | Pa |
| 8 | $T_g = T_\beta + 50 \pm 20$ | Empirical rule (Fredrickson) | K |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

Secondary transitions directly influence:
- **Cryogenic performance** — polyethylene becomes brittle at low T because its γ-transition is suppressed.
- **Impact strength** — the β-transition in polycarbonate contributes to its famous room-temperature toughness.
- **Damping / sound deadening** — polymers with active β-relaxation near room T are excellent vibration dampers.
- **Dielectric loss** — critical for capacitor and insulator design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Activation Energy from DMA Data

**Starting point:** The peak in $\tan\delta$ at temperature $T$ occurs at a fixed frequency $f$ in DMA. From Arrhenius behaviour:

$$\tau(T) = \tau_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

At the peak condition, the measurement frequency equals the natural relaxation frequency:

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau_0}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln f_{max} = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2\pi\tau_0}\right) - \frac{E_a}{RT}$$

Regroup into the linear form $y = mx + c$:

$$\ln f_{max} = \left[-\frac{E_a}{R}\right]\!\cdot\!\frac{1}{T} + \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2\pi\tau_0}\right)$$

Therefore, the slope of $\ln f_{max}$ vs. $1/T$ gives:

$$\boxed{E_a = -R \times (\text{slope})}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A DMA scan of PMMA shows a β-transition peak at:
- $T_1 = 300\,\text{K}$ at $f_1 = 1\,\text{Hz}$
- $T_2 = 320\,\text{K}$ at $f_2 = 100\,\text{Hz}$

Determine the **activation energy** $E_a$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Write the Arrhenius equation at both temperatures:

$$\ln f_1 = \ln f_0 - \frac{E_a}{RT_1} \quad ; \quad \ln f_2 = \ln f_0 - \frac{E_a}{RT_2}$$

Step 2 — Subtract the two equations (eliminates $\ln f_0$):

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{f_2}{f_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{100}{1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{320}\right)$$

Compute each side:

$$\ln(100) = 4.6052$$

$$\frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{320} = \frac{320 - 300}{300 \times 320} = \frac{20}{96000} = 2.0833 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

Step 4 — Solve for $E_a$:

$$E_a = \frac{R \cdot \ln(f_2/f_1)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)}$$

$$E_a = \frac{8.314 \times 4.6052}{2.0833 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = \frac{38.288}{2.0833 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 183{,}800\,\text{J/mol} \approx 183.8\,\text{kJ/mol}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value matches literature data for PMMA's β-transition (ester side-group rotation), which KTU examiners expect students to identify as a 3-mark descriptive answer.

### 3.3 Master Identification Matrix (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Reference Table for Identifying Secondary Transitions in Common Polymers:**

| Polymer | Tg (K) | Tβ (K) | Tγ (K) | Origin of β | Origin of γ |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|-------------|-------------|
| Polyethylene (PE) | 200 | — | 150 | None | Crankshaft (CH2)4 |
| Polystyrene (PS) | 373 | 325 | 140 | Phenyl torsion | — |
| PMMA | 378 | 300 | 110 | –COOCH3 rotation | –CH3 rotation |
| Polycarbonate (PC) | 423 | 380 | 170 | Phenyl–CO3 motion | Methylene crankshaft |
| PTFE | 400 | 300 | 170 | Amorphous phase | (CF2) crankshaft |
| Nylon-6,6 | 333 | 230 | 150 | Amide group motion | (CH2)4 crankshaft |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchy of Polymer Transitions

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the logical hierarchy from primary to secondary transitions:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Polymer Molecular Motion Onset"]:::root
    A --> B["Primary Transition Alpha"]:::primary
    A --> C["Secondary Transitions"]:::secondary

    B --> B1["Tg Glass Transition Cooperative 5 to 10 units"]
    B --> B2["Tm Melting Crystalline polymers only"]

    C --> D["Beta Transition Tg minus 20 to 100 K"]
    C --> E["Gamma Transition Tg minus 100 to 200 K"]
    C --> F["Delta Transition Below 100 K"]

    D --> D1["Side group rotation eg COOH COOCH3"]
    D --> D2["Short backbone segment motion"]

    E --> E1["Crankshaft motion CH2 n where n is 4 or more"]
    E --> E2["Methyl group rotation CH3"]

    F --> F1["Phenyl ring 180 degree flip"]
    F --> F2["End group rotation"]

    B1 --> G["Governed by WLF Equation"]:::theory
    D1 --> H["Governed by Arrhenius Equation"]:::theory
    E1 --> H

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef primary fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef secondary fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef theory fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a DMA Output Curve

The following schematic illustrates how a typical DMA trace reveals each transition:

```mermaid
graph LR
    AXIS["Temperature K"]:::axis --> PLOT["DMA Master Curve"]:::plot

    subgraph REGIONS["Storage Modulus E prime Behavior"]
        R1["Glassy Region E prime 10 to the power 9 Pa"]:::glass
        R2["Leathery Region Beta Transition peak in tan delta"]:::leathery
        R3["Rubbery Plateau Primary Alpha Tg"]:::rubbery
        R4["Flow Region"]:::flow
    end

    PLOT --> R1
    PLOT --> R2
    PLOT --> R3
    PLOT --> R4

    R1 --- NOTE1["Local motions only gamma and delta"]
    R2 --- NOTE2["Side group mobility increases"]
    R3 --- NOTE3["Cooperative backbone motion begins"]
    R4 --- NOTE4["Viscous flow dominates"]

    classDef axis fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef plot fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef glass fill:#a6a6a6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef leathery fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef rubbery fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef flow fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Identifying a Secondary Transition Experimentally

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: Cool sample below Tg"] --> S2["Step 2: Apply sinusoidal stress at fixed frequency f"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Record E prime and E double prime vs Temperature"]
    S3 --> S4{"Step 4: Identify peaks in tan delta or E double prime"}
    S4 --> S5["If peak shifts with frequency: Arrhenius beta or gamma"]
    S4 --> S6["If peak frequency independent: Crystalline melting"]
    S5 --> S7["Step 5: Run at multiple frequencies 0.1 to 100 Hz"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 6: Plot ln f versus 1 over T"]
    S8 --> S9["Step 7: Compute slope x minus R equals E a"]

    classDef step fill:#dbe5f1,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#000

    class S1,S2,S3,S5,S7,S8,S9 step
    class S4,S6 decision
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define secondary transitions in polymers. How are they distinguished from primary transitions?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Secondary transitions are reversible molecular motions occurring **below the glass transition temperature (Tg)**, involving localized rotation of side groups, short-chain segments, or pendant groups without cooperative backbone motion.

> **Distinguishing Features (2 Marks):**
> | Feature | Primary (Tg) | Secondary (Tβ, Tγ) |
> |---------|--------------|-------------------|
> | Cooperative motion | Yes (5–10 units) | No (localized) |
> | Kinetics | WLF | Arrhenius |
> | Activation energy | 300–1000 kJ/mol | 20–100 kJ/mol |
> | Magnitude of modulus drop | Large (~1000×) | Small (~10×) |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Explain the molecular origin of the β-transition in PMMA and the γ-transition in polyethylene.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **β-transition in PMMA (2 Marks):** The β-relaxation in poly(methyl methacrylate) at ~300 K arises from the **rotation of the ester side group (–COOCH₃)** about the C–C bond connecting it to the backbone. This motion requires ~80 kJ/mol of activation energy.

- **γ-transition in polyethylene (1 Mark):** The γ-relaxation in PE at ~150 K originates from the **crankshaft motion of (CH₂)₄ sequences**, where two carbon atoms remain stationary while four methylene units execute a coupled rotational motion.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, discuss the various secondary transitions observed in amorphous polymers. Highlight the molecular mechanisms responsible for each transition. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Amorphous polymers exhibit multiple sub-Tg relaxations labelled β, γ, δ in order of decreasing temperature.

1. **β-transition (7 Marks distributed across 4 points):**
   - **Origin:** Rotation of pendant side groups (e.g., –COOCH₃ in PMMA, –C₆H₅ in PS).
   - **Temperature range:** Tg − 20 K to Tg − 100 K.
   - **Activation energy:** 40–80 kJ/mol.
   - **Mechanical signature:** Small drop in E′ (~10×), distinct peak in tan δ.

2. **γ-transition:**
   - **Origin:** Localized crankshaft motion of (CH₂)ₙ where n ≥ 4 (Boyer's crankshaft mechanism).
   - **Temperature range:** 100–200 K.
   - **Activation energy:** 20–40 kJ/mol.

3. **δ-transition:**
   - **Origin:** Methyl group rotation (–CH₃ on side chains) or phenyl ring flips.
   - **Temperature range:** Below 100 K.
   - **Activation energy:** 10–20 kJ/mol.

> [!NOTE]
> Each transition appears as a **discrete peak in tan δ** in DMA thermograms. The number and intensity of these peaks is a fingerprint of polymer structure.

---

**(b)** Describe Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA). How is it used to detect secondary transitions and calculate their activation energies? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

1. **Principle of DMA (2 Marks):** DMA applies a small sinusoidal oscillating stress to a polymer sample and measures the resultant strain as a function of temperature and frequency. The response yields the complex modulus $E^{*} = E' + iE''$, where $E'$ is the storage modulus (elastic) and $E''$ is the loss modulus (viscous). The ratio $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ quantifies damping.

2. **Detection of Secondary Transitions (3 Marks):**
   - Sample is cooled below Tg and heated at a controlled rate (1–3 K/min).
   - At a fixed frequency (e.g., 1 Hz), $E'$, $E''$, and $\tan\delta$ are recorded.
   - Each transition manifests as a **peak in tan δ** or a **step in E′**.
   - β, γ, δ peaks appear progressively at lower temperatures.

3. **Calculation of Activation Energy (2 Marks):**
   - Repeating the experiment at multiple frequencies (0.1, 1, 10, 100 Hz).
   - Peak temperature $T_p$ shifts to higher values with increasing frequency.
   - Plotting $\ln f_{max}$ vs. $1/T_p$ gives a straight line.
   - From the derived equation:
   $$E_a = -R \cdot \frac{d(\ln f_{max})}{d(1/T)}$$
   - The slope × (−R) yields $E_a$ in J/mol.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Differentiate between primary and secondary transitions in polymers. Explain the WLF and Arrhenius equations and their applicability to each type of transition. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Differences (3 Marks):**

| Property | Primary (α) | Secondary (β, γ) |
|----------|-------------|------------------|
| Cooperative motion | Yes | No |
| Temperature | Highest | Lower than Tg |
| Free volume involved | Yes | No |
| Equation | WLF | Arrhenius |

**WLF Equation (2 Marks):**
$$\log a_T = -\frac{C_1(T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$
Applicable only for $T_g < T < T_g + 100\,\text{K}$. $C_1 \approx 17.44$, $C_2 \approx 51.6$ (universal values).

**Arrhenius Equation (2 Marks):**
$$\tau = \tau_0 \exp(E_a / RT)$$
Applicable to all sub-Tg relaxations because they are barrier-crossing events.

---

**(b)** Discuss the various methods used to study secondary transitions in polymers. Compare their sensitivity and information content. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

1. **Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA) (3 Marks):**
   - Most direct; sensitive to motion of ≥ 1 nm segments.
   - Provides $E'$, $E''$, $\tan\delta$.
   - Limitation: requires macroscopic sample.

2. **Dielectric Relaxation Spectroscopy (DRS) (2 Marks):**
   - Only for polar polymers (e.g., PMMA, PVDF).
   - Measures dipole reorientation via $\varepsilon'$ and $\varepsilon''$.
   - Extremely sensitive to side-group rotations.

3. **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) (1 Mark):**
   - Detects only large enthalpic transitions; often misses weak secondary transitions.

4. **NMR Relaxometry (1 Mark):**
   - Probes molecular dynamics at atomic scale via $T_1$ and $T_2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently lose marks by:
> 1. Confusing **secondary transition with Tg** — remember: secondary is ALWAYS below Tg.
> 2. Forgetting units of $E_a$ — must be **J/mol or kJ/mol**, never just J.
> 3. Writing the Arrhenius equation as $\exp(-E_a/RT)$ — for relaxation TIME use **positive exponent**; for FREQUENCY use negative.
> 4. Failing to mention that secondary transitions give **small** E′ drops compared to Tg.
> 5. Skipping the molecular mechanism in descriptive answers — KTU 2024 expects both *what* and *why*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Secondary Transitions":**

- ✅ Secondary transitions occur **below Tg** and are labelled β, γ, δ in decreasing temperature order.
- ✅ They involve **localized, non-cooperative** molecular motions (side groups, crankshaft, ring flips).
- ✅ They follow **Arrhenius kinetics**; the primary Tg follows WLF.
- ✅ **β-transition in PMMA** = ester rotation (~300 K); **γ in PE** = crankshaft motion (~150 K).
- ✅ Detection methods: **DMA, DRS, NMR, DSC** — DMA is the most sensitive.
- ✅ Activation energy formula: $E_a = -R \cdot \dfrac{d(\ln f_{max})}{d(1/T)}$.
- ✅ Empirical rule: $T_g \approx T_\beta + 50\,\text{K}$.
- ✅ Secondary transitions govern **impact strength, damping, and cryogenic toughness**.
- ✅ Peaks in **tan δ** in DMA are diagnostic; multiple peaks indicate multiple molecular processes.
- ✅ Activation energies: **β (40–80 kJ/mol), γ (20–40 kJ/mol), δ (10–20 kJ/mol)** — memorize the order.
- ✅ KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to link **structure → motion → property** in every descriptive answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Free Volume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Free Volume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🔬 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Free Volume (V_f)** in polymer science is defined as the *unoccupied, interstitial volume* available between polymer chains and segments in the amorphous regions of a polymer. Mathematically, it is the difference between the **specific volume** of the polymer at a given temperature and the **occupied volume** (van der Waals volume plus volume due to vibrational motions of the atoms).

$$
V_f = V_{total} - V_{occupied}
$$

The **Fractional Free Volume (f)** is the normalized dimensionless quantity most frequently used in engineering calculations:

$$
f = \frac{V_f}{V_{total}}
$$

For most amorphous polymers, the **critical fractional free volume at the glass transition temperature (T_g)** is approximately **0.025 ± 0.003 (≈ 2.5%)**. This is one of the most important and frequently quoted numbers in polymer physics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Free volume is NOT the same as "porosity" or "void." It is a *dynamic* quantity — it redistributes continuously as segments move. Above T_g, sufficient free volume exists for cooperative segmental motion; below T_g, the polymer is "frozen" into a glassy state.

## 🍝 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The Spaghetti Bowl

Imagine a bowl of cooked spaghetti:

- **Below T_g (cold bowl):** The spaghetti strands are packed tightly. If you push one strand, *nothing moves* — they are all jammed together. The bowl feels rigid.
- **Above T_g (warm bowl):** The strands have tiny gaps between them. You can push one strand and the others shuffle slightly. The bowl feels "rubbery" or "viscous."

The tiny gaps between the spaghetti strands = **Free Volume**.
The temperature at which gaps appear in just the right amount to let segments move = **Glass Transition Temperature (T_g)**.

| Polymer State | Temperature Range | Free Volume Availability | Physical Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Glassy | T < T_g | Very low (f < 0.025) | Rigid, brittle, hard |
| Rubbery | T_g < T < T_m | Moderate (f > 0.025) | Elastic, flexible |
| Viscous flow | T > T_m | High | Liquid-like flow |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *Free Volume = Freedom to Move*. More free volume = more chain mobility = softer behavior.

## 🎯 Why Free Volume Matters in Polymer Engineering

1. **Diffusion of penetrants** (O₂, CO₂, H₂O through membranes) — directly proportional to free volume.
2. **Viscoelastic behavior** — controls the onset of segmental motion at T_g.
3. **Viscosity-temperature dependence** — governed by the Doolittle equation.
4. **Creep and stress relaxation** — both depend on long-range segmental mobility enabled by free volume.
5. **Membrane separation technology** — free volume controls selectivity and permeability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧠 Conceptual Breakdown: The Four Pillars of Free Volume Theory

### Pillar 1 — The Fox–Flory Free Volume Theory (1950)

T.G. Fox and P.J. Flory proposed that **free volume is the controlling parameter for the glass transition**, not the thermal energy alone.

**Core Postulates:**
1. Free volume is *additive* — each chain end contributes an extra free volume compared to interior segments.
2. A polymer chain end has more free volume than a mid-chain segment because it has fewer bonding constraints.
3. Therefore, **as molecular weight (M_n) increases, T_g increases** because chain-end density decreases, reducing total free volume.
4. At T_g, a *critical* free volume (f_c ≈ 0.025) must be reached.

### Pillar 2 — The Doolittle Equation (1951)

Doolittle empirically established that the viscosity (η) of a liquid is related to its fractional free volume by an Arrhenius-type exponential relationship:

$$
\eta = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{f}\right)
$$

Where:
- **A** = pre-exponential constant (related to vibration frequency)
- **B** ≈ 1 (universal constant, typically 0.5 to 1.0)
- **f** = fractional free volume

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Doolittle equation is the **foundation** of the WLF equation and is tested in virtually every KTU Polymer Science exam.

### Pillar 3 — The WLF Equation (Williams–Landel–Ferry, 1955)

The shift factor (a_T) used in time-temperature superposition (TTS) is expressed as:

$$
\log_{10}(a_T) = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}
$$

**Universal Constants:**
- **C₁ = 17.44**
- **C₂ = 51.6 K**

> [!NOTE]
> The WLF equation is valid only in the range **T_g < T < T_g + 100°C**. Above this range, an Arrhenius equation should be used.

### Pillar 4 — The Simha–Boyer Rule (1962)

This rule connects the **thermal expansion coefficients** above and below T_g with the glass transition temperature:

$$
(\alpha_l - \alpha_g) \cdot T_g \approx 0.113
$$

This is sometimes written using the **bulk expansion coefficients** (β) as:

$$
(\beta_l - \beta_g) \cdot T_g \approx 0.164
$$

> [!TIP]
> Both forms appear in KTU past papers. Memorize **0.113** (linear) and **0.164** (bulk) carefully!

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Constants / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fractional Free Volume | $f = \dfrac{V_f}{V_{total}}$ | Dimensionless; f_c ≈ 0.025 at T_g |
| 2 | Doolittle Equation | $\eta = A \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{B}{f}\right)$ | B ≈ 1 for most polymers |
| 3 | WLF Equation | $\log_{10}(a_T) = -\dfrac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$ | C₁ = 17.44; C₂ = 51.6 K |
| 4 | Fox–Flory (Tg vs M_n) | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{M_n}$ | T_{g,∞} = T_g at infinite MW |
| 5 | Simha–Boyer (linear) | $(\alpha_l - \alpha_g) \cdot T_g \approx 0.113$ | α = linear expansion coefficient |
| 6 | Simha–Boyer (bulk) | $(\beta_l - \beta_g) \cdot T_g \approx 0.164$ | β = bulk (volumetric) expansion coefficient |
| 7 | Free Volume Thermal Expansion | $f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$ | α_f = thermal expansion of free volume; f_g ≈ 0.025 |
| 8 | Shift Factor Definition | $a_T = \dfrac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)}$ | Used in TTS master curves |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse α (linear) with β (volumetric). For an isotropic material, **β ≈ 3α**. Hence 0.113 × 3 ≈ 0.339 ≠ 0.164. The Simha–Boyer 0.164 is an empirical constant — do NOT derive it from 0.113!

## 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Free Volume Role |
|---|---|
| Gas separation membranes (e.g., CO₂/N₂) | High free volume = high permeability, low selectivity |
| Packaging films (food preservation) | Controls O₂ and moisture barrier properties |
| Tire rubber compounding | Affects rolling resistance and wet grip |
| Drug delivery polymers | Free volume controls diffusion rate of API |
| Injection molding | Determines melt viscosity and processing window |
| 3D printing resins | Free volume affects layer adhesion and shrinkage |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔢 Derivation 1: From Doolittle to WLF Equation

The WLF equation is mathematically derived from the Doolittle equation by substituting a linear thermal expansion model for the fractional free volume.

### Step 1 — Start with the Doolittle Equation

The viscosity at any temperature T can be written as:

$$
\eta(T) = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{f(T)}\right)
$$

Similarly, at the reference temperature T_g:

$$
\eta(T_g) = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{f(T_g)}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Shift Factor a_T

The shift factor is the ratio of viscosities:

$$
a_T = \frac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)}
$$

Substituting both Doolittle expressions:

$$
a_T = \frac{A \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{B}{f(T)}\right)}{A \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{B}{f(T_g)}\right)}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the Ratio

The pre-exponential constant A cancels:

$$
a_T = \exp\left[\frac{B}{f(T)} - \frac{B}{f(T_g)}\right]
$$

Taking the common denominator:

$$
a_T = \exp\left\{B \cdot \frac{f(T_g) - f(T)}{f(T) \cdot f(T_g)}\right\}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Linear Free Volume Expansion

Assume free volume expands linearly above T_g with thermal expansion coefficient α_f:

$$
f(T) = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)
$$

Where **f_g ≈ 0.025** is the fractional free volume at T_g. At T = T_g:

$$
f(T_g) = f_g
$$

Substituting into the shift factor:

$$
a_T = \exp\left\{B \cdot \frac{f_g - [f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)]}{[f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)] \cdot f_g}\right\}
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the Numerator

$$
f_g - [f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)] = -\alpha_f (T - T_g)
$$

So:

$$
a_T = \exp\left\{\frac{-B \cdot \alpha_f (T - T_g)}{[f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)] \cdot f_g}\right\}
$$

### Step 6 — Factor Out α_f from the Denominator

$$
a_T = \exp\left\{\frac{-B \cdot \alpha_f (T - T_g)}{\alpha_f f_g \left[\dfrac{f_g}{\alpha_f} + (T - T_g)\right]}\right\}
$$

Canceling α_f:

$$
a_T = \exp\left\{\frac{-B (T - T_g)}{f_g \left[\dfrac{f_g}{\alpha_f} + (T - T_g)\right]}\right\}
$$

### Step 7 — Convert to Base-10 Logarithm

Using $\log_{10}(x) = \dfrac{\ln(x)}{\ln(10)} = \dfrac{\ln(x)}{2.303}$:

$$
\ln(a_T) = \frac{-B (T - T_g)}{f_g \left[\dfrac{f_g}{\alpha_f} + (T - T_g)\right]}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(a_T) = \frac{-B}{2.303 \cdot f_g} \cdot \frac{(T - T_g)}{\left[\dfrac{f_g}{\alpha_f} + (T - T_g)\right]}
$$

### Step 8 — Identify the WLF Constants

Define:

$$
C_1 = \frac{B}{2.303 \cdot f_g}
$$

$$
C_2 = \frac{f_g}{\alpha_f}
$$

### Step 9 — Final WLF Equation

$$
\log_{10}(a_T) = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **frequently asked in KTU exams** (often as a 7-mark derivation). Memorize each step.

---

## 🔢 Derivation 2: Fox–Flory Equation from Free Volume Concepts

### Step 1 — Start with the Specific Volume Expression

For a polymer of molecular weight M_n, the specific volume V can be decomposed:

$$
V = V_{occupied} + V_{free}
$$

The free volume per chain end is greater than per interior segment. Let 2ρ_end be the *excess free volume* per chain end. Then total free volume per gram is:

$$
V_f = V_{free, interior} + \frac{2\rho_{end}}{M_n}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Critical Free Volume Condition at T_g

At T_g, the critical fractional free volume f_c must be reached:

$$
f_c = \frac{V_f(T_g)}{V(T_g)} = \text{constant}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for T_g

After algebraic manipulation (assuming occupied volume is roughly constant with M_n):

$$
T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{2\rho_{end}}{V_{occupied} \cdot \alpha_f \cdot M_n}
$$

Defining K = 2ρ_end / (V_occupied · α_f):

$$
\boxed{T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{M_n}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering meaning:** As M_n → ∞, T_g → T_{g,∞} (the maximum glass transition temperature). Short chains (low M_n) have many chain ends → more free volume → lower T_g.

---

## 🔢 Derivation 3: Simha–Boyer Rule from Thermal Expansion Data

### Step 1 — Define Expansion Coefficients

The volumetric (bulk) thermal expansion coefficient β is:

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P
$$

Above T_g, total free volume expansion = liquid expansion coefficient β_l. Below T_g, only vibrational expansion contributes = β_g.

### Step 2 — Critical Free Volume at T_g

The increase in free volume from 0 K up to T_g is:

$$
\Delta V_f = V \cdot (\beta_l - \beta_g) \cdot T_g
$$

By the Simha–Boyer postulate, this equals a critical free volume:

$$
V_f \cdot 0.025 = V \cdot (\beta_l - \beta_g) \cdot T_g
$$

### Step 3 — Empirical Constant

Dividing both sides by V:

$$
(\beta_l - \beta_g) \cdot T_g \approx 0.164
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is an empirical "rule of thumb" verified for over 50 polymers. It is NOT a derived constant — it comes from experimental data correlation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define free volume. What is the approximate value of the critical fractional free volume at the glass transition temperature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Free volume is defined as the unoccupied interstitial space present between polymer chains and chain segments in an amorphous polymer, representing the difference between the total specific volume and the occupied volume (van der Waals + vibrational volume).

> **Fractional Free Volume:** $f = \dfrac{V_f}{V_{total}}$

> The **critical fractional free volume at the glass transition temperature** is approximately **0.025 (2.5%)**, a value established experimentally for a wide range of amorphous polymers by Fox and Flory and later corroborated by the WLF analysis. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**State the Doolittle equation and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Doolittle equation relates the viscosity of a polymer liquid to its fractional free volume:

> $$
> \eta = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{f}\right)
> $$

> Where:
> - **η** = viscosity of the polymer melt (Pa·s)
> - **A** = pre-exponential constant, related to the vibration frequency of segments
> - **B** = constant (typically ≈ 1 for most polymers)
> - **f** = fractional free volume

> **Significance:** It demonstrates that viscosity is *exponentially* dependent on free volume, providing the theoretical foundation for the WLF equation and explaining the dramatic viscosity drop observed near T_g. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📝 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 1 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the WLF equation starting from the Doolittle equation. State the meaning of each constant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the Fox–Flory theory of free volume. How does molecular weight affect T_g according to this theory? **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📋 Model Solution for Question A (a)

**Derivation of WLF Equation from Doolittle Equation:**

**Step 1:** Write the Doolittle equation at any temperature T:

$$
\eta(T) = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{f(T)}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Write the Doolittle equation at reference temperature T_g:

$$
\eta(T_g) = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{f(T_g)}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Define the shift factor a_T as the ratio:

$$
a_T = \frac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)} = \exp\left[\frac{B}{f(T)} - \frac{B}{f(T_g)}\right]
$$

**Step 4:** Use the linear thermal expansion of free volume above T_g:

$$
f(T) = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)
$$

**Step 5:** Combine and simplify to obtain:

$$
a_T = \exp\left\{\frac{-B (T - T_g)}{f_g\left[\dfrac{f_g}{\alpha_f} + (T - T_g)\right]}\right\}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to base-10 logarithm:

$$
\log_{10}(a_T) = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}
$$

Where:

- **C₁ = B / (2.303 · f_g) ≈ 17.44** (universal constant)
- **C₂ = f_g / α_f ≈ 51.6 K** (universal constant)

**Physical Meaning:**
- **C₁:** Reflects the inverse of the critical free volume (C₁ ≈ 1/2.303 f_g)
- **C₂:** Reflects the ratio of critical free volume to its thermal expansion coefficient
- Both are termed "universal" because they apply to nearly all amorphous polymers. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📋 Model Solution for Question A (b)

**Fox–Flory Theory of Free Volume (1950):**

**Postulates:**
1. The free volume of a polymer is the sum of free volume contributions from chain segments plus extra contributions from chain ends.
2. A chain-end segment has *less constraints* (fewer neighboring bonded segments) than a mid-chain segment, and therefore contributes a larger amount of free volume.
3. The critical free volume (f_c ≈ 0.025) must be reached at T_g for segmental motion to occur.

**Effect of Molecular Weight on T_g:**

The Fox–Flory equation is:

$$
T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{M_n}
$$

Where:
- **T_{g,∞}** = glass transition temperature at infinite molecular weight
- **K** = Fox–Flory constant (polymer-specific)
- **M_n** = number-average molecular weight

**Engineering Implications:**
1. As **M_n increases**, T_g increases and asymptotically approaches T_{g,∞}.
2. As **M_n decreases**, more chain ends per unit mass exist, increasing free volume, reducing T_g.
3. For **M_n > critical value (M_c ≈ 10,000–20,000 g/mol)**, T_g becomes nearly independent of molecular weight.
4. Practical consequence: Low-M_w polymers (oligomers, plasticizers) act as free-volume enhancers and lower T_g — this is the principle behind plasticization.

**Numerical Example:**
For polystyrene, T_{g,∞} = 100°C and K = 1.5 × 10⁵. At M_n = 10,000:
T_g = 100 − (1.5 × 10⁵ / 10,000) = 100 − 15 = **85°C**

This validates the strong effect of M_n on T_g in oligomeric regimes. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students write "T_g increases with molecular weight" without quoting the Fox–Flory equation. Always provide the equation and explain the role of *chain-end free volume*. Marks are lost for missing the physical origin!

---

### ✅ **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 1 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the Simha–Boyer rule. What is its significance in polymer engineering? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer has a fractional free volume of 0.030 at 150°C and 0.040 at 200°C. If the glass transition temperature is 80°C and B = 1.0 in the Doolittle equation, calculate the viscosity at 150°C relative to that at 200°C. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📋 Model Solution for Question B (a)

**Simha–Boyer Rule (1962):**

The rule states that for most amorphous polymers:

$$
(\beta_l - \beta_g) \cdot T_g \approx 0.164
$$

Where:
- **β_l** = bulk (volumetric) thermal expansion coefficient above T_g
- **β_g** = bulk thermal expansion coefficient below T_g
- **T_g** = glass transition temperature (in Kelvin)

Equivalently, for linear expansion coefficients:

$$
(\alpha_l - \alpha_g) \cdot T_g \approx 0.113
$$

**Physical Significance:**

1. **Universality:** Valid for over 50 different polymers — strong evidence that T_g is fundamentally a free-volume phenomenon, not a thermal-energy phenomenon.
2. **Predictive Tool:** If α_l and α_g are measured by dilatometry, T_g can be predicted.
3. **Free-Volume Confirmation:** Combined with the assumption f_c ≈ 0.025, the rule is internally consistent — the jump in free volume at T_g is approximately a constant fraction (≈ 2.5%) of total volume.
4. **Engineering Utility:** Used to select polymers for applications requiring thermal dimensional stability (e.g., precision optical housings, aerospace composites).

**Derivation Hint:**
Starting from $V_f(T_g) = V(T_g) \cdot f_c$:
$V_f(T_g) = V \cdot (\beta_l - \beta_g) \cdot T_g$ — equate to f_c·V → constant ≈ 0.164. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📋 Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Given:**
- f(150°C) = 0.030
- f(200°C) = 0.040
- T_g = 80°C
- B = 1.0
- A = unknown (cancels out)

**Step 1 — Write Doolittle at 150°C:**

$$
\eta_{150} = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{1.0}{0.030}\right) = A \cdot \exp(33.33)
$$

**Step 2 — Write Doolittle at 200°C:**

$$
\eta_{200} = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{1.0}{0.040}\right) = A \cdot \exp(25.00)
$$

**Step 3 — Take the Ratio:**

$$
\frac{\eta_{150}}{\eta_{200}} = \frac{\exp(33.33)}{\exp(25.00)} = \exp(8.33)
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation:**

$$
\frac{\eta_{150}}{\eta_{200}} = e^{8.33} \approx 4128
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

The polymer at **150°C is approximately 4128 times more viscous** than at 200°C. This dramatic drop over just 50°C illustrates the extreme sensitivity of viscosity to free volume — a hallmark of polymeric fluids near T_g.

> **Answer:** $\dfrac{\eta_{150}}{\eta_{200}} \approx 4.13 \times 10^3$ **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget that A *cancels* in the ratio. Do NOT attempt to find A — it is unnecessary! Also, ensure the exponent is calculated to *at least 2 decimal places* (33.33 and 25.00, not 33 and 25). Marks are lost for premature rounding.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Points — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ Free volume = unoccupied space between polymer chains (NOT porosity)
- ✅ Fractional free volume: $f = V_f / V_{total}$, dimensionless
- ✅ Critical free volume at T_g: **f_c ≈ 0.025 (2.5%)**
- ✅ **Doolittle equation:** $\eta = A \exp(B/f)$ — viscosity is exponentially dependent on free volume
- ✅ **WLF equation:** $\log(a_T) = -C_1(T-T_g) / [C_2 + (T-T_g)]$ — valid only T_g < T < T_g + 100°C
- ✅ **Universal WLF constants:** C₁ = **17.44**, C₂ = **51.6 K**
- ✅ **Fox–Flory equation:** $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K/M_n$ — chain ends increase free volume
- ✅ **Simha–Boyer rule (bulk):** $(\beta_l - \beta_g) T_g ≈ 0.164$
- ✅ **Simha–Boyer rule (linear):** $(\alpha_l - \alpha_g) T_g ≈ 0.113$
- ✅ **Linear free volume expansion:** $f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$
- ✅ Below T_g → Arrhenius behavior (slow motion); Above T_g → WLF regime (rapid motion)
- ✅ Practical impact: Plasticizers work by *increasing free volume* and lowering T_g
- ✅ Membrane permeability scales directly with fractional free volume
- ✅ Doolittle equation is the *parent*; WLF is its *child* derived under linear thermal expansion assumption
- ✅ Fox–Flory and Simha–Boyer are *complementary* — both prove that T_g is governed by free volume, not thermal energy
- ✅ For M_n > ~20,000 g/mol, T_g is essentially independent of molecular weight

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"**D**oolittle **D**rops **V**iscosity with **F**ree volume"* — and *"**WLF** has **W**orld **L**aws **F**or **S**hift factors"*. Use these to recall equations under exam pressure!

---

## 🏁 END OF NOTES — MODULE 1: FREE VOLUME
