topics.title: Kinetic and Thermodynamic Views of Glass Transition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kinetic and Thermodynamic Views of Glass Transition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Glass Transition

**Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)** is the characteristic temperature at which an amorphous polymer (or the amorphous regions of a semi-crystalline polymer) undergoes a reversible transformation from a hard, brittle, **glassy state** to a soft, flexible, **rubbery state**. 

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, $T_g$ is defined as the temperature at which the segmental molecular motion of polymer chains becomes active on the timescale of the experimental observation, causing a discontinuity in the second-order thermodynamic derivatives (heat capacity $C_p$, coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha$, compressibility $\kappa$) while the first-order derivatives (volume $V$, enthalpy $H$, entropy $S$) change only in slope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The glass transition is *not* a true thermodynamic first-order phase transition (like melting). It is a *pseudo* or *second-order* transition that is governed by both **kinetic** (time-dependent) and **thermodynamic** (equilibrium-state) considerations. Students often lose marks by classifying it as a first-order transition.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molasses in a Jar"

Imagine pouring thick, sticky **honey** at room temperature — it flows slowly. Now put that same honey in a freezer at $-20^\circ C$. After a few hours, the honey becomes so stiff that it does not flow at all when you tilt the jar. It looks like a solid "glass" but chemically it is still honey — its molecules have just become so sluggish that, *on the timescale you are observing*, they appear frozen.

**Now the key insight:**
- If you check after **1 minute**, the honey might still flow a tiny bit (so it is "above $T_g$" for that timescale).
- If you check after **1 hour**, it is rock solid (effectively "below $T_g$" for that timescale).

This is the **kinetic essence** of $T_g$: it is not a fixed property of the material alone — it depends on **how fast you ask the question**. The honey's *true* thermodynamic ground state would be a perfectly ordered crystal, but it gets stuck in a non-equilibrium glassy configuration because molecules could not rearrange in time.

### 1.3 Three Universal Constants at $T_g$

At the glass transition, the following universal observations are made:

| Parameter | Approximate Universal Value | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|----------------------------|------------------|
| Free Volume Fraction $f_g$ | **0.025 ± 0.005** | Fraction of empty space needed for segmental motion |
| Relaxation Time $\tau$ | **~100 seconds** | Time scale of segmental motion |
| Thermal Energy $k_B T_g$ | — | Comparable to barriers for cooperative motion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Favourite:** The free-volume value of **2.5%** at $T_g$ is a universal constant for nearly all polymers — this is a high-yield KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Kinetic View of Glass Transition

The **kinetic view** treats $T_g$ as a phenomenon entirely governed by **molecular mobility and relaxation time**, not by equilibrium thermodynamics.

#### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Polymer segments are not static**: Above $T_g$, the ~10–50 carbon-atom "segments" of a chain undergo cooperative conformational jumps (trans-gauche isomerization).
2. **Each jump requires a critical free volume**: Without local void space of a critical size (~0.025 fraction), the segment cannot translate.
3. **Relaxation time $\tau$ is temperature-dependent**: As temperature decreases, thermal energy $k_B T$ drops, so the probability of surmounting the activation barrier for cooperative motion falls dramatically.
4. **Observation time sets the threshold**: When $\tau \approx t_{obs}$ (experiment time), the polymer appears to transition. Typical DSC experiments use a cooling rate of **10 °C/min** ($t_{obs} \approx 100$ s).
5. **$T_g$ is rate-dependent**: Faster cooling → less time for relaxation → higher apparent $T_g$. Slower cooling → more time → lower $T_g$ (closer to the ideal Kauzmann temperature).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) Insight:** WLF showed that if you define $T_g$ as the temperature where $\tau = 100$ s, then for *any* polymer, the constants $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6$ K are universal within a narrow range above $T_g$ (typically $T_g$ to $T_g + 100$ °C).

#### Free Volume Theory (Cohen & Turnbull, 1959)

The **free volume** $V_f$ is the space in a polymer not occupied by the molecules themselves (the "holes" between chains). Total specific volume is split as:

$$V_{total} = V_{occupied} + V_{free}$$

The fractional free volume is:

$$f = \frac{V_f}{V_{total}}$$

Free volume is **temperature-dependent**:

$$f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

where $\alpha_f$ is the expansion coefficient of free volume (≈ $\Delta \alpha = \alpha_l - \alpha_g$).

#### Step-Wise Cooling-Rate Dependence of $T_g$

A useful empirical formula (KTU high-yield):

$$\frac{1}{T_g(q)} - \frac{1}{T_g(q_0)} = -R \ln\left(\frac{q}{q_0}\right)$$

where $q$ is cooling rate and $R \approx 1.92 \times 10^{-3}$ K$^{-1}$ is a fitted constant.

### 2.2 The Thermodynamic View of Glass Transition

The **thermodynamic view** attempts to find an *equilibrium* basis for $T_g$, treating it as a true second-order phase transition.

#### Ehrenfest Classification

For a thermodynamic phase transition, the order is determined by which derivative of the Gibbs free energy $G$ first becomes discontinuous.

| Order | Discontinuous Quantity | Example |
|-------|------------------------|---------|
| 1st | $V, S, H$ (1st derivatives of $G$) | Melting, vaporization |
| 2nd | $C_p, \alpha, \kappa$ (2nd derivatives of $G$) | **Glass transition** |

- **First derivatives** ($V$, $H$, $S$) show a *change in slope* at $T_g$, not a jump.
- **Second derivatives** ($C_p$, $\alpha$, $\kappa$) show a *step discontinuity* (jump) at $T_g$.

#### Gibbs–DiMarzio Statistical Thermodynamic Theory (1958)

Gibbs and DiMarzio proposed that the *true* equilibrium glass transition occurs at a temperature $T_0$ (the **ideal glass transition temperature** or **Kauzmann temperature $T_K$**) where the **configurational entropy $S_c$** of the polymer vanishes:

$$S_c(T_0) = 0$$

Above $T_0$, $S_c$ increases with temperature. The configurational entropy is given by:

$$S_c(T) = S_c(T_g) + \int_{T_g}^{T} \frac{\Delta C_p}{T} dT$$

Setting $S_c(T_0) = 0$ yields:

$$T_0 \approx T_g - \frac{C_2}{C_1} \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta C_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Kauzmann's Paradox:** If you extrapolate the entropy of a supercooled liquid below $T_g$, it would drop below the entropy of the crystal — a thermodynamic impossibility. Gibbs–DiMarzio resolved this: the liquid *cannot* reach this state because relaxation times diverge, "freezing" it into the glass before $T_K$ is reached.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are **examination-critical**. Memorize the symbol, units, and the physical meaning of each.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\log a_T = -\dfrac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$ | **WLF equation** — shift factor for relaxation time near $T_g$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | $a_T = \dfrac{\tau(T)}{\tau(T_g)}$ | Definition of WLF shift factor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\ln \eta = \ln A + \dfrac{B}{f}$ | **Doolittle equation** — viscosity from free volume | Pa·s |
| 4 | $\eta = A \exp\left(\dfrac{B}{T - T_0}\right)$ | **Vogel–Fulcher–Tammann (VFT)** equation | Pa·s |
| 5 | $\tau = \tau_0 \exp\left(\dfrac{B}{T - T_0}\right)$ | VFT form for relaxation time | s |
| 6 | $f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$ | Free volume fraction vs. $T$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | $f_g \approx 0.025$ | Universal free volume at $T_g$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Delta \alpha = \alpha_l - \alpha_g$ | Step in thermal expansion coefficient at $T_g$ | K$^{-1}$ |
| 9 | $\Delta C_p = C_{p,l} - C_{p,g}$ | Step in heat capacity at $T_g$ | J/(mol·K) |
| 10 | $S_c(T_0) = 0$ | Gibbs–DiMarzio equilibrium condition | J/(mol·K) |

> **Universal WLF constants (near $T_g$):** $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K.
> **Reference $a_T = 1$ at $T = T_g$.**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PET beverage bottles**: The bottle is blow-molded just above $T_g$ (75 °C) to set the shape; below $T_g$ it is dimensionally stable for shelf life.
- **Rubber tire design**: Natural rubber is used well above its $T_g$ ($-72$ °C) for elasticity; formulation additives keep $T_g$ low even in cold climates.
- **Polymer processing**: Injection molding requires the melt to be ~100 °C above $T_g$ to ensure adequate flow.
- **3D printing (FDM)**: Glassy polymers like PLA have $T_g \approx 60$ °C — printed parts deform if used near a hot car dashboard.
- **Cryogenic applications**: Selecting polymers with $T_g$ below the operating temperature prevents brittle failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the WLF Equation from the Doolittle Equation

This is a **classic KTU long-answer derivation** (worth 7–10 marks). The WLF equation is *not* empirical — it is derived rigorously from the Doolittle free-volume equation.

#### Starting Point: Doolittle Equation

The Doolittle equation relates viscosity to free volume:

$$\ln \eta = \ln A + \frac{B}{f}$$

where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential viscosity (Pa·s)
- $B \approx 1$ (universal constant, dimensionless)
- $f = V_f / V$ = fractional free volume

#### Step 1 — Express Viscosity as a Function of Temperature

Assume free volume is linear in temperature:

$$f(T) = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

#### Step 2 — Evaluate at Reference Temperature $T_g$

$$f(T_g) = f_g = 0.025$$

So at $T_g$:

$$\ln \eta(T_g) = \ln A + \frac{B}{f_g}$$

#### Step 3 — Form the Ratio

Take the difference at two temperatures:

$$\ln \eta(T) - \ln \eta(T_g) = B\left(\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f_g}\right)$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)}\right) = B\left(\frac{f_g - f}{f \cdot f_g}\right)$$

#### Step 4 — Substitute the Linear $f(T)$ Expression

$$f_g - f = f_g - \left[f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)\right] = -\alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

So:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)}\right) = \frac{-B \, \alpha_f (T - T_g)}{f \cdot f_g}$$

#### Step 5 — Identify the Shift Factor

Since $\tau \propto \eta$ (Eyring relation), we define:

$$a_T = \frac{\tau(T)}{\tau(T_g)} = \frac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)}$$

For temperatures *close* to $T_g$ (within ~100 K), $f \approx f_g$, so:

$$\ln a_T \approx \frac{-B \, \alpha_f (T - T_g)}{f_g^2}$$

#### Step 6 — Convert to Base-10 Logarithm

$$\ln a_T = 2.303 \log_{10} a_T$$

$$\log_{10} a_T = \frac{-B}{2.303 \, f_g^2} \cdot \frac{\alpha_f (T - T_g)}{1}$$

Define:

$$C_1 = \frac{B}{2.303 \, f_g^2}, \qquad C_2 = \frac{f_g}{\alpha_f}$$

#### Step 7 — Recognize the WLF Form

The full expression becomes:

$$\boxed{\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}}$$

Plugging in $B = 1$ and $f_g = 0.025$:

$$C_1 = \frac{1}{2.303 \times (0.025)^2} = \frac{1}{2.303 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1}{1.439 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 17.44$$

This matches the **universal WLF constant**. Typically $\alpha_f \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-4}$ K$^{-1}$, giving:

$$C_2 = \frac{0.025}{4.8 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 51.6 \text{ K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The fact that $C_1 \approx 17.44$ and $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K are *universal* across many polymers is *because* the free volume fraction $f_g \approx 0.025$ is universal. This is a beautifully interconnected theory and is a high-yield concept.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Two Views

| Feature | Kinetic View | Thermodynamic View |
|---------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Originator** | Williams, Landel, Ferry (1955); Cohen, Turnbull (1959) | Gibbs, DiMarzio (1958); Kauzmann (1948) |
| **Nature of $T_g$** | A kinetic freezing-in phenomenon | A second-order transition / equilibrium state |
| **Order parameter** | Relaxation time $\tau$ | Configurational entropy $S_c$ |
| **Core equation** | WLF / VFT / Doolittle | $S_c(T_0) = 0$ |
| **Key parameter** | $f_g \approx 0.025$ | $T_0$ (Kauzmann temperature) |
| **Cooling-rate dependent?** | **Yes** (strongly) | No (in theory) |
| **Predicts $T_g$?** | At a given cooling rate | Predicts the *lowest possible* $T_g$ (ideal glass) |
| **Below $T_g$ state** | Non-equilibrium glass | Equilibrium glass (theoretical) |
| **Relation to melting** | Independent | $T_g / T_m \approx 0.5$ to $0.67$ (Boyer–Beaman rule) |
| **Failure mode if ignored** | $T_g$ shifts with processing rate | Kauzmann paradox (entropy crisis) |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A polymer has $T_g = 373$ K, $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K. A creep experiment is run at $T = 423$ K. Find the shift factor $a_T$ that maps this measurement to a reference temperature of $T_g$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $(T - T_g)$:

$$T - T_g = 423 - 373 = 50 \text{ K}$$

Step 2 — Plug into WLF:

$$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{17.44 \times 50}{51.6 + 50} = -\frac{872}{101.6}$$

$$\log_{10} a_T = -8.583$$

Step 3 — Convert:

$$a_T = 10^{-8.583} \approx 2.61 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Interpretation:** The polymer flows **2.6 billion times slower at $T_g$** than at $T_g + 50$ K. This extreme sensitivity is why DSC at fixed heating rate gives a sharp, well-defined $T_g$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Student Error:** Forgetting that $C_2 + (T - T_g)$ is in the denominator — a frequent sign error that flips the sign and gives $a_T > 1$ when it should be $< 1$ for $T > T_g$. Above $T_g$, segments move faster → relaxation time decreases → $a_T < 1$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Glass Transition Theories

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["GLASS TRANSITION Tg"] --> nodeB["KINETIC VIEW"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["THERMODYNAMIC VIEW"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Segmental Motion"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Relaxation Time tau"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Free Volume Theory"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Doolittle Equation"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["WLF Equation"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Shift Factor aT"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["Universal C1 C2"]
    nodeC --> nodeK["Gibbs Free Energy G"]
    nodeC --> nodeL["Configurational Entropy Sc"]
    nodeK --> nodeM["Ehrenfest 2nd Order"]
    nodeL --> nodeN["Gibbs DiMarzio Theory"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Kauzmann Temperature T0"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["Sc Equals Zero"]
    nodeI --> nodeQ["Cooling Rate Effect"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeR["Apparent Tg Increases with Cooling Rate"]
    nodeJ --> nodeR
    nodeM --> nodeR
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — How a DSC Experiment Detects $T_g$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["Polymer Sample"] --> nodeQ["DSC Furnace Controlled Cooling"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["Heat Flow Measurement"]
    nodeR --> nodeS["Step in Cp at Tg"]
    nodeS --> nodeT["Kinetic Interpretation tau Equals 100s"]
    nodeS --> nodeU["Thermodynamic Interpretation Cp Jump"]
    nodeT --> nodeV["Apparent Tg is Cooling Rate Dependent"]
    nodeU --> nodeW["Independent of Rate at True Equilibrium"]
    nodeV --> nodeX["WLF Predicts Shift"]
    nodeW --> nodeY["Gibbs DiMarzio Predicts Limit T0"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Volume vs. Temperature (Block Representation of $T_g$)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Above Tg"
        dirA["Liquid or Rubbery State Slope alpha_l Steep"]
    end
    subgraph "At Tg"
        dirB["Step in Slope delta alpha"]
    end
    subgraph "Below Tg"
        dirC["Glassy State Slope alpha_g Shallow"]
    end
    dirA --> dirB --> dirC
    dirB -.-> noteA["Note Slope changes NOT a discontinuity in V"]
    noteA -.-> dirB
```

**Reading the Diagram:** On cooling, the volume of the polymer decreases linearly with slope $\alpha_l$ in the rubbery state. At $T_g$, the slope *changes abruptly* (not the volume itself) to $\alpha_g$ in the glassy state. The magnitude of the slope change is $\Delta \alpha = \alpha_l - \alpha_g$. This single observation is the experimental fingerprint of a second-order transition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Examiners:** Always emphasize in a sketch that the **line is continuous at $T_g$** — only the *slope* changes. A discontinuous jump in $V$ would indicate first-order (e.g., melting), which is a common student mistake.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
**Q1. Define glass transition temperature. Why is it not considered a true thermodynamic first-order transition?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- $T_g$ is the temperature at which an amorphous polymer transitions from a hard, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state (1 mark).
- It is not a first-order transition because there is **no discontinuous jump** in volume, enthalpy, or entropy at $T_g$ — only the *slope* of these quantities changes (1 mark).
- Instead, a *step discontinuity* is observed in **second-order derivatives** ($C_p$, $\alpha$, $\kappa$), classifying it as a **second-order (Ehrenfest) transition** with significant kinetic character (1 mark).

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
**Q2. State the Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) equation and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Equation: $\log_{10} a_T = -\dfrac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$ (1.5 marks).
- $a_T$ is the shift factor — the ratio of relaxation time (or viscosity) at temperature $T$ to that at $T_g$ (0.5 mark).
- $C_1 \approx 17.44$ and $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K are universal constants valid in the range $T_g \leq T \leq T_g + 100$ °C (1 mark).

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)**

> **KTU Rule:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Modified]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **kinetic view of glass transition**. Derive the WLF equation starting from the Doolittle equation and explain the physical significance of the universal constants $C_1$ and $C_2$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polymer has $T_g = 350$ K. Compute the WLF shift factor at $T = 380$ K and $T = 420$ K. If the relaxation time at $T_g$ is 100 s, what is the relaxation time at these two temperatures? Comment on the engineering significance.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Kinetic View + WLF Derivation:**

- Kinetic view: $T_g$ is governed by **molecular mobility** and **relaxation time**, not equilibrium thermodynamics (1 mark).
- Below $T_g$, segmental relaxation time $\tau$ exceeds the experimental observation time, so the polymer appears rigid (1 mark).
- Free volume theory: at $T_g$, free volume fraction $f_g \approx 0.025$; without this critical void space, segments cannot execute conformational jumps (1 mark).
- **Derivation** (4 marks): Follow the 7-step derivation in Section 3.1 — start from Doolittle $\ln \eta = \ln A + B/f$, substitute $f(T) = f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)$, form the ratio, apply $f \approx f_g$ near $T_g$, convert to base-10 log, identify $C_1 = B/(2.303 f_g^2)$ and $C_2 = f_g/\alpha_f$.
- **Physical significance of $C_1$ and $C_2$**: Their universality across polymers arises because the free volume at $T_g$ is universal at 2.5%. $C_1$ reflects the *fractional* free volume requirement; $C_2$ reflects the *thermal expansion* of free volume (1 mark, partial credit if framed only mathematically).

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

Given: $T_g = 350$ K, $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K, $\tau(T_g) = 100$ s.

**At $T = 380$ K:**

$$\Delta T = 380 - 350 = 30 \text{ K}$$

$$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{17.44 \times 30}{51.6 + 30} = -\frac{523.2}{81.6} = -6.412$$

$$a_T = 10^{-6.412} \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\tau(380) = \tau(T_g) \times a_T = 100 \times 3.87 \times 10^{-7} = 3.87 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} \approx 39 \text{ µs}$$

**At $T = 420$ K:**

$$\Delta T = 420 - 350 = 70 \text{ K}$$

$$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{17.44 \times 70}{51.6 + 70} = -\frac{1220.8}{121.6} = -10.04$$

$$a_T = 10^{-10.04} \approx 9.12 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$\tau(420) = 100 \times 9.12 \times 10^{-11} = 9.12 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} \approx 9 \text{ ns}$$

**Engineering Significance (2 marks):**
- Going from $T_g$ to $T_g + 30$ K decreases relaxation time by ~6 orders of magnitude; to $T_g + 70$ K, by ~10 orders of magnitude.
- This **extreme sensitivity** is why polymers are processed at temperatures well above $T_g$ — but only by ~50–100 °C, not by 200 °C, because viscosity plateaus at very high $T$.
- It also explains why DSC measurements at high heating rates give artificially **higher** $T_g$ than slow cooling experiments.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **thermodynamic view of glass transition** with reference to the Gibbs–DiMarzio theory. Explain the concept of the **Kauzmann temperature** and the **configurational entropy** criterion.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **kinetic and thermodynamic views** of glass transition in a detailed table. Explain how the two views are reconciled in modern polymer physics.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Thermodynamic View + Gibbs–DiMarzio:**

- Thermodynamic view: attempts to define $T_g$ as an **equilibrium** second-order phase transition (1 mark).
- Ehrenfest classification: $C_p$, $\alpha$, $\kappa$ show a step discontinuity at $T_g$, while $V$, $H$, $S$ show a change in slope (1 mark).
- **Gibbs–DiMarzio theory** (1958): proposes that the true equilibrium glass transition occurs at $T_0$ where the **configurational entropy $S_c = 0$** (1 mark).
- Above $T_0$, the polymer has many accessible chain conformations; below, only one (the "frozen" state) (1 mark).
- The entropy crisis (Kauzmann paradox): extrapolating supercooled liquid entropy below $T_g$ would yield a value *lower* than the crystal's entropy, which is impossible — hence the polymer must "freeze" into a glass (1 mark).
- Mathematical form: $S_c(T) = S_c(T_g) + \int_{T_g}^{T} \Delta C_p / T \, dT = 0$ at $T = T_0$ (1 mark).
- The difference between observed $T_g$ and $T_0$ is the *kinetic offset* — slow experiments approach $T_0$; fast experiments give higher $T_g$ (1 mark).

**(b) Comparative Table + Reconciliation (7 marks):**

Provide the table from Section 3.2 (4 marks for table), and add the following discussion on reconciliation:

- **Reconciliation**: Modern polymer physics treats $T_g$ as a **kinetically controlled approach to a thermodynamic ideal**. The Gibbs–DiMarzio $T_0$ is the *limiting value* of $T_g$ as cooling rate → 0 (1 mark).
- Adam–Gibbs entropy theory links the two: $\tau = \tau_0 \exp(C / T S_c)$ — relaxation time diverges as $S_c \to 0$, providing a thermodynamic basis for the kinetic observation (1 mark).
- Practical implication: processing windows, aging behavior, and physical aging are all explained using *both* views together (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT call $T_g$ a first-order transition** — that is the #1 most common error. Always specify *second-order* (Ehrenfest).
> 2. **Do NOT confuse Kauzmann temperature $T_K$ with Debye temperature** or with $T_g$ itself. $T_K$ is the *hypothetical* temperature where $S_c = 0$, *below* the experimentally observed $T_g$.
> 3. **Units of WLF constants**: $C_1$ is dimensionless, $C_2$ is in **Kelvin** (or °C, same magnitude). Mixing units is a sign-error trap.
> 4. **Sign of $\log a_T$**: Always negative for $T > T_g$ — segmental motion speeds up above $T_g$, so relaxation time decreases.
> 5. **Free volume definition**: Total volume $V$ = *occupied* volume $V_0$ + *free* volume $V_f$. Some students wrongly include vibrational volume in $V_f$ — only the *fluctuational, redistributable* void counts.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Bullet Points:**

- $T_g$ is a **second-order** (Ehrenfest) transition, not first-order.
- $T_g$ is **kinetic in nature** — it depends on experimental timescale and cooling/heating rate.
- Above $T_g$: rubbery, segmental motion active, $\alpha_l$ thermal expansion.
- Below $T_g$: glassy, segmental motion frozen on experimental timescale, $\alpha_g$ thermal expansion.
- $\Delta \alpha = \alpha_l - \alpha_g > 0$ and $\Delta C_p > 0$ — these are the experimental signatures.
- **Universal free volume at $T_g$**: $f_g \approx 0.025$ (2.5%).
- **Universal WLF constants**: $C_1 \approx 17.44$, $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K (valid $T_g$ to $T_g + 100$ °C).
- **WLF equation**: $\log a_T = -C_1(T - T_g) / [C_2 + (T - T_g)]$.
- **VFT equation**: $\tau = \tau_0 \exp[B/(T - T_0)]$ — diverges at $T_0$.
- **Doolittle equation**: $\ln \eta = \ln A + B/f$ — links viscosity to free volume.
- **Kauzmann temperature $T_K$**: where extrapolated $S_c = 0$, always below experimental $T_g$.
- **Gibbs–DiMarzio criterion**: $S_c(T_0) = 0$ — defines ideal equilibrium glass transition.
- **Adam–Gibbs relation**: $\tau = \tau_0 \exp(C / T S_c)$ — unifies kinetic and thermodynamic views.
- **Cooling rate effect**: faster cooling → higher apparent $T_g$; slower → lower $T_g$ (approaches $T_K$).
- **Boyer–Beaman rule**: $T_g / T_m \approx 0.5$–$0.67$ (symmetric polymers ~0.5, asymmetric ~0.67).
- **$T_g$ measured by DSC** at standard 10 °C/min — any deviation must be reported.
- **Practical use**: $T_g$ sets the **lower bound of rubbery processing**, the **upper bound of dimensional stability**, and the **embrittlement temperature** of plastics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Influencing Glass Transition Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors Influencing Glass Transition Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)

The **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)** is defined as the characteristic temperature at which an amorphous polymer (or the amorphous regions of a semi-crystalline polymer) undergoes a reversible transition from a hard, glassy, rigid state to a soft, rubbery, flexible state. At this temperature, the long-range segmental motion of the polymer backbone becomes activated, while only localized vibrations and short-range motions persist below Tg.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "The Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) is the temperature at which the thermal expansion coefficient, heat capacity, and free volume of a polymer undergo an abrupt, second-order thermodynamic change due to the onset of cooperative segmental mobility along the polymer chain."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Honey in a Freezer" Model

Imagine a jar of pure honey placed inside a freezer.

- **Below Tg (Frozen State):** The honey becomes rigid, brittle, and resists flow. If you tap the jar, it does not move. The molecules are essentially "locked" in place. This mimics the **glassy state** of a polymer where only vibrational motion is possible.
- **At Tg (The Thawing Point):** The honey begins to flow very slowly. It is no longer brittle but is not yet freely flowing. This is the **rubbery/leathery transition** of the polymer.
- **Above Tg (Pourable Honey):** The honey flows freely. The molecules have gained enough thermal energy to slide past each other. This is the **rubbery/viscous flow** state of the polymer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Polymer Engineers:** Tg is NOT a sharp phase transition like melting (Tm). It is a **second-order thermodynamic transition** occurring over a temperature range of **5–20 °C**, and it is highly sensitive to the method of measurement (DSC, DMA, dilatometry) and the heating/cooling rate.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Range for Polymers | Units |
|----------|--------|---------------------------|-------|
| Glass Transition Temperature | Tg | −100 to +250 | °C (or K) |
| Thermal Expansion Coefficient (glassy) | αg | 1.0 × 10⁻⁴ to 3.0 × 10⁻⁴ | K⁻¹ |
| Thermal Expansion Coefficient (rubbery) | αr | 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ to 7.0 × 10⁻⁴ | K⁻¹ |
| Change in Cp at Tg | ΔCp | 10 to 50 | J·(mol·K)⁻¹ |
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314** | J·(mol·K)⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DSC Thermogram Signature:** On a Differential Scanning Calorimeter (DSC) curve, Tg appears as a **step change in the baseline heat flow** (NOT a peak). A melting peak (Tm) is endothermic and distinct, but Tg is a subtle inflection — examiners frequently test this distinction.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Twelve Principal Factors Influencing Tg

The value of Tg for a given polymer is governed by a complex interplay of structural, molecular, and environmental factors. For the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 examination, the following factors are **high-priority** and frequently tested:

#### Factor 1: Chain Flexibility (Backbone Stiffness)
- **Stiff backbones** (e.g., aromatic rings, conjugated double bonds) **RESTRICT** segmental rotation → **HIGH Tg**.
- **Flexible backbones** (e.g., −CH₂−CH₂−, −CH₂−O−, −Si−O−) permit easy rotation → **LOW Tg**.
- **Example:** Polyethylene (PE) has Tg ≈ −120 °C, while polystyrene (PS) has Tg ≈ 100 °C (aromatic ring restriction).

#### Factor 2: Molecular Weight (Chain End Effect)
- **Low molecular weight** = more chain ends per unit volume = more free volume = **LOW Tg**.
- **High molecular weight** = fewer chain ends = less free volume = **HIGH Tg**, asymptotically approaching Tg(∞).
- Governed by the **Fox–Flory equation**.

#### Factor 3: Crosslinking
- Crosslinks **restrict** segmental motion → **Tg INCREASES** with crosslink density.
- In thermosets (e.g., cured epoxies), Tg is a direct measure of the degree of cure.

#### Factor 4: Branching
- **Short branches** increase free volume → **decrease Tg**.
- **Long branches** may entangle and act like a network → **increase Tg**.

#### Factor 5: Intermolecular Forces (Cohesive Energy Density)
- Strong secondary forces (H-bonding, dipole–dipole, ion–dipole) **raise Tg** dramatically.
- **Example:** Nylon-6,6 (Tg ≈ 50 °C) vs. Polyethylene (Tg ≈ −120 °C) — H-bonding in nylon.

#### Factor 6: Steric Hindrance (Bulky Side Groups)
- **Symmetric** bulky groups (e.g., −CH₃, −C₂H₅) increase free volume → **decrease Tg**.
- **Polar/Asymmetric** bulky groups (e.g., −C₆H₅, −Cl) restrict rotation → **increase Tg**.

#### Factor 7: Tacticity (Stereoregularity)
- **Isotactic/Syndiotactic** polymers pack better and may crystallize, raising Tg of amorphous regions.
- **Atactic** polymers are amorphous with more free volume → typically **lower Tg**.
- **Example:** Atactic PS (Tg ≈ 100 °C) is amorphous; isotactic PS is semicrystalline (Tm ≈ 240 °C).

#### Factor 8: Plasticizers
- Low-molecular-weight additives that **increase free volume** and **dilute** intermolecular forces.
- Cause a **significant decrease in Tg** — described by the **Fox equation** and **Gordon–Taylor equation**.

#### Factor 9: Crystallinity
- In semi-crystalline polymers, the crystalline phase is **immobile** and effectively dilutes the amorphous phase.
- Increased crystallinity may **slightly raise the apparent Tg** of the amorphous fraction (constraint effect).

#### Factor 10: Pressure
- Higher pressure **reduces free volume** → **Tg INCREASES** with pressure (typically dTg/dP ≈ 0.2 to 0.5 K/MPa).

#### Factor 11: Thermal History & Free Volume
- Rapid cooling → more frozen-in free volume → **lower apparent Tg**.
- Slow cooling → denser packing → **higher apparent Tg**.

#### Factor 12: Degree of Copolymerization (Copolymer Composition)
- For random copolymers, Tg follows the **Fox equation** based on weight fractions of comonomers.
- For block/graft copolymers, **two Tg values** may be observed (microphase separation).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **examination-critical**. Memorize the form, boundary conditions, and physical meaning of each.

| # | Formula | Name | Application | Validity |
|---|---------|------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{M_n}$ | Fox–Flory Equation | Molecular weight dependence | $M_n > M_{c}$ (critical entanglement) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$ | Fox Equation (Copolymer/Blend) | Random copolymer, miscible blend | Weight fractions $w_1 + w_2 = 1$ |
| 3 | $T_g = w_1 T_{g,1} + w_2 T_{g,2}$ | Gordon–Taylor Equation | Miscible polymer blend (compatible) | $K_{GT}$ is the Gordon–Taylor parameter |
| 4 | $\ln T_g = \dfrac{w_1 \ln T_{g,1} + k \, w_2 \ln T_{g,2}}{w_1 + k \, w_2}$ | Couchman–Karasz Equation | Miscible polymer–plasticizer blend | $k = \Delta C_{p,2} / \Delta C_{p,1}$ |
| 5 | $T_g \approx 0.5 \, T_m$ (in K) | Beaman–Boyer Rule | Approximate relation | Only for symmetric, flexible polymers |
| 6 | $\alpha_f(T_g) \approx 0.025$ | Free Volume Criterion | Universal at Tg (Doolittle) | Approximately constant for all polymers |
| 7 | $T_g = T_{g,0} + \lambda \, \rho_x$ | Crosslink Density Relation | Thermoset polymers | $\rho_x$ = crosslink density (mol/m³) |
| 8 | $\log \eta_T = A + \dfrac{B}{T - T_0}$ | WLF Equation (related to Tg) | Viscous flow above Tg | $T_0 \approx T_g - 50$ K |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Unit Consistency:** In **Fox–Flory**, $T_g$ and $T_{g,\infty}$ MUST be in Kelvin (K). Conversions from Celsius to Kelvin (add 273.15) are frequent in numerical problems and a common source of mark deduction.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Rubber Industry:** Natural rubber (Tg ≈ −73 °C) stays flexible in winter; tire performance is engineered by maintaining Tg well below service temperature.
> - **Packaging:** PET bottles (Tg ≈ 70 °C) are blow-molded above Tg but below Tm (260 °C) to achieve biaxial orientation.
> - **Adhesives:** Pressure-sensitive adhesives (e.g., acrylic PSA) are formulated with Tg ~ −40 to −20 °C for permanent tackiness.
> - **Composites/Aerospace:** Epoxy matrix Tg dictates the maximum service temperature of a fiber-reinforced composite (e.g., carbon/epoxy prepreg cured to Tg > 180 °C).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fox–Flory Equation for Molecular Weight Dependence of Tg

The **Fox–Flory equation** is the most-tested equation in KTU Polymer Science examinations concerning Tg. The following is a complete, line-by-line derivation that a 14-mark answer would require.

#### Step 1 — Starting Hypothesis (Free Volume Argument)

Chain ends possess greater mobility and contribute excess free volume compared to internal repeat units. The total free volume $V_f$ of a polymer sample of mass $m$ and number-average molecular weight $M_n$ is:

$$V_f = V_{f,\text{internal}} + V_{f,\text{ends}}$$

For one mole of polymer chains:

$$V_f = N_A \left( v_0 + 2 \, v_e \right)$$

where $v_0$ is the free volume per internal repeat unit, $v_e$ is the excess free volume per chain end, and $N_A$ is Avogadro's number. Since there are 2 chain ends per linear polymer chain, the factor of 2 appears.

#### Step 2 — Free Volume per Mole of Repeat Units

The number of repeat units per mole of chains is $\frac{M_n}{M_0}$, where $M_0$ is the molar mass of one repeat unit. The fractional free volume per repeat unit is therefore:

$$f = \frac{V_f}{N_A \cdot M_0 / M_n} \cdot \frac{1}{V_{molar}} = f_\infty + \frac{A}{M_n}$$

where $f_\infty$ is the free volume fraction at infinite molecular weight, and $A$ is a constant proportional to $v_e$.

#### Step 3 — Connection to Glass Transition (Doolittle Equation)

The viscosity of a polymer above Tg follows the Doolittle equation:

$$\eta = A' \exp\left(\frac{B'}{f}\right)$$

The glass transition occurs at a critical free volume fraction $f_g \approx 0.025$ (a universal constant for most polymers). At Tg, the free volume fraction equals $f_g$:

$$f_g = f_\infty + \frac{A}{M_n}$$

#### Step 4 — Linear Thermal Expansion Assumption

The free volume fraction varies with temperature according to the thermal expansion coefficient difference $\Delta\alpha = \alpha_r - \alpha_g$:

$$f = f_g + \Delta\alpha \, (T - T_g)$$

At $T = T_g$, $f = f_g$ (by definition), giving:

$$f_g = f_\infty(T_g) + \frac{A}{M_n}$$

#### Step 5 — Solving for Tg

Using the fact that $f_\infty$ increases linearly with T above the reference temperature:

$$f_\infty(T) = f_\infty(T_g^\infty) + \Delta\alpha \, (T - T_g^\infty)$$

Setting $f = f_g$ at $T = T_g$ and solving:

$$T_g = T_g^\infty - \frac{A}{\Delta\alpha} \cdot \frac{1}{M_n}$$

Defining the Fox–Flory constant $K = \dfrac{A}{\Delta\alpha}$:

$$\boxed{\,T_g = T_g^\infty - \frac{K}{M_n}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** As $M_n \to \infty$, $T_g \to T_g^\infty$ (asymptotic upper limit). As $M_n$ decreases, $T_g$ decreases linearly with $1/M_n$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Fox–Flory)

**Problem:** The glass transition temperature of polystyrene samples is measured as follows: $M_n = 5{,}000$ g/mol gives $T_g = 365$ K, and $M_n = 50{,}000$ g/mol gives $T_g = 375$ K. Determine $T_g^\infty$ and $K$.

**Solution:**

Let $x_i = 1/M_n$ and $y_i = T_g$. Then:
- Point 1: $x_1 = 1/5{,}000 = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$, $y_1 = 365$ K
- Point 2: $x_2 = 1/50{,}000 = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$, $y_2 = 375$ K

The slope (which equals $-K$) is:

$$\text{slope} = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{375 - 365}{2.0 \times 10^{-5} - 2.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10}{-1.8 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$-K = -5.56 \times 10^{4} \text{ K·(g/mol)}$$

$$K = 5.56 \times 10^{4} \text{ K·(g/mol)}$$

Using point 2: $T_g = T_g^\infty - K/M_n$

$$375 = T_g^\infty - \frac{5.56 \times 10^{4}}{50{,}000} = T_g^\infty - 1.11$$

$$T_g^\infty = 375 + 1.11 = 376.11 \text{ K} = 102.96 \text{ °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Validation:** For polystyrene, literature reports $T_g^\infty \approx 373$ K (100 °C) and $K \approx 1.0 \times 10^{5}$ K·(g/mol). Our calculation is consistent with the order of magnitude.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Fox Equation for Copolymer Tg

For a random copolymer composed of monomers 1 and 2 with weight fractions $w_1$ and $w_2$, Fox proposed (based on free volume additivity) that:

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$$

**Step-by-Step Reasoning:**

1. Define the free volume of the copolymer as the sum of free volumes contributed by each monomer type.
2. Assume additivity: $V_{f,\text{copoly}} = w_1 V_{f,1}(T) + w_2 V_{f,2}(T)$.
3. The thermal expansion of free volume gives:

$$f_{\text{copoly}}(T) = w_1 \alpha_1 T + w_2 \alpha_2 T - (\text{constant})$$

4. At the glass transition, $f_{\text{copoly}}(T_g) = f_g$ (universal).
5. Solving the linear equation for $1/T_g$ yields the Fox equation above.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitation of the Fox Equation:** It applies strictly to **random copolymers** or **miscible homopolymer blends** where segmental mixing is intimate. It FAILS for **block copolymers** (which show two Tg values) and **immiscible blends** (also two Tg values).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Effect of Each Factor

| Factor | Direction of Tg Change | Quantitative Model | Magnitude (Typical) |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| ↑ Molecular weight | Tg ↑ | Fox–Flory | +10 to +30 K (per decade of Mn) |
| ↑ Crosslink density | Tg ↑ | $T_g = T_{g,0} + \lambda \rho_x$ | +2 to +5 K per 1% crosslink |
| ↑ Plasticizer content | Tg ↓ | Fox / Couchman–Karasz | −20 to −80 K at 20 wt% |
| ↑ Chain stiffness | Tg ↑ | Empirical/Group Contribution | +50 to +150 K |
| ↑ H-bonding | Tg ↑ | Gordon–Taylor / Kwei | +30 to +100 K |
| ↑ Bulky polar side group | Tg ↑ | Substituent constant σ | +10 to +50 K per group |
| ↑ Symmetric nonpolar side group | Tg ↓ | Free volume model | −10 to −40 K per group |
| ↑ Pressure | Tg ↑ | Ehrenfest relation | +0.2 to +0.5 K/MPa |
| ↑ Cooling rate | Tg (apparent) ↑ | Moynihan / DSC rate | +3 to +5 K per decade of rate |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term "Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)" of a polymer. How is it experimentally determined using Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)?

**Model Answer:**

The Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) is defined as the temperature at which an amorphous polymer undergoes a transition from a hard, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state due to the onset of cooperative segmental mobility of the polymer chains. It is a second-order thermodynamic transition characterized by a step change in heat capacity ($C_p$), thermal expansion coefficient ($\alpha$), and refractive index, without any change in the latent heat.

In **DSC**, a small polymer sample (~5–10 mg) and an inert reference are heated at a controlled rate (typically 10 K/min) under a nitrogen atmosphere. The DSC thermogram plots heat flow versus temperature. Tg appears as a **step change (inflection) in the baseline**, not as a peak. The Tg value is reported as the **midpoint of the inflection region** or, alternatively, as the **onset temperature** of the baseline shift, in accordance with ASTM D3418.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for DSC method and interpretation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
List any **six** factors that influence the glass transition temperature (Tg) of a polymer.

**Model Answer:**

The six major factors influencing Tg are:

1. **Chain flexibility / Backbone stiffness** — Stiffer backbones (aromatic) give higher Tg; flexible backbones (aliphatic −CH₂−) give lower Tg.
2. **Molecular weight** — Lower Mn reduces Tg due to increased free volume from chain ends (Fox–Flory equation).
3. **Intermolecular forces** — Strong H-bonding and dipole–dipole forces increase Tg (e.g., Nylon-6,6 has higher Tg than polyethylene).
4. **Steric effects of side groups** — Bulky polar side groups (e.g., −Cl, −C₆H₅) increase Tg by restricting rotation.
5. **Crosslinking** — Higher crosslink density increases Tg by restricting segmental mobility.
6. **Plasticizers** — Low-molecular-weight additives lower Tg by increasing free volume.

*(Examiner's Note: Each factor carries 0.5 marks; 3 marks for six factors.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Treatment

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the **Fox–Flory equation** relating the glass transition temperature of a polymer to its number-average molecular weight. Clearly state the assumptions made. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a polymer sample, the following data are obtained:

| Sample | $M_n$ (g/mol) | $T_g$ (K) |
|--------|---------------|-----------|
| A | 8,000 | 347 |
| B | 20,000 | 358 |
| C | 80,000 | 367 |

Using the Fox–Flory equation, determine:
(i) The Fox–Flory constant $K$.
(ii) The limiting glass transition temperature $T_g^\infty$ at infinite molecular weight.
(iii) The Tg for a sample of $M_n = 2 \times 10^{6}$ g/mol. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete derivation. Key assumptions:

1. Each chain end contributes excess free volume $v_e$.
2. The free volume fraction at Tg is a universal constant ($f_g \approx 0.025$).
3. The free volume varies linearly with temperature.
4. The polymer is linear (not crosslinked or branched significantly).

The final derived form is:

$$T_g = T_g^\infty - \frac{K}{M_n}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Convert data to $x = 1/M_n$ and $y = T_g$:

- Sample A: $x_A = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/g, $y_A = 347$ K
- Sample B: $x_B = 5.00 \times 10^{-5}$ mol/g, $y_B = 358$ K
- Sample C: $x_C = 1.25 \times 10^{-5}$ mol/g, $y_C = 367$ K

**Two-point linear regression** (using A and C for maximum spread):

$$\text{slope} = \frac{y_C - y_A}{x_C - x_A} = \frac{367 - 347}{1.25 \times 10^{-5} - 1.25 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\text{slope} = \frac{20}{-1.125 \times 10^{-4}} = -1.778 \times 10^{5} \text{ K·g/mol}$$

Therefore:

$$-K = -1.778 \times 10^{5} \implies K = 1.778 \times 10^{5} \text{ K·g/mol}$$

(i) **K = 1.778 × 10⁵ K·g/mol**

(ii) Using Sample C: $T_g^\infty = T_g + K/M_n$

$$T_g^\infty = 367 + \frac{1.778 \times 10^{5}}{80{,}000} = 367 + 2.22 = 369.22 \text{ K}$$

**(ii) $T_g^\infty$ = 369.22 K ≈ 96.07 °C**

(iii) For $M_n = 2 \times 10^{6}$ g/mol:

$$T_g = 369.22 - \frac{1.778 \times 10^{5}}{2{,}000{,}000} = 369.22 - 0.089 = 369.13 \text{ K}$$

**(iii) Tg = 369.13 K ≈ 95.98 °C**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** Students commonly:
> 1. Forget to convert Celsius to Kelvin in Fox–Flory.
> 2. Use **weight-average molecular weight (Mw)** instead of **number-average (Mn)** — Mn is the correct variable.
> 3. Report Tg in °C without Kelvin conversion (always state both units).
> 4. In regression, do not perform least-squares — for 7 marks, all 3 data points should be used with linear least-squares or verified for linearity.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Treatment

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the influence of **molecular weight, crosslinking, and plasticizers** on the glass transition temperature of polymers. Include relevant equations and physical reasoning. (7 marks)

**(b)** A random copolymer is made from 60 wt% methyl methacrylate (Tg₁ = 378 K) and 40 wt% styrene (Tg₂ = 373 K). Using the **Fox equation**:
(i) Calculate the Tg of the copolymer.
(ii) Explain why this equation FAILS for block copolymers and immiscible blends. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) Molecular Weight Effect (3 marks of 7):**
- As Mn decreases, more chain ends exist per unit mass.
- Chain ends are more mobile and create excess free volume.
- More free volume → lower Tg.
- Mathematical relationship: $T_g = T_g^\infty - \dfrac{K}{M_n}$ (Fox–Flory).
- Practical implication: To achieve maximum Tg, polymer must be processed above its critical molecular weight $M_c$ (typically 10,000–30,000 g/mol).

**(ii) Crosslinking Effect (2 marks of 7):**
- Crosslinks form covalent bridges between chains, locking them in a 3D network.
- Segmental mobility is severely restricted; cooperative motion requires more thermal energy.
- $T_g = T_{g,0} + \lambda \, \rho_x$ where $\rho_x$ is crosslink density (mol/m³) and $\lambda$ is a polymer-specific constant.
- In epoxies, vulcanized rubber, and phenolic resins, Tg is a direct measure of the **degree of cure**.
- Highly crosslinked polymers may not exhibit a distinct Tg but rather decompose directly upon heating.

**(iii) Plasticizer Effect (2 marks of 7):**
- Plasticizers are low-MW additives (e.g., DOP, DBP in PVC) that insert between chains.
- They (a) increase free volume, (b) reduce cohesive energy density.
- Tg decreases according to: $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$ (Fox equation for plasticizer blends).
- For polymer–plasticizer systems, the **Couchman–Karasz equation** is more accurate.
- Practical use: Rigid PVC (Tg = 80 °C) becomes flexible (Tg ~ 0 °C) with 30–40 phr plasticizer.

**Model Solution for (b):**

(i) Given: $w_1 = 0.60$, $T_{g,1} = 378$ K, $w_2 = 0.40$, $T_{g,2} = 373$ K.

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.60}{378} + \frac{0.40}{373}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = 1.587 \times 10^{-3} + 1.072 \times 10^{-3} = 2.659 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$T_g = \frac{1}{2.659 \times 10^{-3}} = 376.1 \text{ K} = 102.95 \text{ °C}$$

**(i) Tg = 376.1 K ≈ 102.95 °C**

(ii) **Why Fox Equation Fails for Block Copolymers and Immiscible Blends:**

- The **Fox equation** assumes **intimate, segment-level mixing** of the two components, with each repeat unit experiencing an averaged environment.
- In a **block copolymer** (e.g., PS-b-PMMA), the blocks phase-separate into distinct microdomains (typically 10–100 nm). Each domain exhibits its **own Tg** corresponding to its homopolymer Tg. Two distinct transitions appear in DSC.
- In an **immiscible blend** (e.g., PS + natural rubber), the components form macroscopic phases with sharp interfaces. Each phase retains its bulk Tg.
- The Fox equation therefore gives a fictitious "average" Tg that does **not correspond to any physical transition** in the material.
- For these systems, the correct characterization uses **DMA (Dynamic Mechanical Analysis)**, which resolves two distinct tan δ peaks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B - Q.B):** Students often apply the Fox equation universally. Examiners test the **understanding of the equation's domain of validity** — random copolymer, miscible blend, or plasticizer system only. Always state the assumption before applying.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Tg is NOT a first-order transition** — it has no latent heat; only a step change in $C_p$ and $\alpha$.
- **DSC signature of Tg:** Step change in baseline, NOT a peak.
- **DMA signature of Tg:** Peak in **tan δ** (loss tangent), or peak in $E''$ (loss modulus), or onset of $E'$ drop.
- **Fox–Flory:** $T_g = T_g^\infty - K/M_n$ — Use **Mn**, not Mw. Temperatures in **Kelvin**.
- **Fox equation (copolymers/blends):** $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$ — Applies to **random** copolymers and **miscible** blends only.
- **Gordon–Taylor** is preferred for miscible homopolymer blends; **Couchman–Karasz** for plasticized systems.
- **Stiff backbone → high Tg**; **flexible backbone → low Tg**.
- **Polar groups (H-bonding) → high Tg**; **symmetric nonpolar groups → low Tg** (free volume effect).
- **Crosslinks → higher Tg**; **plasticizers → lower Tg**.
- **Low Mn → lower Tg**; **high Mn → higher Tg**, asymptotically reaching $T_g^\infty$.
- **Pressure → higher Tg** (dTg/dP ~ 0.2–0.5 K/MPa).
- **Faster cooling rate → lower apparent Tg** (kinetic effect, not thermodynamic).
- **Block copolymers and immiscible blends** show **TWO Tg values** — Fox equation is invalid.
- **Universal free volume criterion:** $f_g \approx 0.025$ at Tg (Doolittle).
- **Beaman–Boyer rule** (approximate): $T_g / T_m \approx 0.5$ to 0.67 for symmetric flexible polymers (in Kelvin).
- **Units sanity check:** Always use **Kelvin** in Tg equations; convert °C → K by adding 273.15.
- **Common KTU trap:** $M_w$ vs $M_n$ — Fox–Flory uses **$M_n$** (number-average). Use $M_w$ only when the problem explicitly states viscosity-average or weight-average correlations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crystalline State
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Crystalline State

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crystalline State (Polymer Science Definition):** The **crystalline state** in a polymer refers to the structural condition in which the long-chain macromolecules are **folded, aligned, and packed** into a **three-dimensional, highly ordered, periodic lattice** with a well-defined **repeating unit cell**. Crystallinity in polymers is never 100% — it is described by the **degree of crystallinity (Xc)**, which is the fraction (or percentage) of the polymer material that exists in the ordered crystalline phase, with the remainder being the disordered **amorphous phase**.

### Key Terminology

- **Crystallite (Crystalline Domain):** A small, ordered, parallel-packed region of polymer chains. Crystallites are interconnected by **tie molecules** that pass from one domain to another.
- **Lamellae:** Thin, plate-like crystalline structures (typically 10–20 nm thick) formed by **chain folding**.
- **Spherulite:** A spherical, polycrystalline superstructure (10–500 µm in diameter) composed of radiating lamellar fibrils.
- **Degree of Crystallinity (Xc):** The volume or weight fraction of the polymer that is crystalline.
- **Fringed Micelle Model:** Older model where crystalline and amorphous regions are interspersed along the same chain.
- **Folded-Chain Lamella Model (Keller, 1957):** The currently accepted model where polymer chains fold back on themselves to form thin crystalline platelets.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Polymers are **never 100% crystalline** (unlike metals or small organic molecules). Even highly crystalline polymers such as **HDPE** have a maximum crystallinity of about **70–80%**, while **PVC** has only about **10%**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a **bowl of cooked spaghetti (amorphous)** versus a **neatly stacked bundle of uncooked spaghetti (crystalline)**.

- **Amorphous state:** Polymer chains are tangled, curly, and randomly oriented — like a heap of noodles thrown into a bowl. They have no long-range order.
- **Crystalline state:** The same chains are **straightened, folded neatly, and packed side-by-side** in parallel, like a box of perfectly arranged pencils. The crystalline regions (lamellae) are tied together by curly "loose" chain portions (the tie molecules and amorphous regions).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world example:** Transparent plastic wrap (e.g., **LDPE — low-density polyethylene**) is *mostly amorphous* — you can see through it because the disordered chains don't scatter light. A **white, opaque milk jug (HDPE)** is *partially crystalline* — the millions of tiny crystallites scatter light in all directions, giving the milky appearance.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in Bold)

- **X-ray diffraction (XRD) wavelength** for crystallography: **λ(Cu Kα) = 1.5418 Å**
- **Typical lamellar thickness:** **10–20 nm**
- **Typical spherulite diameter:** **1–500 µm**
- **Density of crystalline PE (ρc):** **≈ 1.00 g/cm³**
- **Density of amorphous PE (ρa):** **≈ 0.86 g/cm³**
- **Avrami exponent (n) range for polymers:** **1 ≤ n ≤ 4**
- **Heat of fusion of 100% crystalline PE (ΔHf°):** **≈ 293 J/g (≈ 70 cal/g)**

---

> **Key Takeaway:** The crystalline state in polymers is a **partial** ordered state, characterized by lamellae and spherulites, and quantified by the **degree of crystallinity Xc**. Crystallinity directly controls mechanical strength, optical clarity, density, and thermal behavior of the polymer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two-Phase Model of a Semi-Crystalline Polymer

A real polymer is treated as a **two-phase composite** consisting of:

1. **Crystalline phase** — ordered, higher density (ρc)
2. **Amorphous phase** — disordered, lower density (ρa)

The bulk specific volume **vs** of the semicrystalline polymer is the **weight-average** of the specific volumes of the two phases:

$$v_s = X_c \cdot v_c + (1 - X_c) \cdot v_a$$

Solving for **Xc (weight fraction crystalline)**:

$$X_c = \frac{v_a - v_s}{v_a - v_c} = \frac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This equation is the **most frequently tested** formula in KTU ESE questions. By simply measuring the polymer's bulk density (ρs) and knowing the pure-phase densities (ρc, ρa) from literature, you can determine Xc. It is used industrially in **quality control** of HDPE, LLDPE, PP, and PET production lines.

## 2.2 The Avrami Equation — Crystallization Kinetics

The **Avrami equation** describes the **isothermal crystallization** of polymers as a function of time:

$$X_c(t) = 1 - \exp(-K \cdot t^n)$$

- $X_c(t)$ = fraction crystallized at time $t$ (0 ≤ Xc ≤ 1)
- $K$ = crystallization rate constant (depends on nucleation density and growth rate)
- $n$ = **Avrami exponent** (depends on nucleation type and growth geometry)

### Avrami Exponent n — Physical Meaning

| Exponent $n$ | Nucleation Type | Growth Geometry |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Pre-determined (athermal) | One-dimensional (rod-like) |
| 2 | Pre-determined (athermal) | Two-dimensional (disc-like) |
| 3 | Pre-determined (athermal) | Three-dimensional (spherulitic) |
| 4 | Sporadic (thermal) | Three-dimensional (spherulitic) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world value:** For most polymer melts cooled below T_m, **n ≈ 3** (spherulitic growth from athermal nuclei), which is what is usually reported in lab measurements.

### Linearized Form (for experimental data plotting)

Taking the **double logarithm**:

$$\log[-\ln(1 - X_c)] = \log K + n \cdot \log t$$

This is plotted as **log[−ln(1−Xc)] vs. log t** to yield a straight line with:
- **Slope = n** (Avrami exponent)
- **Intercept = log K** (rate constant)

### Half-life of Crystallization

The time at which Xc = 0.5 (50% crystallinity) is obtained by setting 1 − Xc = 0.5:

$$t_{1/2} = \left( \frac{\ln 2}{K} \right)^{1/n} = \frac{0.693^{1/n}}{K^{1/n}}$$

## 2.3 Melting Point of Copolymers — Flory Equation

For a **random copolymer**, the melting point is **depressed** relative to the homopolymer. Flory derived:

$$\frac{1}{T_m} - \frac{1}{T_m^{\circ}} = -\frac{R}{\Delta H_f} \cdot \ln X_A$$

- $T_m$ = melting temperature of the copolymer (K)
- $T_m^{\circ}$ = melting temperature of the pure crystallizable homopolymer (K)
- $R$ = gas constant = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- $\Delta H_f$ = heat of fusion per mole of crystallizable repeat unit (J/mol)
- $X_A$ = mole fraction of the crystallizable monomer in the copolymer

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** KTU students often confuse **Tm** (kelvin) with **Tm (°C)**. **Always convert to Kelvin** before applying the Flory equation. The mole fraction $X_A$ is **per repeat unit**, not per chain.

## 2.4 Nucleation Theories

Crystallization is a two-step process:

1. **Primary Nucleation** — formation of a stable critical-size nucleus (ΔG_critical)
2. **Crystal Growth** — chain folding and lamellar deposition onto the nucleus

- **Homogeneous nucleation:** Spontaneous, requires large undercooling (ΔT).
- **Heterogeneous nucleation:** Catalyzed by foreign surfaces, nucleating agents, or impurity particles — occurs at small ΔT (industrially preferred).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $X_c = \dfrac{v_a - v_s}{v_a - v_c}$ | Degree of crystallinity from density |
| 2 | $X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)}$ | Degree of crystallinity (density form) |
| 3 | $X_c(t) = 1 - \exp(-K t^n)$ | Avrami crystallization kinetics |
| 4 | $\log[-\ln(1 - X_c)] = \log K + n \log t$ | Linearized Avrami (for plotting) |
| 5 | $t_{1/2} = (\ln 2 / K)^{1/n}$ | Crystallization half-life |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{T_m} - \dfrac{1}{T_m^{\circ}} = -\dfrac{R}{\Delta H_f} \ln X_A$ | Flory melting point depression (copolymer) |
| 7 | $T_g \approx \dfrac{2}{3} T_m$ (in K) | Boyer–Beaman rule (rough Tg–Tm correlation) |
| 8 | $\Delta T = T_m^{\circ} - T_c$ | Undercooling (driving force for crystallization) |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** Avrami analysis is used in **injection molding process optimization** to control cooling time and prevent warpage in semi-crystalline parts (e.g., PP, PA6, POM). The Flory equation is the basis of **polymer blend / copolymer design** in the elastomer industry.

---

> **Key Takeaway:** The two-phase model gives **Xc from density**; the **Avrami equation** describes *how fast* crystallization proceeds; the **Flory equation** describes *how low* the melting point is depressed in copolymers. All three are **must-know for KTU ESE**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Degree of Crystallinity (Xc) from Density

### Statement
Derive an expression for the **mass fraction crystallinity (Xc)** in terms of the bulk density (ρs), crystalline-phase density (ρc), and amorphous-phase density (ρa).

### Given
- Specific volume of crystalline phase: $v_c = 1/\rho_c$
- Specific volume of amorphous phase: $v_a = 1/\rho_a$
- Specific volume of bulk polymer: $v_s = 1/\rho_s$

### Step 1: Set up the two-phase specific volume relationship
By the **rule of mixtures** for a two-phase composite, the bulk specific volume is the weight-fraction-weighted average of each phase:

$$v_s = X_c \, v_c + (1 - X_c) \, v_a$$

### Step 2: Expand the right-hand side
$$v_s = X_c \, v_c + v_a - X_c \, v_a$$

### Step 3: Collect the Xc terms on one side
$$v_s - v_a = X_c \, (v_c - v_a)$$

### Step 4: Solve for Xc
$$X_c = \frac{v_s - v_a}{v_c - v_a}$$

### Step 5: Substitute $v = 1/\rho$ into all three specific volumes

$$X_c = \frac{\dfrac{1}{\rho_s} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_a}}{\dfrac{1}{\rho_c} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_a}}$$

### Step 6: Multiply numerator and denominator by $\rho_a \rho_c \rho_s$

$$X_c = \frac{\rho_a \rho_c \rho_s \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho_s} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_a} \right)}{\rho_a \rho_c \rho_s \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho_c} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_a} \right)}$$

### Step 7: Simplify

$$X_c = \frac{\rho_a \rho_c - \rho_c \rho_s}{\rho_a \rho_s - \rho_c \rho_s}$$

### Step 8: Factor common terms

$$X_c = \frac{\rho_c (\rho_a - \rho_s)}{\rho_s (\rho_a - \rho_c)}$$

### Step 9: Rearrange signs (multiply numerator and denominator by −1)

$$\boxed{X_c = \frac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** This is the **KTU 2024 KTU-mandated formula** for crystallinity from density. Memorize it verbatim.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU style)

**Problem:** A polyethylene sample has $\rho_s = 0.950\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_c = 1.000\ \text{g/cm}^3$, and $\rho_a = 0.860\ \text{g/cm}^3$. Calculate the **degree of crystallinity**.

**Solution:**

$$X_c = \frac{1.000 \times (0.950 - 0.860)}{0.950 \times (1.000 - 0.860)}$$

$$X_c = \frac{1.000 \times 0.090}{0.950 \times 0.140}$$

$$X_c = \frac{0.0900}{0.1330} = 0.6767$$

$$\boxed{X_c \approx 67.7\%}$$

This is typical of **HDPE** (high-density polyethylene).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Half-Life of Crystallization from the Avrami Equation

### Step 1: Start with the Avrami equation
$$X_c(t) = 1 - \exp(-K t^n)$$

### Step 2: Define $t_{1/2}$ as the time at which $X_c = 0.5$
$$0.5 = 1 - \exp(-K \, t_{1/2}^n)$$

### Step 3: Isolate the exponential
$$\exp(-K \, t_{1/2}^n) = 0.5 = \frac{1}{2}$$

### Step 4: Take the natural logarithm of both sides
$$-K \, t_{1/2}^n = \ln(0.5) = -\ln 2$$

### Step 5: Solve for $t_{1/2}^n$

$$t_{1/2}^n = \frac{\ln 2}{K}$$

### Step 6: Raise both sides to power $1/n$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \left( \frac{\ln 2}{K} \right)^{1/n} = \frac{0.693^{1/n}}{K^{1/n}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A higher **K** (faster crystallization) gives a **shorter $t_{1/2}$**, meaning the polymer solidifies quickly. This is exploited in **3D printing** with PLA and in **injection molding** of polypropylene to shorten cycle times.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Avrami Plot Analysis (KTU style)

**Problem:** The isothermal crystallization of **polypropylene (PP)** at 130 °C gives the following data:

| Time $t$ (min) | Crystallinity $X_c$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 1.0 | 0.08 |
| 2.0 | 0.22 |
| 4.0 | 0.48 |
| 8.0 | 0.74 |

Determine the Avrami exponent **n** and rate constant **K**.

**Solution:**

Compute the linearized Avrami quantities:

| $t$ (min) | $\log t$ | $X_c$ | $(1-X_c)$ | $-\ln(1-X_c)$ | $\log[-\ln(1-X_c)]$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1.0 | 0.000 | 0.08 | 0.92 | 0.0834 | −1.079 |
| 2.0 | 0.301 | 0.22 | 0.78 | 0.2485 | −0.605 |
| 4.0 | 0.602 | 0.48 | 0.52 | 0.6539 | −0.184 |
| 8.0 | 0.903 | 0.74 | 0.26 | 1.3471 |  0.129 |

Performing a **least-squares linear regression** on $\log[-\ln(1-X_c)]$ vs. $\log t$:

- Slope $\approx 1.34$
- Intercept $\approx -1.07$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{n \approx 1.34, \quad K = 10^{-1.07} \approx 0.085\ \text{min}^{-1.34}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An exponent near 1.34 indicates **one-dimensional (rod-like) growth with sporadic nucleation**, possibly due to the presence of nucleating agents limiting the dimensionality of the growth front. A pure spherulitic growth would give n = 3.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Crystallinity (for KTU Part A)

| Factor | Effect on Crystallinity | Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Tacticity (isotactic > atactic) | ↑ | Stereoregular chains pack better |
| Chain linearity (no side branches) | ↑ | Branches prevent close packing |
| Slow cooling from melt | ↑ | Chains get time to align and fold |
| Plasticizers / solvents | ↓ | Increase free volume, disrupt order |
| Crosslinking | ↓ sharply | Crosslinks freeze chains in place |
| High molecular weight | ↓ (slightly) | Long chains have higher melt viscosity, slower diffusion |
| Nucleating agents | ↑ rate, ↑ total | Heterogeneous nucleation reduces induction time |
| High undercooling (ΔT) | ↑ rate, ↓ lamellar thickness | Faster nucleation but less perfect crystals |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Spherulite Formation from Melt

The following block diagram traces the **pathway from a polymer melt to a fully developed spherulite** — the key unit of crystalline morphology in polymers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1["POLYMER MELT"] --> n2{"COOLING RATE"}
    n2 -->|"Slow cooling (low ΔT)"| n3["PRIMARY NUCLEATION"]
    n2 -->|"Quench (high ΔT)"| n4["AMORPHOUS GLASS"]
    n3 --> n5["STABLE CRITICAL NUCLEUS"]
    n5 --> n6["CHAIN FOLDING ONTO NUCLEUS"]
    n6 --> n7["LAMELLAR GROWTH"]
    n7 --> n8["RADIAL FIBRIL BUNDLE"]
    n8 --> n9["SPHERULITE FORMATION"]
    n9 --> n10["TIE MOLECULES BRIDGE LAMELLAE"]
    n10 --> n11["SEMI CRYSTALLINE SOLID"]
    n4 --> n11
    n11 --> n12["MELTING AT T_m ON HEATING"]
    n12 --> n1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read-out:** A slow-cooled polymer melt nucleates, the chains fold into lamellae, the lamellae splay outward radially to form a **spherulite** (visible under a polarized optical microscope as a **Maltese cross** pattern), and tie molecules link neighboring crystallites.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Two-Phase Model of a Semi-Crystalline Polymer

This block diagram represents the **Fringed Micelle + Folded-Chain Lamella** coexistence in a single polymer sample, with crystalline and amorphous regions joined by tie molecules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nA["POLYMER BULK"] --> nB["AMORPHOUS REGION"]
    nA --> nC["CRYSTALLINE LAMELLA"]
    B1["DISORDERED CHAIN SEGMENTS"] --> nB
    C1["FOLDED CHAIN SEGMENTS"] --> nC
    nB --> nD["TIE MOLECULE"]
    nC --> nD
    nD --> nE["NEXT CRYSTALLITE"]
    nB --> nF["FREE CHAIN ENDS"]
    nC --> nG["LOOP REGION"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Read-out:** The amorphous region (low density, transparent) and crystalline lamella (high density, ordered) are connected by tie molecules. This is the **physical origin of toughness** in semi-crystalline polymers: the tie molecules must be broken to propagate a crack, absorbing energy.

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Factors That Promote vs. Inhibit Crystallinity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1["FACTORS GOVERNING CRYSTALLINITY"] --> q2["PROMOTING FACTORS"]
    q1 --> q3["INHIBITING FACTORS"]
    q2 --> q2A["Isotactic / syndiotactic tacticity"]
    q2 --> q2B["Linear chain architecture"]
    q2 --> q2C["Slow cooling from melt"]
    q2 --> q2D["Nucleating agents e.g. talc, benzoate salts"]
    q2 --> q2E["High undercooling within optimum range"]
    q2 --> q2F["Annealing above Tg below Tm"]
    q3 --> q3A["Atactic tacticity random coil"]
    q3 --> q3B["Branched chains e.g. LDPE"]
    q3 --> q3C["Rapid quenching"]
    q3 --> q3D["Plasticizers and solvents"]
    q3 --> q3E["Chemical crosslinking"]
    q3 --> q3F["Very high molecular weight chain entanglement"]
```

## 4.4 Schematic of the Avrami Plot (log[−ln(1−Xc)] vs. log t)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["log t axis"] --> p2["log minus ln 1 minus Xc axis"]
    p3["STRAIGHT LINE"] --> p4["SLOPE EQUALS n"]
    p3 --> p5["INTERCEPT EQUALS log K"]
    p6["INDUCTION PERIOD"] --> p7["Negligible for heterogeneous nucleation"]
    p8["PRIMARY CRYSTALLIZATION"] --> p9["Linear Avrami region"]
    p10["SECONDARY CRYSTALLIZATION"] --> p11["Deviation from straight line at high Xc"]
    p3 --> p8
    p3 --> p9
    p3 --> p10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering note:** Deviation at high conversion is called **secondary crystallization** — slow perfection of imperfect crystals, often due to chain segments trapped between lamellae gradually aligning.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the term "degree of crystallinity" in a polymer. Why is 100% crystallinity not attainable in polymers?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

- **(1 mark)** **Definition:** The degree of crystallinity (Xc) is defined as the weight or volume fraction of the polymer that exists in the ordered, three-dimensionally periodic crystalline state, with the remainder being disordered amorphous material.
- **(1 mark)** **Reason 1:** Polymer chains are long and entangled; complete chain unfolding and perfect registry along the entire chain length is statistically impossible.
- **(1 mark)** **Reason 2:** Chain ends, entanglements, tie molecules, and stereoregular defects act as **defects** that break the periodic lattice, leaving residual amorphous content. Even highly crystalline HDPE tops out at ~70–80%.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between the Fringed Micelle model and the Folded-Chain Lamella model of polymer crystallization.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

| Feature | Fringed Micelle Model | Folded-Chain Lamella Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Chain conformation** | Chain passes through alternating crystalline and amorphous regions | Chain folds back on itself to form regular platelets |
| **Crystallite shape** | Small, irregular micelles | Thin lamellae (10–20 nm thick) |
| **Proposed by** | Gerngross–Herrmann (1930) | Keller, Fischer, Till (1957) |
| **Modern acceptance** | Largely obsolete | Universally accepted |
| **Crystallinity evidence** | Explains broad XRD peaks | Explains sharp lamellar diffraction, electron microscopy |

**(3 marks):** Awarded for tabulated comparison and brief explanation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 regulations, students answer **ONE full question (14 marks)** with internal choice. Each question has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Modified]`

**A polymer sample has the following data:** $\rho_s = 0.945\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_c = 1.005\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_a = 0.855\ \text{g/cm}^3$.

**(a) Derive the expression for the degree of crystallinity of a semi-crystalline polymer in terms of the densities of the crystalline, amorphous, and bulk phases.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Calculate the degree of crystallinity of the given sample, and comment on whether the sample is closer to HDPE, LDPE, or a random copolymer.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the two-phase rule of mixtures for specific volume.

$$v_s = X_c \, v_c + (1 - X_c) \, v_a$$

where $v_s$, $v_c$, $v_a$ are the specific volumes of bulk, crystalline, and amorphous phases respectively.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute $v = 1/\rho$.

$$\frac{1}{\rho_s} = \frac{X_c}{\rho_c} + \frac{1 - X_c}{\rho_a}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Expand and collect Xc terms.

$$\frac{1}{\rho_s} - \frac{1}{\rho_a} = X_c \left( \frac{1}{\rho_c} - \frac{1}{\rho_a} \right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Solve for Xc.

$$X_c = \frac{\dfrac{1}{\rho_s} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_a}}{\dfrac{1}{\rho_c} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_a}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Convert to a single rational form (final boxed form).

$$X_c = \frac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)}$$

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Substitute the given values into the derived formula.

$$X_c = \frac{1.005 \times (0.945 - 0.855)}{0.945 \times (1.005 - 0.855)}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the differences.

$$X_c = \frac{1.005 \times 0.090}{0.945 \times 0.150}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Evaluate the numerator and denominator.

$$X_c = \frac{0.09045}{0.14175}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final result.

$$\boxed{X_c \approx 0.638\ \text{or}\ 63.8\%}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Comment on identification.
- HDPE: Xc ≈ 70–80%
- LDPE: Xc ≈ 40–55%
- Random PE copolymer: Xc ≈ 10–30%

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Conclusion — Since Xc = 63.8%, the sample is **closer to HDPE** than to LDPE, but with slightly reduced crystallinity (possibly due to short-chain branching or comonomer incorporation).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Modified]`

**(a) What is the Avrami equation? Explain the significance of the Avrami exponent n and the rate constant K. List the typical n values for different nucleation and growth geometries.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) The isothermal crystallization of a polymer at 140 °C is monitored by DSC. The data are given below. Determine the Avrami exponent n and the rate constant K using the linearized Avrami equation.** *(7 marks)*

| $t$ (min) | 1 | 2 | 4 | 8 | 16 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $X_c$ | 0.10 | 0.26 | 0.51 | 0.77 | 0.91 |

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the Avrami equation.

$$X_c(t) = 1 - \exp(-K \, t^n)$$

where $X_c(t)$ = fraction of polymer crystallized at time $t$, $K$ = crystallization rate constant, and $n$ = Avrami exponent.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Significance of $K$.
- $K$ contains both the nucleation density and the linear growth rate. It is **temperature-dependent** — typically passes through a maximum at the optimum crystallization temperature $T_c$ (between $T_g$ and $T_m$).

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Significance of $n$.
- $n$ is the **dimension-related exponent** whose value reflects the nucleation mechanism (athermal vs. thermal) and the growth geometry (1D, 2D, 3D). Integer values occur for idealized cases; **fractional values** are common experimentally due to mixed nucleation and secondary crystallization.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Typical $n$ values table.

| $n$ | Nucleation | Growth Geometry |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Pre-determined | 1D (fibrillar) |
| 2 | Pre-determined | 2D (disc-like) |
| 3 | Pre-determined | 3D (spherulitic) |
| 4 | Sporadic | 3D (spherulitic) |

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the linearized Avrami equation.

$$\log[-\ln(1 - X_c)] = \log K + n \log t$$

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Compute the transformed values.

| $t$ (min) | $\log t$ | $X_c$ | $1 - X_c$ | $-\ln(1 - X_c)$ | $\log[-\ln(1 - X_c)]$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.000 | 0.10 | 0.90 | 0.1054 | −0.977 |
| 2 | 0.301 | 0.26 | 0.74 | 0.3011 | −0.521 |
| 4 | 0.602 | 0.51 | 0.49 | 0.7133 | −0.147 |
| 8 | 0.903 | 0.77 | 0.23 | 1.4697 |  0.167 |
| 16 | 1.204 | 0.91 | 0.09 | 2.4079 |  0.382 |

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Linear regression slope and intercept (using first 4 data points, ignoring the high-conversion secondary crystallization point at $X_c = 0.91$).

- Slope $n \approx 1.49$
- Intercept $\log K \approx -0.95$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute K.

$$K = 10^{-0.95} \approx 0.112\ \text{min}^{-1.49}$$

**Step 5 (0.5 mark):** Final answer.

$$\boxed{n \approx 1.5, \quad K \approx 0.112\ \text{min}^{-1.5}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** $n = 1.5$ suggests a **mixed nucleation mode** with predominantly 1D (fibrillar) growth, possibly from a nucleated melt where growth is constrained by surrounding crystallites.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points — Examiner Insights**
> 1. **Units confusion in Flory equation:** Writing temperatures in °C instead of K. The Flory equation is **only valid in Kelvin** because the natural logarithm is dimensionless. Always **convert °C → K** by adding 273.15.
> 2. **Forgetting the chain-folded lamella model:** In Part A questions asking "define crystallinity," students often omit the mention of **lamellae and tie molecules**. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Using $X_c$ as a volume fraction in density problems:** The formula $X_c = (v_a - v_s)/(v_a - v_c)$ gives the **weight (mass) fraction**. If the problem specifies *volume* fraction, use the slightly different form involving only densities.
> 4. **Avrami exponent bounded 1–4:** Some students write $n = 0.5$ or $n = 5$ from mis-plotting. Reinforce that $n$ is bounded by 1 (one-dimensional athermal) to 4 (three-dimensional sporadic).
> 5. **Neglecting secondary crystallization:** In Avrami problems, the highest-$X_c$ data points often deviate from the straight line. Examiners expect you to mention "secondary crystallization" as the cause of deviation.
> 6. **Mixing amorphous and crystalline density notation:** Some textbooks use $v$ for specific volume, others for velocity. State your notation clearly at the start of the answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (pin this on your desk!)**

- ☐ **Crystalline state** = ordered 3D lattice; **amorphous state** = random-coil disorder. Real polymers are **semi-crystalline**.
- ☐ **Degree of crystallinity $X_c$** = (ordered mass / total mass), ranges from 0 (fully amorphous) to ~0.8 (HDPE).
- ☐ **Density-based formula:** $X_c = \rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a) / [\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)]$ — **must-memorize**.
- ☐ **Lamellae** = thin folded-chain platelets (10–20 nm thick). **Spherulites** = spherical aggregates of lamellae visible under polarized optical microscopy (Maltese cross pattern).
- ☐ **Tie molecules** link adjacent crystallites — they are the **source of toughness** in HDPE, PP, Nylon.
- ☐ **Fringed Micelle model** is **obsolete**; the **Folded-Chain Lamella model** (Keller, 1957) is the accepted model.
- ☐ **Avrami equation:** $X_c(t) = 1 - \exp(-K t^n)$. Linearized form: $\log[-\ln(1 - X_c)] = \log K + n \log t$.
- ☐ **Avrami exponent $n$** = 1 (1D/athermal), 2 (2D/athermal), 3 (3D/athermal), 4 (3D/sporadic). Real polymers: $n \approx 3$.
- ☐ **Half-life:** $t_{1/2} = (\ln 2 / K)^{1/n}$. Higher $K$ → faster crystallization.
- ☐ **Flory equation** for random copolymer melting point: $1/T_m - 1/T_m^\circ = -(R/\Delta H_f)\ln X_A$. **Always use Kelvin.**
- ☐ **Factors increasing crystallinity:** isotactic tacticity, linear chains, slow cooling, nucleating agents, annealing.
- ☐ **Factors decreasing crystallinity:** atactic structure, branching (LDPE), rapid quenching, plasticizers, crosslinks, high molecular weight.
- ☐ **Undercooling $\Delta T = T_m - T_c$** is the **driving force** for crystallization. Too high $\Delta T$ gives many small imperfect crystals; too low $\Delta T$ gives few large crystals.
- ☐ **Boyer–Beaman rule (KTU bonus):** $T_g \approx (2/3) T_m$ (in Kelvin) — a quick check on Tg–Tm relationships in semicrystalline polymers.
- ☐ **Why polymers are not 100% crystalline:** chain entanglements, chain ends, stereoregular defects, and tie molecules guarantee residual amorphous content.
