- ☐ **Real-world applications:** HDPE milk jugs (crystalline, opaque), LDPE films (amorphous, transparent), PP bottle caps (spherulitic), Nylon fibers (highly crystalline, drawn).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymorphism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Polymorphism # MODULE 1 — POLYMORPHISM IN POLYMER SCIENCE ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **POLYMORPHISM (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition)** > Polymorphism is the phenomenon by which a given polymer (or any solid chemical substance) of identical chemical composition is able to crystallize into **two or more distinct crystalline forms** (called *polymorphs*) that differ in their **unit cell geometry, chain conformation, and/or molecular packing arrangement**, while retaining the same molecular formula. Each distinct crystalline arrangement is referred to as a **polymorph**, and the conditions of crystallization (temperature, pressure, solvent, mechanical stress, cooling rate) dictate which polymorph is obtained. > [!NOTE] > **Related Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlight)** > - **Allotropy** → Polymorphism strictly applied to *elements* (e.g., carbon as graphite vs diamond). > - **Polytypism** → Different stacking sequences along one crystallographic axis only (1D polymorphism). > - **Pseudopolymorphism** → Solvates or hydrates that look polymorph-like but contain solvent in the lattice. --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Same LEGO Castle, Three Ways" Imagine you are given **1,000 identical LEGO bricks (the polymer chains)**. You are told to build a "fortress" (the crystal). Depending on **how you stack them** — 1. Build straight walls and square corners → **Form A (Orthorhombic)** 2. Tilt the walls slightly into a lean-to shape → **Form B (Monoclinic)** 3. Twist the bricks into a helix before stacking → **Form C (Triclinic / Hexagonal)** The bricks are *chemically identical*, but the **finished fortress looks and behaves differently** — different strength, different stability, different melting point. This is exactly what happens in polymer polymorphism. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Reality Check:** In the polymer industry, selecting the *correct* polymorph is a billion-dollar decision. For example, the α-form of **PVDF (Polyvinylidene fluoride)** is non-polar, while the β-form is highly piezoelectric — making it the heart of flexible sensors, energy harvesters, and even bulletproof armor. --- ### 1.3 Polymorphism vs. Amorphous vs. Crystalline Polymers | State | Order | Behaviour | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Amorphous** | Disordered chains | Glassy / Rubbery transition (Tg only) | Atactic Polystyrene | | **Crystalline (Single Polymorph)** | Orderly 3D lattice | Sharp melting (Tm) | α-form Polyethylene | | **Polymorphic** | Multiple ordered lattices possible | Multiple Tm peaks, DSC shows phase transitions | PVDF (α, β, γ, δ) | | **Liquid Crystalline** | Partial order | Anisotropic fluid phases | Kevlar solution | --- ### 1.4 The Unit Cell — The Heart of Polymorphism A **unit cell** is the smallest repeating 3D box that, when translated in space, builds the entire crystal. The cell is defined by **six lattice parameters**: $$a, \ b, \ c \ (\text{edge lengths}), \quad \alpha, \ \beta, \ \gamma \ (\text{interfacial angles})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Point:** Two polymorphs of the *same* polymer must differ in at least **one** of these six parameters $(a, b, c, \alpha, \beta, \gamma)$. If all six are identical, they are the *same* polymorph. --- ### 1.5 The Seven Crystal Systems (Foundation for Polymorphism) | System | Edges | Angles | Typical Polymer Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Cubic** | a = b = c | α = β = γ = 90° | Polyoxymethylene (POM) | | **Tetragonal** | a = b ≠ c | α = β = γ = 90° | β-form PVDF | | **Orthorhombic** | a ≠ b ≠ c | α = β = γ = 90° | α-form Polyethylene (PE) | | **Hexagonal** | a = b ≠ c | α = β = 90°, γ = 120° | γ-form PVDF | | **Rhombohedral** | a = b = c | α = β = γ ≠ 90° | Some polyamides | | **Monoclinic** | a ≠ b ≠ c | α = γ = 90° ≠ β | α-form PVDF | | **Triclinic** | a ≠ b ≠ c | α ≠ β ≠ γ | Folded-chain crystals | A polymer crossing from one system to another (e.g., monoclinic → hexagonal) is undergoing a **polymorphic transition**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Classes of Polymer Polymorphism Polymer polymorphism is classified into **two distinct mechanistic categories** (this is the *core KTU exam classification*): > [!IMPORTANT] > **CLASS 1 — PACKING POLYMORPHISM** > The chain **conformation remains identical** but the chains are *packed* into different unit cells (different spatial arrangement). > Example: Polyethylene can pack into **orthorhombic** or **monoclinic** — chain is the same zigzag, only the stacking differs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **CLASS 2 — CONFORMATIONAL POLYMORPHISM** > The chain itself adopts **different backbone geometries** (e.g., all-trans vs. helical). > Example: Isotactic Polypropylene — **α-form (3₁ helix)** vs **β-form (planar zigzag)** vs **γ-form (helical with cross-hatching)**. --- ## 2.2 Classic Case Study 1 — Polyethylene (PE) — Packing Polymorphism Polyethylene's chain is always an **all-trans planar zigzag**, but it packs in two ways: | Polymorph | Crystal System | Unit Cell Parameters (Å) | Density (g/cm³) | |---|---|---|---| | **α-form (Orthorhombic)** | Orthorhombic | a = 7.40, b = 4.93, c = 2.534 | **1.00** | | **β-form (Monoclinic)** | Monoclinic | a = 8.09, b = 4.79, c = 2.534, β = 107.9° | **0.997** | The α-form is **thermodynamically stable** and obtained on slow cooling from the melt. The β-form forms under **stress or shear crystallization** (e.g., drawing fibers). --- ## 2.3 Classic Case Study 2 — PVDF — Conformational + Packing Polymorphism PVDF is the **poster child** of polymer polymorphism, exhibiting **four major polymorphs**: | Polymorph | Chain Conformation | Crystal System | Dipole Behaviour | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **α (Form II)** | TGTG′ | Monoclinic / Orthorhombic | Non-polar (anti-parallel) | Inert films | | **β (Form I)** | All-Trans (TTT) | Orthorhombic | **Polar (Piezoelectric)** | Sensors, transducers | | **γ (Form III)** | TTTGTTTG′ | Monoclinic | Polar | High-temp capacitors | | **δ (Form IV)** | TGTG′ (polar packing) | Orthorhombic | Polar | Research | > [!NOTE] > The β-form is the most technologically valuable because its all-trans conformation aligns the C–F dipoles in a single direction, creating a **net spontaneous polarization (Ps)** — the source of piezoelectricity. --- ## 2.4 Driving Forces — *Why* Polymorphism Exists Polymorphism arises because **multiple free-energy minima** can exist for the same composition under different conditions. The Gibbs free energy of the crystal is: $$G_{\text{crystal}} = H_{\text{crystal}} - T \cdot S_{\text{crystal}}$$ At a given $(T, P)$, the polymorph with the **lowest G** is the most stable. **Polymorphs cross over in stability** at a precise transition point: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Enantiotropy vs. Monotropy (Van't Hoff / Ostwald Rules)** > - **Enantiotropic system** — Two polymorphs have a *reversible* transition below the melting point. Both are stable in different temperature ranges. > - **Monotropic system** — Only ONE polymorph is stable at all temperatures below melting. The other is metastable *forever*. --- ## 2.5 Ostwald's Step Rule (Frequently asked KTU Concept) > [!IMPORTANT] > **OSTWALD'S STEP RULE (1897):** > When a system crystallizes, it tends to pass through a *sequence of metastable states* before reaching the most stable form. > → First you get Form B (metastable), then Form A (most stable). > Engineering implication: Slow crystallization from melt gives the most stable polymorph; rapid quenching gives a metastable polymorph. --- ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Bragg's Law (XRD) | $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | Determines interplanar spacing *d* | | 2 | Interplanar spacing (orthorhombic) | $\frac{1}{d^2} = \frac{h^2}{a^2} + \frac{k^2}{b^2} + \frac{l^2}{c^2}$ | Indexing XRD peaks | | 3 | Density of unit cell | $\rho = \frac{n \cdot M}{N_A \cdot V_{\text{cell}}}$ | $n$ = chains/cell, $M$ = molar mass of repeat unit | | 4 | Volume of monoclinic cell | $V = a b c \sin\beta$ | For PE β-form | | 5 | Volume of triclinic cell | $V = abc\sqrt{1-\cos^2\alpha - \cos^2\beta - \cos^2\gamma + 2\cos\alpha\cos\beta\cos\gamma}$ | General case | | 6 | Crystallinity index (XRD) | $X_c = \frac{\sum A_{\text{cryst}}}{\sum A_{\text{cryst}} + \sum A_{\text{amorph}}}$ | Quantifies polymorphic purity | | 7 | Clausius-Clapeyron (transition) | $\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{T \cdot \Delta V}$ | Pressure-driven polymorph switch | | 8 | Equilibrium transition T | $T_t = \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}$ | Temperature of polymorphic transition | | 9 | Thermodynamic form preference | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Selects stable polymorph | | 10 | Chain repeat distance | $c = n \cdot l_{\text{bond}} \cdot \cos(\theta/2)$ | Identity period of zigzag / helix | | 11 | Spherulite growth rate | $G = G_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)\exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G^*}{k_B T}\right)$ | Kinetics of polymorph nucleation | | 12 | Avrami equation (transformation) | $X(t) = 1 - \exp(-kt^n)$ | Fraction of polymorph formed vs time | > ⚠️ **Note:** All cell edge lengths ($a, b, c$) and angles ($\alpha, \beta, \gamma$) used above are in standard crystallographic units: **Å (angstroms)** and **degrees**, respectively. --- ## 2.7 Processing Routes that Control Polymorphism | Processing Variable | Polymorph Obtained | Mechanism | |---|---|---| | Slow cooling from melt | Most stable (e.g., α-PE) | Thermodynamic control | | Rapid quenching (ice bath) | Metastable (e.g., γ-PVDF) | Kinetic trapping | | Solvent casting (DMF, DMSO) | Polar forms (e.g., β-PVDF) | Solvent-induced dipolar alignment | | Mechanical drawing / stretching | Oriented polymorphs (β-PVDF, β-PP) | Strain-induced conformation change | | High pressure crystallization | Denser polymorphs | Packing-volume minimization | | Annealing below Tm | Conversion to most stable | Solid-state transition | | Electric poling | All-trans polar polymorph | Dipole alignment | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating Unit Cell Density of α-Polyethylene **Given (KTU standard values):** - α-PE unit cell: $a = 7.40 \text{ Å}, \ b = 4.93 \text{ Å}, \ c = 2.534 \text{ Å}$ - Orthorhombic system: $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$ - Number of repeat units per cell: $n = 2$ (two ethylene chains traverse the cell) - Molar mass of –CH₂–CH₂– repeat unit: $M = 28.05 \text{ g/mol}$ - Avogadro's number: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$ **Step 1 — Compute cell volume.** For orthorhombic, $V = a \cdot b \cdot c$. $$ V = (7.40 \text{ Å}) \cdot (4.93 \text{ Å}) \cdot (2.534 \text{ Å}) $$ Converting: $1 \text{ Å} = 10^{-8} \text{ cm}$, so $1 \text{ Å}^3 = 10^{-24} \text{ cm}^3$. $$ V = 92.43 \ \text{Å}^3 = 92.43 \times 10^{-24} \text{ cm}^3 = 9.243 \times 10^{-23} \text{ cm}^3 $$ **Step 2 — Apply the density formula.** $$ \rho = \frac{n \cdot M}{N_A \cdot V} $$ $$ \rho = \frac{2 \cdot (28.05 \text{ g/mol})}{(6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}) \cdot (9.243 \times 10^{-23} \text{ cm}^3)} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate numerator and denominator separately.** $$ \text{Numerator} = 56.10 \text{ g/mol} $$ $$ \text{Denominator} = (6.022 \times 10^{23}) \cdot (9.243 \times 10^{-23}) = 55.66 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol} $$ **Step 4 — Divide.** $$ \rho = \frac{56.10}{55.66} = 1.008 \ \text{g/cm}^3 $$ **Result:** $\rho_{\alpha\text{-PE}} \approx 1.00 \text{ g/cm}^3$ — matches the experimentally reported crystalline density of polyethylene. > [!NOTE] > This confirms that the α-form has 2 chains per orthorhombic unit cell. The slight density difference between α and β forms reflects packing efficiency, not chemistry. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Bragg Angle for α-Polyethylene (110) Plane **Given:** - X-ray wavelength: $\lambda = 1.542 \text{ Å}$ (Cu Kα radiation) - Miller indices of reflection: $(h, k, l) = (1, 1, 0)$ - $a = 7.40 \text{ Å}, \ b = 4.93 \text{ Å}$ **Step 1 — Orthorhombic $d$-spacing formula.** $$ \frac{1}{d^2} = \frac{h^2}{a^2} + \frac{k^2}{b^2} + \frac{l^2}{c^2} $$ For $(1,1,0)$: $$ \frac{1}{d_{110}^2} = \frac{1^2}{(7.40)^2} + \frac{1^2}{(4.93)^2} + 0 $$ $$ \frac{1}{d_{110}^2} = \frac{1}{54.76} + \frac{1}{24.30} = 0.01826 + 0.04115 = 0.05941 \ \text{Å}^{-2} $$ $$ d_{110}^2 = \frac{1}{0.05941} = 16.83 \ \text{Å}^2 \Rightarrow d_{110} = 4.103 \ \text{Å} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Bragg's Law** for first-order reflection ($n = 1$). $$ \sin\theta = \frac{\lambda}{2d_{110}} = \frac{1.542}{2 \cdot 4.103} = \frac{1.542}{8.206} = 0.1879 $$ $$ \theta = \arcsin(0.1879) = 10.83° $$ **Step 3 — Final answer.** $$ 2\theta = 21.66° $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This 2θ ≈ 21.5° peak is the **diagnostic fingerprint** of α-polyethylene in XRD scans. PVDF β-form appears at 2θ ≈ 20.8° (110/200), a commonly confused but distinct signature. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Clausius-Clapeyron Polymorphic Transition Pressure A polymer shows two polymorphs with molar volumes $V_1$ and $V_2$. The transition enthalpy is $\Delta H = -2.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$ at $T_t = 410 \text{ K}$. Given: $V_2 - V_1 = -1.8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$ (Form 2 is denser). **Step 1 — Volume change.** $$ \Delta V = V_2 - V_1 = -1.8 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^3/\text{mol} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Clausius-Clapeyron.** $$ \frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{T \cdot \Delta V} $$ Plug in (note unit consistency: $\Delta H = -2500 \text{ J/mol}$): $$ \frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{-2500}{410 \cdot (-1.8 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{-2500}{-7.38 \times 10^{-4}} = +3.39 \times 10^{6} \ \text{Pa/K} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation.** A **positive** slope $\left(\dfrac{dP}{dT} > 0\right)$ means: increasing pressure on this system at constant $T$ *favors the denser Form 2* — consistent with Le Chatelier's principle. > [!NOTE] > This kind of calculation explains why pressure crystallization (e.g., in injection molding of PVDF) biases toward denser polymorphs like the β-form. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Polymorph Selection ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Does the polymer chain have DIPOLES? │ │ (e.g., C–F, C=O, C≡N) │ └─────────────┬───────────────┬────────────┘ │ YES │ NO ▼ ▼ ┌──────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ Polar forms │ │ Only packing │ │ (β-PVDF, γ-PA) │ │ polymorphs │ │ are POSSIBLE │ │ (e.g., PE) │ └────────┬─────────┘ └──────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Did you apply MECHANICAL STRAIN? │ └────────┬───────────────────────┬────┘ │ YES │ NO ▼ ▼ ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ │ β-form likely │ │ Solvent used? │ │ (All-trans, │ └────┬────────┬───┘ │ polar) │ │ YES │ NO └─────────────────┘ ▼ ▼ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ │ DMF/DMSO │ │ Slow │ │ → β-form │ │ cool → │ └──────────┘ │ α-form │ └──────────┘ ``` --- ## 3.5 Classification Table of Common Polymeric Polymorphs | Polymer | Forms | Conformational / Packing | Stable Form | Tm (°C) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Polyethylene | α, β | Packing | α | ~137 | | Isotactic Polypropylene | α, β, γ, δ | Both | α (Monoclinic) | ~165 | | PVDF | α, β, γ, δ | Both | α | ~170 | | Nylon-6 | α, γ | Conformational | α | ~225 | | Nylon-66 | α, β | Both | α (Triclinic) | ~265 | | PET | α, β | Conformational | α (Triclinic) | ~260 | | Polyoxymethylene (POM) | Orthorhombic, Hexagonal | Packing | Orthorhombic | ~180 | | Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) | Phase II, IV | Conformational | Phase IV | ~327 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Polymer Polymorphism ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Polymer Melt]:::melt --> B{Crystallization Condition} B -->|Slow Cooling| C[Form A - Stable] B -->|Rapid Quench| D[Form B - Metastable] B -->|Mechanical Strain| E[Form C - Oriented] B -->|Solvent Cast| F[Form D - Polar] B -->|High Pressure| G[Form E - Dense] C --> H[Property Set 1
High Tm, Equilibrium Density] D --> I[Property Set 2
Lower Tm, Trapped Conformation] E --> J[Property Set 3
Piezoelectric, Anisotropic] F --> K[Property Set 4
Polar, Dipole-Aligned] G --> L[Property Set 5
Maximum Density, New Symmetry] H --> M[Application Tailoring] I --> M J --> M K --> M L --> M M --> N[Tough Structural Parts] M --> O[Flexible Sensors] M --> P[Barrier Films] M --> Q[High-Pressure Engineering Polymers] classDef melt fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — PVDF Polymorph Transition Map ```mermaid flowchart LR PVDFmelt[PVDF Melt T greater than 170 C] --> X1 PVDFmelt --> X2 PVDFmelt --> X3 PVDFmelt --> X4 X1[Slow Cooling T less than 100 C]:::route --> alpha[alpha Form TGTG Antipara] X2[Stretching at 80 C]:::route --> beta[beta Form TTT Polar] X3[High Temp Annealing]:::route --> gamma[gamma Form TTTGTTTG] X4[Strong Electric Field]:::route --> delta[delta Form Polar Packed] alpha -->|Stretch| beta beta -->|High T Anneal| alpha gamma -->|Stretch| beta delta -->|Heat| alpha classDef route fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Unit Cell Packing Polymorphism in Polyethylene ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph AForm[Alpha Form Orthorhombic] A1[Chain 1 along b axis]:::chain A2[Chain 2 staggered along b]:::chain A3[Chain 3 along b axis]:::chain A1 -.parallel.- A2 A2 -.parallel.- A3 end subgraph BForm[Beta Form Monoclinic] B1[Chain 1 sheared]:::chainB B2[Chain 2 sheared]:::chainB B1 -.tilted.- B2 end AForm -->|Stress or Shear| BForm BForm -->|Anneal| AForm classDef chain fill:#d4f1d4,stroke:#006600,color:#000 classDef chainB fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Polymorphism Characterization Toolbox ```mermaid flowchart TD Sample[Polymer Sample with Polymorphs] --> XRD[X Ray Diffraction] Sample --> DSC[Differential Scanning Calorimetry] Sample --> FTIR[Fourier Transform IR Spectroscopy] Sample --> RAMAN[Raman Spectroscopy] Sample --> POM[Polarized Optical Microscopy] XRD --> XRDout[Crystal System d spacing Unit Cell] DSC --> DSCout[Melting Points Enthalpies Transitions] FTIR --> FTIRout[Conformational Bands 840 1275 cm-1 for beta PVDF] RAMAN --> RAMANout[Phase specific Vibrational Modes] POM --> POMout[Spherulite Morphology Birefringence] ``` ## 4.5 Block Architecture — Engineering Decision Tree for Selecting a Polymorph ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Need a Specific Polymer Property]:::start --> Q1{Polar Activity Required?} Q1 -->|YES| Q2{Stable or Switchable?} Q1 -->|NO| Q3{Mechanical Strength Priority?} Q2 -->|Stable| SelectBeta[Select beta PVDF or gamma Nylon] Q2 -->|Switchable| SelectAlphaDelta[Select alpha to delta transitions] Q3 -->|YES| SelectAlphaPE[Select alpha form Polyethylene] Q3 -->|NO| Q4{High Temperature Use?} Q4 -->|YES| SelectGamma[Select gamma form PVDF or alpha Nylon 66] Q4 -->|NO| SelectDefault[Process by slow cooling default alpha form] classDef start fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A] **Define polymorphism in polymers. Give two examples of polymers that exhibit polymorphism.** **Model Answer:** Polymorphism in polymers is the ability of a polymer of the same chemical composition to exist in **more than one crystalline form**, differing in unit cell parameters, chain conformation, or molecular packing arrangement, depending on crystallization conditions. Each distinct form is called a **polymorph**, and they can exhibit different melting points, densities, and physical properties. **Examples:** 1. **PVDF (Polyvinylidene fluoride)** — α, β, γ, δ forms. 2. **Polyethylene** — α (orthorhombic) and β (monoclinic) forms. *(1 mark definition, 1 mark example 1, 1 mark example 2)* --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Distinguish between conformational polymorphism and packing polymorphism with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Conformational Polymorphism | Packing Polymorphism | |---|---|---| | What differs | Chain backbone geometry (e.g., zigzag vs helix) | Manner of packing in 3D space | | Cause | Different rotational states around bonds | Different van der Waals / dipole stacking | | Example | **PVDF**: TGTG′ (α) vs TTT (β) | **Polyethylene**: same zigzag, orthorhombic vs monoclinic | *(1 mark each row + 1 mark for examples)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Polymorphism in PVDF: Crystal Forms & Engineering Significance* `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Choice A]` #### (a) Discuss the four major polymorphs of PVDF. Compare their chain conformations and dipole arrangements. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) is a semi-crystalline fluoropolymer with repeat unit –(CH₂–CF₂)ₙ–. Its extraordinary technological importance arises from its ability to crystallize into **four principal polymorphs**, summarized below: **1. α-form (Form II)** - Chain conformation: **TGTG′** (trans–gauche–trans–gauche-prime). - Crystal system: **Monoclinic / Orthorhombic** (cell dimensions a = 4.96 Å, b = 9.64 Å, c = 4.62 Å). - Dipoles: The C–F bonds align anti-parallel, giving a **net zero dipole** (non-polar). - Formation: Obtained by cooling from the melt or solution casting from low-boiling solvents. **2. β-form (Form I)** - Chain conformation: **All-trans planar zigzag (TTT)**. - Crystal system: **Orthorhombic** (a = 8.58 Å, b = 4.91 Å, c = 2.56 Å). - Dipoles: All C–F bonds point **in the same direction** → maximum spontaneous polarization (~7 µC/cm²). **Highly piezoelectric and pyroelectric**. - Formation: Mechanical stretching of α-form, poling under high electric field, or solvent casting from DMF/DMSO. **3. γ-form (Form III)** - Chain conformation: **TTTGTTTG′** (intermediate between α and β). - Crystal system: **Monoclinic**. - Dipoles: **Partially polar** — intermediate properties. - Formation: Crystallization at high temperature (~160 °C) or annealing of α at moderate temperatures. **4. δ-form (Form IV)** - Chain conformation: **TGTG′** (like α), but with **polarized packing**. - Crystal system: **Orthorhombic**. - Dipoles: Polar packing yields a **net dipole**, unlike α. - Formation: Poling α-form under strong electric field without mechanical deformation. **Comparative Summary Table:** | Property | α | β | γ | δ | |---|---|---|---|---| | Conformation | TGTG′ | All-trans TTT | TTTGTTTG′ | TGTG′ | | Crystal System | Monoclinic/Orth | Orthorhombic | Monoclinic | Orthorhombic | | Dipole | Cancelled | Maximum | Intermediate | Polar | | Piezoelectricity | None | **High** | Moderate | Low–Moderate | *(1 mark for each form (4 marks) + 3 marks for comparison/conclusion)* --- #### (b) Explain the role of processing conditions in controlling the polymorphic form of PVDF, with specific examples. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Processing parameters directly determine **which polymorph crystallizes**, since each polymorph is favored under different thermodynamic and kinetic conditions. The key parameters are: **1. Crystallization Temperature** - At **low undercooling** (slow cooling, T near Tm): α-form dominates — thermodynamic product. - At **high undercooling** (quenching to ice): γ-form becomes competitive — kinetic product. **2. Mechanical Stretching** - Stretching α-PVDF films at 80 °C (draw ratio 4–5×) converts TGTG′ to all-trans TTT → **β-form**. - This is the industrial route used in piezoelectric film manufacturing (e.g., Kynar® piezo films). **3. Solvent Casting** - Casting from **DMF, DMSO, or acetone** under controlled evaporation favors β-form because polar solvents stabilize the polar chain conformation. - Casting from **MEK (methyl ethyl ketone)** favors α-form. **4. Electric Field Poling** - Applying **DC electric field** (>100 MV/m) on melt-cast or solution-cast films aligns dipoles → **δ or β** forms. - Used in transducer fabrication. **5. High-Pressure Crystallization** - Pressures above 400 MPa favor β-form due to volume reduction (β is denser packing). **6. Thermal Annealing** - Annealing α-form at 130 °C converts it slowly to γ-form (solid-state transition). - Annealing β-form above 100 °C causes reversion to α — metastable behavior. **Engineering Significance:** - α-form → commodity packaging, chemically inert tubing. - β-form → piezoelectric sensors, energy harvesters, medical ultrasound transducers, MEMS actuators. - γ-form → high-temperature capacitors. *(2 marks temperature + 2 marks stretching + 2 marks solvent/poling + 1 mark industrial relevance)* --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Polyethylene Polymorphism and Structure-Property Relations* `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Choice B]` #### (a) Explain the polymorphism in polyethylene. Discuss the unit cell parameters of the α-form and β-form. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Polyethylene (PE), –(CH₂–CH₂)ₙ–, is the **simplest and most-studied** polymorphous polymer. Despite its simple chemical formula, it exhibits two well-known crystalline forms: **1. α-form (Orthorhombic)** - Chain conformation: **All-trans planar zigzag** — characteristic identity period c = 2.534 Å. - Crystal system: **Orthorhombic** - Unit cell parameters: - a = 7.40 Å, b = 4.93 Å, c = 2.534 Å - α = β = γ = 90° - Chains per unit cell: **2** (one at corner, one at center of ac-face) - Density: ~**1.00 g/cm³** (calculated) - Formation: Slow cooling from melt, equilibrium crystallization - Stability: **Thermodynamically most stable** **2. β-form (Monoclinic)** - Chain conformation: Same **all-trans zigzag** — only packing changes. - Crystal system: **Monoclinic** - Unit cell parameters: - a = 8.09 Å, b = 4.79 Å, c = 2.534 Å, β = 107.9° - Chains per unit cell: **2** - Density: ~**0.997 g/cm³** (slightly lower) - Formation: **Stress-induced or shear-induced crystallization** (e.g., extrusion, fiber drawing) - Stability: **Metastable** **3. Comparison of the Two Forms:** | Parameter | α-form | β-form | |---|---|---| | Crystal system | Orthorhombic | Monoclinic | | a (Å) | 7.40 | 8.09 | | b (Å) | 4.93 | 4.79 | | c (Å) | 2.534 | 2.534 (same) | | β angle | 90° | 107.9° | | Chains/cell | 2 | 2 | | Density (g/cm³) | 1.00 | 0.997 | | Stability | Stable | Metastable | The identical c-parameter confirms that the **chain conformation is preserved** — this is a **classic packing polymorphism**, NOT conformational. **4. Melting Behavior:** - α-form: Tm ≈ 137 °C (sharp DSC peak). - β-form: Tm ≈ 141 °C (slightly higher — characteristic signature of monoclinic PE). *(2 marks α-form description + 2 marks β-form description + 2 marks comparison + 1 mark DSC/melting remark)* --- #### (b) Discuss the X-ray diffraction technique for distinguishing polymorphs in polymers. How would you differentiate α-PE and β-PE using XRD? **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **1. Principle of XRD for Polymorph Identification:** When X-rays of known wavelength λ strike a crystalline sample at angle θ, constructive interference occurs when **Bragg's Law** is satisfied: $$ n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta $$ Each polymorph has a unique set of interplanar spacings $d_{hkl}$, giving a unique diffraction pattern — its **"fingerprint"**. **2. Instrumentation:** - Source: Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.542 Å). - Detector: Scintillation counter or array detector scanning 2θ from ~5° to ~50°. - Output: Plot of intensity vs. 2θ. **3. Bragg Angle Calculations:** For α-PE (orthorhombic, a = 7.40 Å, b = 4.93 Å): - (110) plane → 2θ ≈ 21.6° - (200) plane → 2θ ≈ 23.9° - (020) plane → 2θ ≈ 36.4° For β-PE (monoclinic, a = 8.09 Å, b = 4.79 Å, β = 107.9°): - Strong characteristic (200) reflection → 2θ ≈ 22.0° - Unique (020)/(011) doublet visible - Absence of distinct (110) at 21.6° **4. Diagnostic Fingerprints:** | Reflection | α-PE 2θ (°) | β-PE 2θ (°) | |---|---|---| | (110) | 21.6 | absent or weak | | (200) | 23.9 | 22.0 | | (020) | 36.4 | — | | β-form marker | absent | **22.0 (strong)** | **5. Quantitative Analysis:** The **degree of crystallinity** $X_c$ can be calculated from peak areas: $$ X_c = \frac{A_{\text{crystalline}}}{A_{\text{crystalline}} + A_{\text{amorphous}}} \times 100\% $$ The ratio of the β-form characteristic peak to α-form peak gives an estimate of polymorph fraction in a mixed sample. **6. Other Techniques Used in Combination:** - **DSC:** Differing Tm peaks distinguish polymorphs. - **FTIR:** Conformational bands (e.g., 730/720 cm⁻¹ doublet for α-PE). - **Raman Spectroscopy:** Internal mode shifts between polymorphs. - **Polarized Optical Microscopy:** Different spherulite birefringence patterns. *(2 marks principle + 2 marks α-PE peaks + 2 marks β-PE distinction + 1 mark quantitative crystallinity + bonus 1 mark for complementary techniques)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Do NOT confuse α-form and β-form of PVDF with those of Polyethylene** — they are completely different polymers and have unrelated crystal systems. Many students mix them up. > 2. **Always mention the chain conformation** (TGTG′ vs TTT for PVDF) — examiners deduct heavily if only packing is discussed without conformation. > 3. **Cite specific 2θ values from Bragg's law** — generic statements like "XRD peaks differ" earn zero marks. > 4. **PVDF β-form requires STRETCHING or POLING**, not just cooling — this is a frequent error in viva-voce. > 5. **Use correct Miller indices** — for α-PE the strongest reflection is (110), NOT (200). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **POLYMORPHISM — 30-SECOND REVISION BULLETS (KTU Module 1 Must-Knows)** - **Definition:** Same chemistry, multiple crystal structures → different unit cells $(a, b, c, \alpha, \beta, \gamma)$. - **Two Classes:** - **Packing polymorphism** → chain same, packing differs (α/β Polyethylene). - **Conformational polymorphism** → chain differs (PVDF α vs β). - **Polyethylene:** - α-form: **Orthorhombic**, $a = 7.40, b = 4.93, c = 2.534 \text{ Å}$, stable, density 1.00 g/cm³. - β-form: **Monoclinic**, $\beta = 107.9°$, metastable, stress-induced. - **PVDF (4 forms):** - α: TGTG′, **non-polar**, from melt cooling. - β: TTT, **polar & piezoelectric**, from stretching/poling. - γ: TTTGTTTG′, intermediate. - δ: TGTG′ polarized packing. - **Key Equations:** - Bragg's Law: $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$. - Density: $\rho = \dfrac{n M}{N_A V_{\text{cell}}}$. - Crystallinity: $X_c = \dfrac{A_{\text{cryst}}}{A_{\text{cryst}} + A_{\text{amorph}}}$. - Clausius-Clapeyron: $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{T \cdot \Delta V}$. - **Ostwald's Step Rule:** Metastable forms appear first, then convert to stable. - **Enantiotropic ↔ Monotropic** transitions — know the difference. - **Characterization triad:** XRD (crystal system) + DSC (Tm peaks) + FTIR (conformational bands). - **Application memory:** α-PE → films; β-PVDF → sensors; α-PP → fibres; γ-PVDF → capacitors. - **Exam trick:** Always quote **cell parameters in Å** and **2θ in degrees** — examiners spot-check units!

topics.title: Polymer Single Crystals
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polymer Single Crystals

---

# 🔬 Polymer Single Crystals — KTU Premier Study Notes

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymer Single Crystal:** A polymer single crystal is an *isolated, geometrically regular, three-dimensional crystalline entity* in which a single macroscopic orientation of polymer chains exists throughout the entire crystal volume. These crystals are typically grown from **dilute solutions** (concentration < 0.01 % w/w) under carefully controlled conditions of temperature and solvent quality, and they manifest predominantly as thin, plate-like **lamellae** with lateral dimensions of the order of **1–50 µm** and thicknesses typically in the range of **5–50 nm**.

The polymer chains, despite having contour lengths of several micrometres (10⁴–10⁵ covalent bonds long), traverse the thin lamellar crystal perpendicular to the large flat face in a regular, **folded-chain conformation**. This remarkable structural arrangement was first conclusively demonstrated by **A. Keller, E.W. Fischer, and P.H. Till** independently in **1957**, revolutionizing polymer morphology.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stack of Folded Ribbons"

Imagine a very long, thin satin ribbon about 5 metres long but only 1 cm wide. Now, fold this ribbon back and forth (accordion-style, but neatly) and stack the folds on top of each other. You would get a thin, flat, plate-like object (~ 10 nm thick) made entirely from one continuous ribbon that "zig-zags" up and down. **This is precisely what a polymer single crystal looks like at the nanoscale!**

- The **ribbon** = a single long polymer chain (macromolecule).
- The **folds** = tight chain folds (each fold ≈ 1–2 monomer units).
- The **stacked flat object** = the **single crystal lamella**.
- The **long axis of the ribbon** = the chain direction (which is perpendicular to the lamellar face).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The polymer chain axis (the *c*-axis of the unit cell) is always oriented **perpendicular** to the large flat face of the lamellar crystal. This perpendicular orientation is what defines the folded-chain nature of polymer single crystals.

### ⚙️ Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Lamellar thickness ($L$) | **5 – 50 nm** |
| Lateral dimensions | **1 – 50 µm** |
| Chain-axis orientation | $\perp$ to lamellar face (i.e. **along [001]**) |
| Crystallization T (from melt) | $T_c < T_m^0$ |
| Typical concentration | **< 0.01 %** (dilute solution) |
| Discovery year | **1957** (Keller, Fischer, Till) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔍 Conditions Required for Polymer Single Crystal Formation

Polymer single crystals **do NOT form** under ordinary solidification conditions. They require very specific thermodynamic and kinetic conditions:

1. **Dilute solution growth:** Concentration must be very low (< 0.1 %, typically < 0.01 %) so that individual chains can crystallize in isolation rather than entangling with neighbours.
2. **Slow, isothermal crystallization:** The solution must be held at a *constant*, carefully chosen temperature for long periods (hours to days).
3. **Choice of solvent:** A "poor" solvent or one with a suitable interaction parameter $\chi$ is preferred — it promotes chain collapse and orderly folding.
4. **Self-seeding:** Re-heating and re-cooling may be used to eliminate defective crystals and produce uniform, regular lamellae.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The study of polymer single crystals is essential because it provides the **microscopic experimental proof** of the *folded-chain conformation* of polymer chains in the solid state, which is the foundation for understanding **semicrystalline polymer morphology**, **lamellar thickness–temperature relationships**, and **kinetic theories of crystallization (Lauritzen–Hoffman)**.

### 📐 Morphological Hierarchy of Polymer Crystals

When crystallized from the **melt** (bulk), single crystals cannot form due to chain entanglement. Instead, one observes a hierarchical morphology:

$$\text{Polymer Chain} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Lamella (10 nm)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Fibril / Lamellar Stack} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Spherulite (µm–mm)}$$

From **dilute solution**, however, the lowest level of this hierarchy — the **isolated lamellar single crystal** — can be obtained directly and examined by **transmission electron microscopy (TEM)** and **electron diffraction**.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{1}{L} = \dfrac{1}{L^*} + \dfrac{2\sigma_e}{\Delta h_f \,\Delta T}$ | **Lauritzen–Hoffman equation** (fold-length vs supercooling) |
| 2 | $L \approx \dfrac{2\sigma_e \, T_m^0}{\Delta h_f \,(T_m^0 - T_c)}$ | Simplified $L$–$T_c$ relation |
| 3 | $\Delta T = T_m^0 - T_c$ | Degree of **supercooling** |
| 4 | $\Delta G = 2\sigma_e \, A - \Delta h_f \, \Delta T$ | Net free-energy change for nucleus of fold length $L$ |
| 5 | $\sigma = \dfrac{\Delta l}{L_d} \cdot \dfrac{d}{\lambda}$ | **Moiré pattern** spacing relation (for crystal defects) |
| 6 | $\rho_c \approx 0.9\,\rho_{amorphous}$ | Crystallographic density of folded-chain crystal |
| 7 | $n = \dfrac{L}{l_0}$ | Number of monomer repeats along fold-stem |
| 8 | $G = G_0 \,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta E}{R\,T_c}\right)\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{K_g}{T_c\,\Delta T}\right)$ | Lauritzen–Hoffman **growth rate** equation |

> Notation used:
> - $L$ = lamellar (fold) thickness (nm)
> - $L^*$ = minimum fold length (≈ 5 nm for PE)
> - $\sigma_e$ = **fold surface free energy** (≈ 90 mJ/m² for polyethylene)
> - $\Delta h_f$ = enthalpy of fusion per unit volume
> - $T_m^0$ = equilibrium melting temperature
> - $T_c$ = isothermal crystallization temperature
> - $K_g$ = nucleation constant

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Knowledge of lamellar thickness $L$ directly controls **stiffness, modulus, drawability, melting point, and density** of the resulting semi-crystalline polymer product (e.g. films, fibres, moulded parts). Tailoring $L$ via processing temperature is a cornerstone of industrial polymer engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 📘 Derivation 1 — Lauritzen–Hoffman Fold-Length Equation

**Objective:** Derive the relationship between lamellar thickness $L$ and isothermal crystallization temperature $T_c$.

---

#### **Step 1 — Free Energy of Forming a Lamellar Nucleus**

When a chain segment of length $L$ crystallizes to form a thin lamella, two new **fold surfaces** of area $A$ each are created (top and bottom faces of the lamella). The total free energy of forming the crystallite is therefore:

$$\Delta G_{\text{total}} \;=\; \underbrace{2\,\sigma_e \, A}_{\text{new fold surface}} \;-\; \underbrace{\Delta h_f \,\Delta T \,V}_{\text{crystallization driving force}}$$

where:
- $\sigma_e$ = fold-surface free energy per unit area (J/m²)
- $A$ = area of each fold face (m²)
- $\Delta h_f$ = bulk enthalpy of fusion per unit volume (J/m³)
- $\Delta T = T_m^0 - T_c$ = supercooling (K)
- $V$ = volume of the crystallite (m³)

#### **Step 2 — Geometry of the Lamella**

For a thin, plate-like lamella of lateral dimensions much larger than its thickness $L$, we can approximate:

$$V \approx L\,A \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A = \dfrac{V}{L}$$

Substituting into the free-energy expression:

$$\Delta G_{\text{total}} \;=\; 2\,\sigma_e\,\dfrac{V}{L} \;-\; \Delta h_f \,\Delta T \, V$$

#### **Step 3 — Identify the Critical (Stable) Fold Length**

The polymer crystal grows with the fold length that **minimizes** the total free energy per unit volume. Setting $\dfrac{\partial \Delta G_{\text{total}}}{\partial L} = 0$:

$$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial L}\!\left(\dfrac{2\sigma_e}{L} - \Delta h_f \,\Delta T\right) \;=\; -\,\dfrac{2\sigma_e}{L^2} \;=\; 0$$

This trivial root gives $L \to \infty$, so we must include the **kinetic constraint** that a chain must fold with at least a *minimum stem length* $L^*$. The kinetic theory of Lauritzen and Hoffman adds a term for the work of forming the *first* stem of length $L^*$ (the *substrate completion*), giving the **observed fold length**:

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{1}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{L^*} \;+\; \dfrac{2\sigma_e}{\Delta h_f \,\Delta T}\;}$$

#### **Step 4 — Approximate Form for $T_c \ll T_m^0$**

When $L \gg L^*$ (i.e. for moderate supercooling):

$$L \;\approx\; \dfrac{2\sigma_e \,T_m^0}{\Delta h_f \,(T_m^0 - T_c)} \;\equiv\; \dfrac{C}{\Delta T}$$

This shows that **lamellar thickness $L$ increases linearly with $1/\Delta T$**, the classic *Hoffman–Weeks* behaviour.

#### **Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)**

**Given:** For polyethylene, $T_m^0 = 414.6$ K, $\sigma_e = 90 \times 10^{-3}$ J/m², $\Delta h_f = 2.8 \times 10^{8}$ J/m³. The sample is crystallized at $T_c = 393$ K.

**Find:** Lamellar thickness $L$.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta T = 414.6 - 393 = 21.6 \;\text{K}$$

$$L = \dfrac{2\,(90\times10^{-3})\,(414.6)}{(2.8\times10^{8})\,(21.6)}$$

$$L = \dfrac{7.4628\times10^{-2}}{6.048\times10^{9}}$$

$$L \;\approx\; 1.234 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{m} \;\approx\; \boxed{12.3\;\text{nm}}$$

This matches experimentally observed PE lamellar thicknesses (~ 10–15 nm) — confirming the theory. ✅

---

### 📘 Derivation 2 — Moiré Pattern Spacing for Polymer Crystals

When two single crystals of slightly different lattice parameter overlap on a TEM grid, they produce a **Moiré pattern** whose spacing $D$ is given by:

$$D = \dfrac{d_1\,d_2}{\vert d_1 - d_2 \vert}$$

where $d_1$ and $d_2$ are the two lattice spacings producing the Moiré. Using the small-difference approximation $\delta = d_2 - d_1 \ll d_1$:

$$D \;\approx\; \dfrac{d^{\,2}}{\vert\delta\vert}$$

This is a powerful TEM tool to detect **tiny differences in unit-cell parameters** between two overlaid polymer single crystals.

---

### 📋 Analytical Matrix — Factors Influencing Polymer Single Crystal Morphology

| Factor | Effect on Lamellar Thickness $L$ | Effect on Morphology |
|---|---|---|
| Increasing $T_c$ | $L$ **increases** (thicker lamellae) | More regular, fewer defects |
| Increasing concentration | $L$ **decreases slightly**; tendency to form **axialitic / multilayer** crystals | Stacking & multilayer habits |
| Solvent quality (poor solvent) | $L$ **decreases** | More dendritic, less regular |
| Molecular weight $\uparrow$ | $L$ largely **unchanged**; defect density $\uparrow$ | Fold irregularities, loose loops |
| Cooling rate $\uparrow$ | $L$ **decreases** | Many small irregular lamellae, no spherulites |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🧬 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Polymer Single Crystal Formation

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the hierarchical assembly pathway from a dilute polymer solution to an isolated, observable polymer single crystal — a process route that is central to KTU Module 1 morphology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Dissolve polymer in poor solvent at high T, c less than 0.01 percent] --> B[Step2: Raise T to dissolution temperature Tdiss, then quickly quench]
    B --> C[Step3: Hold isothermally at crystallization temperature Tc for several hours]
    C --> D[Step4: Nucleation event primary homogeneous or heterogeneous]
    D --> E[Step5: Chain folding on growth face, chain axis perpendicular to lamellar face]
    E --> F[Step6: Mature lamellar single crystal, lozenge or hexagonal habit, 5 to 50 nm thick]
    F --> G[Step7: Recovery by filtration or centrifugation and washing]
    G --> H[Step8: TEM observation with electron diffraction confirms single crystal nature]
    H --> I[Step9: Indexed diffraction pattern gives unit cell and chain orientation]
    I --> J[Output: Confirmed polymer single crystal with known fold length L and chain orientation]

    subgraph "Chain Geometry Inside Lamella"
        F1[Long macromolecule of contour length Lc] --> F2[Folds back and forth as tight chain folds, fold period equals L]
        F2 --> F3[Stem segment of length L, oriented along c-axis]
        F3 --> F4[Lamellar surface covered by folds, amorphous fold surface]
    end

    subgraph "Habit Morphology"
        G1[Polyethylene: lozenge, hollow pyramid] --> G2[Polypropylene: lath shaped or rectangular] --> G3[Nylon: rhombic or dendritic]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The two sub-graphs illustrate that (i) the *internal* chain geometry is **folded-chain**, with stems running perpendicular to the lamellar face, and (ii) the *external* habit depends strongly on the chemical structure of the polymer and on the solvent–temperature history.

### 📐 Schematic of Chain Folding Within a Single Lamellar Crystal

```
        ↗──── fold surface (top) ────↖
       /                                \
      |  ┌────┐    ┌────┐    ┌────┐      |
      |  │stem│    │stem│    │stem│      |   ← lamellar thickness L
      |  └────┘    └────┘    └────┘      |
       \                                /
        ↘──── fold surface (bottom) ───↙

  ——————————————→  lateral dimension (µm)  ———→

  Each vertical "stem" = portion of one macromolecule
  Each "fold"        = tight U-turn (1–2 CH₂ units for PE)
  Stem direction     = crystallographic c-axis ⟂ lamellar face
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a polymer single crystal. Mention the conditions necessary for its formation.*

**Model Answer:**

> A **polymer single crystal** is an isolated, geometrically regular, three-dimensional crystalline lamella in which all polymer chains share a single crystallographic orientation, with the chain axis oriented *perpendicular* to the large flat face of the lamella.

**Conditions for formation:**
1. Growth from a **dilute solution** (concentration < 0.01 % w/w).
2. **Slow, isothermal crystallization** at a fixed temperature $T_c$ below the equilibrium melting point $T_m^0$.
3. Use of an appropriate (preferably **poor**) solvent.
4. Sufficient time (hours to days) for full crystal maturation.

*(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the term "chain folding" with reference to polymer single crystals.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Chain folding** is the structural arrangement in which a long polymer chain, on crystallizing into a lamella, repeatedly folds back on itself with a tight *U-turn* (adjacent re-entry) of ~1–2 monomer units, so that successive **stems** of length $L$ (≈ 5–50 nm) stack parallel to one another and perpendicular to the lamellar face. This is the *folded-chain model* first proposed by **Keller (1957)** to explain the observed lamellar thickness being much smaller than the chain contour length.

*(3 marks)*

---

### 📝 PART-B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **ESE Note:** KTU 2024 scheme ESE questions of 14 marks carry **two sub-parts** of 7 marks each. Two parallel-choice questions are provided below — answer **either** Q-A **or** Q-B in full.

---

### 🅰️ QUESTION-A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With neat schematics, describe the **morphology** of polymer single crystals. Discuss how **electron microscopy** and **electron diffraction** are used to confirm their single-crystal nature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Lauritzen–Hoffman equation** relating lamellar thickness $L$ to supercooling $\Delta T$, and explain why lamellar thickness increases with crystallization temperature. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a):**

A polymer single crystal is an **isolated lamellar crystallite** grown from dilute solution. The lamella is typically a *lozenge* (for polyethylene), *hexagonal* (for nylon), or *lath-shaped* (for isotactic polypropylene), with thickness 5–50 nm and lateral dimensions 1–50 µm.

**Confirmation by TEM:** Thin lamellae cast on a TEM grid appear as **electron-transparent thin plates** with sharp, regular geometric outlines. A *shadowing technique* (Pt–C evaporation at an oblique angle) reveals their **3-D pyramidal habit**.

**Confirmation by Electron Diffraction:** A *selected-area electron diffraction (SAED)* pattern from a single lamella shows **discrete, sharp Bragg spots** — exactly like an inorganic single crystal — proving that the entire illuminated area is one single crystalline orientation. Indexing the pattern gives the unit cell dimensions and confirms that the chain axis (c-axis) is **perpendicular** to the lamellar face.

*(7 marks — distributed: morphology 3 m, TEM 2 m, diffraction 2 m)*

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Derivation (as in Section 3, Derivation 1):**

Free energy of forming a lamella of thickness $L$ and area $A$:

$$\Delta G = 2\sigma_e\,A - \Delta h_f \,\Delta T \,V$$

With $V = L\,A$:

$$\Delta G = A\left(\dfrac{2\sigma_e}{L} - \Delta h_f\,\Delta T\right)L$$

Minimizing with respect to $L$ subject to the kinetic constraint of a minimum stem length $L^*$ (introduced by the Lauritzen–Hoffman substrate-completion term) gives:

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{1}{L} = \dfrac{1}{L^*} + \dfrac{2\sigma_e}{\Delta h_f\,(T_m^0 - T_c)}\;}$$

**Physical interpretation:**

- As $T_c$ increases (i.e. $\Delta T = T_m^0 - T_c$ decreases), the term $\dfrac{2\sigma_e}{\Delta h_f\,\Delta T}$ **decreases**, hence $\dfrac{1}{L}$ decreases, so **$L$ increases**.
- Physically: at higher $T_c$ the chain has more **thermal mobility** and **time** to find thicker, more perfect folds before being locked into the crystal.
- At very low $T_c$ (high supercooling), many thin lamellae nucleate rapidly → small $L$ and many defects.

*(7 marks — distributed: free-energy setup 3 m, minimization + final form 2 m, physical interpretation 2 m)*

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION-B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **historical significance** of the discovery of polymer single crystals (1957). Name the scientists involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Moiré pattern** in the context of polymer single crystals? Derive an expression for the Moiré spacing $D$ in terms of the two overlapping lattice spacings. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a):**

In 1957, **A. Keller (Bristol, UK)**, working independently with **E.W. Fischer (Mainz, Germany)** and **P.H. Till (Chicago, USA)**, observed that polyethylene crystallized from dilute xylene solution gave **thin, regular, lozenge-shaped lamellae** (~10 nm thick).

**Historical significance:**

1. **Resolved the fringed-micelle vs folded-chain controversy.** Until 1957, the prevailing *fringed-micelle* model (Hermann, Gerngross, 1930) proposed chains meandered between many crystallites. Keller's TEM + electron-diffraction evidence proved that **chains fold** and lie perpendicular to the lamellar face.
2. **Explained the very small lamellar thickness** observed (10 nm) compared to chain contour length (µm).
3. **Established the foundation of modern polymer morphology** — leading directly to the concepts of *spherulites*, *shish-kebabs*, *lamellar stacks*, and the *Lauritzen–Hoffman crystallization theory*.
4. **Enabled quantitative treatment** of polymer crystallization kinetics.

*(7 marks — distributed: history 2 m, scientists 1 m, significance 4 m)*

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

A **Moiré pattern** is a large-scale interference pattern produced when two periodic lattices of nearly equal spacing are superimposed. In polymer science, if two thin polymer single-crystal lamellae of slightly different unit-cell parameters are placed one on top of the other in the TEM, the resulting image shows a **beat pattern** with a much larger spacing $D$ — the *Moiré spacing*.

**Derivation:**

Consider two 1-D lattices of spacings $d_1$ and $d_2$ overlapping. The bright fringes occur where the lattice points coincide. If the origin of the second lattice is shifted by $x$ relative to the first, coincidence occurs when:

$$n\,d_1 = (n+1)\,d_2 \quad \text{where } n \text{ is an integer}$$

Solving:

$$n = \dfrac{d_2}{d_1 - d_2}$$

The position of the $n$-th bright fringe is:

$$x_n = n\,d_1 = \dfrac{d_1\,d_2}{d_2 - d_1}$$

Hence the **Moiré spacing**:

$$\boxed{\;D \;=\; \dfrac{d_1\,d_2}{\vert d_1 - d_2 \vert}\;}$$

For the small-difference limit, $d_1 \approx d_2 = d$, $d_2 - d_1 = \delta$:

$$D \;\approx\; \dfrac{d^{\,2}}{\vert \delta \vert}$$

**Engineering utility:** Moiré patterns allow measurement of **strain**, **unit-cell distortions**, and **lattice mismatch** between two overlaid polymer crystals with a precision far exceeding direct TEM imaging — important for studying deformation and epitaxy in semicrystalline polymers.

*(7 marks — distributed: definition 2 m, derivation 3 m, utility 2 m)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "single crystal" with "single crystal of an inorganic solid".** Polymer single crystals are *always* lamellar and *never* equi-axed. Examiners deduct marks if you draw bulk crystals.
> 2. **Chain orientation is perpendicular, NOT parallel** to the lamellar face. This is the single most-tested concept in this topic — losing it loses ~3 marks immediately.
> 3. **Conditions of formation (dilute solution + slow isothermal crystallization) must be stated explicitly.** Students who skip this lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **In the Lauritzen–Hoffman derivation, do not forget the term $\frac{1}{L^*}$** — omitting it costs the derivation's full credit. Always write both terms in the final boxed equation.
> 5. **Moiré derivation: students commonly write $D = d_1 - d_2$ (incorrect — this is the *beat wavelength* of two waves, not the Moiré fringe spacing). The correct form has $d_1 d_2$ in the numerator.**

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Polymer single crystals are **isolated, lamellar, plate-like crystals** grown from **dilute solutions** under **isothermal conditions**.
- ✅ Discovered independently by **Keller, Fischer, and Till (1957)**.
- ✅ Polymer chains are **folded** with the chain axis (c-axis) **perpendicular** to the lamellar face.
- ✅ Typical lamellar thickness $L$ = **5–50 nm**; lateral dimensions = **1–50 µm**.
- ✅ Habit depends on polymer: PE → lozenge; iPP → lath; nylon → rhombic/dendritic.
- ✅ Confirmed by **TEM** (morphology) + **electron diffraction** (single-crystal pattern).
- ✅ **Lauritzen–Hoffman equation**: $\dfrac{1}{L} = \dfrac{1}{L^*} + \dfrac{2\sigma_e}{\Delta h_f\,\Delta T}$ — $L$ increases with $T_c$.
- ✅ **Fold surface free energy $\sigma_e$** ≈ 90 mJ/m² for polyethylene.
- ✅ **Moiré spacing**: $D = \dfrac{d_1 d_2}{\vert d_1 - d_2 \vert}$ — a TEM tool for detecting tiny lattice differences.
- ✅ Equilibrium melting temperature $T_m^0$ is the **thermodynamic upper limit** for crystallization; single crystals are obtained at $T_c < T_m^0$.
- ✅ Single crystals **cannot form from the melt** due to chain entanglements — only from dilute solution.
- ✅ Self-seeding (re-heating + slow cooling) is used to obtain **uniform, defect-free** lamellae.
- ✅ The discovery of polymer single crystals is the **single most important event** that established *folded-chain* polymer morphology — a foundation of KTU Module 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lamellae
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lamellae

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lamellae (singular: Lamella):** In polymer science, lamellae are thin, plate-like crystalline regions found within **semicrystalline polymers**, where the long polymer chains are arranged in a highly ordered, parallel fashion. These crystallites are typically formed by **chain folding**, where a single polymer chain repeatedly folds back upon itself like a zipper, with a characteristic fold period of approximately **10–20 nm** and lateral dimensions of the order of **1–50 μm**.

A semicrystalline polymer does not crystallize entirely into a perfect single crystal. Instead, it organizes into alternating **crystalline lamellae** and **amorphous regions**, which together determine the bulk mechanical, thermal, and optical properties of the material. The lamellar thickness (often denoted $l$ or $L$) and the regularity of chain folding directly govern properties such as melting temperature ($T_m$), stiffness, density, and ductility.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Lamellae Matter in Engineering:**
> Lamellae are the fundamental building blocks of crystalline morphology in polymers. Understanding their formation, dimensions, and arrangement is critical for:
> - Designing packaging films (Polyethylene, Polypropylene)
> - Fiber spinning (Nylon, PET)
> - Medical implants (UHMWPE in joint replacements)
> - 3D-printed polymer parts with controlled crystallinity

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Folded Newspaper Stack

Imagine you receive a long, continuous printed newspaper (representing a **single polymer chain**) that is several meters long. You need to store it neatly on a shelf:

1. You do not crumple it randomly — that would be like an **amorphous polymer**.
2. Instead, you fold it back and forth in a regular accordion style, creating a compact stack about 2 cm thick.
3. This folded stack represents a **lamella**.
4. When many such stacks are grouped together with disordered loose papers between them, you get the **semicrystalline polymer morphology**.

The folds in the newspaper are sharp and periodic — exactly mimicking how a polyethylene chain folds every ~10 nm. The "loose papers" between stacks are the **amorphous tie regions** that connect one lamella to the next, providing mechanical cohesion.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Lamellar thickness ($l$) | **10–20 nm** | Determines $T_m$ via Thomson-Gibbs equation |
| Lateral dimension | 1–50 μm | Visible under optical/electron microscopy |
| Fold period | ~5–15 CH₂ units | Molecular origin of periodicity |
| Density of crystalline lamella | ~1.00 g/cm³ (PE) | Higher than amorphous (~0.85 g/cm³) |
| Surface free energy ($\sigma_e$) | ~0.05–0.1 J/m² | Critical to fold formation energy |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Evolution of Lamellar Theory — Two Competing Models

### 2.1.1 Fringed Micelle Model (1920s–1950s)
Proposed by **Hermann Staudinger** and later refined, this older model suggests that:
- A single polymer chain passes through **multiple crystalline and amorphous regions** alternately.
- Crystallites are small and connected by fringed, disordered tie chains.
- Suitable for explaining low crystallinity and broad melting behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Fails to explain single-crystal growth from dilute polymer solutions observed by Keller, Fischer, and Till in 1957.

### 2.1.2 Folded-Chain Lamella Model (1957 — Keller, Till, Fischer)
This is the **currently accepted model** for crystalline lamellae:
- Polymer chains fold back and forth in a regular, **adjacent re-entry** pattern.
- Fold period is governed by **kinetic crystallization conditions** (undercooling $\Delta T$).
- Explains the rhombic/hexagonal single crystals grown from dilute solution.
- Provides the molecular basis for the **Gibbs-Thomson relationship** between lamellar thickness and melting point.

---

## 2.2 Structural Anatomy of a Lamella

A single lamella consists of the following molecular components:

1. **Crystal Core (Stem Region):**
   The interior of the lamella where chain stems are packed in a regular lattice (orthorhombic in polyethylene).

2. **Fold Surface (Top and Bottom Faces):**
   The sharp chain folds that close each trajectory. These are tight loops of ~5–10 chain atoms.

3. **Cilia:**
   Loose chain ends protruding from the fold surface into the amorphous phase.

4. **Tie Molecules:**
   Polymer segments that originate in one lamella, traverse the amorphous region, and enter another lamella. These provide **mechanical strength** to the bulk polymer.

5. **Loose Loops:**
   Long, non-adjacent re-entry folds that extend into the amorphous phase before re-entering the crystal.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Lamellae & Crystallinity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Polymer Science exam.**

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_m = T_m^0 \left(1 - \dfrac{2\sigma_e}{\Delta H_f \cdot l}\right)$ | **Gibbs-Thomson equation** — relates lamellar thickness $l$ to observed melting point $T_m$ |
| 2 | $l^* = \dfrac{2\sigma_e \cdot T_m^0}{\Delta H_f \cdot \Delta T}$ | Critical lamellar thickness for a given undercooling $\Delta T$ |
| 3 | $X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100\%$ | **% Crystallinity** from DSC enthalpy |
| 4 | $\Delta T = T_m^0 - T_c$ | Undercooling driving crystallization |
| 5 | $1 - X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c}{\rho_s} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_s - \rho}{\rho_c - \rho}$ | Density-based crystallinity (where $\rho_c$, $\rho_a$, $\rho_s$ are crystalline, amorphous, and sample densities) |
| 6 | $G = G_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{\Delta E}{RT}\right) \exp\left(-\dfrac{\Delta F^*}{kT}\right)$ | Lamellar **growth rate** (secondary nucleation theory) |

Where the symbols mean:
- $T_m^0$ = Equilibrium melting temperature of an infinitely thick crystal (e.g., **414.6 K** for polyethylene)
- $\sigma_e$ = Surface free energy of the fold surface (~**0.09 J/m²** for PE)
- $\Delta H_f$ = Bulk enthalpy of fusion per unit volume (~**2.8 × 10⁸ J/m³** for PE)
- $l$ = Lamellar thickness (m)
- $\Delta T$ = Undercooling (K)
- $X_c$ = Degree of crystallinity
- $\rho_c$, $\rho_a$, $\rho_s$ = Densities of crystalline, amorphous, and sample phases

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where These Equations Are Used

1. **Processing Optimization:**
   Injection molders use $l^*$ to predict and control crystallinity by adjusting mold temperature (which sets $T_c$ and hence $\Delta T$).

2. **Material Selection for High-Temperature Service:**
   Annealing increases lamellar thickness, raising $T_m$. The Gibbs-Thomson equation predicts the resulting service temperature ceiling.

3. **Biomedical Implants:**
   UHMWPE hip liners require controlled lamellar morphology to minimize wear. Lamellar thickness is a Quality Control parameter.

4. **Recycled Polymer Property Prediction:**
   Each thermal reprocessing cycle alters lamellar thickness. The Gibbs-Thomson equation estimates the resulting drop in $T_m$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gibbs-Thomson Equation for Lamellar Melting Point

We derive the relationship between the melting temperature of a lamellar crystal and its thickness, starting from thermodynamic equilibrium at the melting interface.

### Step 1: Define the Free Energy of the Lamellar Crystal

For a crystalline lamella of thickness $l$ and lateral area $A$, the total Gibbs free energy relative to the liquid state is:

$$G = G_{bulk} + G_{surface}$$

The bulk contribution is the free energy of fusion times the lamellar volume:

$$G_{bulk} = -A \cdot l \cdot \Delta g_f$$

where $\Delta g_f$ is the free energy of fusion per unit volume (negative, since the crystal is more stable than the melt).

### Step 2: Add the Surface Free Energy of the Two Fold Surfaces

The top and bottom fold surfaces each contribute a surface free energy $\sigma_e$:

$$G_{surface} = 2A\sigma_e$$

### Step 3: Write the Total Free Energy Expression

Combining the two contributions:

$$G(l) = -A \cdot l \cdot \Delta g_f + 2A\sigma_e$$

### Step 4: Equilibrium Condition at the Melting Point

At the observed melting point $T_m$ (for a finite-thickness crystal), the free energy of the crystal equals that of the liquid:

$$\Delta g_f(T_m) = \dfrac{2\sigma_e}{l}$$

### Step 5: Linearize $\Delta g_f$ Near the Equilibrium Melting Point

Using a Taylor expansion around $T_m^0$ (the melting point of an infinite crystal):

$$\Delta g_f(T) \approx \Delta H_f \cdot \dfrac{(T_m^0 - T)}{T_m^0}$$

Substituting $T = T_m$:

$$\Delta H_f \cdot \dfrac{(T_m^0 - T_m)}{T_m^0} = \dfrac{2\sigma_e}{l}$$

### Step 6: Solve for $T_m$ in Terms of $l$

$$\boxed{T_m = T_m^0 \left(1 - \dfrac{2\sigma_e}{\Delta H_f \cdot l}\right)}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A thinner lamella melts at a lower temperature. This is why **ultra-thin nanolamellae** in nanocomposites melt far below the bulk $T_m$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Lamellar Thickness from Undercooling

Starting from the kinetic theory of polymer crystallization (Hoffman-Lauritzen), the lamellar thickness that a growing crystal selects is the one that **maximizes the growth rate** under a given undercooling $\Delta T$.

### Step 1: Nucleation Rate Expression

The rate of secondary nucleation on a substrate of width $a$ is:

$$i = i_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{4\sigma\sigma_e b_0 T_m^0}{k_B T \Delta H_f \Delta T}\right)$$

where $\sigma$ is the lateral surface energy and $b_0$ is the stem width.

### Step 2: Critical Stem Length ($l^*$)

The minimum stable nucleus length is found by balancing bulk and surface energies:

$$l^* = \dfrac{2\sigma_e T_m^0}{\Delta H_f \Delta T}$$

### Step 3: Substitution Check

$$\boxed{l^* = \dfrac{2\sigma_e T_m^0}{\Delta H_f \cdot \Delta T}}$$

For polyethylene with $\sigma_e = 0.09$ J/m², $T_m^0 = 414.6$ K, $\Delta H_f = 2.8 \times 10^8$ J/m³, and $\Delta T = 20$ K:

$$l^* = \dfrac{2 \times 0.09 \times 414.6}{2.8 \times 10^8 \times 20}$$

$$l^* = \dfrac{74.628}{5.6 \times 10^9}$$

$$l^* \approx 1.33 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} = \mathbf{13.3 \text{ nm}}$$

This matches the experimentally observed **10–20 nm** range perfectly.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: Crystallinity Calculation from Density

Given: Polypropylene sample with $\rho_s = 0.905$ g/cm³, $\rho_c = 0.946$ g/cm³, $\rho_a = 0.854$ g/cm³.

$$X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100\%$$

$$X_c = \dfrac{0.946 \times (0.905 - 0.854)}{0.905 \times (0.946 - 0.854)} \times 100\%$$

$$X_c = \dfrac{0.946 \times 0.051}{0.905 \times 0.092} \times 100\%$$

$$X_c = \dfrac{0.04825}{0.08326} \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{57.9\%}$$

### Example 2: Predicting Melting Point from Lamellar Thickness

Given: PE lamella with $l = 15$ nm, $T_m^0 = 414.6$ K, $\sigma_e = 0.09$ J/m², $\Delta H_f = 2.8 \times 10^8$ J/m³.

$$T_m = 414.6 \left(1 - \dfrac{2 \times 0.09}{2.8 \times 10^8 \times 15 \times 10^{-9}}\right)$$

$$T_m = 414.6 \left(1 - \dfrac{0.18}{4.2}\right) = 414.6 \times (1 - 0.0429)$$

$$T_m = 414.6 \times 0.9571 \approx \mathbf{396.8 \text{ K} = 123.6 \text{ °C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Bulk PE typically melts at ~130–135 °C. Our 15 nm lamella melts ~7–10 °C lower, consistent with experimental observations for solution-grown single crystals.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Molecular Architecture of a Folded-Chain Lamella

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FoldSurface["FOLD SURFACE (Top)"]
        Fold1["Sharp chain fold"]
        Fold2["Sharp chain fold"]
        Fold3["Sharp chain fold"]
    end

    subgraph CrystalCore["CRYSTAL CORE (Stems)"]
        Stem1["Aligned chain stem"]
        Stem2["Aligned chain stem"]
        Stem3["Aligned chain stem"]
        Stem4["Aligned chain stem"]
    end

        BFold1["Sharp chain fold"]
        BFold2["Sharp chain fold"]
        BFold3["Sharp chain fold"]
    end

    subgraph AmorRegion["AMORPHOUS REGION"]
        TieMol["Tie molecule bridging to next lamella"]
        Cilium["Loose chain end - cilia"]
        LooseLoop["Loose loop in amorphous phase"]
    end

    Fold1 --> Stem1
    Fold2 --> Stem2
    Fold3 --> Stem3
    Stem1 --> BFold1
    Stem2 --> BFold2
    Stem3 --> BFold3

    BFold2 -.-> Cilium
```

## 4.2 Lamellar Hierarchy → Spherulitic Structure (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
    end

    end

    end

    Chain -->|"Folds periodically"| Lamella
    Lamella -->|"Stacked via tie molecules"| Stack
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Fringed Micelle vs. Folded Chain Models

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define crystalline lamellae in polymers. Mention their typical dimensions.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

> Crystalline lamellae are thin, plate-like regions of order in semicrystalline polymers where long-chain molecules fold back and forth in a regular adjacent re-entry pattern, forming a well-defined crystalline lattice. They are characterized by a thickness of approximately **10–20 nm** and lateral dimensions ranging from **1–50 μm**. These lamellae are interconnected by tie molecules and separated by amorphous regions, giving rise to the typical fringed or banded spherulitic morphology observed under polarized optical microscopy.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for thickness, 1 mark for lateral dimension and tie molecules)*

---

### Question 2: Why does a polymer lamella of smaller thickness melt at a lower temperature?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> A lamella of smaller thickness has a higher surface-area-to-volume ratio. Since the fold surfaces carry an excess surface free energy $\sigma_e$, a thinner crystal has a higher total free energy and is therefore thermodynamically less stable. The Gibbs-Thomson equation:
> $$T_m = T_m^0 \left(1 - \dfrac{2\sigma_e}{\Delta H_f \cdot l}\right)$$
> shows that as $l$ decreases, $T_m$ decreases linearly. This is why ultrathin nanolamellae in polymer nanocomposites melt far below the bulk $T_m$.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for surface-to-volume reason, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for conclusion)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **folded-chain lamella model** of polymer crystallization. Compare it with the fringed micelle model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Gibbs-Thomson equation** relating lamellar thickness to melting temperature. A polyethylene lamella has thickness **12 nm**. Given $T_m^0 = 414.6$ K, $\sigma_e = 0.09$ J/m², $\Delta H_f = 2.8 \times 10^8$ J/m³, calculate its melting point. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

| Feature | Fringed Micelle Model | Folded-Chain Lamella Model |
|---|---|---|
| Chain trajectory | Chains pass through many crystallites | Chains fold within a single lamella |
| Crystallite shape | Bundles / micelles | Thin plates (lamellae) |
| Crystallite size | Variable, broad | Uniform ~10–20 nm thick |
| Fold nature | No regular folds | Adjacent re-entry chain folding |
| Evidence | X-ray diffraction of fibers | Electron microscopy of single crystals (1957, Keller) |
| Modern relevance | Partially valid for low crystallinity | Standard model for melt/solution-grown crystals |

*(7 marks distributed across: 2 marks for folded chain explanation with diagram, 2 marks for fringed micelle explanation, 2 marks for comparison table, 1 mark for historical evidence)*

**Part (b):**

**Derivation:** (Already shown in SECTION 3, Step-by-Step)

Final result:

$$T_m = T_m^0 \left(1 - \dfrac{2\sigma_e}{\Delta H_f \cdot l}\right)$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $l = 12 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $T_m^0 = 414.6$ K, $\sigma_e = 0.09$ J/m², $\Delta H_f = 2.8 \times 10^8$ J/m³.

$$T_m = 414.6 \left(1 - \dfrac{2 \times 0.09}{2.8 \times 10^8 \times 12 \times 10^{-9}}\right)$$

$$T_m = 414.6 \left(1 - \dfrac{0.18}{3.36}\right)$$

$$T_m = 414.6 \times (1 - 0.0536)$$

$$T_m = 414.6 \times 0.9464 = \mathbf{392.4 \text{ K} = 119.2 \text{ °C}}$$

*(7 marks: 4 for derivation steps, 2 for substitution, 1 for final answer with units)*

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With the aid of neat sketches, describe the **structural features of a single polymer lamella**, including tie molecules, cilia, loose loops, and fold surfaces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polypropylene sample has a density of **0.905 g/cm³**. The crystalline density is **0.946 g/cm³** and amorphous density is **0.854 g/cm³**. Calculate the **percentage crystallinity**. Comment on how this crystallinity influences its mechanical strength. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):** (Refer to Section 4.1 diagram and Section 2.2 description for full answer structure)

Key points to cover (7 marks):
1. Definition and dimensions of lamella (1 mark)
2. Crystal core / stem region (1 mark)
3. Fold surface with adjacent re-entry (1 mark)
4. Cilia and loose loops (1 mark)
5. Tie molecules and their mechanical role (2 marks)
6. Neat labeled sketch (1 mark)

**Part (b):**

Using the density-based crystallinity equation:

$$X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100\%$$

Substituting the values:

$$X_c = \dfrac{0.946 \times (0.905 - 0.854)}{0.905 \times (0.946 - 0.854)} \times 100\%$$

$$X_c = \dfrac{0.946 \times 0.051}{0.905 \times 0.092} \times 100\%$$

$$X_c = \dfrac{0.04825}{0.08326} \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{57.9\%}$$

**Comment on Mechanical Strength:**
A crystallinity of ~58% indicates that PP has a substantial load-bearing lamellar network interconnected by tie molecules. This gives PP:
- Higher tensile strength than amorphous polymers
- Good stiffness and dimensional stability
- Higher melting point and heat distortion temperature
- However, the 42% amorphous content provides some ductility and impact resistance

*(7 marks: 4 for formula and substitution, 1 for answer, 2 for mechanical property discussion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting units:** Always state thickness in nm when converting to the Gibbs-Thomson equation (use $l \times 10^{-9}$ m).
> - **Mixing up models:** Students often confuse "fringed micelle" with "fringed fibril" — they are distinct concepts.
> - **DSC vs Density crystallinity:** KTU examiners often give DSC data ($\Delta H_f$) — use $X_c = \Delta H_f / \Delta H_f^0$, NOT the density formula.
> - **Tie molecules vs Cilia:** Tie molecules connect TWO lamellae (mechanical role); Cilia are loose ends sticking into the amorphous phase (no structural role).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Lamellae**

- ✅ Lamellae are **plate-like crystalline regions** of thickness **10–20 nm** in semicrystalline polymers.
- ✅ The **folded-chain model** (Keller, 1957) is the accepted theory — chains fold in **adjacent re-entry** fashion.
- ✅ The **fringed micelle model** is older and explains only low-crystallinity systems.
- ✅ **Gibbs-Thomson equation**: $T_m = T_m^0 \left(1 - \dfrac{2\sigma_e}{\Delta H_f \cdot l}\right)$ — smaller $l$ → lower $T_m$.
- ✅ **Critical thickness**: $l^* = \dfrac{2\sigma_e T_m^0}{\Delta H_f \Delta T}$ — controlled by undercooling $\Delta T$.
- ✅ Lamella consists of: **crystal core, fold surface, cilia, loose loops, tie molecules**.
- ✅ **Tie molecules** are critical for mechanical strength — they bridge adjacent lamellae.
- ✅ Lamellae assemble into **spherulites**, which are the visible morphology under crossed polarizers.
- ✅ Crystallinity can be measured by: **DSC** ($X_c = \Delta H_f/\Delta H_f^0$), **density**, or **X-ray diffraction**.
- ✅ Annealing increases lamellar thickness → raises $T_m$ → improves high-temperature performance.
- ✅ Lamellar thickness is a **kinetically controlled** parameter (not thermodynamically fixed).
- ✅ Surface free energy $\sigma_e$ of fold surface is typically **0.05–0.1 J/m²** for PE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spherulites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Spherulites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: SPHERULITES — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Spherulite (KTU Standard Definition):**
> A *spherulite* is a polycrystalline, spherical morphological superstructure found in **semicrystalline polymers**, formed by the radial outward growth of chain-folded crystalline lamellae from a common central nucleus, embedded within a continuous amorphous matrix, typically ranging in diameter from **1 µm to 100 µm**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, spherulites are classified as the **secondary morphological unit** of semicrystalline polymers — sitting hierarchically between the **unit cell** (Å scale), the **lamella** (~10 nm), and the **bulk semicrystalline solid** (mm scale).

| Hierarchy Level | Structural Entity | Typical Size Scale |
|-----------------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Unit Cell | ~ 5 – 10 Å |
| 2 | Lamellar Crystal | ~ 10 – 20 nm thick |
| 3 | **Spherulite** | **1 – 100 µm** |
| 4 | Bulk Polymer Article | mm – m |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Popcorn" or "Snowball" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are rolling a **snowball down a snowy hill** (this is the polymer melt cooling below its melting/crystallization temperature). The first tiny ice crystal that forms becomes the **nucleus**. As the snowball rolls, it picks up snow from all sides — but instead of rolling, the crystal grows by sending out tiny crystal "fingers" (lamellae) in *every* radial direction, branching as they go, like the arms of a sea urchin. The space *between* the arms fills with disordered, non-crystalline material (the "dirt patches" in the snow). When viewed under a microscope between two crossed polarizers, the arms look bright on a dark background, and the dark cross in the middle is called the **Maltese Cross** — the optical fingerprint of a spherulite.

## 1.3 Why Spherulites Matter in Polymer Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU examiners love this topic:** Spherulite size, distribution, and degree of crystallinity **directly control** the mechanical strength, optical transparency, density, impact resistance, and chemical resistance of a finished polymer product. For example:
> - **Large spherulites** → brittle, opaque, weak (e.g., slow-cooled HDPE)
> - **Small/numerous spherulites** → tough, sometimes translucent (e.g., injection-molded PP)
> - **Spherulitic structure** governs the failure mode under tensile and impact loading.

## 1.4 Optical Signature — The Maltese Cross

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maltese Cross Extinction Pattern:** When a spherulite is viewed under a **cross-polarized optical microscope (POM)**, two dark isogyres appear perpendicular to the polarizer and analyzer axes, forming a characteristic cross. This occurs because the polymer chains in the lamellae are oriented *tangentially* (perpendicular to the radius), and the radial symmetry causes complete extinction along the polarizer/analyzer directions.

The standard metric for spherulite observation is the **spherulitic radius (r)**, measured in **micrometers (µm)**, and the **spherulitic growth rate (G = dr/dt)**, measured in **µm/min** or **µm/s**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Four-Stage Formation Mechanism of a Spherulite

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks the sequence of spherulite formation. Memorize the order: **Nucleation → Primary Lamellar Growth → Non-Crystallographic Branching → Impingement**.

### Stage 1 — Primary Nucleation
A sub-microscopic embryo of ordered chain segments forms a **stable nucleus** once it exceeds a critical size (ΔG*).

- **Homogeneous nucleation:** nucleus forms spontaneously in pure melt (rare, requires high supercooling).
- **Heterogeneous nucleation:** nucleus forms on a foreign surface (catalyst residue, dust, container wall) — **the dominant mechanism in industrial polymer processing**.

### Stage 2 — Primary Lamellar Growth
