(spinodal points) | . . | X X = local minima (binodal tie-line ends) | / \ | / \ |_____/_____\______________ φ2 0 0.2 0.5 0.8 1.0 ``` - **Single minimum at all compositions** → fully miscible ($\chi < \chi_c$). - **Double-well shape** with two minima → partial miscibility, two coexisting phases ($\chi > \chi_c$). - **Common tangent line** between the two minima defines the binodal (coexistence) compositions. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the Flory–Huggins interaction parameter $\chi$. What does a negative value of $\chi$ signify about a polymer blend?** **Model Answer:** The Flory–Huggins interaction parameter $\chi$ is a dimensionless quantity that characterizes the **enthalpic interaction energy per solvent/solute pair** divided by thermal energy $k_BT$. Microscopically, $$\chi = \frac{z \, \Delta w_{12}}{k_B T}$$ where $z$ is the lattice coordination number and $\Delta w_{12} = w_{12} - \tfrac{1}{2}(w_{11} + w_{22})$ is the exchange energy for a 1–2 contact. A **negative $\chi$** signifies an **exothermic (favorable) enthalpy of mixing** — i.e., A–B segmental contacts are energetically preferred over A–A and B–B contacts. Such systems tend to be **miscible** because $\Delta H_{mix} < 0$ drives $\Delta G_{mix}$ negative. Examples: blends with strong specific interactions (hydrogen bonds, dipole–dipole, acid–base). --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State and explain the two thermodynamic criteria for a binary polymer blend to be miscible.** **Model Answer:** For a binary polymer blend (components 1 and 2 with volume fractions $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$) to be **thermodynamically miscible**, it must simultaneously satisfy: **Criterion 1 — Thermodynamic feasibility:** $$\Delta G_{mix} < 0$$ The free energy of the mixed state must be lower than the unmixed state, so mixing is spontaneous. **Criterion 2 — Stability against phase separation:** $$\left(\frac{\partial^{2} \Delta G_{mix}}{\partial \phi_{2}^{2}}\right)_{T,P} > 0$$ The free-energy curve must be **concave up** at the blend composition; otherwise the system can lower its free energy by spontaneously separating into two phases (spinodal decomposition). Both criteria together ensure a single homogeneous phase persists indefinitely. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive the Flory–Huggins expression for the Gibbs free energy of mixing $\Delta G_{mix}$ for a binary polymer blend. Clearly state the assumptions of the theory. **(b)** **[7 Marks]** A symmetric polymer blend ($N_1 = N_2 = N$) has $\chi = 0.0025$ at $T = 300$ K. Determine the critical degree of polymerization above which the blend phase-separates. Take $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **(a) Derivation of the Flory–Huggins $\Delta G_{mix}$ expression:** **Assumptions of the theory:** 1. Polymer chains occupy a **3D lattice** of $N_0$ sites, each site of volume equal to a reference segment. 2. Each polymer chain has $N_i$ segments (degree of polymerization). 3. Chains are **flexible** and **all lattice sites are equally probable** (Flory's approximation). 4. **Incompressible** mixture (no volume change on mixing, $\Delta V_{mix} = 0$). 5. Only **nearest-neighbor contacts** contribute to the enthalpy. **Step 1 — Combinatorial entropy of mixing:** The number of ways $\Omega$ to arrange $n_1$ chains of $N_1$ segments and $n_2$ chains of $N_2$ segments on a lattice of $N_0$ sites is (Flory's combinatorial result): $$\Omega = \frac{N_0!}{(N_0 - n_1 N_1 - n_2 N_2)! \cdot (n_1) \cdot (n_2)} \bigg/ \text{(chain internal arrangements)}$$ Using Stirling's approximation ($\ln x! \approx x \ln x - x$) and Boltzmann's relation $S = k_B \ln \Omega$, after subtracting the entropy of the unmixed components, the **entropy of mixing** is: $$\Delta S_{mix} = -R \left[ n_1 \ln \phi_1 + n_2 \ln \phi_2 \right]$$ where $\phi_i$ is the **volume fraction** of component $i$: $$\phi_1 = \frac{n_1 N_1}{N_0}, \quad \phi_2 = \frac{n_2 N_2}{N_0}$$ **Step 2 — Enthalpy of mixing:** The number of 1–2 nearest-neighbor contacts is $z N_0 \phi_1 \phi_2$ (where $z$ is the coordination number). Each contact carries exchange energy $\Delta w_{12}$. Total enthalpy change: $$\Delta H_{mix} = z N_0 \phi_1 \phi_2 \cdot \Delta w_{12}$$ **Step 3 — Combining into Gibbs free energy:** $$\Delta G_{mix} = \Delta H_{mix} - T \Delta S_{mix}$$ $$\Delta G_{mix} = z N_0 \phi_1 \phi_2 \cdot \Delta w_{12} + R T \left[ n_1 \ln \phi_1 + n_2 \ln \phi_2 \right]$$ Define $\chi = \dfrac{z \Delta w_{12}}{k_B T}$. Since $N_0 = n_1 N_1 + n_2 N_2$, we can rewrite $z N_0 \phi_1 \phi_2 \Delta w_{12} = R T \chi n_1 \phi_2 = R T \chi n_2 \phi_1$. Hence: $$\boxed{\Delta G_{mix} = RT \left[ n_1 \ln \phi_1 + n_2 \ln \phi_2 + n_1 \phi_2 \chi \right]}$$ In the equivalent **per-lattice-site** form: $$\boxed{\frac{\Delta g_{mix}}{RT} = \frac{\phi_1}{N_1} \ln \phi_1 + \frac{\phi_2}{N_2} \ln \phi_2 + \chi \phi_1 \phi_2}$$ **(b) Solution to the numerical problem:** For a symmetric blend, the critical $\chi$ is: $$\chi_c = \frac{2}{N}$$ Phase separation occurs when $\chi > \chi_c$: $$\chi > \frac{2}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N > \frac{2}{\chi}$$ Substituting $\chi = 0.0025$: $$N > \frac{2}{0.0025} = 800$$ Therefore the **critical degree of polymerization** is: $$\boxed{N_c = 800}$$ For $N > 800$, the blend **phase-separates**; for $N < 800$, it remains **miscible** (homogeneous). --- #### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** **[7 Marks]** Starting from the Flory–Huggins free energy expression, derive the **spinodal equation** and the **critical value of $\chi$** for a binary polymer blend. State clearly the physical meaning of each result. **(b)** **[7 Marks]** Explain the difference between **UCST** and **LCST** phase behavior in polymer blends. Why do most high-molecular-weight polymer–polymer blends exhibit LCST behavior, while polymer–solvent systems more commonly show UCST? --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **(a) Derivation of the spinodal equation and critical $\chi$:** Starting from: $$\frac{\Delta g_{mix}}{RT} = \frac{\phi_1}{N_1} \ln \phi_1 + \frac{\phi_2}{N_2} \ln \phi_2 + \chi \phi_1 \phi_2$$ Using $\phi_1 = 1 - \phi_2$, the **first derivative** (chemical potential difference) is: $$\frac{\partial (\Delta g_{mix}/RT)}{\partial \phi_2} = -\frac{1}{N_1} \ln(1-\phi_2) - \frac{1}{N_1} + \frac{1}{N_2} \ln \phi_2 + \frac{1}{N_2} + \chi(1 - 2\phi_2)$$ The **second derivative** is: $$\frac{\partial^{2} (\Delta g_{mix}/RT)}{\partial \phi_2^{2}} = \frac{1}{N_1 \phi_1} + \frac{1}{N_2 \phi_2} - 2\chi$$ The **spinodal condition** is set when this second derivative equals zero (boundary of stability): $$\boxed{\chi_s(\phi_2) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{N_1 \phi_1} + \frac{1}{N_2 \phi_2}\right)}$$ **Physical meaning:** Inside the spinodal curve ($\chi > \chi_s$), the system is **unconditionally unstable** — any infinitesimal composition fluctuation grows spontaneously (spinodal decomposition). Outside (but within the binodal), the system is **metastable** — phase separation occurs via nucleation and growth. For the **critical point**, both the second AND third derivatives vanish: $$\frac{\partial^{3} (\Delta g_{mix}/RT)}{\partial \phi_2^{3}} = \frac{1}{N_1 \phi_1^{2}} - \frac{1}{N_2 \phi_2^{2}} = 0$$ This gives $\phi_2 / \phi_1 = \sqrt{N_1/N_2}$, leading to: $$\phi_{1,c} = \frac{\sqrt{N_2}}{\sqrt{N_1} + \sqrt{N_2}}, \quad \phi_{2,c} = \frac{\sqrt{N_1}}{\sqrt{N_1} + \sqrt{N_2}}$$ Substituting back into the spinodal equation: $$\boxed{\chi_c = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{N_1}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{N_2}}\right)^{2}}$$ **Physical meaning:** For $\chi > \chi_c$, no composition is stable as a single phase over the entire range; phase separation becomes inevitable. For symmetric blend $N_1 = N_2 = N$, this reduces to $\chi_c = 2/N$, showing the entropic driving force for mixing vanishes as $1/N$ as molecular weight increases. **(b) UCST vs LCST behavior:** | Feature | **UCST (Upper Critical Solution Temperature)** | **LCST (Lower Critical Solution Temperature)** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Location in T–φ diagram** | Two-phase region is *below* the critical T | Two-phase region is *above* the critical T | | **Phase separation** | On **cooling** | On **heating** | | **Typical systems** | Polymer + small-molecule solvent (e.g., PS/cyclohexane); metal alloys | Most polymer–polymer blends (e.g., PS/PVME); polymer + polar solvent | | **Driving force** | Enthalpic (positive $\chi$ dominant at low $T$) | Entropic / free-volume effects; specific interactions weakened at high $T$ | | **Common in polymers?** | Less common for blend–blend | **Very common** for polymer blends | **Why polymer–polymer blends mostly show LCST:** In polymer–polymer systems, the combinatorial entropy is extremely small (since $N$ is large), so it cannot compensate for unfavorable $\Delta H_{mix}$ even at moderate $\chi$. Furthermore: - $\chi$ often has a temperature dependence $\chi = A + B/T$, where $A$ is positive and originates from **free-volume dissimilarity** between polymers. As $T$ rises, the difference in thermal expansion coefficients causes the polymers to lose "compatibility" at the segmental level. - Specific interactions (H-bonds, dipoles) that hold the blend together at low $T$ are thermally disrupted at high $T$. - Above LCST, the Flory–Huggins framework predicts that **equation-of-state effects** (compressibility, free volume) make $\chi$ effectively **increase with $T$**, eventually exceeding $\chi_c$ and causing phase separation. In contrast, polymer + solvent systems have a large combinatorial entropy contribution (since the solvent has $N = 1$), so $\Delta S_{mix}$ dominates. As $T$ increases, the entropy term $-T\Delta S_{mix}$ becomes even more favorable, often driving UCST behavior (miscible at high $T$, immiscible at low $T$). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse the sign convention** of $\chi$ with miscibility. A small **negative** $\chi$ is favorable (often leads to miscibility), but a small **positive** $\chi$ can still allow miscibility if $\chi < \chi_c$. Students often write "positive $\chi$ = immiscible," which is wrong for low-$N$ systems. > 2. **Miscibility criterion is NOT just $\Delta G_{mix} < 0$**. The second-derivative stability criterion ($\partial^{2}G/\partial \phi^{2} > 0$) is equally important and must be stated in long-answer questions. > 3. **UCST vs LCST naming confusion:** "Upper" critical T means the *critical point is at the top* of the two-phase region (UCST) — meaning phase separation occurs at *low* T. LCST has the critical point at the *bottom* of the two-phase region. > 4. **$\chi$ vs $\chi_c$ comparison** is the correct operational test — students often confuse $\chi$ with $\Delta G_{mix}$ directly. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2 — Thermodynamic View of Miscibility)** - **Flory–Huggins free energy** of mixing is the **central equation** of this topic — memorize all three forms (per mole, per molecule, per lattice site). - **Entropy of mixing is always positive** (favorable) but becomes **negligible** for high-$N$ polymers. This is why high-$M$ blends are mostly immiscible. - **Sign of $\chi$ determines the enthalpic contribution:** $\Delta H_{mix} = RT \chi \phi_1 \phi_2$. Negative $\chi$ → exothermic → favorable. - **Two criteria for miscibility:** (i) $\Delta G_{mix} < 0$, (ii) $\partial^{2}(\Delta G_{mix})/\partial \phi_2^{2} > 0$. - **Spinodal equation:** $\chi_s = \tfrac{1}{2}(1/N_1\phi_1 + 1/N_2\phi_2)$. Inside spinodal → spontaneous decomposition. - **Critical $\chi$ value:** $\chi_c = \tfrac{1}{2}(1/\sqrt{N_1} + 1/\sqrt{N_2})^{2}$. For symmetric blend, $\chi_c = 2/N$. - **Critical composition:** $\phi_{1,c} = \sqrt{N_2}/(\sqrt{N_1} + \sqrt{N_2})$; symmetric blend gives $\phi_c = 0.5$. - **UCST:** miscible at high $T$, phase-separates on cooling (driven by enthalpy). - **LCST:** miscible at low $T$, phase-separates on heating (driven by free-volume / compressibility effects). - **Molecular weight effect:** $\chi_c \propto 1/N$. Higher $M$ → smaller $\chi_c$ → easier demixing. - **Compatibilizers** (block/graft copolymers) lower effective interfacial tension and reduce the effective $\chi$ at interfaces. - **Specific interactions** (H-bonding, dipole–dipole, ionic) can make $\chi$ negative, promoting miscibility even at high $M$. - **Cloud point** (experimental) ≈ **binodal** (theoretical); **clear point** (experimental) ≈ **spinodal** (theoretical). - **Rule of thumb for industrial blends:** For a blend to be miscible at $M \sim 10^{5}$, typically need $\chi < 10^{-3}$, which requires strong specific interactions.

topics.title: Upper Critical Solution Temperature (UCST)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Upper Critical Solution Temperature (UCST)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Upper Critical Solution Temperature (UCST)** is the highest temperature on the phase boundary (binodal curve) of a partially miscible polymer–solvent or polymer–polymer system, above which the system exists as a **single homogeneous phase** (fully miscible) for **all compositions**, and below which the system phase-separates into two coexisting liquid phases of different compositions (a polymer-rich phase and a solvent-rich phase).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> UCST and LCST are introduced in the Thermodynamics of Polymer Solutions unit. UCST behavior is the **classical, "normal" type of phase boundary** predicted by the Flory–Huggins lattice theory and is observed in polymer solutions where the polymer–solvent interaction is **endothermic** (unfavorable, $\chi > \chi_c$) at low temperatures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Oil in Cold Water"

Imagine pouring a thick vegetable oil into cold water and stirring. At room temperature (the "cold" side of the analogy), the oil and water refuse to mix — they form two distinct layers. Now imagine heating the system above a specific threshold temperature. Suddenly, the oil droplets dissolve completely into the water and you obtain a clear, single-phase solution. That threshold temperature is exactly the **Upper Critical Solution Temperature**.

In polymer terms:
- **Below UCST** → polymer chains prefer to coil up and segregate away from solvent molecules (like oil droplets in cold water).
- **Above UCST** → thermal motion breaks the unfavorable polymer–solvent contacts, and entropy drives everything into one transparent phase.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Quantities to Remember

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value/Range |
|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | **$R$** | **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** |
| Absolute Temperature | **$T$** | **K (Kelvin)** |
| Flory–Huggins $\chi$ at UCST | $\chi_c$ | **$\approx 0.5$** (polymer–solvent limit) |
| Volume fraction of polymer | $\phi_2$ | **$0 \le \phi_2 \le 1$** (dimensionless) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Identifier for UCST vs LCST:** The "U" in UCST stands for **Upper** — the binodal curve has its **maximum temperature at the top**, so the two-phase region lies *below* the dome. (In LCST, the dome is *inverted* and the two-phase region lies *above* it.)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of UCST — The Thermodynamic "Why"

The Gibbs free energy of mixing, $\Delta G_m$, governs phase behavior:

$$\Delta G_m = \Delta H_m - T\Delta S_m$$

For a polymer solution, both $\Delta H_m$ and $\Delta S_m$ carry specific physical meaning:

- **Enthalpy of mixing ($\Delta H_m$):** Determined by the balance between polymer–solvent contacts and polymer–polymer / solvent–solvent contacts. For UCST systems, $\Delta H_m > 0$ at low $T$ because breaking polymer–polymer contacts and forming polymer–solvent contacts is energetically *unfavorable* (endothermic mixing).
- **Entropy of mixing ($\Delta S_m$):** Always positive, but **small for polymers** because one polymer chain replaces many solvent molecules (combinatorial entropy scales as $1/N$).

At low $T$, the $T\Delta S_m$ term cannot overcome the positive $\Delta H_m$, so $\Delta G_m > 0$ in some composition range → **two phases form**. As $T$ rises, the $T\Delta S_m$ contribution grows, eventually making $\Delta G_m < 0$ for all compositions → **single phase**. The boundary between these regimes peaks at the **UCST**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Chain (KTU Board Pattern)

1. **Construct $\Delta G_m(\phi, T)$** using Flory–Huggins lattice theory.
2. **Locate the binodal** by setting $\partial \Delta G_m / \partial \phi = 0$ and equating chemical potentials of the two phases.
3. **Locate the spinodal** where $\partial^2 \Delta G_m / \partial \phi^2 = 0$.
4. **Find the critical point** — the intersection of binodal and spinodal — which gives $(\phi_c, T_c)$.
5. **Identify $T_c$** as the UCST (since UCST is the maximum on the binodal).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G_m = RT\left[\dfrac{\phi_1}{N_1}\ln \phi_1 + \phi_2 \ln \phi_2 + \chi\,\phi_1\phi_2\right]$ | Flory–Huggins free energy of mixing (polymer = 2, solvent = 1) | Predicting phase behavior |
| 2 | $\Delta \mu_1^{excess} = RT\left[\ln \phi_1 + \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N}\right)\phi_2 + \chi \phi_2^2\right]$ | Excess chemical potential of solvent | Chemical-potential equality at binodal |
| 3 | $\chi = A + \dfrac{B}{T}$ | Temperature dependence of $\chi$ | Locating UCST vs LCST crossover |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\partial^2 (\Delta G_m / RT)}{\partial \phi_2^2}\bigg\vert_{\phi_c} = 0$ | Spinodal condition | Critical point |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\partial^3 (\Delta G_m / RT)}{\partial \phi_2^3}\bigg\vert_{\phi_c} = 0$ | Critical composition | Solving for $\phi_c$ |
| 6 | $\chi_c = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)^2$ | Critical interaction parameter | Determining UCST |
| 7 | $T_{UCST}^{-1} = \dfrac{2\sigma}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}} - \chi\right)$ | Form used when $\chi = A + B/T$ | Numerical UCST calculation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipeline symbol rule:** In all KTU exam scripts and these notes, the absolute value or "divided by" is written as `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\partial \Delta G / \partial \phi \big\vert_{\phi_c}$) to prevent markdown table corruption.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polymer Processing:** Determines the safe temperature window for solution casting, spinning, and film preparation.
- **Membrane Manufacture:** UCST dictates whether a polymer solution will precipitate (phase-inversion membranes).
- **Pharmaceutical Formulation:** Drug–polymer solubility windows.
- **Coatings and Adhesives:** Storage-temperature stability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Critical Composition $\phi_{2,c}$ for a Polymer Solution

Starting from the Flory–Huggins free energy of mixing (per mole of lattice sites):

$$\dfrac{\Delta G_m}{RT} = \dfrac{\phi_1}{N_1}\ln \phi_1 + \phi_2 \ln \phi_2 + \chi\,\phi_1\phi_2$$

For a polymer solution, label the **solvent as component 1** ($N_1 = 1$, since solvent occupies a single lattice site) and the **polymer as component 2** (degree of polymerization $N_2 = N$). Substitute $N_1 = 1$:

$$\dfrac{\Delta G_m}{RT} = \phi_1 \ln \phi_1 + \phi_2 \ln \phi_2 + \chi\,\phi_1\phi_2$$

Using $\phi_1 = 1 - \phi_2$, the function depends only on $\phi_2$. Differentiate twice with respect to $\phi_2$ to obtain the **spinodal condition**:

$$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \phi_2}\left(\dfrac{\Delta G_m}{RT}\right) = -\ln(1 - \phi_2) - 1 + \ln \phi_2 + 1 + \chi(1 - 2\phi_2)$$

Simplify the two $+1$ and $-1$ terms cancel:

$$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \phi_2}\left(\dfrac{\Delta G_m}{RT}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{\phi_2}{1 - \phi_2}\right) + \chi(1 - 2\phi_2)$$

Differentiate once more:

$$\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial \phi_2^2}\left(\dfrac{\Delta G_m}{RT}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\phi_2(1 - \phi_2)} - 2\chi$$

Set this equal to zero for the **spinodal**:

$$\dfrac{1}{\phi_2(1 - \phi_2)} - 2\chi = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \chi = \dfrac{1}{2\phi_2(1 - \phi_2)}$$

Differentiate a third time:

$$\dfrac{\partial^3}{\partial \phi_2^3}\left(\dfrac{\Delta G_m}{RT}\right) = \dfrac{1 - 2\phi_2}{[\phi_2(1 - \phi_2)]^2}$$

Set this equal to zero for the **critical point**:

$$1 - 2\phi_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi_{2,c} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result for KTU Board Exams:** For a *polymer–solvent* system (asymmetric, $N_1 = 1$), the critical polymer volume fraction is **always $\phi_{2,c} = 0.5$** — independent of molecular weight! This is a *signature* of polymer solutions and is a very common exam trap.

Substitute $\phi_{2,c} = 0.5$ back into the spinodal equation:

$$\chi_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1 - 0.5)} = \dfrac{1}{0.5} = 2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Correction for general $N$:** When the polymer degree of polymerization $N$ is retained (i.e., we do *not* set $N_1 = 1$), the rigorous result is
> $$\phi_{2,c} = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{1 + \sqrt{N}}, \qquad \chi_c = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)^{\!2}$$
> For very large $N$, $\phi_{2,c} \to 1$ and $\chi_c \to 0.5$. Always check whether your KTU question specifies a symmetric blend or an asymmetric polymer solution.

## 3.2 Derivation of the UCST Temperature

Assume the Flory–Huggins parameter obeys

$$\chi = A + \dfrac{B}{T}$$

The phase boundary occurs at $\chi = \chi_c$. Therefore the UCST temperature $T_{UCST}$ satisfies

$$A + \dfrac{B}{T_{UCST}} = \chi_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{UCST} = \dfrac{B}{\chi_c - A}$$

For a polymer solution with large $N$ (so $\chi_c \approx 0.5$):

$$T_{UCST} \approx \dfrac{B}{0.5 - A}$$

If $A < 0.5$ and $B > 0$, then $T_{UCST}$ is positive and finite → **UCST exists**.

If $A < 0.5$ but $B < 0$, then $T_{UCST}$ is negative → only **LCST exists** in physically accessible temperatures.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Question:** For a polymer of $N = 10{,}000$ in a solvent at $\chi = A + B/T$ with $A = 0.20$ and $B = 200$ K, calculate $T_{UCST}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $\chi_c$:

$$\chi_c = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{10000}}\right)^{\!2} = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(1 + 0.01\right)^{\!2} = \dfrac{1}{2}(1.0201) = 0.51005$$

Step 2 — Set $\chi = \chi_c$ and solve for $T$:

$$0.20 + \dfrac{200}{T} = 0.51005$$

$$\dfrac{200}{T} = 0.31005$$

$$T_{UCST} = \dfrac{200}{0.31005} \approx 645.1 \text{ K} \approx 372\,^\circ\text{C}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Phase Diagram Showing UCST (T vs $\phi_2$)

```mermaid
graph TD
    axisX["Composition phi2 axis (0 to 1)"]
    axisY["Temperature T axis (K)"]
    binodal["BINODAL CURVE - dome shape, peak at UCST"]
    ucst["UCST - top of dome, Tc phi2c 0.5"]
    spinodal["SPINODAL CURVE - inside binodal, inflects"]
    region1["REGION A - Single Phase - T above UCST"]
    region2["REGION B - Two Phase Region - T below UCST"]
    region3["REGION C - Metastable - between spinodal and binodal"]
    region4["REGION D - Unstable - inside spinodal"]

    axisY --> region1
    axisY --> binodal
    binodal --> ucst
    binodal --> region2
    binodal --> spinodal
    spinodal --> region3
    spinodal --> region4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Walk *up* the temperature axis. Above the UCST peak, *all* compositions are miscible → Region A. Below the dome, the system splits into a polymer-rich phase ($\phi_2$ on the right branch) and a solvent-rich phase ($\phi_2$ on the left branch).

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Factors Governing UCST

```mermaid
graph LR
    f1["FACTOR 1 - Enthalpy of mixing - Endothermic gives UCST"]
    f2["FACTOR 2 - Molecular weight - Higher N raises UCST"]
    f3["FACTOR 3 - Chi parameter - Temperature dependent chi"]
    f4["FACTOR 4 - Pressure - Minor effect usually"]
    f5["FACTOR 5 - Solvent quality - Poor solvent promotes UCST"]

    f1 --> ucst["UCST VALUE"]
    f2 --> ucst
    f3 --> ucst
    f4 --> ucst
    f5 --> ucst
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — How to Identify UCST Behavior

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Step 1 - Measure solubility vs T"]
    B["Step 2 - Does solubility increase with T?"]
    C["Step 3 - Is there a maximum T on binodal?"]
    D["Step 4 - YES means UCST system"]
    E["Step 5 - NO means LCST or monotonic"]

    A --> B
    B -- Yes --> C
    B -- No --> E
    C -- Yes --> D
    C -- No --> E
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Define the Upper Critical Solution Temperature (UCST). With the help of a neat phase diagram, explain how it differs from LCST.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Upper Critical Solution Temperature (UCST)** is defined as the highest temperature on the phase boundary (binodal curve) of a partially miscible polymer–solvent system, above which the components are miscible in all proportions and below which they separate into two coexisting liquid phases.

**Difference from LCST:**

| Feature | UCST | LCST |
|---|---|---|
| Position of two-phase region | Below the dome | Above the dome |
| Shape of binodal | Normal (convex up) | Inverted |
| Common cause | Endothermic mixing ($\Delta H_m > 0$) | Exothermic mixing (hydrogen-bonding water systems) |
| Typical example | Polystyrene in cyclohexane | Poly(ethylene oxide) in water |

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Write the Flory–Huggins expression for the free energy of mixing of a polymer solution and identify the term that dominates the appearance of UCST.**

**Model Answer:**

$$\Delta G_m = RT\left[\phi_1 \ln \phi_1 + \phi_2 \ln \phi_2 + \chi\,\phi_1\phi_2\right]$$

where $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ are the volume fractions of solvent and polymer respectively, and $\chi$ is the Flory–Huggins interaction parameter. The **third term, $\chi\,\phi_1\phi_2$**, dominates the appearance of UCST because $\chi$ depends on temperature as $\chi = A + B/T$. When $\chi$ exceeds $\chi_c$, the enthalpic penalty outweighs the entropic gain, producing a two-phase region below the UCST.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model — 14 Marks]`

**(a)** *Derive the expression for the critical value of the Flory–Huggins interaction parameter, $\chi_c$, for a polymer–solvent system with polymer degree of polymerization $N$.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Using the relation $\chi = A + B/T$, obtain an expression for the UCST in terms of $A$, $B$, and $N$. Discuss how UCST varies with molecular weight.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $\chi_c$:**

Start with the Flory–Huggins free energy per mole of lattice sites:

$$\dfrac{\Delta G_m}{RT} = \dfrac{\phi_1}{N_1}\ln \phi_1 + \dfrac{\phi_2}{N_2}\ln \phi_2 + \chi\,\phi_1\phi_2$$

For a polymer–solvent system, $N_1 = 1$ (solvent) and $N_2 = N$ (polymer). Substitute:

$$\dfrac{\Delta G_m}{RT} = \phi_1 \ln \phi_1 + \dfrac{\phi_2}{N}\ln \phi_2 + \chi\,\phi_1\phi_2$$

Apply the **spinodal condition** $\partial^2 (\Delta G_m/RT) / \partial \phi_2^2 = 0$:

$$\dfrac{1}{\phi_2(1 - \phi_2)} + \dfrac{1}{N\phi_2^2} - 2\chi = 0$$

Apply the **critical-point condition** $\partial^3 (\Delta G_m/RT) / \partial \phi_2^3 = 0$:

$$- \dfrac{1}{\phi_2^2(1 - \phi_2)} + \dfrac{1 - 2\phi_2}{\phi_2^3(1 - \phi_2)^2} - \dfrac{2}{N\phi_2^3} = 0$$

Solving these two equations simultaneously gives

$$\phi_{2,c} = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{1 + \sqrt{N}}, \qquad \chi_c = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)^{\!2}$$

**(b) UCST expression:**

Setting $\chi = \chi_c$ with $\chi = A + B/T$:

$$A + \dfrac{B}{T_{UCST}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)^{\!2}$$

Solving for $T_{UCST}$:

$$\dfrac{1}{T_{UCST}} = \dfrac{1}{2B}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)^{\!2} - \dfrac{A}{B}$$

$$\boxed{T_{UCST} = \dfrac{B}{\dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)^{\!2} - A}}$$

**Discussion of molecular-weight dependence:** As $N \to \infty$, $\chi_c \to 0.5$. Hence $T_{UCST}$ approaches a finite asymptotic limit. For finite $N$, increasing $N$ *increases* $\chi_c$ (since $1 + 1/\sqrt{N}$ decreases toward 1), which generally **lowers the UCST** (or raises it, depending on the sign of $A$ and $B$). This is consistent with the experimental observation that high-molecular-weight polymers exhibit sharper phase boundaries.

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model — 14 Marks]`

**(a)** *Sketch and label the phase diagram for a polymer solution exhibiting UCST. Mark the binodal, spinodal, critical point, and the regions of single-phase, two-phase, metastable, and unstable compositions.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A polymer solution with $N = 10000$ exhibits $\chi = 0.30 + 250/T$. Calculate the UCST and the critical composition. State clearly the assumptions made.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Phase Diagram:**

The diagram is a $T$ vs $\phi_2$ plot showing:
- A **binodal** (outer dome-shaped curve) — separates single-phase (above) from two-phase (below) regions.
- A **spinodal** (inner curve) — separates metastable (between spinodal and binodal) from unstable (inside spinodal) regions.
- **Critical point** at the top of the binodal at $\phi_{2,c} = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{1 + \sqrt{N}}$.
- **UCST** at the maximum temperature on the binodal.

```
T axis vertical, phi2 axis horizontal
   T
   |
   |        BINODAL (dome)
   |       /         \
   |      /  SPINODAL \
   |     /   /     \   \
   |    /   /       \   \
   |___/__/___________\___\___ phi2
   0   phi2_c=0.5       1
```

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

Critical composition for $N = 10000$:

$$\phi_{2,c} = \dfrac{\sqrt{10000}}{1 + \sqrt{10000}} = \dfrac{100}{101} = 0.9901$$

Critical $\chi$:

$$\chi_c = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{100}\right)^{\!2} = \dfrac{1}{2}(1.0201) = 0.51005$$

UCST by setting $\chi = \chi_c$:

$$0.30 + \dfrac{250}{T_{UCST}} = 0.51005$$

$$\dfrac{250}{T_{UCST}} = 0.21005$$

$$\boxed{T_{UCST} = 1190.2 \text{ K} \approx 917\,^\circ\text{C}}$$

**Assumptions stated:**
1. Flory–Huggins lattice theory is valid (athermal lattice, uniform segment size).
2. $\chi$ depends only on temperature through $\chi = A + B/T$.
3. Polymer is monodisperse.
4. Pressure is constant (no pressure dependence).
5. The system is in equilibrium (no kinetic effects).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the asymmetry factor** in $\chi_c$. Symmetric blends use $\chi_c = 2$; polymer solutions use $\chi_c = (1 + 1/\sqrt{N})^2 / 2$. Mixing these up costs ~3 marks.
> 2. **Using $N_1 = N_2$** by mistake. Always read carefully whether the question is about a **polymer blend** or a **polymer solution**.
> 3. **Dropping the $\ln \phi_1$ term** in the derivative chain.
> 4. **Not specifying the units** of $T$ in the final answer (must be Kelvin).
> 5. **Not sketching the binodal vs spinodal** correctly — they touch only at the critical point, do not cross.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **UCST = maximum temperature on binodal.** Above it, fully miscible; below it, two phases form.
- **For polymer solutions:** $\phi_{2,c} = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{1 + \sqrt{N}} \to 1$ as $N \to \infty$ — critical point shifts to polymer-rich side for high-MW polymers.
- **Critical $\chi$:** $\chi_c = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)^{\!2}$, not 2 (which is for symmetric blends).
- **Temperature dependence:** $\chi = A + B/T$. UCST appears when $A < \chi_c$ and $B > 0$.
- **Driving force:** Positive $\Delta H_m$ at low $T$ (endothermic mixing) outweighed by $T\Delta S_m$ at high $T$.
- **Spinodal is inside the binodal** — they meet only at the critical point.
- **Industrial relevance:** UCST sets the lower bound for safe solution processing of polymers.
- **Examiner's hot tip:** Always state "phase diagram shows binodal with maximum at UCST, single phase above, two-phase below, $\phi_{2,c}$ shifted toward polymer-rich side for high-MW polymers" for a complete answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lower Critical Solution Temperature (LCST)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lower Critical Solution Temperature (LCST)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Lower Critical Solution Temperature (LCST)** is the lowest temperature on a binary phase diagram of a polymer–solvent (or polymer–polymer) system at which the system transitions from a single, homogeneous, miscible phase to a two-phase (demixed) region **upon heating**. Equivalently, below the LCST the mixture is fully miscible in all proportions, and above the LCST it phase separates.

The term "Lower" arises because this critical temperature forms the **lower boundary** of the two-phase region on the temperature axis, in direct contrast to the Upper Critical Solution Temperature (UCST), which marks the **upper boundary** of immiscibility. The LCST is therefore conceptually inverted relative to classical small-molecule phase behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** LCST behavior is a hallmark of systems exhibiting **specific interactions** (H-bonding, dipole–dipole) such as **poly(N-isopropylacrylamide)/water**, **poly(ethylene oxide)/water**, and **poly(vinyl methyl ether)/water**. It is the thermodynamic basis of **thermoresponsive "smart" polymers**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you have a **friend (polymer) who absolutely loves being in a swimming pool (water)**. When the pool is **cool and calm** (low temperature), your friend swims happily — the water and your friend mix freely (one phase). 

Now imagine you slowly **heat up the pool**. As the water warms, your friend gets uncomfortable — the hydrogen bonds that previously held them together break from the added thermal energy, and your friend climbs out of the pool to sit on the deck. Now there are two distinct groups: your friend **on the deck** and the **swimmers in the water** (two phases).

> [!TIP]
> The "pool warming" is the temperature rise past LCST. The "friend getting uncomfortable" is the entropic penalty overcoming the enthalpic gain of mixing. The "friend climbing out" is the **phase separation**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Critical Values

- **Standard Units:** Temperature in **°C** or **K** (Kelvin preferred for thermodynamic calculations).
- **Characteristic χ_critical ≈ 0.5** at the spinodal critical point for a high-molecular-weight polymer in a small-molecule solvent.
- **LCST values for canonical systems** (bold constants are widely tested in KTU exams):
  - **PNIPAM/H₂O: LCST ≈ 32 °C (305 K)**
  - **PEO/H₂O: LCST ≈ 100 °C and above**
  - PVME/H₂O: LCST ≈ 37 °C
  - PMMA in some solvents shows combined UCST + LCST "hourglass" diagram.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Origin of LCST

The LCST arises from the **temperature dependence of the Flory–Huggins interaction parameter χ**. In contrast to UCST systems (where χ decreases with T), LCST systems show χ **increasing** with temperature until the critical miscibility threshold is crossed.

The interaction parameter can be decomposed as:

$$\chi(T) = A + \frac{B}{T}$$

where the **enthalpic** term scales as $1/T$ and the **entropic** contribution $A$ may itself be a function of T in extended theories. The condition for phase separation in the Flory–Huggins framework is:

$$\chi > \chi_{crit} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)^2 \approx 0.5 \quad (N \gg 1)$$

### Key Bulleted Logic:

- **Step 1 (Mixing Enthalpy):** At low T, specific interactions (H-bonds, dipole pairs) provide a *negative* enthalpy of mixing, $\Delta H_m < 0$, favouring dissolution.
- **Step 2 (Entropy Penalty):** Polymer dissolution incurs a large *configurational entropy* cost because polymer chains lose conformational freedom. More importantly, **ordering of water molecules around hydrophobic groups (e.g., the isopropyl group in PNIPAM) creates a large negative mixing entropy** at low T.
- **Step 3 (Temperature Rise):** As $T$ increases, the $-T\Delta S_m$ term dominates, and the structured water "cages" around the polymer collapse. The $\Delta G_m = \Delta H_m - T\Delta S_m$ becomes positive above LCST, causing demixing.
- **Step 4 (Phase Separation):** The system splits into a polymer-rich phase and a polymer-lean (solvent-rich) phase.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description / KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flory–Huggins free energy | $\frac{\Delta G_m}{RT} = \phi_1 \ln\phi_1 + \frac{\phi_2}{N}\ln\phi_2 + \chi\phi_1\phi_2$ | Foundation of polymer solution thermodynamics |
| 2 | Chemical potential of solvent | $\frac{\Delta \mu_1}{RT} = \ln\phi_1 + \left(1-\frac{1}{N}\right)\phi_2 + \chi\phi_2^2$ | Used for osmotic pressure & VLE |
| 3 | Spinodal condition | $\frac{\partial^2 \Delta G_m}{\partial \phi_2^2} = 0$ | Boundary of metastable region |
| 4 | Critical point condition | $\frac{\partial^3 \Delta G_m}{\partial \phi_2^3} = 0$ | Defines the LCST/UCST point |
| 5 | Critical composition | $\phi_{2,crit} = \frac{1}{1+\sqrt{N}}$ | For high N, $\phi_{2,crit} \approx 1/\sqrt{N}$ |
| 6 | Critical $\chi$ value | $\chi_{crit} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)^2$ | For $N \to \infty$, $\chi_{crit} \to 0.5$ |
| 7 | Temperature-dependent χ | $\chi(T) = A + B/T$ | Predicts UCST (A < 0) or LCST (B very small/negative effective) |
| 8 | LCST condition | $T_{LCST} = \dfrac{B}{\chi_{crit}-A}$ | Solving $\chi(T_{LCST}) = \chi_{crit}$ |
| 9 | Reduced critical T | $T_c = \dfrac{B}{0.5 - A}$ | Approximation for high-MW polymers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** In all Flory–Huggins tables and equations above, subscripts are: **1 = solvent**, **2 = polymer**, $\phi$ = volume fraction, $N$ = degree of polymerisation (in lattice units). The condition for **miscibility** is $\chi < \chi_{crit}$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Smart Materials:** Thermoresponsive polymers with LCST near body temperature (e.g., PNIPAM, 32 °C) are used in **drug delivery**, where the polymer precipitates above body temperature to release a drug payload.
- **Tissue Engineering:** LCST polymers form **injectable hydrogels** that gel upon warming inside the body.
- **Separation Membranes:** Temperature-triggered phase separation enables **membrane fouling control** and selective filtration.
- **Smart Textiles & Coatings:** Fabrics that change wettability with temperature.
- **3D Bioprinting:** Thermoresponsive bio-inks that solidify on warming.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Composition and Critical χ for a Polymer–Solvent System

We begin from the Flory–Huggins expression for the free energy of mixing (per lattice site, divided by $RT$):

$$f = \frac{\Delta G_m}{RT} = \phi_1 \ln\phi_1 + \frac{\phi_2}{N}\ln\phi_2 + \chi \phi_1 \phi_2$$

Using the constraint $\phi_1 + \phi_2 = 1$, we eliminate $\phi_1 = 1 - \phi_2$ and consider $f$ as a function of $\phi_2$ alone.

### Step 1: First Derivative (Equality of Chemical Potentials at Equilibrium)

The first derivative with respect to $\phi_2$ is set to zero to find extrema of the free energy:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial \phi_2} = -\ln(1-\phi_2) - 1 + \frac{1}{N}\ln\phi_2 + \frac{1}{N} - \chi + 2\chi\phi_2$$

> **Logic:** The derivative of $\phi_1 \ln\phi_1$ with respect to $\phi_2$ yields $-\ln\phi_1 - 1$ by chain rule (since $\partial \phi_1/\partial \phi_2 = -1$); the derivative of $(\phi_2/N)\ln\phi_2$ gives $(1/N)(\ln\phi_2 + 1)$; and the derivative of $\chi\phi_1\phi_2 = \chi(1-\phi_2)\phi_2$ gives $\chi - 2\chi\phi_2$.

At equilibrium, the binodal condition equates the chemical potentials of each component in the coexisting phases; the critical point lies on the binodal.

### Step 2: Second Derivative (Spinodal Condition)

Setting the second derivative to zero locates the **spinodal** (boundary of absolute instability):

$$\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial \phi_2^2} = \frac{1}{1-\phi_2} + \frac{1}{N\phi_2} - 2\chi = 0$$

> **Logic:** Differentiating the first derivative term-by-term: derivative of $-\ln(1-\phi_2)$ is $+1/(1-\phi_2)$; derivative of $(1/N)\ln\phi_2$ is $1/(N\phi_2)$; derivative of $-2\chi\phi_2$ is $-2\chi$.

### Step 3: Third Derivative (Critical Point Condition)

The critical point occurs at the inflection point of the free-energy curve, where the second derivative is *simultaneously* zero *and* the third derivative vanishes:

$$\frac{\partial^3 f}{\partial \phi_2^3} = \frac{1}{(1-\phi_2)^2} - \frac{1}{N\phi_2^2} = 0$$

> **Logic:** Differentiating $1/(1-\phi_2)$ with respect to $\phi_2$ gives $1/(1-\phi_2)^2$; differentiating $1/(N\phi_2)$ gives $-1/(N\phi_2^2)$.

### Step 4: Solving for Critical Composition

From Step 3, rearranging:

$$\frac{1}{(1-\phi_2)^2} = \frac{1}{N\phi_2^2}$$

Taking the positive square root (since volume fractions are positive):

$$\frac{1}{1-\phi_{2,c}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}\,\phi_{2,c}}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$\sqrt{N}\,\phi_{2,c} = 1 - \phi_{2,c}$$

$$\phi_{2,c}(\sqrt{N} + 1) = 1$$

$$\boxed{\phi_{2,c} = \frac{1}{1+\sqrt{N}}}$$

For $N \gg 1$ (high-MW polymer), $\phi_{2,c} \approx 1/\sqrt{N} \to 0$. This explains why LCST compositions are typically **very dilute in polymer**.

### Step 5: Solving for Critical χ

Substituting $\phi_{2,c}$ into the spinodal condition from Step 2:

$$\chi_c = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{1-\phi_{2,c}} + \frac{1}{N\phi_{2,c}}\right]$$

Using $\phi_{2,c} = 1/(1+\sqrt{N})$ and $1-\phi_{2,c} = \sqrt{N}/(1+\sqrt{N})$:

$$\frac{1}{1-\phi_{2,c}} = \frac{1+\sqrt{N}}{\sqrt{N}}$$

$$\frac{1}{N\phi_{2,c}} = \frac{1+\sqrt{N}}{N}$$

Adding these two terms:

$$\frac{1}{1-\phi_{2,c}} + \frac{1}{N\phi_{2,c}} = \frac{1+\sqrt{N}}{\sqrt{N}} + \frac{1+\sqrt{N}}{N} = (1+\sqrt{N})\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} + \frac{1}{N}\right)$$

Factorising:

$$(1+\sqrt{N}) \cdot \frac{N + \sqrt{N}}{N\sqrt{N}} = \frac{(1+\sqrt{N})(N+\sqrt{N})}{N^{3/2}}$$

Recognising that $(1+\sqrt{N})^2 = 1 + 2\sqrt{N} + N$, and noting $N + \sqrt{N} = \sqrt{N}(\sqrt{N}+1)$:

$$\chi_c = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(1+\sqrt{N})^2}{N} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)^2$$

$$\boxed{\chi_c = \frac{1}{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)^2 \xrightarrow{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: LCST from Temperature-Dependent χ

Assume a phenomenological temperature dependence:

$$\chi(T) = A + \frac{B}{T}$$

At the LCST, $\chi(T_{LCST}) = \chi_c \approx 0.5$ for high-MW polymer:

$$0.5 = A + \frac{B}{T_{LCST}}$$

Solving:

$$T_{LCST} = \frac{B}{0.5 - A}$$

> **Logic:** For LCST to exist with $T_{LCST} > 0$, the numerator $B$ and the denominator $0.5 - A$ must have the same sign. In typical hydrogen-bonded systems, $A < 0.5$ and $B > 0$ (or vice versa for closed-loop behaviour).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: LCST vs UCST

| Property | UCST Behaviour | LCST Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Miscibility region | Above the curve (high T) | Below the curve (low T) |
| Phase separation on | Cooling | Heating |
| Sign of $\partial\chi/\partial T$ | Negative (χ decreases with T) | Positive (χ increases with T) |
| Dominant interaction | Dispersive (van der Waals) | Specific (H-bonding, hydrophobic effect) |
| Typical example | Polystyrene/acetone, PS/cyclohexane | PNIPAM/water, PVME/water, PEO/water |
| Physical origin | $\Delta H_m > 0$, drives unmixing on cooling | $\Delta S_m$ becomes negative (structured water) above LCST |
| χ at the critical T | $\chi_c = 0.5$ | $\chi_c = 0.5$ (same) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **Lower Critical Solution Temperature (LCST)**. Mention the physical reason for LCST behaviour in a polymer–solvent system.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
LCST is the temperature **above** which a polymer–solvent system phase separates on heating and **below** which it exists as a single homogeneous phase. (1 mark)
The phenomenon arises from **specific interactions** such as hydrogen bonding between the polymer and solvent at low temperature, combined with a **large negative entropy of mixing** caused by the ordering of solvent molecules (e.g., water structuring around hydrophobic groups). (2 marks)
As temperature increases, the entropic penalty $-T\Delta S$ dominates over the favourable $\Delta H$, and $\Delta G_m$ becomes positive, causing demixing. (Bonus for completeness)

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **UCST** and **LCST** with one example for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | UCST | LCST |
|---|---|---|
| Phase separation on | Cooling | Heating |
| Origin | $\Delta H_m > 0$ | Specific interactions + negative $\Delta S_m$ |
| Example | Polystyrene/cyclohexane | PNIPAM/water |

(1 mark each for the difference, 1 mark for the examples)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** **(a)** Derive the **Flory–Huggins expressions for the critical composition ($\phi_{2,c}$)** and **critical interaction parameter ($\chi_c$)** for a polymer–solvent system using the spinodal and inflection point conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **origin of LCST behaviour** in hydrogen-bonded polymer–solvent systems. Show mathematically how a temperature-dependent $\chi(T) = A + B/T$ leads to a finite LCST, and quote the approximate LCST for the **PNIPAM/water** system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1.5 M):** Write down the Flory–Huggins free energy of mixing per lattice site:

$$\frac{\Delta G_m}{RT} = \phi_1 \ln\phi_1 + \frac{\phi_2}{N}\ln\phi_2 + \chi\phi_1\phi_2$$

with $\phi_1 + \phi_2 = 1$.

**Step 2 (1.5 M):** First derivative (chemical potential) — set to zero for binodal. Then compute the **second derivative** and set to zero for the spinodal:

$$\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial \phi_2^2} = \frac{1}{1-\phi_2} + \frac{1}{N\phi_2} - 2\chi = 0$$

**Step 3 (1.5 M):** Compute the **third derivative** and set to zero for the critical point (inflection):

$$\frac{\partial^3 f}{\partial \phi_2^3} = \frac{1}{(1-\phi_2)^2} - \frac{1}{N\phi_2^2} = 0$$

**Step 4 (1.5 M):** Solve simultaneously to obtain:

$$\phi_{2,c} = \frac{1}{1+\sqrt{N}}, \quad \chi_c = \frac{1}{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)^2$$

For $N \to \infty$: $\phi_{2,c} \to 0$ and $\chi_c \to 0.5$. (1 mark for the high-MW limit)

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 M):** Discuss the molecular origin. In PNIPAM/water, the amide group of NIPAM forms **H-bonds with water** at low T, and the isopropyl group induces **hydrophobic hydration** (ordered "ice-like" water cages). This produces a strongly **negative** $\Delta S_m$ because water loses configurational entropy.

**Step 2 (2 M):** Total free energy: $\Delta G_m = \Delta H_m - T\Delta S_m$. At low T, $\Delta H_m < 0$ dominates (mixing is favourable). As T rises, $-T\Delta S_m > 0$ overwhelms $\Delta H_m$, making $\Delta G_m > 0$ above LCST.

**Step 3 (1.5 M):** Mathematical model. Using $\chi(T) = A + B/T$ and the critical condition $\chi_c = 0.5$:

$$T_{LCST} = \frac{B}{0.5 - A}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 M):** State that for **PNIPAM/water, LCST ≈ 32 °C (305 K)**, a value widely cited in KTU textbooks. The collapse of water cages around the isopropyl group at ~32 °C triggers phase separation, releasing ~1 kcal/mol of energy per isopropyl group.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** With the help of a **schematic phase diagram**, explain the differences between **UCST and LCST** behaviour. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer of degree of polymerisation $N = 10{,}000$ is dissolved in a small-molecule solvent. Compute (i) the **critical composition** and (ii) the **critical Flory–Huggins χ parameter**. If experimentally, phase separation is observed at a temperature $T$ corresponding to $\chi = 0.55$, comment on the **thermodynamic state** of the system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (3 M):** Draw and label a **UCST diagram** (closed-loop with critical point at top) and an **LCST diagram** (closed-loop with critical point at bottom) on a temperature vs. composition plot. Indicate the binodal, spinodal, single-phase, and two-phase regions.

**Step 2 (2 M):** Explain the **molecular origins**: UCST arises from unfavourable (endothermic) $\Delta H_m$ at low T (e.g., PS/cyclohexane where dispersive interactions differ). LCST arises from specific interactions (H-bonds, hydrophobic effect) that give favourable $\Delta H_m$ but unfavourable $\Delta S_m$ at high T.

**Step 3 (2 M):** Provide **one example for each**: PS/acetone (UCST) and PNIPAM/water (LCST ≈ 32 °C). Mention that some systems (e.g., PMMA in select solvents) display both — a **"closed loop"** with UCST and LCST together (hourglass shape).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 M):** Apply the critical composition formula with $N = 10{,}000$:

$$\phi_{2,c} = \frac{1}{1+\sqrt{N}} = \frac{1}{1+\sqrt{10{,}000}} = \frac{1}{1+100} = \frac{1}{101} \approx 0.0099$$

So $\phi_{2,c} \approx 0.99\%$ polymer by volume. (2 M)

**Step 2 (2 M):** Apply the critical χ formula:

$$\chi_c = \frac{1}{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{100}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(1.01)^2 = \frac{1.0201}{2} \approx 0.510$$

So $\chi_c \approx 0.510$. (2 M)

**Step 3 (3 M):** Compare the experimental value $\chi_{exp} = 0.55$ with $\chi_c = 0.510$. Since $\chi_{exp} > \chi_c$, the system is in the **two-phase region** (phase separated). The driving force for demixing is the **second derivative of free energy being negative** (unstable region interior to the spinodal if the composition differs significantly from $\phi_{2,c}$). The polymer-rich and polymer-lean phases have compositions determined by the **binodal**, not the critical point.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls on LCST Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing LCST with UCST** — Many students state "below LCST, two phases" — this is **wrong**. Below LCST = one phase; **above** LCST = two phases.
> 2. **Sign errors in $\chi(T)$** — Forgetting that $\chi_c = 0.5$ is the **threshold** (not the value at any specific T) costs 1–2 marks routinely.
> 3. **Mis-applying $1-\phi_2$ vs. $\phi_2$** — When $N$ is large, $\phi_{2,c} \approx 0$ but it is *not exactly* zero. Do not substitute $\phi_2 = 0$ in the spinodal formula.
> 4. **Forgetting the constraint $\phi_1 + \phi_2 = 1$** — This leads to a 1-mark deduction in derivations.
> 5. **Mixing up solvent and polymer subscripts** — Always state "$\phi_2$ = polymer" in your opening line to avoid ambiguity. KTU examiners note this as a frequent deduction.
> 6. **Neglecting the role of water structure** — For LCST, you *must* mention the **entropy contribution from structured water**; just stating "H-bonds break" is incomplete and loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **LCST = lowest T at which demixing occurs on heating** (single phase below, two phases above).
- ✅ **Critical χ** at the LCST point: $\chi_c \approx 0.5$ for high-MW polymers.
- ✅ **Critical composition** is very dilute in polymer: $\phi_{2,c} \approx 1/\sqrt{N}$.
- ✅ **Molecular origin:** Specific interactions (H-bonds) + **negative entropy of mixing** from structured water (hydrophobic hydration).
- ✅ **Thermodynamic condition:** $\Delta G_m = \Delta H_m - T\Delta S_m$ becomes positive above LCST as the $-T\Delta S_m$ term dominates.
- ✅ **Mathematical model:** $\chi(T) = A + B/T$; $T_{LCST} = B/(0.5 - A)$.
- ✅ **Classic example:** **PNIPAM/water, LCST ≈ 32 °C** — body-temperature-responsive smart polymer.
- ✅ **Spinodal condition:** $\partial^2(\Delta G_m/RT)/\partial\phi_2^2 = 0$.
- ✅ **Critical point condition:** $\partial^3(\Delta G_m/RT)/\partial\phi_2^3 = 0$.
- ✅ **Applications:** Drug delivery, smart hydrogels, thermoresponsive membranes, 3D bioprinting bio-inks.
- ✅ **Contrast with UCST:** UCST separates on cooling (dispersive systems); LCST separates on heating (specific-interaction systems). Some polymer solutions show **both** (closed-loop/hourglass phase diagram).
- ✅ **Key KTU equation trio to memorise:** (i) Flory–Huggins free energy, (ii) spinodal condition, (iii) critical $\chi$ value.
- ✅ **For PNIPAM:** The isopropyl group's **hydrophobic dehydration** at ~32 °C is the microscopic trigger; this is your most-likely viva question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concentration Regimes in Polymer Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concentration Regimes in Polymer Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In polymer science, a polymer solution is classified into distinct **concentration regimes** based on the relative concentration of macromolecular chains compared to a critical reference concentration. The three primary regimes are:

1. **Dilute Regime** ($c < c^*$) — Isolated polymer coils separated by solvent.
2. **Semi-Dilute Regime** ($c^* < c < c_e$) — Coils overlap but remain largely unentangled.
3. **Concentrated Regime / Polymer Melt** ($c > c_e$) — Chains entangle and interpenetrate extensively.

Where:
- $c$ = polymer concentration (typically in g/cm³ or wt%)
- $c^*$ = **overlap concentration** (boundary between dilute and semi-dilute)
- $c_e$ = **entanglement concentration** (boundary between semi-dilute and concentrated)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Concentration regimes govern virtually every macroscopic property of polymer solutions — **viscosity, osmotic pressure, diffusion, and chain conformation**. Mastery of this topic is essential for Module 2, as it underpins viscosity models, Flory-Huggins theory, and gelation behavior.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spaghetti Bowl" Picture

Imagine cooked spaghetti strands floating in a pot of water:

- **Dilute regime** → Only a few strands floating, each curled into a separate, undisturbed ball. They don't know the others exist. A spoon moves easily.
- **Semi-dilute regime** → Many more strands, but they still mostly form their own coils. The coils just begin to **touch** at their peripheries — like balls of yarn loosely piled in a basket. The spoon encounters noticeable resistance.
- **Concentrated (entangled) regime** → So many strands that they form a **tangled mesh**, like a bird's nest. Moving the spoon requires significant force because the chains are physically hooked around each other.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The transition from semi-dilute to concentrated isn't a continuous change — there's a sharp **entanglement threshold** $c_e$ where viscosity jumps by orders of magnitude. This is the same reason **honey flows slower than water** even when both are liquids.

### 1.3 The Three Reference Concentrations

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|------------------|
| Overlap Concentration | $c^*$ | Concentration at which coils just begin to touch |
| Entanglement Concentration | $c_e$ | Concentration at which topological entanglements form |
| Critical Concentration (sometimes used) | $c^{**}$ | Alternative notation for $c_e$ in older texts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in This Module:**
> - **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
> - **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Regime Analysis

#### **2.1.1 Dilute Solution Regime ($c < c^*$)**

- Each polymer chain exists as an **isolated, non-interacting coil**.
- The **radius of gyration** $R_g$ is independent of concentration.
- Chain conformation follows **unperturbed Gaussian statistics** in a theta solvent.
- The solution behaves nearly ideally; interactions are weak.
- **Viscosity** follows the intrinsic-viscosity relation:

$$\eta_{sp} = [\eta] c \quad \text{for } c \to 0$$

- **Osmotic pressure** follows van't Hoff's law (modified by second virial coefficient $A_2$):

$$\frac{\Pi}{c} = RT\left(\frac{1}{M} + A_2 c + A_3 c^2 + \cdots\right)$$

#### **2.1.2 Semi-Dilute (Unentangled) Regime ($c^* < c < c_e$)**

- Polymer coils **overlap** but do not yet form a permanent entanglement network.
- The solution is sometimes called a "**moderately concentrated**" solution.
- Chain dimensions **swell** or **contract** depending on solvent quality:
  - **Good solvent:** Excluded volume effects partially screened.
  - **Theta solvent:** Chain behaves as an ideal Gaussian coil.
- **Viscosity scaling** (de Gennes):

$$\eta \propto c^{\alpha}, \quad \alpha \approx 1 \text{ (theta)} \text{ to } 1.3 \text{ (good solvent)}$$

- **Correlation length** $\xi$ becomes the relevant length scale, smaller than $R_g$:

$$\xi \propto c^{-\nu/(3\nu-1)}$$

where $\nu$ is the Flory exponent.

#### **2.1.3 Concentrated / Entangled Regime ($c > c_e$)**

- Chains form a **transient entanglement network**.
- **Tube model** (de Gennes, Doi-Edwards) applies — each chain is confined within a "tube" of diameter $a$ (tube diameter).
- **Viscosity scaling** becomes dramatically stronger:

$$\eta_0 \propto c^{3.75} \text{ to } c^{4.0}$$

- Above $c_e$, the **zero-shear viscosity** can be expressed as:

$$\eta_0 = K \cdot M^{3.4} \cdot c^{\beta}$$

- The reptation concept governs long-chain dynamics.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Overlap concentration (mass/volume) | $c^* = \dfrac{M}{(4/3)\pi N_A R_g^3}$ | When coils just touch |
| Overlap concentration (volume fraction form) | $\phi^* \approx \dfrac{3M}{(4\pi N_A R_g^3 \rho_p)}$ | $\rho_p$ = polymer density |
| Entanglement concentration | $c_e \approx 10 \cdot c^*$ | Empirical rule |
| Radius of gyration (good solvent) | $R_g = a \cdot N^{\nu}, \quad \nu \approx 0.588$ | Flory exponent |
| Radius of gyration (theta solvent) | $R_g = a \cdot N^{0.5}$ | Ideal chain |
| Intrinsic viscosity relation | $[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0} \dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c}$ | Mark-Houwink definition |
| Mark-Houwink equation | $[\eta] = K M^{a}$ | $a = 0.5$ theta, $a = 0.7$–$0.8$ good |
| Specific viscosity | $\eta_{sp} = \dfrac{\eta - \eta_s}{\eta_s}$ | Relative viscosity minus 1 |
| Huggins equation | $\dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 c$ | For dilute regime |
| Kraemer equation | $\dfrac{\ln \eta_r}{c} = [\eta] + k_K [\eta]^2 c$ | Complementary to Huggins |
| Osmotic pressure (virial) | $\dfrac{\Pi}{RT} = \dfrac{c}{M} + A_2 c^2 + A_3 c^3 + \cdots$ | Concentration regimes |
| Concentration in g/dL | $c_{g/dL} = \dfrac{c_{g/cm^3}}{10}$ | Unit conversion |
| Number of chains per volume | $n = \dfrac{c \cdot N_A}{M}$ | Molar concentration |
| Volume fraction of polymer | $\phi = \dfrac{c}{\rho_p}$ | $\rho_p$ = polymer density |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Coatings & Paints:** Viscosity control of polymer solutions depends critically on whether formulation lies in semi-dilute or entangled regime.
- **Membrane Casting (Ultrafiltration):** Polymer dope concentration determines pore structure; typically operated in semi-dilute regime.
- **Spinning of Fibers:** Dry-jet wet spinning requires precise control near $c^*$ to obtain uniform fibers.
- **Pharmaceutical formulations:** Drug-polymer solutions in semi-dilute regime provide stable viscosity.
- **3D Printing (Polymer Inks):** Entangled solutions provide the elastic recovery needed for layer stability.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Overlap Concentration $c^*$

The **overlap concentration** is defined as the concentration at which the **volume occupied by polymer coils equals the total solution volume**. In other words, the polymer coils just begin to touch one another.

**Step 1: Volume of a single polymer coil**

The volume occupied by one polymer chain of radius of gyration $R_g$ is the volume of a sphere of radius $R_g$:

$$V_{coil} = \frac{4}{3}\pi R_g^3$$

**Step 2: Number of polymer chains per unit volume at concentration $c$**

The number of moles of polymer per unit volume is $c/M$ (where $M$ is molar mass). Multiplying by Avogadro's number gives the number of chains per unit volume:

$$n_{chains} = \frac{c \cdot N_A}{M}$$

**Step 3: Total volume fraction occupied by coils**

The total volume fraction occupied by the coils is:

$$\phi_{coils} = n_{chains} \cdot V_{coil} = \frac{c \cdot N_A}{M} \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R_g^3$$

**Step 4: Setting overlap condition**

At overlap, $\phi_{coils} = 1$ (coils just fill the space):

$$\frac{c^* N_A}{M} \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R_g^3 = 1$$

**Step 5: Solving for $c^*$**

Rearranging for $c^*$:

$$\boxed{c^* = \frac{M}{\frac{4}{3}\pi N_A R_g^3} = \frac{3M}{4\pi N_A R_g^3}}$$

**Step 6: Substituting $R_g$ for a good solvent**

Using $R_g = a N^{\nu}$ where $N$ is the degree of polymerization and $M = M_0 N$ ($M_0$ = monomer molar mass):

$$c^* = \frac{3 M_0 N}{4\pi N_A (a N^{\nu})^3} = \frac{3 M_0}{4\pi N_A a^3 N^{3\nu - 1}}$$

Since $\nu \approx 0.588$, we have $3\nu - 1 \approx 0.764$, so $c^* \propto N^{-0.764}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Higher molecular weight polymers have **lower** overlap concentration. This is why high-MW polymers form entangled networks at very low concentrations — they are already in the concentrated regime at concentrations that would be considered "dilute" for low-MW chains.

### 3.2 Derivation of Entanglement Concentration $c_e$

The **entanglement concentration** can be estimated using the **packing length** concept (Lovell, 1990).

**Step 1: Definition of Packing Length**

The packing length $p$ is defined as the ratio of chain contour length to pervaded volume:

$$p = \frac{N \cdot l}{\pi R_g^2 \cdot R_g} = \frac{N l}{\pi R_g^3}$$

where $l$ is the Kuhn segment length.

**Step 2: Entanglement molecular weight**

Empirically, the entanglement molecular weight $M_e$ scales as:

$$M_e \propto p^{-1} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad M_e \propto R_g^3 / (N l)$$

A more common empirical form:

$$M_e = k \cdot \rho_p \cdot p^{-2} \cdot (\text{function of stiffness})$$

**Step 3: Converting to concentration**

The entanglement concentration (in g/cm³) is:

$$c_e = \frac{\rho_p \cdot M_e}{N_A \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R_g^3}$$

**Step 4: Empirical rule**

For most flexible polymers:

$$\boxed{c_e \approx 10 \cdot c^*}$$

This means entanglement occurs at a concentration roughly **10× higher** than the overlap concentration.

### 3.3 Viscosity Scaling Laws (de Gennes)

The viscosity of polymer solutions exhibits **distinct power-law scalings** in each regime:

**Step 1: Dilute regime** — Non-interacting coils

$$\eta - \eta_s \propto c \cdot [\eta] \cdot \eta_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \eta_{sp} = [\eta]c$$

So: $\eta \propto c^{1.0}$

**Step 2: Semi-dilute unentangled regime** — Overlapping, non-entangled

$$\eta - \eta_s \propto c^{(3\nu - 1)/\nu} \cdot M^{\text{const}}$$

For $\nu = 0.5$ (theta): exponent $= (1.5-1)/0.5 = 1.0$

For $\nu = 0.588$ (good): exponent $= (1.764-1)/0.588 \approx 1.3$

**Step 3: Semi-dilute entangled regime** — Tube constraints dominate

$$\eta_0 \propto c^{(3\nu - 1)/(3\nu - 2)} \cdot M^3$$

For $\nu = 0.5$ (theta): exponent $= 0.5/(0.5-0.2) = 1.0$ (theta gives 1.0)

Actually, the standard result is:

$$\eta_0 \propto c^{3.0} \text{ (theta)} \quad \eta_0 \propto c^{3.75} \text{ (good solvent)}$$

**Step 4: Concentrated/melt regime**

$$\eta_0 \propto c^{3.4 \text{ to } 4.0} \cdot M^{3.4}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A polymer has $M = 10^6$ g/mol, $R_g = 30$ nm, $\rho_p = 1.0$ g/cm³. Calculate (a) the overlap concentration $c^*$, (b) the entanglement concentration $c_e$, and (c) classify a solution of $c = 0.05$ g/cm³.

**Given:**
- $M = 10^6$ g/mol
- $R_g = 30$ nm $= 30 \times 10^{-7}$ cm $= 3 \times 10^{-6}$ cm
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹

**Part (a): Overlap concentration**

First, calculate $R_g^3$:

$$R_g^3 = (3 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm})^3 = 27 \times 10^{-18} \text{ cm}^3 = 2.7 \times 10^{-17} \text{ cm}^3$$

Now apply the formula:

$$c^* = \frac{3 M}{4 \pi N_A R_g^3}$$

Substituting values:

$$c^* = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{4 \pi \times (6.022 \times 10^{23}) \times (2.7 \times 10^{-17})}$$

Computing denominator:

$$4 \pi \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 7.566 \times 10^{24}$$

$$7.566 \times 10^{24} \times 2.7 \times 10^{-17} = 20.43 \times 10^{7} = 2.043 \times 10^{8}$$

Therefore:

$$c^* = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{2.043 \times 10^{8}} = 1.469 \times 10^{-2} \text{ g/cm}^3 \approx 0.0147 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Part (b): Entanglement concentration**

$$c_e = 10 \times c^* = 10 \times 0.0147 = 0.147 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Part (c): Classification**

Given $c = 0.05$ g/cm³:
- $c^* = 0.0147 < 0.05$ ✓
- $c = 0.05 < c_e = 0.147$ ✓

Therefore $c^* < c < c_e$, meaning the solution is in the **semi-dilute unentangled regime**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always state the regime with the correct inequality chain. Examiners expect: "$c^*$ to $c_e$" range explicitly. Always show numerical computation in two decimal places minimum.

### 3.5 Comparison Matrix: All Three Regimes

| Property | Dilute ($c < c^*$) | Semi-dilute ($c^* < c < c_e$) | Concentrated ($c > c_e$) |
|----------|---------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Chain interaction | None (isolated) | Overlap, no entanglements | Entangled network |
| Relevant length scale | $R_g$ | Correlation length $\xi$ | Tube diameter $a$ |
| Viscosity scaling $\eta \propto c^x$ | $x = 1$ | $x = 1.0$–$1.3$ | $x = 3.4$–$4.0$ |
| Chain dynamics | Zimm dynamics | Rouse-like | Reptation |
| $M$-dependence | $[\eta] \propto M^a$ | $\eta \propto M^1$ | $\eta \propto M^{3.4}$ |
| Osmotic pressure | $\Pi \propto c/M$ | Excluded volume screened | Mean-field |
