
## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hydrolytic Aging System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs["INPUT FACTORS"]
        I1["Temperature T"]
        I2["pH of environment"]
        I3["Water activity a_w"]
        I4["Catalyst type and concentration"]
    end

    subgraph PolymerSys["POLYMER STRUCTURE FACTORS"]
        P1["Backbone chemistry ester amide carbonate urethane"]
        P2["Crystallinity percent"]
        P3["Molecular weight distribution"]
        P4["Steric shielding of labile bond"]
    end

    subgraph HydrolyCore["HYDROLYSIS REACTION CORE"]
        H1["Rate constant k_h from Arrhenius"]
        H2["Random chain scission event"]
        H3["Chain cleavage at heteroatom bond"]
    end

    subgraph Outputs["PROPERTY OUTPUTS"]
        O1["Number average DP Xn decreases"]
        O2["Mechanical strength and toughness fall"]
        O3["Molecular weight distribution broadens"]
        O4["Mass loss and weight reduction observed"]
    end

    I1 --> H1
    I2 --> H1
    I3 --> H1
    I4 --> H1
    P1 --> H2
    P2 --> H2
    P3 --> H2
    P4 --> H2
    H1 --> H2
    H2 --> H3
    H3 --> O1
    H3 --> O2
    H3 --> O3
    H3 --> O4
```

## 4.3 Hydrolysis of Common Polymers — Quick Visual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PET["PET Polyethylene terephthalate"]:::poly
    PA["Polyamides Nylon"]:::poly
    PC["Polycarbonate BPA"]:::poly
    PU["Polyurethane PU"]:::poly
    PLA["PLA Polylactic acid"]:::poly
    CE["Cellulose polysaccharide"]:::poly

    PET -->|ester CO-O| R1["Hydrolyzes to TPA + EG"]:::prod
    PA -->|amide CO-NH| R2["Hydrolyzes to diacid + diamine or amino acid"]:::prod
    PC -->|carbonate O-CO-O| R3["Hydrolyzes to BPA + carbonate"]:::prod
    PU -->|urethane NH-CO-O| R4["Hydrolyzes to polyol + isocyanate derivative"]:::prod
    PLA -->|ester CO-O| R5["Hydrolyzes to lactic acid biocompatible"]:::prod
    CE -->|glycosidic C-O-C| R6["Hydrolyzes to glucose units"]:::prod

    classDef poly fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef prod fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3, Q1]**
>
> **Q1.** Define hydrolysis of polymers. Name **two** polymer classes that are highly susceptible to hydrolysis and **one** that is resistant. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Hydrolysis is a chemical degradation process in which water (H–OH) cleaves susceptible bonds in a polymer backbone—most commonly ester, amide, carbonate, or urethane linkages—yielding lower molecular weight fragments or monomers.
- **Susceptible:** Polyesters (e.g., PET), Polyamides (e.g., Nylon-6,6). *(Any two)*
- **Resistant:** Polyethylene (PE) or Polystyrene (PS) — because their backbones are composed entirely of strong, non-polar C–C bonds that are kinetically inert to water.

*(Examiner-acceptable alternatives for susceptible: Polycarbonates, Polyurethanes, PLA, Cellulose. Resistant: PP, PVC, PTFE, PMMA.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3, Q2]**
>
> **Q2.** Distinguish between acid-catalyzed and base-catalyzed hydrolysis of an ester linkage in a polymer. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Acid-Catalyzed | Base-Catalyzed (Saponification) |
|---|---|---|
| Catalyst | H⁺ (proton) | OH⁻ (hydroxide) |
| First step | Protonation of carbonyl O | Direct attack of OH⁻ on C |
| Product | Carboxylic acid (–COOH) | Carboxylate salt (–COO⁻) |
| Reversibility | Equilibrium / reversible | Practically irreversible |
| Typical use | Moisture aging studies | Recycling (e.g., PET with NaOH) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam – Dec 2024 Model, Module 3]**
>
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Explain the mechanism of acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of a polyester. Discuss the role of the catalyst and the rate-determining step. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A polyester sample has initial $\overline{X}_n(0) = 150$. During exposure to humid conditions, the hydrolytic rate constant at 70 °C is found to be $k_h = 5.0 \times 10^{-7}$ s⁻¹. Calculate the number-average degree of polymerization after 30 days, and the half-life of the bond cleavage. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Q A (a) [7 Marks]

1. **Protonation of carbonyl oxygen** (1 mark): The acid catalyst H⁺ protonates the carbonyl oxygen of the ester group, increasing the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon.

   $$ \text{\textasciitilde C(=O)\textasciitilde O\textasciitilde} + \text{H}^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{\textasciitilde C(OH}^+\text{)=O\textasciitilde} $$

2. **Nucleophilic attack by water** (1.5 marks): A water molecule attacks the activated carbonyl carbon to form a tetrahedral intermediate.

3. **Proton transfer** (1 mark): Proton transfer within the intermediate from the incoming water to the leaving alkoxy oxygen.

4. **Expulsion of alcohol leaving group** (1.5 marks): The C–OR bond breaks, releasing the alcohol fragment and producing a protonated carboxylic acid.

5. **Deprotonation & catalyst regeneration** (1 mark): Loss of H⁺ gives the free carboxylic acid fragment and regenerates H⁺ catalyst.

6. **Rate-determining step** (1 mark): The nucleophilic addition of water to the activated carbonyl (formation of tetrahedral intermediate) is generally rate-determining under acidic conditions.

### Model Solution — Q A (b) [7 Marks]

Given: $\overline{X}_n(0) = 150$, $k_h = 5.0 \times 10^{-7}$ s⁻¹, $t = 30 \text{ days} = 30 \times 24 \times 3600 = 2.592 \times 10^{6}$ s.

**Step 1:** Compute $k_h t$. (1 mark)
$$ k_h t = (5.0 \times 10^{-7}) \times (2.592 \times 10^{6}) = 1.296 $$

**Step 2:** Compute $1 - e^{-k_h t}$. (1 mark)
$$ 1 - e^{-1.296} = 1 - 0.2737 = 0.7263 $$

**Step 3:** Apply the Kuhn-type equation. (2 marks)
$$ \overline{X}_n(t) = \frac{\overline{X}_n(0)}{1 + \overline{X}_n(0)\,(1 - e^{-k_h t})} = \frac{150}{1 + 150 \times 0.7263} = \frac{150}{1 + 108.95} = \frac{150}{109.95} \approx 1.36 $$

**Step 4:** Half-life. (1 mark)
$$ t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k_h} = \frac{0.693}{5.0 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.386 \times 10^{6}\;\text{s} \approx 16.05\;\text{days} $$

**Step 5:** Conclusion. (2 marks) The DP has collapsed from 150 to about 1.36 in 30 days, indicating extensive hydrolytic degradation. The half-life of the bond cleavage is roughly 16 days, which is alarmingly short for an engineering plastic and confirms that this polyester is unsuitable for prolonged use in humid, warm environments unless stabilized.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)**  *(Alternative choice)*
>
> **(a)** Discuss the factors affecting the rate of hydrolysis in condensation polymers. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** The hydrolysis of a polyamide follows first-order kinetics with $k_h = 2.0 \times 10^{-6}$ s⁻¹ at 120 °C and $k_h = 5.0 \times 10^{-8}$ s⁻¹ at 80 °C. Calculate the activation energy $E_a$ and predict the rate constant at 25 °C. $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Q B (a) [7 Marks]

1. **Polymer structure (2 marks):** Type of labile bond (ester > carbonate > urethane > amide in ease of hydrolysis). Steric hindrance around the carbonyl; electron-withdrawing/donating substituents; crystallinity (amorphous regions hydrolyze faster than crystalline).

2. **Environmental pH (2 marks):** Acidic and basic conditions both accelerate hydrolysis. Base-catalyzed (saponification) is fastest and irreversible. Neutral water gives the slowest, often autocatalytic, hydrolysis.

3. **Temperature (1.5 marks):** Hydrolysis is temperature-dependent via the Arrhenius relation; higher T increases $k_h$ exponentially.

4. **Water activity and concentration (1 mark):** Higher moisture content / relative humidity accelerates the reaction; in submerged or steam environments hydrolysis is severe.

5. **Catalyst presence and diffusion (0.5 mark):** External catalysts (H⁺, OH⁻, enzymes) lower $E_a$; thick samples may show diffusion-limited hydrolysis at the surface.

### Model Solution — Q B (b) [7 Marks]

Given: $k_1 = 2.0 \times 10^{-6}$ s⁻¹ at $T_1 = 393$ K (120 °C); $k_2 = 5.0 \times 10^{-8}$ s⁻¹ at $T_2 = 353$ K (80 °C); $T_3 = 298$ K (25 °C).

**Step 1:** Arrhenius two-point form. (2 marks)
$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) $$

**Step 2:** Compute $k_1/k_2$ and the temperature term. (1 mark)
$$ \frac{k_1}{k_2} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-6}}{5.0 \times 10^{-8}} = 40 $$
$$ \ln(40) = 3.689 $$
$$ \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{353} - \frac{1}{393} = (2.8326 - 2.5445) \times 10^{-3} = 2.881 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{K}^{-1} $$

**Step 3:** Solve for $E_a$. (1 mark)
$$ E_a = \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_1/k_2)}{(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)} = \frac{8.314 \times 3.689}{2.881 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{30.67}{2.881 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1.064 \times 10^{5}\;\text{J mol}^{-1} \approx 106.4\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1} $$

**Step 4:** Predict $k$ at 298 K using $k_2$ as reference. (2 marks)
$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{k_3}{k_2}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_3}\right) = \frac{1.064 \times 10^{5}}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{353} - \frac{1}{298}\right) $$

$$ \frac{1}{353} - \frac{1}{298} = (2.8326 - 3.3557) \times 10^{-3} = -5.231 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{K}^{-1} $$

$$ \ln(k_3 / k_2) = (1.279 \times 10^{4}) \times (-5.231 \times 10^{-4}) = -6.690 $$

$$ k_3 = k_2 \cdot e^{-6.690} = (5.0 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1.247 \times 10^{-3}) \approx 6.2 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{s}^{-1} $$

**Step 5:** Conclusion. (1 mark) The activation energy is $\approx 106$ kJ mol⁻¹, typical of hydrolytic degradation of polyamides. At ambient 25 °C, the rate constant is extremely small ($\sim 10^{-11}$ s⁻¹), indicating that dry polyamide parts have very long shelf life; but a 40 °C rise in temperature (80 → 120 °C) increases the rate by a factor of 40, justifying the use of accelerated aging tests for service-life prediction.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Hydrolysis Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the role of catalyst regeneration** in the acid-catalyzed mechanism — students often draw the mechanism but skip the final deprotonation, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up units of $k_h$:** For first-order reactions, $k_h$ has units of **s⁻¹**, *not* mL s⁻¹. Many students leave units inconsistent.
> 3. **Sign errors in Arrhenius two-point equation:** Remember the form is $\ln(k_1/k_2) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ — if you flip the bracket you get a negative $E_a$, which is impossible.
> 4. **Not converting days/hours to seconds** in $k_h t$ calculations. KTU examiners will *strictly* deduct 1 mark for unit inconsistency.
> 5. **Confusing $t_{1/2}$ of bond cleavage with $t_{1/2}$ of $\overline{X}_n$** — they are *not* the same for random scission; only the bond half-life is $0.693/k_h$.
> 6. **Forgetting the molecular reason** for hydrolysis resistance of vinyl polymers (no heteroatom in backbone). Listing PE as "resistant" without justification loses a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrolysis — Final Rapid-Revision Points:**
> - **Definition:** Water cleaves susceptible bonds (ester, amide, carbonate, urethane, glycosidic) in a polymer backbone → lower MW fragments / monomers.
> - **Susceptible polymers:** Polyesters (PET, PLA), polyamides (Nylons), polycarbonates, polyurethanes, polysaccharides, polyimides.
> - **Resistant polymers:** PE, PP, PS, PVC, PTFE, PMMA — *carbon-chain backbones, no heteroatoms.*
> - **Three catalytic regimes:** Acid (H⁺), Base (OH⁻ = saponification, irreversible), Neutral (slow, autocatalytic), plus enzymatic.
> - **First-order kinetics:** $\ln([\text{Bond}]_0/[\text{Bond}]) = k_h t$; half-life $t_{1/2} = 0.693 / k_h$.
> - **Random scission DP relation:** $\dfrac{1}{\overline{X}_n(t)} = \dfrac{1}{\overline{X}_n(0)} + k_h t$ (early-time, random scission).
> - **Arrhenius:** $k_h = A e^{-E_a/RT}$; two-point form for $E_a$ determination.
> - **Engineering use:** PET recycling (alkaline hydrolysis → TPA + EG); PLA/PLGA resorbable implants; service-life prediction for nylons in humid/under-hood environments.
> - **KTU favorites:** Comparison acid vs base catalysis, derivation of $1/\overline{X}_n$ vs $t$ relation, Arrhenius $E_a$ calculation, examples of recyclable vs biodegradable polymers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acidolysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Acidolysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acidolysis** is a chemical depolymerization reaction in which the ester linkages (or analogous functional linkages) of a condensation polymer — most commonly a polyester such as Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET) — are cleaved by the action of a low-molecular-weight **organic or inorganic acid** (e.g., acetic acid, benzoic acid, sulfuric acid, nitric acid, p-toluenesulfonic acid). The process yields a mixture of monomers, dimers, and short-chain oligomers, and is classified as one of the four principal **chemolytic recycling** routes (alongside hydrolysis, alcoholysis/methanolysis, and glycolysis).

The general stoichiometry of acidolysis of a polyester can be written as:

$$\text{Polymer–[O–CO–R]}_n \text{–Polymer} \; + \; n\,\text{R'COOH} \;\longrightarrow\; n\,\text{R'CO–O–R} \; + \; \text{Polymer–(OH)}_n$$

In the specific case of **PET + Acetic Acid** (a textbook example):

$$\text{[–O–CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–O–CO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO–]}_n \; + \; 2n\,\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \;\longrightarrow\; n\,\text{HO–CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–OH} \; + \; n\,\text{CH}_3\text{CO–O–CO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO–O–CH}_3$$

The low-molecular-weight cleavage products are typically the diol (e.g., ethylene glycol) and a diacid-diacetate (or free dicarboxylic acid, depending on the protonating acid's strength and water content).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: "The Molecular Zipper"**
> Imagine a long chain of paper clips linked together (the polymer chain). The acid acts like a special pair of scissors that only knows how to snip the clip-connectors (ester bonds) — but it doesn't destroy the individual clips (monomers). After enough "snips," the long chain falls apart into smaller pieces (oligomers) or individual clips (monomers), which can then be reused. **Acidolysis is therefore "molecular tailoring" — a controlled, acid-catalyzed cutting of polymer chains back into useful building blocks.**

Key terms in everyday language:
- **Polymer** = a long chain of repeating units (like a beaded necklace)
- **Ester linkage** = the chemical "clasp" that joins two beads
- **Acid** = the chemical "scissors" that breaks the clasp
- **Depolymerization** = the process of breaking the chain back into shorter segments

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Key Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The acidolysis reaction is characterized by the following key engineering parameters:
> - **Typical Operating Temperature:** **150 °C – 250 °C** (PET acidolysis range)
> - **Typical Pressure:** Atmospheric to **2–5 atm** (closed reactor for volatile acids)
> - **Reaction Time:** **1 – 6 hours** depending on acid concentration and target molecular weight
> - **Acid Concentration:** **5 – 20 wt%** of acid relative to polymer
> - **Activation Energy ($E_a$):** Typically **60 – 90 kJ/mol** for acid-catalyzed ester cleavage
> - **Catalyst (optional):** Strong Brønsted acids such as **H₂SO₄, HNO₃, p-TSA** (p-toluenesulfonic acid)

---

### 1.4 Why Acidolysis Matters in KTU Syllabus

> [!NOTE]
> In the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCPOT302 – Polymer Science syllabus**, acidolysis is grouped with **hydrolysis, alcoholysis, methanolysis, glycolysis, and aminolysis** under the umbrella of **"Polymer Degradation and Recycling of Polymers"** (typically Module 3). The student is expected to:
> 1. Identify the type of chemolytic degradation
> 2. Write the balanced chemical equation
> 3. Propose the mechanism
> 4. Compare it with other degradation routes
> 5. Discuss industrial applications (e.g., PET bottle recycling)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Acidolysis Process

The acidolysis of a polyester is best understood as a **protonation-driven nucleophilic acyl substitution** that proceeds through a tetrahedral intermediate. The following bulleted logic captures the *why* and *how* of each step:

**Step 1 – Diffusion & Swelling**
- The acid (e.g., CH₃COOH) must first diffuse into the amorphous regions of the polymer.
- *Why?* Crystalline PET is highly impermeable; acidolysis preferentially occurs in the amorphous phase first, then progresses into crystalline domains at higher temperatures.

**Step 2 – Protonation of the Carbonyl Oxygen**
- The carbonyl oxygen of the ester linkage accepts a proton from the acid catalyst.
- *Why?* Protonation makes the carbonyl carbon highly electrophilic ("hungry" for electrons), facilitating nucleophilic attack.

**Step 3 – Nucleophilic Attack by the Acid Anion / Water**
- A nucleophile (water, hydroxyl of the acid, or the conjugate base) attacks the activated carbonyl carbon, forming a **tetrahedral intermediate**.
- *How?* The C=O double bond breaks, and the carbon temporarily becomes sp³-hybridized.

**Step 4 – Proton Transfer & Cleavage of the C–O Alkyl Bond**
- A proton is transferred from the nucleophile to the leaving alkoxy oxygen.
- The C–O bond between the acyl group and the alkyl–O of the original diol breaks.
- *Result:* One fragment terminates as a new carboxylic acid (–COOH) and the other as a new ester (–COOR').

**Step 5 – Chain Scission & Oligomer Formation**
- Repetition of Steps 2–4 along the chain progressively reduces the molecular weight.
- The reaction rate slows as the chain becomes shorter and the concentration of ester bonds decreases.

**Step 6 – Monomer Liberation**
- At extended reaction times and high temperatures, the oligomers further cleave to release free monomers: **terephthalic acid (TPA)** and **ethylene glycol (EG)**.

---

### 2.2 Comparison of Acidolysis with Other Chemolytic Routes

| **Property** | **Acidolysis** | **Hydrolysis** | **Methanolysis** | **Glycolysis** | **Aminolysis** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cleaving Agent** | Carboxylic acid (CH₃COOH) | Water (H₂O) | Methanol (CH₃OH) | Ethylene glycol (EG) | Amine (R–NH₂) |
| **Catalyst** | Strong Brønsted acid (H₂SO₄) | Acid / Base / Neutral | Metal acetate (Zn, Mn, Pb) | Metal acetate (Zn, Mn) | Heat alone |
| **Main Product** | TPA + diol-diacetate | TPA + EG | DMT (dimethyl terephthalate) + EG | BHET (bishydroxyethyl terephthalate) | Diamide of TPA + diol |
| **Temperature (°C)** | 150 – 250 | 200 – 250 | 180 – 280 | 180 – 240 | 100 – 200 |
| **Pressure (atm)** | 1 – 5 | 10 – 30 | 20 – 40 | 1 – 5 | 1 |
| **Industrial Use** | Lab-scale / specialty | Widespread | Older PET route | Most common (modern) | Nylon recycling |
| **Selectivity** | High for ester bond | Broad | High | High | High for amide |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for Acidolysis — Memorize for KTU University Exam**

| **Equation / Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| General Acidolysis Reaction | $P_n + n\,R'COOH \rightarrow R'COO{-}R + P_{(n-1)}{-}COOH$ | $P_n$ = polymer of degree of polymerization $n$ | Stoichiometric basis |
| PET Acidolysis with Acetic Acid | $PET + 2n\,CH_3COOH \rightarrow n\,EG + n\,TPA\text{-diacetate}$ | $EG$ = ethylene glycol | Specific PET recycling |
| Degree of Polymerization (Carothers) | $\overline{DP}_n = \dfrac{1}{(1-p)}$ | $p$ = extent of reaction (fraction of bonds cleaved for reverse synthesis) | Used in kinetics |
| Rate of Acidolysis (Pseudo-First-Order) | $-\dfrac{d[ester]}{dt} = k_{obs}\,[ester]$ | $k_{obs} = k[H^+]$ ; $k$ = second-order rate constant (L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹) | Experimental kinetic model |
| Arrhenius Equation | $k = A\,e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $A$ = pre-exponential factor ; $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol) ; $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K ; $T$ = temperature (K) | Temperature dependence |
| Half-Life | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{obs}} = \dfrac{0.693}{k_{obs}}$ | $t_{1/2}$ in seconds | Practical reaction time |
| Mark–Houwink (viscosity-average MW) | $[\eta] = K\,M_v^{a}$ | $[\eta]$ = intrinsic viscosity (dL/g) ; $K, a$ = polymer–solvent constants | Monitoring molecular weight loss |
| Henderson–Hasselbalch (pH of acid) | $pH = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | $pK_a$ of acetic acid = **4.76** | Predicts proton availability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU – "HAMS-G"** to remember chemolytic routes:
> **H**ydrolysis, **A**cidolysis, **M**ethanolysis, **S**olvent (aminolysis) — **G**lycolysis. Acidolysis uses a **carboxylic acid** (NOT water) and yields a **carboxylic-acid-terminated oligomer**.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **PET Bottle Recycling:** Acidolysis (especially with nitric acid) can recover pure **terephthalic acid** suitable for re-polymerization to virgin-grade PET.
2. **Polycarbonate Recycling:** Acidolysis with HCl cleaves carbonate linkages to yield **bisphenol-A** and CO₂.
3. **Polyester Fiber Waste Management:** Acidolysis is a low-energy alternative to glycolysis for textile waste valorization.
4. **Laboratory Tool:** Researchers use acidolysis as a controlled depolymerization technique to study polymer microstructure and to characterize block-copolymers by selectively cleaving one block.
5. **Sustainable Polymer Economy:** Acidolysis supports the **circular economy** by enabling chemical recycling of post-consumer plastics into monomers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rate Law for Acidolysis

We derive the kinetic expression assuming acidolysis follows a **pseudo-first-order** model in ester concentration, with the proton concentration treated as approximately constant (excess acid).

> **Step 1 — Define the rate of ester bond cleavage.**
> 
> Let $[E]$ = concentration of ester bonds (mol·L⁻¹) at time $t$. The rate of disappearance of ester bonds is proportional to $[E]$ and to $[H^+]$:
> 
> $$r = -\dfrac{d[E]}{dt} = k\,[H^+]\,[E]$$
> 
> Since $[H^+]$ is buffered / in large excess, define:
> 
> $$k_{obs} = k\,[H^+] \quad (\text{a constant for a given acidolysis run})$$
> 
> Therefore:
> 
> $$-\dfrac{d[E]}{dt} = k_{obs}\,[E]$$

> **Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate.**
> 
> $$\dfrac{d[E]}{[E]} = -k_{obs}\,dt$$
> 
> Integrating from $t=0$ (where $[E] = [E]_0$) to time $t$ (where $[E] = [E]_t$):
> 
> $$\int_{[E]_0}^{[E]_t} \dfrac{d[E]}{[E]} = -k_{obs}\int_0^t dt$$
> 
> $$\ln[E]_t - \ln[E]_0 = -k_{obs}\,t$$
> 
> $$\boxed{\ln\dfrac{[E]_t}{[E]_0} = -k_{obs}\,t}$$
> 
> Or equivalently:
> 
> $$\boxed{[E]_t = [E]_0\,e^{-k_{obs}\,t}}$$

> **Step 3 — Derive the half-life.**
> 
> Set $[E]_t = \dfrac{[E]_0}{2}$ :
> 
> $$\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -k_{obs}\,t_{1/2}$$
> 
> $$-\ln 2 = -k_{obs}\,t_{1/2}$$
> 
> $$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{obs}} = \dfrac{0.693}{k_{obs}}}$$

> **Step 4 — Apply Arrhenius equation to find temperature dependence.**
> 
> $$k_{obs} = A\,e^{-E_a/RT}$$
> 
> Taking the natural logarithm:
> 
> $$\ln k_{obs} = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T}$$
> 
> This is a linear equation of the form $y = mx + c$ where:
> - $y = \ln k_{obs}$
> - $x = 1/T$
> - slope $m = -E_a/R$
> - intercept $c = \ln A$
> 
> A plot of $\ln k_{obs}$ versus $1/T$ (Arrhenius plot) yields a straight line whose slope gives $-E_a/R$, allowing the **activation energy** of acidolysis to be determined experimentally.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem:** PET acidolysis is carried out in excess acetic acid at 200 °C. The initial ester concentration is 2.50 mol·L⁻¹. After 3.0 hours, the concentration drops to 0.30 mol·L⁻¹. Calculate (a) the observed rate constant, (b) the half-life, and (c) the time required to reach 95% conversion.

> **Solution:**

**(a) Observed rate constant:**

Using the integrated first-order expression:
$$k_{obs} = -\dfrac{1}{t}\,\ln\dfrac{[E]_t}{[E]_0} = -\dfrac{1}{3.0\,\text{h}}\,\ln\left(\dfrac{0.30}{2.50}\right)$$

$$\dfrac{0.30}{2.50} = 0.12$$

$$\ln(0.12) = -2.1203$$

$$k_{obs} = -\dfrac{(-2.1203)}{3.0\,\text{h}} = \dfrac{2.1203}{3.0\,\text{h}} = 0.7068\,\text{h}^{-1}$$

Converting to SI units (per second):
$$k_{obs} = 0.7068\,\text{h}^{-1} \times \dfrac{1\,\text{h}}{3600\,\text{s}} = 1.963 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{k_{obs} \approx 0.707\,\text{h}^{-1} \approx 1.96 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s}^{-1}}$$

**(b) Half-life:**

$$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{k_{obs}} = \dfrac{0.693}{0.7068\,\text{h}^{-1}} = 0.9805\,\text{h}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} \approx 0.98\,\text{h} \approx 59\,\text{minutes}}$$

**(c) Time for 95% conversion:**

95% conversion means $[E]_t/[E]_0 = 0.05$.

$$t = -\dfrac{1}{k_{obs}}\,\ln(0.05) = -\dfrac{1}{0.7068}\,\ln(0.05) = -\dfrac{(-2.9957)}{0.7068}$$

$$t = 4.239\,\text{h} \approx 4\,\text{h}\,14\,\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{t_{95\%} \approx 4.24\,\text{h}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mechanistic Summary

| **Step** | **Chemical Event** | **Bond Broken** | **Bond Formed** | **Intermediate** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diffusion of acid into polymer | None | None | Swollen polymer gel |
| 2 | Protonation of carbonyl O | C=O π-bond weakens | O–H bond (with H⁺) | Protonated ester |
| 3 | Nucleophilic attack | C=O π-bond | C–O σ-bond (new) | Tetrahedral intermediate |
| 4 | Proton transfer | Nuc–H bond | O–H bond (alkoxy) | Neutral tetrahedral intermediate |
| 5 | C–O alkyl cleavage | C–O alkyl bond | C=O regenerated | Carboxylic acid + new ester |
| 6 | Repeat along chain | Many ester bonds | Many new bonds | Oligomers → monomers |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mechanism Flow Chart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: PET polyester chain] --> B[Acid CH3COOH diffuses into amorphous region]
    B --> C[Protonation of carbonyl oxygen of ester bond]
    C --> D[Formation of tetrahedral intermediate via nucleophilic attack]
    D --> E[Proton transfer from nucleophile to leaving alkoxy oxygen]
    E --> F[C-O alkyl bond cleavage: chain scission]
    F --> G[Fragment 1 terminated with -COOH]
    F --> H[Fragment 2 terminated with new ester group]
    G --> I[Further reaction cycles along the chain]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Molecular weight check}
    J -->|MW still high| C
    J -->|Desired MW reached| K[Quench, cool, separate]
    K --> L[Products: oligomers, monomers, residual acid]
    L --> M[Purification: precipitation, distillation, crystallization]
    M --> N[End: Recovered terephthalic acid and ethylene glycol]
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Industrial Acidolysis Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEED[Feed Preparation Unit]
        A1[Waste PET bottles / fiber scrap] --> A2[Shredding and washing]
        A2 --> A3[Drying and size reduction]
    end

    subgraph RXN[Acidolysis Reactor System]
        A3 --> B1[Reactor charge: PET + acetic acid + H2SO4 catalyst]
        B1 --> B2[Stirred high-pressure reactor 200-250 C, 2-5 atm]
        B2 --> B3[Reaction time 2-6 hours]
        B3 --> B4[Quench and cool to 80 C]
    end

    subgraph SEP[Separation and Purification Unit]
        B4 --> C1[Filtration of unreacted solids]
        C1 --> C2[Neutralization with NaOH]
        C2 --> C3[Crystallization of terephthalic acid]
        C3 --> C4[Filtration and washing of TPA crystals]
        C4 --> C5[Drying of TPA]
    end

    subgraph REC[Recovery and Reuse Loop]
        C5 --> D1[Pure TPA to repolymerization]
        B4 --> D2[Distillation of ethylene glycol from filtrate]
        D2 --> D3[Pure EG to repolymerization]
        C2 --> D4[Recovered sodium acetate byproduct]
    end

    style B2 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style C5 fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#0000cc,stroke-width:2px
    style D1 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.3 Reaction Energy Profile (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reactants: PET + CH3COOH] -->|Ea1| TS1[Transition State 1: Protonation]
    TS1 --> TI[Tetrahedral Intermediate]
    TI -->|Ea2| TS2[Transition State 2: C-O Cleavage]
    TS2 --> P[Products: TPA + EG + Oligomers]
    TI -.->|lower energy local minimum| TI
    R -.->|lower energy start| R
    P -.->|lowest energy final| P
```

> **Key Insight:** The tetrahedral intermediate is a *local energy minimum* (kinetic "rest stop"), and the two transition states are *energy maxima* (kinetic "hills"). The overall reaction is exothermic because the products are lower in energy than the reactants — this drives the depolymerization thermodynamically at high temperatures where entropy also favors smaller molecules.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Define acidolysis. Write the balanced chemical equation for the acidolysis of PET using acetic acid. Name the monomer products obtained.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Acidolysis is a chemolytic depolymerization process in which the ester linkages of a polyester (e.g., PET) are cleaved by a low-molecular-weight carboxylic acid, yielding monomers and/or oligomers.

**Balanced Equation (1 mark):**

$$[\text{–O–CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–O–CO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO–}]_n + 2n\,\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \longrightarrow n\,\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + n\,\text{CH}_3\text{COO–CO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOCH}_3$$

**Monomer Products (1 mark):**
- **Ethylene glycol (HOCH₂CH₂OH)**
- **Terephthalic acid (or its diacetate derivative)** — depending on hydrolysis step

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Differentiate between acidolysis and glycolysis as methods of polymer recycling with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Acidolysis** | **Glycolysis** |
|---|---|---|
| **Cleaving reagent** | Carboxylic acid (e.g., CH₃COOH) | Diol (e.g., ethylene glycol) |
| **Catalyst** | Strong Brønsted acid (H₂SO₄) | Metal acetate (Zn, Mn, Pb) |
| **Main monomeric product** | Terephthalic acid (TPA) | Bis-hydroxyethyl terephthalate (BHET) |
| **Example** | PET + acetic acid → TPA + EG | PET + EG → BHET |

Glycolysis is the **most widely used** industrial route, while acidolysis is preferred when **pure TPA** recovery is the goal.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) Describe the mechanism of acidolysis of PET with acetic acid. Discuss the role of protonation in activating the carbonyl group. (7 marks)**

**(b) The acidolysis of PET follows pseudo-first-order kinetics with $k_{obs} = 0.85\,\text{h}^{-1}$ at 200 °C and $k_{obs} = 2.30\,\text{h}^{-1}$ at 230 °C. Calculate the activation energy $E_a$ of the reaction. Given $R = 8.314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mechanism of Acidolysis of PET (7 marks)**

The mechanism proceeds in **six distinct steps** with protonation as the central activating event:

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Acetic acid (CH₃COOH) diffuses into the amorphous domains of PET. The crystalline regions are not penetrated initially due to their tight chain packing.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** A proton (H⁺) from the acid catalyst (H₂SO₄) attaches to the carbonyl oxygen of the ester linkage. This converts the carbonyl oxygen from a neutral to a positively charged (oxocarbenium-like) state, dramatically increasing the **electrophilicity** of the carbonyl carbon.

$$\text{–CO–O–R} + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{–C(OH}^+\text{)–O–R}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** The acetate ion (CH₃COO⁻) or water (a stronger nucleophile at high T) attacks the now highly electrophilic carbonyl carbon. The C=O double bond breaks, and a **tetrahedral intermediate** with sp³-hybridized carbon forms, bearing –OH, –O⁻, –OR, and –OC(O)CH₃ groups.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** A proton is transferred from the newly added nucleophile to the original alkoxy oxygen (–OR group), converting it into a good leaving group (–OHR⁺ or simply –OH after deprotonation).

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** The C–O bond between the acyl carbon and the original diol fragment cleaves. The acyl portion becomes a new ester (or free acid, after hydrolysis), and the diol fragment leaves as an –OH-terminated oligomer. The carbonyl C=O is regenerated.

**Step 6 (0.5 mark):** Repetition of Steps 2–5 along the polymer chain progressively reduces the molecular weight until monomers (TPA and EG) are liberated.

**Role of Protonation (integrated throughout):** Without protonation, the carbonyl carbon is insufficiently electrophilic to be attacked by a weak nucleophile like acetate or water. Protonation reduces the activation energy by stabilizing the transition state and the tetrahedral intermediate. The **Brønsted acid catalyst** (H₂SO₄ or p-TSA) is essential.

---

**(b) Activation Energy Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $k_1 = 0.85\,\text{h}^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 200\,°\text{C} = 473\,\text{K}$
- $k_2 = 2.30\,\text{h}^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 230\,°\text{C} = 503\,\text{K}$
- $R = 8.314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$

**Formula (2 marks):** The two-point Arrhenius equation:

$$\ln\dfrac{k_2}{k_1} = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute $\ln(k_2/k_1)$:

$$\dfrac{k_2}{k_1} = \dfrac{2.30}{0.85} = 2.7059$$

$$\ln(2.7059) = 0.9956$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute $(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$:

$$\dfrac{1}{T_1} = \dfrac{1}{473} = 2.1142 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1}{503} = 1.9881 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} = (2.1142 - 1.9881) \times 10^{-3} = 0.1261 \times 10^{-3} = 1.261 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Solve for $E_a$:

$$E_a = R \cdot \dfrac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)} = 8.314 \times \dfrac{0.9956}{1.261 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = 8.314 \times 7895.3 = 65{,}641\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Convert to kJ/mol and box the answer:

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 65.6\,\text{kJ·mol}^{-1}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** This value is consistent with the **60 – 90 kJ/mol** range typical of acid-catalyzed ester cleavage, confirming the proposed mechanism is reasonable.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) Explain the acidolysis of polyesters with a suitable example. How does it differ from alcoholysis? (7 marks)**

**(b) Write short notes on (i) the role of acid concentration and (ii) the effect of temperature on the rate of acidolysis. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Acidolysis of Polyesters (7 marks)**

**Definition and Example (3 marks):**
Acidolysis is the depolymerization of a polyester by a low-molecular-weight **carboxylic acid**, typically at elevated temperature, with or without a strong acid catalyst. The carboxylic acid attacks the ester linkages, breaking them into shorter fragments.

**Example — Acidolysis of PET with acetic acid (2 marks):**

$$[\text{–OCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OCO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO–}]_n + 2n\,\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} n\,\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + n\,\text{CH}_3\text{OCO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOCH}_3$$

The products are **ethylene glycol (EG)** and **dimethyl terephthalate-related oligomers** (or TPA after further hydrolysis). This is a **chemical recycling** route that converts post-consumer PET bottles into monomers that can be re-polymerized into virgin PET.

**Difference from Alcoholysis (2 marks):**
- In **alcoholysis**, the cleaving agent is a **monohydric alcohol** (e.g., methanol, ethanol). The product is typically the corresponding **alkyl ester of the dicarboxylic acid** (e.g., dimethyl terephthalate, DMT, in methanolysis).
- In **acidolysis**, the cleaving agent is a **carboxylic acid** (e.g., acetic acid). The product retains the original alcohol (diol) and the acid introduces new ester end-groups derived from the cleaving acid.
- Alcoholysis is generally **faster** and is catalyzed by **metal transesterification catalysts** (Zn, Mn, Pb acetates), while acidolysis is catalyzed by **Brønsted acids** (H₂SO₄, p-TSA).

---

**(b) Short Notes (7 marks)**

**(i) Role of Acid Concentration (3.5 marks):**

Acid concentration directly affects the rate of acidolysis through the **proton availability** and the **nucleophile concentration**.

- The proton from the acid **protonates the carbonyl oxygen** of the ester bond, activating it toward nucleophilic attack. Higher $[H^+]$ means more activated ester bonds per unit time, increasing the rate.
- The acid itself (in its neutral form) is also a **nucleophile** that attacks the activated carbonyl, directly participating in the cleavage.
- The rate law is **second-order overall** (first-order in $[ester]$ and first-order in $[acid]$) but reduces to **pseudo-first-order** when the acid is in large excess.
- Empirical observation: increasing acid concentration from 5 wt% to 20 wt% can increase the rate constant by **3–5 times**.
- *Caveat:* Excessively high acid concentration can cause **side reactions** like charring or sulfonation (with H₂SO₄), reducing product purity.

**(ii) Effect of Temperature (3.5 marks):**

Temperature has a **strong, exponential** effect on the acidolysis rate, as described by the Arrhenius equation $k = A\,e^{-E_a/RT}$.

- A **10 °C rise** in temperature typically **doubles the rate** (the rule of thumb "Q₁₀ ≈ 2" for many chemical reactions), corresponding to an activation energy of ~50 kJ/mol.
- Higher temperatures also (a) increase the **diffusion rate** of acid into the polymer matrix, (b) increase the **amorphous fraction** of the polymer (melting of crystalline regions), exposing more ester bonds, and (c) shift the equilibrium toward **depolymerization** (positive entropy change for fragmentation of long chains into many small molecules).
- *Practical limit:* Above 260 °C, PET begins to **thermally degrade** (yellowing, chain scission, gas evolution), so industrial acidolysis is typically capped at 250 °C.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Acidolysis Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing acidolysis with hydrolysis** — Acidolysis uses a **carboxylic acid** (RCOOH), NOT water. Writing "H₂O" as the cleaving agent is an **instant 0.5–1 mark deduction** in Section A and 1–2 marks in Section B.
> 
> 2. **Wrong products in the balanced equation** — A frequent error is writing "TPA" as a direct product of acidolysis with acetic acid. The actual direct product is the **diacetate ester of terephthalic acid**; TPA requires a follow-up hydrolysis step.
> 
> 3. **Skipping the catalyst mention** — KTU examiners expect explicit mention of **H₂SO₄** or **p-TSA** as the Brønsted acid catalyst. Omitting this loses 0.5 mark.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting units in the Arrhenius calculation** — Always convert °C to K (add 273.15) and report $E_a$ in **kJ/mol**, not J/mol. Wrong units = 1 mark deduction.
> 
> 5. **Mislabelling mechanism steps** — The intermediate is a **tetrahedral intermediate** (sp³ carbon with four substituents), NOT a "carbocation." A carbocation would imply a different (SN1) mechanism. This distinction is tested.
> 
> 6. **No comparison table in long answers** — KTU board examiners reward well-structured answers. Using a **table or bullet points** for comparing acidolysis with glycolysis/alcoholysis consistently scores higher than prose.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Acidolysis — KTU Module 3**

- **Definition:** Acidolysis = cleavage of polymer ester (or analogous) bonds by a **carboxylic acid** to yield monomers/oligomers.
- **Class:** Chemolytic depolymerization (chemical recycling) route.
- **Reagent:** Carboxylic acid — most commonly **acetic acid (CH₃COOH)**; catalysts: **H₂SO₄, p-TSA, HNO₃**.
- **Substrate of choice:** **PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)** — the most commonly cited polyester in KTU.
- **Mechanism (6 steps):** (1) Diffusion → (2) Protonation of carbonyl O → (3) Nucleophilic attack → (4) Tetrahedral intermediate → (5) Proton transfer → (6) C–O alkyl cleavage + chain scission.
- **Key intermediate:** **Tetrahedral intermediate** (sp³ carbon, not a carbocation).
- **Main products:** **Ethylene glycol (EG)** + **diacetate ester of terephthalic acid** (or TPA after workup).
- **Conditions:** 150–250 °C, 1–5 atm, 1–6 hours, 5–20 wt% acid.
- **Kinetics:** **Pseudo-first-order** in $[ester]$ when acid is in excess; rate = $k_{obs}[E]$.
- **Half-life:** $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k_{obs}$.
- **Arrhenius:** $k = A\,e^{-E_a/RT}$; typical $E_a$ = **60–90 kJ/mol**.
- **Two-point Arrhenius:** $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E_a/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$.
- **Comparison triplet:** Acidolysis ↔ Hydrolysis (uses H₂O) ↔ Alcoholysis (uses ROH) ↔ Glycolysis (uses diol).
- **Mnemonic:** **"HAMS-G"** = Hydrolysis, Acidolysis, Methanolysis, Solvolysis(aminolysis), Glycolysis.
- **Industrial relevance:** Chemical recycling of PET bottles; circular polymer economy.
- **Characterization technique used to monitor progress:** **Intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$ via Mark–Houwink equation** $[\eta] = K M_v^a$ — as $M_v$ decreases, $[\eta]$ decreases, signaling depolymerization.
- **Reaction is exothermic** thermodynamically, but requires high T to overcome kinetic barrier and to mobilize polymer chains.
- **Crystalline vs. amorphous:** Acidolysis begins in **amorphous** regions; crystalline regions resist until ~220–250 °C.
- **By-products:** Sodium acetate (if neutralized with NaOH), unreacted acid (recyclable), wash water.
- **Environmental advantage:** Diverts PET from landfill/incineration; produces monomers usable in **food-grade** re-polymerization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aminolysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Aminolysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Aminolysis — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Aminolysis** is a *chemical (chain-scission) degradation* process in which an **amine** (primary R–NH₂, secondary R₂NH, or even diamines/polyamines) acts as an effective nucleophile and attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of an ester, amide, urethane, or carbonate linkage inside a polymer backbone. The net result is the **cleavage of the polymer chain** and the formation of a new **amide bond** (–CO–NH–) at the chain end, accompanied by the release of the corresponding alcohol (or amine, in the case of aminolysis of an amide).

In the KTU 2024 PCPOT302 syllabus, aminolysis is grouped along with **hydrolysis, alcoholysis, glycolysis, and acidolysis** as one of the four main classes of *solvolytic degradation reactions* used in polymer characterization, surface modification, and chemical recycling.

$$ \text{Polymer–COOR} \; + \; \text{R'\!-\!NH}_{2} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Polymer–CONHR'} \; + \; \text{ROH} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPOT302 – Module 3):**
> Aminolysis is treated as a *solvolytic chain-scission technique* for condensation polymers such as **polyesters (PET, PBT), polyamides (Nylon-6, Nylon-6,6), polycarbonates, and polyurethanes**. Questions in the End Semester Exam (ESE) are commonly asked on the *mechanism, products, and effect of amine structure/nature* on the rate of degradation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a long railway train made of identical **"ester-coaches"** joined by strong metallic couplers (the **ester linkages –COO–**). The train cannot be split easily by normal pushing or pulling.

Now pour a **chemical reagent called "Amine Juice"** on the train. Each amine molecule is shaped like a **"magnetic key"** that is specifically attracted to one type of coupler (the carbonyl group). When the key touches a coupler, it **unhooks one coach** and **attaches itself there as a new "amide-coach"**. After enough amine molecules act, the long polymer train is broken into many **shorter trains (oligomers/monomers)** — and each new short train now carries an **amide end-group** that was not there before.

In one sentence: *Aminolysis = using amines to chemically "snip" ester (or amide) bonds in a polymer, replacing them with amide bonds and releasing small alcohol/amine molecules.*

## 1.3 Why Aminolysis is Important (KTU Industrial Perspective)

- **Chemical Recycling of PET bottles:** Aminolysis with amines like **methylamine, ethylamine, allylamine, or hydrazine** yields **terephthalamides** — useful monomers for high-value polyamides and liquid-crystal polymers.
- **Surface Functionalization of polyester fibres:** Mild aminolysis introduces **–NH₂ and –CONH–** groups on the surface, improving dye uptake, adhesion, biocompatibility, and printability.
- **Polymer Characterization:** End-group analysis via aminolysis is used to estimate the **number-average molecular weight (M̄ₙ)** of condensation polymers.
- **Block / Graft Copolymer Synthesis:** Controlled aminolysis of one block can generate reactive chain-ends that re-polymerize with a different monomer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant / Benchmark Value to Remember (bold):**
> Typical aminolysis of PET is performed at **180 – 220 °C** with excess amine. Reaction times vary from **30 min to 8 h** depending on the amine structure. The **activation energy (Eₐ)** for PET aminolysis by methylamine is reported in the range of **80 – 110 kJ mol⁻¹**, significantly lower than that of pure thermal depolymerization (~ 200 kJ mol⁻¹).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Aminolysis — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Scope of Aminolysis — Which Polymers Are Susceptible?

Aminolysis is feasible wherever the polymer backbone contains an **electrophilic carbonyl carbon** adjacent to a good leaving group:

| Polymer Family | Cleavable Linkage | Amine Reacts with | Small Molecule Released |
|---|---|---|---|
| Polyester (PET, PBT, PEN) | –CO–O– | Ester carbonyl | Alcohol / diol (e.g., EG) |
| Polyamide (Nylon-6, Nylon-6,6) | –CO–NH– | Amide carbonyl | Free amine (R–NH₂) |
| Polycarbonate (PC, Bisphenol-A type) | –O–CO–O– | Carbonate carbonyl | Phenol / bisphenol |
| Polyurethane (PU) | –O–CO–NH– | Urethane carbonyl | Alcohol + free amine |
| Poly(ethylene naphthalate) PEN | –CO–O– | Ester carbonyl | Ethylene glycol |

## 2.2 Operational Concept — The Solvolytic Logic (Step-by-Step Logic)

Aminolysis belongs to the broad family of **nucleophilic acyl substitution (NAS)** reactions. The amine nitrogen supplies the electron pair, the carbonyl carbon accepts it, and the alkoxy/amide fragment departs as a leaving group. Logically, every aminolysis follows this **five-stage operational sequence**:

1. **Stage 1 — Diffusion:** Amine molecules diffuse from the bulk solution into the **amorphous regions** of the semi-crystalline polymer (crystalline regions react much more slowly because of tight chain packing).
2. **Stage 2 — Nucleophilic Attack:** Lone pair on the amine nitrogen attacks the **sp² carbonyl carbon** of the ester/amide, generating a zwitterionic tetrahedral intermediate.
3. **Stage 3 — Proton Transfer:** A proton shuttle (often a second amine molecule or solvent) moves a proton from the N⁺H₃ onto the alkoxide oxygen, producing a neutral tetrahedral intermediate.
4. **Stage 4 — Leaving-Group Departure:** The C–OR (or C–NHR) bond breaks, expelling **RO⁻ (or RNH⁻)** and forming a new **C–NH bond (amide)**.
5. **Stage 5 — Chain Scission & Diffusion Out:** The released alcohol/amine diffuses out, exposing fresh carbonyls. Each cut event reduces the polymer chain length and **increases the number of –NH₂ / –CONHR end-groups** by exactly one.

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Stage (Accessible Explanation)

- **Why does the amine attack the carbonyl?** Because the C=O carbon carries a large partial positive charge (δ⁺) due to oxygen's higher electronegativity — it is an *electrophilic hotspot*.
- **Why is the tetrahedral intermediate formed?** sp² → sp³ rehybridization is required to temporarily accommodate four substituents.
- **Why does a good leaving group depart easily?** Alkoxides (RO⁻) are stabilized by the carbonyl π-system that re-forms (resonance-stabilized) once the leaving group leaves.
- **Why does the chain break?** A single ester (or amide) bond **between two repeat units** IS the connection — breaking it *is* chain scission.

## 2.4 Factors Affecting the Rate of Aminolysis (KTU High-Yield)

| Factor | Effect on Rate | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Nucleophilicity of amine** | Higher nucleophilicity → faster rate | Stronger N lone-pair donates more electron density |
| **Steric hindrance on amine** | Bulky amine → slower rate | Blocks approach to carbonyl |
| **Basicity vs. nucleophilicity** | In protic solvents, smaller amines (MeNH₂, EtNH₂) react faster | Less steric shielding |
| **Temperature** | Higher T → exponentially faster (Arrhenius) | Overcomes Eₐ barrier |
| **Catalyst (metal alkoxides, TBD, DBU)** | Accelerates aminolysis by 5–50× | Activates carbonyl / deprotonates amine |
| **Crystallinity of polymer** | Higher crystallinity → slower | Amine cannot penetrate crystallites easily |
| **Swelling / solvent** | Better swelling → faster | Enhances diffusion into amorphous phase |
| **Molar ratio of amine : repeat unit** | Excess amine shifts equilibrium forward | Le Chatelier's principle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Aminolysis vs. Hydrolysis — a subtle but FAVOURITE KTU question:**
> Hydrolysis uses H₂O (a weak nucleophile) and needs a catalyst (acid / base) and high T. Aminolysis uses a *stronger nucleophile* (R–NH₂), so it proceeds **faster and under milder conditions** than hydrolysis for the same polymer.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Equations + Units)

| Quantity / Equation | Mathematical Form | Units / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Aminolysis stoichiometry (PET)** | PET unit + 2 R–NH₂ → R–NH–CO–C₆H₄–CO–NH–R + HOCH₂CH₂OH | mol : mol : mol : mol |
| **Pseudo-first-order rate law** | $r = k \, [\text{ester}] \, [\text{amine}]^{n}$ | $k$ in L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹ or s⁻¹ |
| **Arrhenius equation** | $k = A \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a}}{RT}\right)$ | $E_{a}$ in kJ mol⁻¹; $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ |
| **Degree of polymerization after scission** | $\dfrac{1}{\overline{X}_{n,t}} = \dfrac{1}{\overline{X}_{n,0}} + \nu_{\text{cut}} \cdot t$ | dimensionless |
| **Number of cuts per chain** | $\nu_{\text{cut}} = \dfrac{\overline{M}_{n,0}}{\overline{M}_{n,t}} - 1$ | dimensionless |
| **Activation energy (PET + MeNH₂)** | $E_{a} \approx 80 - 110$ | **kJ mol⁻¹** |
| **Amine basicity reference (pKₐ of conjugate acid)** | MeNH₂ : 10.66 ; EtNH₂ : 10.81 ; PhNH₂ : 4.60 | pKₐ units |
| **Gas constant (KEEP BOLD)** | **R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** | SI |
| **Carboxylic-acid + amine equilibrium** | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ | J mol⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** The Arrhenius expression lets a chemical engineer predict *how long* PET bottles must be held in an aminolysis reactor at 200 °C to reach a target M̄ₙ — critical for **industrial chemical recycling plants** that re-claim terephthalamide monomers for re-polymerization into virgin-grade polyester.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Aminolysis — Section 3: Step-by-Step Mechanism & Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Detailed Mechanism of Aminolysis of a Polyester (Generic R–CO–OR')

The mechanism is a textbook **nucleophilic acyl substitution (B_{AC}2 pathway for bases / amines)**. Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step** electronic pathway that you must be able to draw in the KTU ESE.

### Step 1 — Nucleophilic Attack on the Carbonyl Carbon

The lone pair on the **nitrogen atom of the amine (R'–NH₂)** is directed toward the **δ⁺ carbonyl carbon** of the ester. A new **C–N σ bond** forms, while the π electrons of C=O shift onto the oxygen, generating a **zwitterionic tetrahedral intermediate** (negative on oxygen, positive on nitrogen).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R–C(=O)–OR''} \; + \; \text{R'\!-\!NH}_{2} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–C(O}^{-}\text{)(OR'')(N}^{+}\text{H}_{2}\text{R')} \\
\text{(sp}^{2}\text{ carbonyl)} &\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(sp}^{3}\text{ tetrahedral intermediate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Why this happens:* The C=O carbon is electrophilic because oxygen pulls the π electrons towards itself, leaving δ⁺ on C. The nitrogen lone pair is high in energy and readily donated, especially when the nitrogen is *not* too sterically hindered.

### Step 2 — Proton Transfer (Acid–Base Tautomerism)

A second amine molecule (acting as a Brønsted base) **abstracts one of the N–H protons** from the protonated nitrogen and delivers it onto the alkoxide oxygen. The result is a **neutral tetrahedral intermediate**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R–C(O}^{-}\text{)(OR'')(N}^{+}\text{H}_{2}\text{R')} \; + \; \text{R'\!-\!NH}_{2} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–C(OH)(OR'')(NHR')} \; + \; \text{R'\!-\!NH}_{3}^{+} \\
\text{(zwitterion)} &\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(neutral intermediate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Why this happens:* The neutral form is thermodynamically more stable than the zwitterion; the proton transfer is fast and is *not* rate-determining in most aminolyses.

### Step 3 — Collapse of the Tetrahedral Intermediate

The lone pair on the **alkoxide oxygen** re-forms the C=O π bond, **expelling the –OR'' group** as a leaving group (an alkoxide, R''O⁻). A new **C=O bond is regenerated** and the **C–OR'' bond breaks**, leaving a *protonated amide*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R–C(OH)(OR'')(NHR')} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–C(=O)–NHR'} \; + \; \text{R''OH} \\
\text{(tetrahedral intermediate)} &\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(protonated amide) + alcohol}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Why this happens:* Re-formation of the strong C=O π bond (≈ 745 kJ mol⁻¹) provides the thermodynamic driving force; the alkoxide is a *tolerable* leaving group because the carbonyl stabilizes the developing negative charge by resonance as it departs.

### Step 4 — Deprotonation of the Amide

A final proton transfer (again catalysed by free amine) removes the remaining N–H proton, giving the **neutral amide product**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R–C(=O)–N}^{+}\text{H}_{2}\text{R'} \; + \; \text{R'\!-\!NH}_{2} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–C(=O)–NHR'} \; + \; \text{R'\!-\!NH}_{3}^{+} \\
\text{(protonated amide)} &\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(free amide)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The net stoichiometry is exactly the reaction shown in Section 1.1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Overall Bond-Energy Logic (KTU-style explanation):**
> Bonds broken in one repeat unit: 1 × C=O π (≈ 375 kJ mol⁻¹) + 1 × C–OR (≈ 340 kJ mol⁻¹) ≈ 715 kJ mol⁻¹.
> Bonds formed: 1 × C=O π (≈ 375 kJ mol⁻¹) + 1 × C–N (≈ 305 kJ mol⁻¹) + 1 × O–H (≈ 460 kJ mol⁻¹) ≈ 1140 kJ mol⁻¹.
> **Net ΔH ≈ – 425 kJ mol⁻¹** → strongly exothermic, hence aminolysis is *thermodynamically favourable* and goes essentially to completion when the alcohol by-product is removed.

## 3.2 Worked Derivations for PET Aminolysis (Model Polymer)

### 3.2.1 Stoichiometric Mass Balance

**Problem:** 100 g of PET (repeat unit = C₁₀H₈O₄, M₀ = 192 g mol⁻¹) is reacted with excess methylamine (CH₃NH₂, M = 31 g mol⁻¹). Write the balanced reaction and find the theoretical mass of **N,N′-dimethylterephthalamide (DMTAm, C₁₀H₁₂N₂O₂, M = 208 g mol⁻¹)** recovered.

**Balanced reaction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{[–O–CH}_{2}\text{CH}_{2}\text{–O–CO–C}_{6}\text{H}_{4}\text{–CO–]}_{n} \; + \; 2n\,\text{CH}_{3}\text{NH}_{2} &\;\longrightarrow\; n\,\text{CH}_{3}\text{NH–CO–C}_{6}\text{H}_{4}\text{–CO–NHCH}_{3} \; + \; n\,\text{HOCH}_{2}\text{CH}_{2}\text{OH} \\
\text{PET repeat unit} &\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{DMTAm (target)} \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{ethylene glycol (by-product)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Moles of PET repeat units:**

$$
n_{\text{PET}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{PET}}}{M_{0}} = \dfrac{100 \text{ g}}{192 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.5208 \text{ mol}
$$

**Moles of DMTAm produced (1 : 1 stoichiometry with repeat unit):**

$$
n_{\text{DMTAm}} = n_{\text{PET}} = 0.5208 \text{ mol}
$$

**Theoretical mass of DMTAm:**

$$
m_{\text{DMTAm}} = n_{\text{DMTAm}} \times M_{\text{DMTAm}} = 0.5208 \times 208 = 108.33 \text{ g}
$$

If the industrial yield is 92 %, the actual recovered mass = **108.33 × 0.92 = 99.66 g**.

### 3.2.2 Kinetics — Time Required to Reach a Target M̄ₙ

A PET sample with initial M̄ₙ,₀ = 30 000 g mol⁻¹ is subjected to aminolysis at 200 °C. The pseudo-first-order rate constant (excess amine) is k = 1.2 × 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹. Estimate the time required to reduce M̄ₙ to 6 000 g mol⁻¹.

For random chain scission (most common aminolysis model), the number of chain scissions per original chain at time t is:

$$
\nu_{\text{cut}} \;=\; \dfrac{\overline{M}_{n,0}}{\overline{M}_{n,t}} \;-\; 1 \;=\; \dfrac{30\,000}{6\,000} - 1 \;=\; 5 - 1 \;=\; 4 \text{ cuts per original chain}
$$

The pseudo-first-order integrated form for aminolysis (assuming each cut is independent and amine is in large excess) is:

$$
\nu_{\text{cut}}(t) \;=\; k_{\text{cut}} \, t
$$

where k_cut is the *cut-rate constant* (≈ k, the chemical rate constant, in random scission limit). Therefore:

$$
t = \dfrac{\nu_{\text{cut}}}{k_{\text{cut}}} = \dfrac{4}{1.2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s}^{-1}} = 3.33 \times 10^{4} \text{ s} \approx 9.26 \text{ h}
$$

### 3.2.3 Arrhenius Estimate of Eₐ from Two Temperatures

Suppose k₁ = 2.0 × 10⁻⁵ s⁻¹ at T₁ = 180 °C = 453 K, and k₂ = 1.4 × 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ at T₂ = 210 °C = 483 K. Find Eₐ.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right) &= -\dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right) \\
\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}} &= \dfrac{1.4 \times 10^{-4}}{2.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.0 \\
\ln(7.0) &= 1.9459 \\
\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}} &= \dfrac{1}{483} - \dfrac{1}{453} = 2.0704 \times 10^{-3} - 2.2079 \times 10^{-3} = -1.375 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1} \\
E_{a} &= -\dfrac{R \cdot \ln(k_{2}/k_{1})}{(1/T_{2}) - (1/T_{1})} = -\dfrac{8.314 \times 1.9459}{-1.375 \times 10^{-4}} \\
E_{a} &= \dfrac{16.171}{1.375 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.176 \times 10^{5} \text{ J mol}^{-1} = \mathbf{117.6 \; kJ \, mol^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This sits inside the 80 – 120 kJ mol⁻¹ band reported in literature, validating the calculation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Aminolysis — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Process Map of PET Aminolysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start: PET pellets + R-NH2 in autoclave])
    A1[Heat to 180-220 deg C with stirring]
    A2[Amine diffuses into amorphous regions]
    A3{Nucleophilic attack on ester carbonyl?}
    A4[Tetrahedral intermediate forms]
    A5[Proton transfer via second amine]
    A6[Leaving group -OR departs]
    A7[New C-N amide bond formed]
    A8[Chain scission event recorded]
    A9{All ester links cleaved?}
    A10[Cool reactor and remove excess amine]
    A11[Filter and wash terephthalamide crystals]
    A12[Purify by recrystallization]
    A13[End: Pure N,N'-dialkyl terephthalamide]
    A14[Side stream: Ethylene glycol recovery]

    A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3
    A3 -- yes --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9
    A9 -- no --> A2
    A9 -- yes --> A10 --> A11 --> A12 --> A13
    A1 -. by-product .-> A14
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Mechanism Block Diagram — Five Reaction Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Ester carbonyl sp2]
    B2[Zwitterionic tetrahedral intermediate]
    B3[Neutral tetrahedral intermediate]
    B4[Protonated amide + alkoxide]
    B5[Neutral amide + alcohol R-OH]

    B1 -- Step 1 nucleophilic attack by R-NH2 --> B2
    B2 -- Step 2 proton transfer by 2nd R-NH2 --> B3
    B3 -- Step 3 collapse of intermediate --> B4
    B4 -- Step 4 deprotonation by R-NH2 --> B5

    C1[Inputs: Polymer-COOR and R-NH2]
    C2[Outputs: Polymer-CONHR and ROH]

    C1 --> B1
    B5 --> C2
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Aminolysis — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024, Module 3]**
**Define aminolysis. With a suitable example, write the general reaction scheme for aminolysis of a polyester.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Aminolysis is a *solvolytic chain-scission degradation* of a polymer in which an **amine (R–NH₂)** attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of an ester (or amide) bond in the polymer backbone, resulting in cleavage of the chain and formation of a new amide bond. An alcohol (or amine) is liberated as a by-product.

**Example — Aminolysis of PET with methylamine:**

$$
\text{[–O–CH}_{2}\text{CH}_{2}\text{–O–CO–C}_{6}\text{H}_{4}\text{–CO–]}_{n} \; + \; 2n\,\text{CH}_{3}\text{NH}_{2} \;\longrightarrow\; n\,\text{CH}_{3}\text{NH–CO–C}_{6}\text{H}_{4}\text{–CO–NHCH}_{3} \; + \; n\,\text{HOCH}_{2}\text{CH}_{2}\text{OH}
$$

Products: *N,N′-dimethylterephthalamide* and *ethylene glycol*. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for general scheme, 1 mark for example with products.)

---

### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**List four factors that influence the rate of aminolysis of a polyester. Which type of amine reacts faster — a primary aliphatic or a primary aromatic amine? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Four factors affecting aminolysis rate:
1. **Nucleophilicity / basicity** of the amine (higher → faster).
2. **Steric hindrance** at the nitrogen (lower → faster).
3. **Reaction temperature** (higher → faster, Arrhenius behaviour).
4. **Crystallinity of polymer** and **diffusion / swelling** by the solvent (lower crystallinity & better swelling → faster).

**Primary aliphatic amines (e.g., CH₃NH₂, C₂H₅NH₂) react faster than primary aromatic amines (e.g., C₆H₅NH₂).** Justification: in aniline, the nitrogen lone pair is delocalized into the benzene ring, making it a *weaker nucleophile* and *weaker base* (pKₐ of conjugate acid = 4.60) compared with methylamine (pKₐ = 10.66). Hence the lone pair is less available to attack the carbonyl carbon. (2 marks for factors list, 1 mark for comparison + justification.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 marks each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024, Module 3, Q.4 (a)(b)]**

#### (a) **Discuss the mechanism of aminolysis of a polyester with a primary amine. Illustrate all the intermediate steps with electron flow arrows.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The aminolysis of an ester linkage (R–CO–OR') by a primary amine R''–NH₂ follows a *nucleophilic acyl substitution (B_AC2)* pathway consisting of four elementary steps.

**Step 1 — Nucleophilic attack:**
The lone pair on the nitrogen of R''–NH₂ attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of the ester. The C=O π electrons shift onto oxygen, producing a *zwitterionic tetrahedral intermediate* with N⁺H₂ and O⁻.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R–C(=O)–OR'} + \text{R''–NH}_{2} &\longrightarrow \text{R–C(O}^{-}\text{)(OR')(N}^{+}\text{H}_{2}\text{R'')} \\
\text{(sp}^{2}\text{)} &\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad \text{(sp}^{3}\text{ zwitterion)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Proton transfer:**
A second molecule of R''–NH₂ acts as a Brønsted base, abstracting one N–H proton and depositing it on the O⁻, forming a *neutral tetrahedral intermediate*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R–C(O}^{-}\text{)(OR')(N}^{+}\text{H}_{2}\text{R'')} + \text{R''–NH}_{2} &\longrightarrow \text{R–C(OH)(OR')(NHR'')} + \text{R''–NH}_{3}^{+}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Collapse of the intermediate:**
The lone pair on oxygen re-forms the C=O π bond; the C–OR' bond breaks heterolytically, expelling R'OH (alcohol) and yielding a *protonated amide*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R–C(OH)(OR')(NHR'')} &\longrightarrow \text{R–C(=O)–N}^{+}\text{H}_{2}\text{R''} + \text{R'OH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Deprotonation:**
A second R''–NH₂ molecule removes the remaining N–H proton, giving the *neutral amide product* and an ammonium ion.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R–C(=O)–N}^{+}\text{H}_{2}\text{R''} + \text{R''–NH}_{2} &\longrightarrow \text{R–C(=O)–NHR''} + \text{R''–NH}_{3}^{+}
\end{aligned}
$$

The net effect is **ester → amide** conversion, with the **polymer chain being cleaved** between the carbonyl carbon and the original alkoxy oxygen. (Each of the four steps carries 1.5 marks; 1 mark reserved for the final overall net equation and 1 mark for the *why* of the mechanism — i.e., the thermodynamic drive from re-forming the strong C=O π bond.)

---

#### (b) **A PET sample of initial number-average molecular weight 24 000 g mol⁻¹ is subjected to aminolysis with excess ethylamine at 190 °C. The pseudo-first-order cut rate constant is 8.0 × 10⁻⁵ s⁻¹. Calculate the time required to reduce M̄ₙ to 4 000 g mol⁻¹. Also estimate Eₐ if the same reaction at 220 °C gives k = 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Time required (3 marks):**

Number of cuts per original chain:

$$
\nu_{\text{cut}} = \dfrac{\overline{M}_{n,0}}{\overline{M}_{n,t}} - 1 = \dfrac{24\,000}{4\,000} - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5
$$

For pseudo-first-order aminolysis with excess amine:

$$
t = \dfrac{\nu_{\text{cut}}}{k_{\text{cut}}} = \dfrac{5}{8.0 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{s}^{-1}} = 6.25 \times 10^{4} \text{ s} \approx \mathbf{17.36 \; h}
$$

**Part (ii) — Activation energy (4 marks):**

Use the two-temperature Arrhenius equation:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right) = -\dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right)
$$

Given T₁ = 190 °C = 463 K, k₁ = 8.0 × 10⁻⁵ s⁻¹; T₂ = 220 °C = 493 K, k₂ = 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}} &= \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{-4}}{8.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 5.0, \qquad \ln(5.0) = 1.609 \\
\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}} &= \dfrac{1}{493} - \dfrac{1}{463} = 2.0284 \times 10^{-3} - 2.1598 \times 10^{-3} = -1.314 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1} \\
E_{a} &= -\dfrac{R \cdot \ln(k_{2}/k_{1})}{(1/T_{2}) - (1/T_{1})} = -\dfrac{8.314 \times 1.609}{-1.314 \times 10^{-4}} \\
E_{a} &= \dfrac{13.378}{1.314 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.018 \times 10^{5} \text{ J mol}^{-1} = \mathbf{101.8 \; kJ \, mol^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This value is consistent with the literature range of **80 – 120 kJ mol⁻¹** for aminolysis of PET.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2023, Module 3, Q.4 (a)(b)]**

#### (a) **Explain the role of aminolysis in (i) chemical recycling of PET, and (ii) surface modification of polyester fibres. Write the chemical reactions involved.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Chemical Recycling of PET (3.5 marks):**
PET aminolysis uses primary amines (methylamine, ethylamine, allylamine, hydrazine, ethanolamine) to depolymerize post-consumer PET bottles into **N,N′-dialkyl terephthalamide monomers** that can be repolymerized or converted to other value-added products. The reaction is typically carried out in an **autoclave at 180 – 220 °C** for 2 – 8 h with a large excess of amine.

$$
\text{[–O–CH}_{2}\text{CH}_{2}\text{–O–CO–C}_{6}\text{H}_{4}\text{–CO–]}_{n} + 2n\,\text{R–NH}_{2} \longrightarrow n\,\text{R–NH–CO–C}_{6}\text{H}_{4}\text{–CO–NH–R} + n\,\text{HOCH}_{2}\text{CH}_{2}\text{OH}
$$

The terephthalamide product can be hydrolysed to pure terephthalic acid (TPA) for repolymerization with ethylene glycol to make virgin PET, or used directly to synthesize **high-performance polyamides and liquid-crystal polymers**. Ethylene glycol recovered as a by-product is also re-used. Advantages: **mild conditions, high purity monomers, low energy** compared to glycolysis or methanolysis in some cases.

**(ii) Surface Modification of Polyester Fibres (3.5 marks):**
Polyester (PET) fibres are hydrophobic and difficult to dye. Controlled (mild) aminolysis with dilute aqueous amines (e.g., ethylamine, ethanolamine) at **room temperature to 60 °C** introduces **–NH₂ and –CONHR** groups on the fibre surface. These polar groups:

- Increase **surface energy and wettability**.
- Provide **dye sites for anionic / reactive dyes**, improving colour depth and wash-fastness.
- Improve **adhesion to rubber, coatings, and biological molecules** (used in medical textiles).

Surface reaction (controlled, partial):

$$
\text{–[PET]–CO–O–CH}_{2}\text{CH}_{2}\text{– + H}_{2}\text{N–CH}_{2}\text{CH}_{2}\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{–[PET]–CO–NH–CH}_{2}\text{CH}_{2}\text{OH + HOCH}_{2}\text{CH}_{2}\text{–}
$$

Note: Reaction conditions are chosen so that only the **outer few nanometres** of the fibre are attacked — the bulk mechanical properties (tenacity, modulus) of the fibre remain essentially unchanged. (Marks: 2 for the chemistry of recycling, 1.5 for its advantages; 2 for surface modification chemistry, 1.5 for the property improvements.)

---

#### (b) **Compare aminolysis with hydrolysis and alcoholysis of polyesters. Discuss how the nature of the amine (primary aliphatic vs. primary aromatic) affects the rate of aminolysis with proper justification.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Hydrolysis | Alcoholysis | Aminolysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nucleophile | H₂O (weak) | R–OH (moderate) | R–NH₂ (strong) |
| Catalyst | Acid / base required | Acid catalyst often needed | Usually no catalyst; base helps |
| Temperature | 200 – 250 °C | 150 – 220 °C | **100 – 220 °C** (mildest) |
| Rate | Slowest | Intermediate | **Fastest** |
| By-product | H₂O (reversible) | R–OH (must be removed) | R–OH + R–NH₃⁺ salt (drives rxn) |
| Industrial use | Not preferred for PET recycling | Methanolysis for PET → DMT | Recycling of PET, surface functionalization |

**Effect of Amine Nature on Rate (3.5 marks):**

Rate order: **primary aliphatic > secondary aliphatic > primary aromatic > secondary aromatic >> tertiary aromatic**.

- **Primary aliphatic amines (CH₃NH₂, C₂H₅NH₂, n-PrNH₂):** The N lone pair is *localized* on nitrogen (no resonance with the alkyl group). High basicity (pKₐ ≈ 10.6 – 10.8) and high nucleophilicity. Steric hindrance is minimal. → **Fastest aminolysis.**

- **Primary aromatic amines (C₆H₅NH₂):** The N lone pair is *delocalized into the aromatic ring* by resonance, drastically reducing both basicity (pKₐ = 4.60) and nucleophilicity. → **Slowest among common amines.**

- **Secondary amines ((CH₃)₂NH):** Slightly less reactive than primary because of steric bulk, but still good nucleophiles (pKₐ ≈ 10.7).

- **Tertiary amines ((CH₃)₃N):** Cannot form a stable tetrahedral amide product — they act as **catalysts/base**, not as aminolysis reagents.

**Quantitative Justification (1 mark):** Hammett-type correlations confirm that electron-donating substituents on aniline (e.g., –CH₃, –OCH₃) *increase* the rate, while electron-withdrawing groups (–NO₂, –CN) *decrease* the rate, supporting the role of nitrogen lone-pair availability in the rate-determining nucleophilic-attack step.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks on aminolysis questions:**
> 1. **Confusing aminolysis with ammonolysis** — Ammonolysis uses *NH₃* (forms –CONH₂), aminolysis uses *R–NH₂* or *R₂NH* (forms –CONHR). Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting the second amine molecule's role** in the proton-transfer step — the B_AC2 mechanism needs a *base* (often the amine itself) to shuttle protons. Students who show only Step 1 and Step 3 (skipping Step 2) lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Writing the wrong product of PET + methylamine** — many students write *terephthalic acid + ethanolamine* (this is hydrolysis/amidation, **NOT** aminolysis). Correct product is *N,N′-dimethylterephthalamide + ethylene glycol*.
> 4. **Unit mistakes in Arrhenius calculations** — if Eₐ is to be expressed in kJ mol⁻¹, you MUST divide the J mol⁻¹ result by 1000. A common slip costs 1 mark.
> 5. **In the surface-modification question, failing to mention that only the OUTER surface is attacked** — full marks require clarifying that mild conditions are used to preserve bulk mechanical properties.
> 6. **Forgetting the gas constant R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** in calculations — examiners expect this constant to be stated explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Aminolysis = nucleophilic acyl substitution** of a polymer's ester / amide / urethane / carbonate bond by an amine.
- The **net transformation** is *–COOR → –CONHR'*, releasing R–OH.
- A **zwitterionic tetrahedral intermediate** is the key mechanistic signature.
- For **PET + R–NH₂** → **N,N′-dialkyl terephthalamide + ethylene glycol** — this is the KTU-favourite example.
- **Primary aliphatic amines** are the fastest reagents; **aromatic amines** are slow because the N lone pair is delocalized into the ring.
- **Crystallinity lowers** the rate — only amorphous regions react fast.
- **Excess amine + high temperature** drive the reaction to completion (Le Chatelier).
- **Activation energy Eₐ ≈ 80 – 120 kJ mol⁻¹** for PET aminolysis — much lower than thermal pyrolysis.
- **Use Arrhenius equation** $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ with **R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** to predict reactor times.
- **Number of cuts per chain** $ \nu_{\text{cut}} = \dfrac{\overline{M}_{n,0}}{\overline{M}_{n,t}} - 1 = k_{\text{cut}} \cdot t $ (random scission model).
- Applications: **chemical recycling, surface modification, end-group analysis, copolymer synthesis.**
- Aminolysis is **milder and faster** than hydrolysis or alcoholysis because amines are stronger nucleophiles than H₂O or ROH.
- A second amine molecule is **always** required as a **proton shuttle** in the B_AC2 mechanism — do not omit Step 2.
- Catalyst options for difficult systems: **TBD, DBU, metal alkoxides (Ti(OBu)₄), ionic liquids.**
- Industrial recycling of PET via aminolysis is sometimes called the **"Terephthalamide route"** and yields monomers of high purity (≥ 99 %).
- Aminolysis of **polyamides** is *very slow* (amide C–N bond is already an amide — leaving group is RNH⁻, a poor one). This is why aminolysis is best suited for **polyesters and polycarbonates**, not polyamides.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
