topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydrogenation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hydrogenation** is a chemical addition reaction in which molecular hydrogen (H₂) is added across an unsaturated bond (most commonly a carbon–carbon double bond, C=C) of a substrate, converting it into a saturated single bond (C–C). In the context of **Polymer Science (PCPOT302)**, hydrogenation refers specifically to the catalytic saturation of residual C=C unsaturation present in the backbone or side groups of polymers (and polymer precursors) using H₂ gas, typically in the presence of a transition-metal catalyst.

The generalized stoichiometry for a single olefinic unit in a polymer is:

$$\text{[\,-CH=CH-\,]}_{n} \;+\; n\,H_{2} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst}}\; \text{[\,-CH_{2}\text{-}CH_{2}-\,]}_{n}$$

**Industrially hydrogenated polymers include:**
- **Hydrogenated Nitrile Butadiene Rubber (HNBR)** from NBR
- **Hydrogenated Styrene-Butadiene Rubber (HSBR / SEBS precursors)** from SBR/SBS
- **Hydrogenated Polybutadiene (HPBD)** from 1,2- and 1,4-polybutadiene
- **Hydrogenated Natural Rubber (HNR)** from cis-1,4-polyisoprene
- **Hydrogenated DCPD resins** from polydicyclopentadiene

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hydrogenation of polymers is a *post-polymerization modification* technique. It is **not** polymerization. The molecular weight distribution is essentially preserved; only the microstructural (microstructural) saturation level changes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **roller-coaster seat** on a polymer chain. The C=C double bond is a "seat" that is technically a "two-seater" — but only one electron-pair is holding things together, leaving the bond highly *reactive and unstable*. 

Now, **molecular hydrogen (H₂)** is like a pair of passengers looking for a seat. When the **catalyst** (the ticket-checker at the gate) admits them, each H atom slots into one side of the double bond, converting the unstable C=C into a calm, saturated C–C single bond. The chain becomes:
- More **thermally stable** (no double bonds to break easily)
- More **chemically resistant** (ozone, acids, oxidative attack)
- More **flexible** (single bonds rotate freely)
- More **UV-resistant** (no π-bonds to absorb UV)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Memory):**
> - Bond dissociation energy of H–H = **436 kJ/mol**
> - Bond dissociation energy of C=C = **611 kJ/mol** (approx.)
> - Bond dissociation energy of C–C = **347 kJ/mol**
> - Activation energy barrier (uncatalyzed) ≈ **150–200 kJ/mol** → catalyst lowers this to **40–80 kJ/mol**
> - Standard operating H₂ pressure range: **1–200 bar**
> - Standard temperature range: **40–180 °C**

## 1.3 Why Hydrogenate a Polymer? — The Engineering "Why"

| Property | Before Hydrogenation (Unsaturated) | After Hydrogenation (Saturated) |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal stability | Degrades ~200 °C | Stable up to **>450 °C** (HNBR) |
| Ozone resistance | Poor (cracks rapidly) | Excellent |
| Oil/swell resistance | Moderate | Significantly improved |
| UV aging | Yellows, embrittles | High resistance |
| Glass transition (Tg) | Lower | Slightly **higher** (~5–15 °C shift) |
| Mechanical strength | Moderate | Retained or improved |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hydrogenation Processes (in Polymer Context)

Hydrogenation is classified by **three engineering axes**:

### A. By Catalyst Phase
1. **Heterogeneous Hydrogenation** — Catalyst is a solid (Pd/C, Raney Ni, PtO₂); polymer substrate is in solution or melt.
2. **Homogeneous Hydrogenation** — Catalyst is soluble (Wilkinson's Rh complex, Crabtree's Ir complex); superior selectivity.
3. **Transfer Hydrogenation** — H₂ is supplied by a donor molecule (e.g., formic acid, hydrazine) instead of gaseous H₂.

### B. By Reaction Medium
1. **Solution Hydrogenation** — Polymer dissolved in solvent (e.g., xylene, cyclohexane, MEK).
2. **Melt Hydrogenation** — Polymer in molten state, no solvent (energy efficient, harder to control).
3. **Latex/Aqueous Hydrogenation** — Performed directly on polymer latex emulsion.

### C. By Degree of Hydrogenation
1. **Partial Hydrogenation** — Only some C=C bonds reduced (e.g., SBS → SEBS, where 1,2-vinyl units remain).
2. **Full (Quantitative) Hydrogenation** — >99% of C=C bonds saturated (e.g., NBR → HNBR).

## 2.2 The Operational Logic — Stepwise Mechanism (Heterogeneous Catalytic)

Heterogeneous catalytic hydrogenation follows the **Horiuti–Polanyi mechanism** (1934), which is the KTU-mandated mechanism for this module:

**Step 1 — Diffusion:** H₂ molecules dissolve in the polymer solution and diffuse to the catalyst surface.

**Step 2 — Dissociative Chemisorption:** H₂ adsorbs onto the metal surface and splits into two surface-bound H atoms (M–H bonds). This is the *rate-determining step* in many industrial systems.

**Step 3 — π-Complexation:** The polymer's C=C double bond approaches the metal surface and forms a π-complex (M···C=C).

**Step 4 — Migratory Insertion:** One surface H atom adds to one carbon of the C=C, forming a σ-alkyl metal intermediate (M–C–C–H).

**Step 5 — Reductive Elimination:** The second H atom transfers to the other carbon, releasing the now-saturated C–C bond and freeing the metal site for the next cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently test the *rate-determining step* and the *role of catalyst surface area*. Always mention that **catalyst poisoning** (by S, CO, halides) shuts down the cycle by blocking M–H sites.

## 2.3 Critical Process Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Range | Engineering Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature (T) | **40 – 180 °C** | ↑ T → ↑ rate, but ↑ risk of polymer degradation |
| H₂ Pressure (P_H₂) | **1 – 200 bar** | ↑ P → linear ↑ in dissolved [H₂] → ↑ rate |
| Catalyst Loading | **0.05 – 5 wt%** (of polymer) | ↑ loading → ↑ rate, but cost ↑ |
| Agitation/RPM | **200 – 1500 rpm** | Affects H₂ mass transfer (gas–liquid) |
| Reaction Time | **2 – 12 hours** | Determines final HD% |
| Solvent Type | Xylene, cyclohexane, MEK | Affects polymer swelling & H₂ solubility |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in ~70% of ESE questions on this module.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{HD\%} \;=\; \dfrac{IV_{0} \;-\; IV_{t}}{IV_{0}} \;\times\; 100$ | Degree of Hydrogenation from Iodine Value | dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $IV \;=\; \dfrac{126.91 \;\times\; V_{IB} \;\times\; N_{IB}}{m_{\text{sample}}}$ | Iodine Value (Wijs method) | g I₂ / 100 g sample |
| 3 | $n_{H_{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{polymer}} \;\times\; IV_{0}}{253.82}$ | Moles of H₂ needed to fully hydrogenate (per 100 g polymer basis) | mol |
| 4 | $V_{H_{2},\,STP} \;=\; n_{H_{2}} \;\times\; 22.414$ | Volume of H₂ at STP for full hydrogenation | L |
| 5 | $-\dfrac{d[C=C]}{dt} \;=\; k\,\text{[Cat]}\,\text{[H}_{2}\text{]}\,\text{[C=C]}$ | Rate law (heterogeneous, H₂-saturated regime) | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $k \;=\; A\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a}}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius dependence of rate constant | s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $[H_{2}]_{\text{dissolved}} \;=\; k_{H}\,\text{P}_{H_{2}}$ | Henry's Law for dissolved H₂ | mol·L⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \;\approx\; -120 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Enthalpy of C=C hydrogenation | kJ/mol |
| 9 | $\text{SE} \;=\; \dfrac{n_{H_{2},\text{consumed}}}{n_{H_{2},\text{fed}}} \;\times\; 100$ | Stoichiometric Efficiency (process economics) | % |
| 10 | $\text{TOF} \;=\; \dfrac{n_{C=C\,\text{reduced}}}{n_{\text{cat}} \;\times\; t}$ | Turnover Frequency (catalyst activity metric) | s⁻¹ or h⁻¹ |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Note:** Per KTU norms, in the above table the symbol \vert and the delimiters used in column entries have been replaced with $\text{[ ... ]}$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydrogenated polymers are used in:
- **Automotive**: HNBR timing belts, O-rings (under-hood temperature resistance).
- **Oil & Gas**: HNBR seals for downhole tools (H₂S, CO₂, sour crude resistance).
- **Medical**: SEBS tubing (no toxic unsaturation byproducts).
- **Electronics**: Hydrogenated DCPD resins for 5G PCB substrates (low dielectric loss).
- **Adhesives & TPEs**: SEBS in hot-melt adhesives and shoe soles.

The global HNBR market alone exceeds **USD 1.2 billion/year**, making this a high-impact industrial process.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrogen Consumption from Iodine Value (Standard KTU Numerical)

**Problem Statement (Typical ESE format):** A 100 g sample of NBR has an initial iodine value of $IV_0 = 28$ g I₂/100 g. It is hydrogenated to a final iodine value of $IV_t = 1.4$ g I₂/100 g. Calculate: (a) the degree of hydrogenation, (b) the moles of H₂ consumed per kg of polymer, and (c) the volume of H₂ at STP.

**Given:**
- $IV_0 = 28$ g I₂ per 100 g polymer
- $IV_t = 1.4$ g I₂ per 100 g polymer
- Molecular weight of I₂ (M_I₂) = **253.82 g/mol**
- Molar volume of ideal gas at STP = **22.414 L/mol**

### Step 1: Calculate the Degree of Hydrogenation (HD%)

We substitute directly into the master formula:

$$\text{HD\%} \;=\; \frac{IV_0 - IV_t}{IV_0} \times 100$$

Plugging in the values:

$$\text{HD\%} \;=\; \frac{28 - 1.4}{28} \times 100$$

$$\text{HD\%} \;=\; \frac{26.6}{28} \times 100$$

$$\text{HD\%} \;=\; 95.0\,\%$$

**Interpretation:** 95% of the C=C unsaturation has been saturated — this is classified as a "fully hydrogenated" commercial-grade HNBR.

### Step 2: Moles of H₂ Consumed

**Logic:** Each mole of C=C consumes 1 mole of H₂. The iodine value measures C=C equivalents via I₂ addition (1 mole I₂ ≡ 1 mole C=C). Therefore, the reduction in iodine value (in g I₂/100 g polymer) is proportional to the H₂ consumed.

Change in IV per 100 g polymer:

$$\Delta IV \;=\; IV_0 - IV_t \;=\; 28 - 1.4 \;=\; 26.6 \text{ g I}_2 \text{ per 100 g}$$

Convert g I₂ to moles I₂:

$$n_{I_2} \;=\; \frac{26.6 \text{ g}}{253.82 \text{ g/mol}} \;=\; 0.10480 \text{ mol (per 100 g)}$$

Since 1 mole I₂ reacts with 1 mole C=C, and 1 mole C=C consumes 1 mole H₂:

$$n_{H_2} \;=\; 0.10480 \text{ mol per 100 g polymer}$$

Scaling to per kilogram (×10):

$$n_{H_2} \;=\; 1.0480 \text{ mol per kg polymer}$$

### Step 3: Volume of H₂ at STP

Using the ideal gas law at STP (1 atm, 273.15 K):

$$V_{H_2,\,STP} \;=\; n_{H_2} \times 22.414 \text{ L/mol}$$

For 1 kg of polymer:

$$V_{H_2,\,STP} \;=\; 1.0480 \times 22.414$$

$$V_{H_2,\,STP} \;=\; 23.490 \text{ L (per kg polymer)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of Hydrogenation Rate

**Problem Statement:** The hydrogenation of SBR is studied at two temperatures. At $T_1 = 80\,^\circ\text{C}$ the rate constant is $k_1 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{min}^{-1}$. At $T_2 = 120\,^\circ\text{C}$ the rate constant is $k_2 = 1.8 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{min}^{-1}$. Calculate the activation energy $E_a$.

**Step 1: Convert temperatures to Kelvin.**

$$T_1 \;=\; 80 + 273.15 \;=\; 353.15 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 \;=\; 120 + 273.15 \;=\; 393.15 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Apply the two-temperature Arrhenius form.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) \;=\; \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 3: Compute the LHS.**

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} \;=\; \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 7.2$$

$$\ln(7.2) \;=\; 1.9741$$

**Step 4: Compute the RHS bracket.**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \;=\; \frac{1}{353.15} - \frac{1}{393.15}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \;=\; 0.002832 - 0.002544$$

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \;=\; 2.880 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5: Solve for E_a.**

$$E_a \;=\; \frac{R \cdot \ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)}$$

$$E_a \;=\; \frac{8.314 \times 1.9741}{2.880 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a \;=\; \frac{16.413}{2.880 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a \;=\; 56\,990 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{E_a \;\approx\; 57.0 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

This is consistent with the **catalyst-lowered activation energy** range (40–80 kJ/mol) cited in Section 1.2.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: First-Order Kinetic Profile for Degree of Hydrogenation

Assuming a pseudo-first-order regime (constant [H₂] dissolved, constant [Cat]):

$$-\frac{d[C=C]}{dt} \;=\; k_{\text{obs}}\,[C=C]$$

Integrating from $t=0$ to $t=t$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{[C=C]_t}{[C=C]_0}\right) \;=\; -k_{\text{obs}}\,t$$

Since $[C=C]$ is directly proportional to the iodine value ($IV$):

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{IV_t}{IV_0}\right) \;=\; -k_{\text{obs}}\,t$$

Solving for the time required to reach a target HD%:

$$t \;=\; -\frac{1}{k_{\text{obs}}}\,\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{\text{HD\%}}{100}\right)$$

**Example:** For HD% = 99% with $k_{\text{obs}} = 0.04\,\text{min}^{-1}$:

$$t \;=\; -\frac{1}{0.04}\,\ln(1 - 0.99)$$

$$t \;=\; -\frac{1}{0.04}\,\ln(0.01)$$

$$t \;=\; -\frac{1}{0.04} \times (-4.6052)$$

$$t \;=\; 115.13 \text{ min} \;\approx\; 1.92 \text{ hours}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Catalyst Selection (Engineering Decision Table)

| Catalyst | Form | Activity | Selectivity | Cost | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Raney Nickel** | Heterogeneous | High | Moderate (all C=C) | Low | NBR → HNBR (industrial workhorse) |
| **Pd/C (5–10%)** | Heterogeneous | Very High | High | High | SBR → HSBR; lab/pilot scale |
| **PtO₂ (Adams' catalyst)** | Heterogeneous | Moderate | High | High | Specialty polymers |
| **Wilkinson's (RhCl(PPh₃)₃)** | Homogeneous | High | Excellent (1,4 > 1,2) | Very High | Research, fine chemicals |
| **Crabtree's (Ir-complex)** | Homogeneous | Very High | Excellent | Very High | Selective hydrogenation |
| **Pd(OAc)₂ + dppf** | Homogeneous | High | Tunable by ligand | High | NBR → HNBR (LANXESS process) |

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Industrial Polymer Hydrogenation Plant

        B1 --> B2[Catalyst Addition Pd/C 1 wt percent]

        C3 --> C4

1. The **green Feed Section** prepares the polymer solution.

4. The **pink Separation Section** removes the catalyst and solvent.
5. The **light-blue Product Section** gives the final hydrogenated polymer and recycles solvent.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define the term "Degree of Hydrogenation" (HD%) in the context of polymer modification. How is it experimentally determined?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Degree of Hydrogenation (HD%)** is defined as the *percentage of carbon–carbon double bonds (C=C) in a polymer that have been converted to saturated C–C single bonds* during the catalytic hydrogenation process. It is a quantitative measure of how completely the unsaturation has been removed from the polymer backbone or side groups.

$$\text{HD\%} \;=\; \frac{IV_0 - IV_t}{IV_0} \times 100$$

**Experimental Determination (Wijs Method):**
- The polymer is dissolved in a suitable solvent (e.g., chloroform or CCl₄).
- A known excess of **Wijs solution** (ICl in glacial acetic acid) is added; the ICl adds across the remaining C=C double bonds.
- The unreacted ICl is back-titrated with standard **sodium thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃)** using starch indicator.
- The **Iodine Value (IV)** is calculated from the titration volume: $IV = \dfrac{126.91 \times V_{IB} \times N_{IB}}{m_{\text{sample}}}$ (g I₂ / 100 g).
- HD% is then computed using the initial and final IV values.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for procedure)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**List any three industrially important hydrogenated polymers and state one improved property for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Hydrogenated Polymer | Source Polymer | Improved Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **HNBR** (Hydrogenated Nitrile Butadiene Rubber) | NBR | Excellent heat & ozone resistance (service temp ↑ from 120 °C to **150 °C**) |
| 2 | **SEBS** (Styrene-Ethylene-Butylene-Styrene) | SBS | UV stability + improved processability for TPEs |
| 3 | **HSBR** (Hydrogenated SBR) | SBR | Improved aging resistance and dynamic properties for tyre treads |

(3 marks: 1 for each correct pair)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **Horiuti–Polanyi mechanism** of heterogeneous catalytic hydrogenation of an unsaturated polymer. Explain each step and identify the rate-determining step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **heterogeneous and homogeneous catalytic hydrogenation** of polymers with respect to (i) catalyst recovery, (ii) selectivity, (iii) typical catalysts used, and (iv) suitability for industrial scale. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

The Horiuti–Polanyi mechanism describes hydrogenation on a solid metal catalyst (e.g., Pd, Pt, Ni) and proceeds in **five steps**:

**Step 1 — Diffusion of H₂:** Molecular hydrogen dissolves in the polymer solution and diffuses to the metal surface through the boundary layer.

**Step 2 — Dissociative Chemisorption:** H₂ adsorbs onto the metal surface and splits into two M–H bonds:

$$H_2 \;+\; 2M \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,M\text{-}H$$

This is **the rate-determining step (RDS)** in most industrial hydrogenations because breaking the strong H–H bond (436 kJ/mol) requires significant activation, even on metal surfaces.

**Step 3 — π-Complexation:** The polymer's C=C double bond approaches the metal and forms a π-complex, weakly bonded through its π-electrons.

**Step 4 — Migratory Insertion:** One surface H atom transfers to one carbon of the C=C, forming a σ-alkyl metal intermediate (M–C–C–H).

**Step 5 — Reductive Elimination:** The second H atom transfers to the remaining carbon, releasing the saturated C–C bond and regenerating the free metal site for the next cycle.

**Summary equation:** $\text{[–CH=CH–]} + H_2 \xrightarrow{M} \text{[–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–]}$

**Factors affecting the rate:** H₂ pressure, temperature, catalyst surface area, mass transfer, and absence of catalyst poisons (S, CO, Cl⁻).

(7 marks distributed: 1 mark per step × 5 = 5, 1 for identification of RDS, 1 for engineering factors)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

| Criterion | Heterogeneous | Homogeneous |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Catalyst recovery** | Easy — simple **filtration** or centrifugation | Difficult — requires **distillation/precipitation** |
| **(ii) Selectivity** | Moderate — reduces all C=C indiscriminately | **Excellent** — can be tuned via ligand design |
| **(iii) Typical catalysts** | Pd/C, Raney Ni, PtO₂, Pd/BaSO₄ | Wilkinson's (RhCl(PPh₃)₃), Crabtree's Ir-complex |
| **(iv) Industrial suitability** | **High** — preferred for bulk production (HNBR, HSBR) | Limited — used for specialty polymers and research |

**Conclusion:** Heterogeneous catalysts dominate industrial polymer hydrogenation due to ease of separation and lower cost. Homogeneous catalysts are preferred when high chemo- or regio-selectivity is required.

(7 marks: 1.5 per criterion + 0.5 for conclusion)

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **role of process parameters** (temperature, pressure, catalyst loading, agitation) on the rate and selectivity of polymer hydrogenation. Justify each with underlying theory. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 250 g sample of NBR has an initial iodine value of 35 g I₂/100 g. After hydrogenation, the IV drops to 1.75 g I₂/100 g. Calculate the **(i) degree of hydrogenation**, **(ii) moles of H₂ consumed**, and **(iii) volume of H₂ at NTP (T = 25 °C, P = 1 atm)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Temperature:**
Increasing T exponentially increases the rate constant via the Arrhenius equation $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$. However, too high T (>180 °C) causes polymer chain scission, gel formation, and color degradation. Optimum range: 80–150 °C.

**Pressure:**
By **Henry's Law**, dissolved H₂ concentration is proportional to H₂ pressure: $[H_2]_{\text{dissolved}} = k_H \cdot P_{H_2}$. Higher P → higher $[H_2]$ → higher rate (linear in many regimes). Industrial H₂ pressures range from 30–100 bar for polymer hydrogenation.

**Catalyst Loading:**
Rate is typically first-order in catalyst concentration: $r \propto [\text{Cat}]$. More catalyst → faster reaction, but cost increases nonlinearly. Typical loading: 0.1–2 wt% Pd/C.

**Agitation:**
Agitation controls **gas–liquid mass transfer** of H₂. Below a critical RPM, the system is mass-transfer limited; above it, intrinsic kinetics dominate. Industrial reactors use 400–1000 rpm with sparging rings for H₂.

**Synergistic effect:** All four parameters must be optimized jointly using **Design of Experiments (DoE)** to maximize HD% while minimizing cost and degradation.

(7 marks: ~1.5 per parameter + 0.5 for synthesis)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Given:**
- Sample mass = 250 g NBR
- $IV_0 = 35$ g I₂ / 100 g
- $IV_t = 1.75$ g I₂ / 100 g
- M_I₂ = 253.82 g/mol; R = 0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K)
- NTP: T = 298.15 K, P = 1 atm

**(i) Degree of Hydrogenation:**

$$\text{HD\%} \;=\; \frac{35 - 1.75}{35} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{33.25}{35} \times 100 \;=\; 95.0\,\%$$

**(ii) Moles of H₂ consumed:**

$\Delta IV = 33.25$ g I₂ per 100 g polymer.

For 250 g polymer:

$$m_{I_2} \;=\; 33.25 \times \frac{250}{100} \;=\; 83.125 \text{ g I}_2$$

$$n_{H_2} \;=\; n_{I_2} \;=\; \frac{83.125}{253.82} \;=\; 0.3275 \text{ mol}$$

**(iii) Volume of H₂ at NTP:**

Using ideal gas law: $V = nRT/P$:

$$V_{H_2,\,NTP} \;=\; \frac{0.3275 \times 0.08206 \times 298.15}{1}$$

$$V_{H_2,\,NTP} \;=\; 8.014 \text{ L}$$

**Sanity check:** Compare with STP: $V_{STP} = 0.3275 \times 22.414 = 7.34$ L. NTP is warmer, so the volume should be larger ✓.

(7 marks: 2 for HD%, 2.5 for moles, 2.5 for volume)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Hydrogenation Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to scale the iodine value to the actual sample mass** — Students compute per 100 g but forget ×(m_sample/100). This costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing "degree of hydrogenation" with "conversion"** — They are NOT the same; conversion is C=C consumed per total fed, HD% is per initial.
> 3. **Using STP volume (22.414 L/mol) when the question specifies NTP or other conditions** — Always re-derive using PV = nRT with the given T and P.
> 4. **Writing the catalyst poison list incorrectly** — S, CO, and halides are poisons; O₂ is also a poison (forms metal oxides).
> 5. **Mixing up homogeneous and heterogeneous** — Homogeneous = single phase, all-soluble; Heterogeneous = solid catalyst + liquid phase.
> 6. **Not mentioning the rate-determining step (RDS)** explicitly in mechanism questions — Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for RDS identification.
> 7. **Forgetting units in Iodine Value definition** — Always write g I₂/100 g polymer (not just "g").

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Hydrogenation = catalytic addition of H₂ across C=C** in polymers, converting unsaturation to saturation.
- ✅ **Master equation:** $\text{HD\%} = \dfrac{IV_0 - IV_t}{IV_0} \times 100$
- ✅ **Industrially key products:** HNBR, HSBR, SEBS, HPBD, HNR.
- ✅ **Horiuti–Polanyi mechanism:** 5 steps — diffusion → dissociative chemisorption → π-complexation → migratory insertion → reductive elimination.
- ✅ **RDS = dissociative chemisorption of H₂** on metal surface (in most cases).
- ✅ **Catalysts:** Heterogeneous = Pd/C, Raney Ni, PtO₂; Homogeneous = Wilkinson's Rh, Crabtree's Ir.
- ✅ **Iodine Value measurement:** Wijs method using ICl + back-titration with Na₂S₂O₃.
- ✅ **Henry's Law:** $[H_2]_{dissolved} = k_H P_{H_2}$ — explains pressure effect.
- ✅ **Arrhenius:** $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ — explains temperature effect; $E_a \approx$ 40–80 kJ/mol with catalyst.
- ✅ **Catalyst poisons:** S, CO, halides, O₂ — must be excluded from feed.
- ✅ **Degree of unsaturation → C=C equivalents → moles of H₂** (1:1 stoichiometry with C=C).
- ✅ **Pseudo-first-order kinetics:** $\ln(IV_t/IV_0) = -k_{obs} t$.
- ✅ **Enthalpy of reaction:** $\Delta H_{rxn} \approx -120$ kJ/mol (exothermic — cooling required at industrial scale).
- ✅ **Heterogeneous catalysts dominate industry** (easy recovery); homogeneous dominate research (high selectivity).
- ✅ **Global HNBR market > USD 1.2 billion/year** — industrial relevance for KTU viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Addition and Substitution Reactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Addition Reactions (Chain-Growth / Addition Polymerization)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Addition Polymerization** is a chain-growth reaction in which monomers possessing unsaturated functional groups (typically C=C double bonds in vinyl/allyl monomers) add successively to an active chain-end without the loss of any small molecule. The empirical formula of the polymer is identical to that of the monomer (no by-product elimination).

The general scheme is:
$$n\,CH_2=CHX \xrightarrow{\text{Initiator}} \left[ -CH_2-CHX- \right]_n$$

where **X** may be **H, Cl, CH₃, C₆H₅, CN, COOCH₃, OCOR**, etc.

## 1.2 Substitution Reactions (Step-Growth / Polymer Modification)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substitution Reactions in Polymers** are reactions in which an atom or group on the polymer backbone/side-chain is replaced by another atom or group, OR a step-growth polymerization proceeds through condensation with elimination of a small molecule (H₂O, HCl, CH₃OH). This includes *polymer-analogous transformations* where pre-formed polymers undergo chemical modification.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Train Coupling vs. Lego Replacement"**
> - **Addition Polymerization** is like **coupling train coaches one-by-one** to a moving engine (the active center). Each coach (monomer) joins the chain end and the engine moves forward. **No coach is destroyed in the process.**
> - **Substitution Reactions** are like **swapping the wheels of an already-built Lego car** — the structure exists, but atoms/groups are exchanged at specific sites, OR monomers join while dropping a small "exhaust" (water, HCl) at each joint.

---

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot (KTU 2024 Syllabus Scope)

| Reaction Type | Sub-class | Active Center | Typical Monomer |
|---------------|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| Addition | Free-radical | R• | Styrene, MMA, VAc |
| Addition | Cationic | R⁺ | Isobutylene, Vinyl ether |
| Addition | Anionic | R⁻ | Styrene, Butadiene |
| Addition | Coordination (Ziegler-Natta) | M–C | Ethylene, Propylene |
| Substitution | Electrophilic (Ar-SE) | Ar–H → Ar–E | Polystyrene, Phenolics |
| Substitution | Nucleophilic (SN1/SN2) | C–LG → C–Nu | PVC, Poly(vinyl alcohol) |
| Substitution | Hydrolysis/Halogenation | C–X → C–OH | Cellulose, PVC, PVAc |

**Critical Constants to Memorize:**
- **Gas Constant:** R = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Avogadro's Number:** Nₐ = **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**
- **Kinetic Chain Length (ν)** is dimensionless
- **Degree of Polymerization (X̄ₙ)** is dimensionless

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Addition Polymerization — The Four Elementary Steps

### Step 1: Initiation
A free radical initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide, AIBN) decomposes to form radicals, which then attack a monomer double bond.

$$I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2R^{\bullet}$$

$$R^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_i} RM_1^{\bullet}$$

**Why it matters:** The first radical generation is the *rate-determining* initiation step; it sets the population of active chains.

### Step 2: Propagation
The active chain-end adds monomer units successively.

$$RM_n^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} RM_{n+1}^{\bullet}$$

**How it works:** Each propagation adds one monomer in milliseconds; thousands of monomers add per second, causing rapid molecular weight growth.

### Step 3: Termination (Two modes)
- **Combination (coupling):** $RM_n^{\bullet} + RM_m^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_{tc}} R-M_{n+m}-R$
- **Disproportionation:** $RM_n^{\bullet} + RM_m^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_{td}} R-M_n-H + R-M_m\text{ (unsaturated)}$

**Why it matters:** Termination mode controls whether the polymer end-groups are saturated (combination) or contain a double bond (disproportionation).

### Step 4: Chain Transfer
The active center is transferred to a previously dormant species (monomer, solvent, polymer, or initiator).

$$RM_n^{\bullet} + XH \xrightarrow{k_{tr}} RM_nH + X^{\bullet}$$

**How it works:** Chain transfer to polymer creates **branches**; transfer to monomer limits molecular weight.

---

## 2.2 Substitution Reactions on Polymers — Mechanistic Classes

### 2.2.1 Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution (EAS) on Polystyrene
The aromatic ring of polystyrene undergoes nitration, sulfonation, halogenation, and Friedel-Crafts acylation.

$$\text{Poly}(styrene) + HNO_3/H_2SO_4 \longrightarrow \text{Poly}(p\text{-nitrostyrene}) + H_2O$$

### 2.2.2 Nucleophilic Substitution (SN1/SN2) on PVC
The C–Cl bond in PVC is susceptible to nucleophilic attack by OH⁻, CN⁻, amines, and carboxylates.

$$\text{Poly}(vinyl\,chloride) + NaOH \longrightarrow \text{Poly}(vinyl\,alcohol) + NaCl$$

### 2.2.3 Hydrolysis of Poly(vinyl acetate) → Poly(vinyl alcohol)
$$\text{Poly}(VAc) + CH_3OH/NaOH \longrightarrow \text{Poly}(vinyl\,alcohol) + CH_3COOCH_3$$

### 2.2.4 Halogenation of Natural Rubber
$$-CH_2-C(CH_3)=CH-CH_2- + Cl_2 \longrightarrow -CH_2-C(CH_3)Cl-CHCl-CH_2-$$

**Why it matters:** Substitution reactions enable *tailor-making* functional polymers for ion-exchange resins, membranes, and drug-delivery systems.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MUST MEMORIZE**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Rate of Initiation | $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹; $f$ = initiator efficiency |
| 2 | Rate of Polymerization | $R_p = k_p [M^{\bullet}] [M]$ | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 3 | Steady-State Radical Conc. | $[M^{\bullet}] = \left( \dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}$ | mol·L⁻¹ |
| 4 | Rate of Polymerization (Steady State) | $R_p = k_p \left( \dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2} [M]$ | $k_t = k_{tc} + k_{td}$ |
| 5 | Kinetic Chain Length | $\nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2(f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Degree of Polymerization (X̄ₙ) — Termination by Combination | $\overline{X_n} = 2\nu$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Degree of Polymerization (X̄ₙ) — Termination by Disproportionation | $\overline{X_n} = \nu$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Mayo Equation (with Chain Transfer) | $\dfrac{1}{\overline{X_n}} = \dfrac{1}{\overline{X_n}_0} + C_M + C_S \dfrac{[S]}{[M]} + C_I \dfrac{[I]}{[M]}$ | $C$ = transfer constant |
| 9 | Carothers Equation (Step-Growth) | $\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{1}{(1-p)}$ | $p$ = extent of reaction |
| 10 | Critical Extent of Reaction (Gel Point) | $p_c = \dfrac{2}{f_{avg}}$ | $f_{avg}$ = avg functionality |
| 11 | Chain Transfer Constant | $C_X = \dfrac{k_{tr,X}}{k_p}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Activation Energy (Overall) | $E_a = E_p + \dfrac{E_d}{2} - \dfrac{E_t}{2}$ | J·mol⁻¹ (Arrhenius) |

> **Note on Carothers Equation:** Applies to **substitution / step-growth** polymerizations (e.g., polyester, polyamide, polycarbonate formation), where each bond formed ejects a small molecule.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Addition polymerization** underpins **polyolefins (PE, PP)**, **PVC pipes**, **PMMA (acrylic glass)**, and **synthetic rubber** — produced at multi-million-ton scale globally.
- **Substitution reactions** enable **ion-exchange resins** (sulfonated polystyrene for water softeners), **membrane separators** (chloromethylated polystyrene for dialysis), **photoresists** (diazotization reactions), and **biodegradable polymer synthesis** (hydrolyzable polyesters).
- **Chain transfer to polymer** is exploited to produce **LDPE** (Long-Chain Branching) — a deliberate engineering choice to tune processability.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Steady-State Rate of Polymerization ($R_p$)

**Step 1: Write individual rate equations**

The rate of initiator decomposition is:
$$R_d = -\dfrac{d[I]}{dt} = 2 f k_d [I]$$

The rate of initiation (each radical generated that successfully initiates a chain) is:
$$R_i = R_d = 2 f k_d [I]$$

The rate of propagation is:
$$R_p = -\dfrac{d[M]}{dt} = k_p [M^{\bullet}] [M]$$

The rate of termination is:
$$R_t = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

**Step 2: Apply the Steady-State Approximation (SSA)**

At steady state, the rate of formation of radicals equals the rate of their destruction:
$$R_i = R_t$$
$$2 f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

**Step 3: Solve for the radical concentration**

$$[M^{\bullet}]^2 = \dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}$$
$$[M^{\bullet}] = \left( \dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}$$

**Step 4: Substitute back into the propagation rate**

$$R_p = k_p \left( \dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2} [M]$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{2}$ for the conventional KTU form:
$$R_p = k_p \left( \dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2} [M] \quad \text{(conventional KTU form)}$$

**Conversion Logic:** SSA holds because radicals are highly reactive and have very short lifetimes (≈ 1 second). Hence, $[M^{\bullet}]$ remains nearly constant during the bulk of the reaction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Kinetic Chain Length ($\nu$)

**Step 1: Definition of $\nu$**

The kinetic chain length is the average number of monomers consumed per active center initiated:
$$\nu = \dfrac{\text{Rate of monomer consumption}}{\text{Rate of radical generation}} = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the expressions**

$$\nu = \dfrac{k_p [M^{\bullet}] [M]}{2 f k_d [I]}$$

**Step 3: Eliminate $[M^{\bullet}]$ using SSA result**

Since $[M^{\bullet}] = \left( \dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}$:

$$\nu = \dfrac{k_p [M] \left( \dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}}{2 f k_d [I]}$$

**Step 4: Simplify algebraically**

$$\nu = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d k_t)^{1/2} [I]^{1/2}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Since $R_p \propto [M][I]^{1/2}$, doubling $[I]$ increases $R_p$ by $\sqrt{2}$ but **halves** $\nu$. Thus, more initiator → faster reaction but **shorter chains**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Degree of Polymerization with Chain Transfer (Mayo Equation)

**Step 1: Define $\overline{X_n}$ in the presence of chain transfer**

The number-average degree of polymerization equals the rate of monomer consumption divided by the rate of *all* chain-stopping events (termination + transfer):
$$\dfrac{1}{\overline{X_n}} = \dfrac{R_t + R_{tr,M} + R_{tr,S} + R_{tr,I}}{R_p}$$

**Step 2: Express each transfer rate in terms of $k_{tr}$**

$$R_{tr,M} = k_{tr,M} [M^{\bullet}] [M]$$
$$R_{tr,S} = k_{tr,S} [M^{\bullet}] [S]$$
$$R_{tr,I} = k_{tr,I} [M^{\bullet}] [I]$$

**Step 3: Define transfer constants**

$$C_M = \dfrac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p}, \quad C_S = \dfrac{k_{tr,S}}{k_p}, \quad C_I = \dfrac{k_{tr,I}}{k_p}$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify**

$$\dfrac{1}{\overline{X_n}} = \dfrac{2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]}{k_p [M^{\bullet}] [M]} + \dfrac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p} + \dfrac{k_{tr,S} [S]}{k_p [M]} + \dfrac{k_{tr,I} [I]}{k_p [M]}$$

**Step 5: Combine into the canonical Mayo equation**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{\overline{X_n}} = \dfrac{1}{(\overline{X_n})_0} + C_M + C_S \dfrac{[S]}{[M]} + C_I \dfrac{[I]}{[M]}}$$

where $\dfrac{1}{(\overline{X_n})_0} = \dfrac{2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]}{k_p [M^{\bullet}] [M]}$ is the chain-stopping term without transfer.

**Conversion Logic:** Plotting $\dfrac{1}{\overline{X_n}}$ vs $\dfrac{[S]}{[M]}$ at constant $[I]$ yields a straight line with **slope = $C_S$** and **intercept = $\dfrac{1}{(\overline{X_n})_0} + C_M + C_I \dfrac{[I]}{[M]}$** — the standard Mayo plot for solvent-transfer studies.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Carothers Equation for Step-Growth (Substitution) Polymerization

**Step 1: Define extent of reaction**

Let $N_0$ = initial number of monomer molecules, $N$ = number remaining at time $t$.

$$p = \dfrac{N_0 - N}{N_0}$$

**Step 2: Relate $N$ to $\overline{X_n}$**

Each surviving molecule is one polymer chain. Therefore:
$$\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{N_0}{N} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$$

**Step 3: For the case of unequal stoichiometry (r < 1, where r = N_B / N_A < 1)**

Let $N_A$ be the limiting reagent. Then the number of unreacted A groups is $N_A (1-p)$, and total molecules at any time is $N_A (1-p) + N_A(r - p)$.

After substitution and simplification:
$$\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

**Conversion Logic:** For $r = 1$ (stoichiometric), $\overline{X_n} \to \infty$ as $p \to 1$, meaning **high molecular weight in step-growth demands > 99% conversion** — a stark contrast to chain-growth.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Mechanism

| Monomer Type | Electronic Nature | Preferred Mechanism | Why? |
|--------------|-------------------|---------------------|------|
| Ethylene (CH₂=CH₂) | Non-polar | Radical, Coordination | Symmetric; no EWG/EWG bias |
| Vinyl chloride (CH₂=CHCl) | EWG (Cl) | Radical, Anionic | Cl stabilizes carbanion |
| Styrene (CH₂=CH–C₆H₅) | Conjugated EWG | Radical, Anionic, Cationic | Resonance stabilization of all 3 |
| Isobutylene (CH₂=C(CH₃)₂) | Electron-donating alkyl | Cationic | +I effect stabilizes carbocation |
| Vinyl ether (CH₂=CH–OR) | Strong +M | Cationic | O lone pair stabilizes cation |
| Methyl methacrylate (CH₂=C(CH₃)COOCH₃) | EWG (COOR) | Radical, Anionic | Stabilizes radical and anion |
| Acrylonitrile (CH₂=CH–CN) | Strong EWG | Radical, Anionic | CN stabilizes anion |
