<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK AND TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Apr 2024 Set A]**
> **Q1.** Define Polymer Analogous Reactions. State any two essential conditions that must be satisfied for a reaction to qualify as a true PAR.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Polymer Analogous Reaction (PAR)** is a chemical transformation of a pre-formed polymer in which the functional groups on the polymer are converted to other functional groups, *without* changing the degree of polymerization, the polymer chain structure, or the molecular weight distribution.

**Two essential conditions:**
1. The **degree of polymerization ($\overline{X}_n$)** of the polymer must remain constant before and after the reaction.
2. The **main-chain backbone** (i.e., the carbon–carbon or heteroatom skeleton) must remain **intact**, with only the pendant groups undergoing transformation.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for conditions)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set B]**
> **Q2.** What is a polymer bound reagent? Mention any two advantages of using polymer bound reagents over conventional solution-phase reagents.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Polymer Bound Reagent (PBR)** is a reagent that is chemically immobilized (covalently anchored) onto an insoluble polymer support (most commonly crosslinked polystyrene) and is used to perform a chemical transformation on a small-molecule substrate.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Simple work-up** — the polymer reagent is recovered by simple filtration, avoiding tedious extraction, distillation, or chromatography.
2. **Reagent recyclability and selectivity** — site isolation on the polymer prevents side reactions, and many PBRs can be regenerated and reused, lowering cost and waste.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for advantages)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> ### **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**
> **Q3A.** (a) Explain in detail the classification of Polymer Analogous Reactions with suitable examples. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Discuss the kinetics of PARs with reference to the first-neighbor effect and derive the integrated rate law. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Classification of PARs (7 Marks)

Polymer analogous reactions are classified into four major types:

1. **Hydrolysis Reactions (2 marks):**
   Conversion of side-chain esters, amides, nitriles, or halides into alcohols, acids, or amines.
   - *Example:* Alkaline hydrolysis of poly(vinyl acetate) to poly(vinyl alcohol) using NaOH/methanol.
   - *Example:* Acid hydrolysis of polyacrylamide to poly(acrylic acid).

2. **Addition Reactions (1.5 marks):**
   Addition across double bonds or to electrophilic centers on the polymer chain.
   - *Example:* Chlorination of natural rubber (1,4-polyisoprene) by $Cl_2$ to give epoxidized or chlorinated rubber.
   - *Example:* Hydroboration of polybutadiene.

3. **Substitution Reactions (1.5 marks):**
   Nucleophilic or electrophilic displacement on the side chain.
   - *Example:* Conversion of poly(vinyl chloride) to poly(vinyl pyridinium) by reaction with pyridine.
   - *Example:* Quaternization of poly(4-vinyl pyridine) with alkyl halides to form polymer-supported phase-transfer catalysts.

4. **Cyclization and Intramolecular Reactions (2 marks):**
   Formation of ring structures within the polymer.
   - *Example:* Cyclization of polyacrylonitrile upon heating to form a ladder polymer (used as a precursor for carbon fibers).
   - *Example:* Intramolecular imide formation from poly(amic acid) → polyimide.

#### Part (b) — First-Neighbor Kinetics (7 Marks)

In a polymer, the reaction of a functional group $A_i$ at position $i$ can be affected by the state of its neighbor $A_{i-1}$ (and, in more advanced models, $A_{i+1}$).

**Assumption:** Two distinct rate constants:
- $k_0$: rate constant when **both** neighbors are unreacted.
- $k_1$: rate constant when **one or more** neighbors have already reacted.

**Define:**
- $P(t)$: fraction of unreacted groups with both neighbors unreacted.
- $C(t)$: fraction of unreacted groups with at least one reacted neighbor.

**Differential equations:**

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = -k_0 P \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dC}{dt} = -k_1 C$$

**Integrate:**

$$P(t) = P_0 \, e^{-k_0 t}, \quad C(t) = C_0 \, e^{-k_1 t}$$

**Total unreacted fraction:**

$$1 - \alpha(t) = P_0 e^{-k_0 t} + C_0 e^{-k_1 t}$$

**Initial conditions (Bernoullian distribution):**

$$P_0 = (1 - \alpha_0)^2, \quad C_0 = 1 - (1 - \alpha_0)^2$$

**Final integrated rate law:**

$$1 - \alpha(t) = (1 - \alpha_0)^2 e^{-k_0 t} + \left[1 - (1 - \alpha_0)^2\right] e^{-k_1 t}$$

**Special case — $k_0 = k_1 = k$:**

$$\lim_{k_0 \to k_1} 1 - \alpha(t) = e^{-kt}$$

This is the **diagnostic test** for the absence of neighboring group effects.

---

> ### **[Alternative Question, Internal Choice]**
> **Q3B.** (a) Describe the concept, classification, and general method of preparation of Polymer Bound Reagents. (7 marks)
> **(b)** With a neat schematic, explain the Merrifield solid-phase peptide synthesis as an application of polymer bound reagents. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Concept and Preparation of PBRs (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** A polymer bound reagent is an insoluble, high-molecular-weight polymer to which an active chemical reagent is covalently attached. The active site performs chemistry on a substrate in solution, while the polymer remains a recoverable solid.

**Classification (3 marks):** PBRs are classified based on their function:

1. **Polymer Substrates** — the polymer is the substrate (anchored and modified stepwise, e.g., in peptide synthesis).
2. **Polymer Reagents** — the polymer carries the active reagent stoichiometrically (e.g., polymer-bound borohydride, periodate).
3. **Polymer Catalysts** — the polymer carries a catalytic site (e.g., polymer-bound phase-transfer catalysts, polymer-bound Pd for hydrogenation).
4. **Polymer Scavengers** — the polymer captures and removes excess reagents or byproducts from solution (e.g., polymer-bound isocyanate to scavenge amines).

**General Method of Preparation (3 marks):**
1. Start with a **chloromethylated polystyrene-DVB resin** (Merrifield resin).
2. Functionalize via **nucleophilic substitution** ($S_N2$) of the benzyl chloride with the desired reagent anion (e.g., $^-BH_4$, $^-OH$, $^-IO_4$, $^-PPh_2$).
3. If a spacer is needed, react with a long-chain amino alcohol or PEG derivative first, then anchor the active site.
4. Characterize by **elemental analysis, IR, and loading determination** (typically 1–3 mmol/g).

#### Part (b) — Merrifield SPPS (7 Marks)

**Concept (2 marks):** In 1963, R. Bruce Merrifield introduced the use of chloromethylated polystyrene (now called "Merrifield resin") as a *solid support* for stepwise peptide synthesis. The C-terminal amino acid is anchored to the resin, and subsequent amino acids are added in a C→N direction. After each coupling, byproducts and excess reagents are removed by **simple washing**.

**Schematic Cycle (5 marks):**

1. **Loading:** First Fmoc-amino acid is coupled to the resin via its carboxyl group (ester or amide bond).
2. **Deprotection:** Fmoc group is removed with piperidine (20% in DMF).
3. **Coupling:** The next Fmoc-amino acid is activated with HBTU/HOBt/DIPEA and coupled.
4. **Wash:** DMF, DCM washes remove unreacted materials.
5. **Repeat** steps 2–4 for each amino acid.
6. **Cleavage:** TFA (with scavengers) cleaves the peptide from the resin.

**Merits (already covered in 5.3):**
- Automation-friendly.
- High yields (≥99% per step).
- Easy purification by filtration.
- Nobel Prize in Chemistry (1984).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - In PARs, students **often confuse the degree of polymerization (DP) with the molecular weight ($M_n$)**. The DP stays constant in a true PAR, but $M_n$ may change because the side-group mass changes. **Always state both DP invariance and $M_n$ change explicitly.**
> - In PBR questions, students often forget to mention the **role of swelling**. A non-swollen resin (e.g., dry PS-DVB in non-polar solvent) is *inactive*. **Always mention the need for a good swelling solvent (DMF, DCM, THF).**
> - Do not confuse **Merrifield resin** (chloromethylated PS) with **Wang resin** (4-alkoxybenzyl alcohol PS). They differ in cleavage conditions.
> - The "site isolation" effect is a *positive* feature of PBRs and **must be mentioned** to fetch full marks for selectivity questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Recap — Read This the Night Before the Exam!**

- ✅ **PAR Definition:** Functional group transformation on a pre-formed polymer; DP and main chain are unchanged.
- ✅ **PAR Criterion:** $\overline{X}_n^{\text{before}} = \overline{X}_n^{\text{after}}$. Side groups may change.
- ✅ **PBR Definition:** Active reagent is *anchored* on an insoluble polymer; it transforms a small molecule and is recovered.
- ✅ **PBR ≠ PAR:** In PBR, the polymer is the **tool**; in PAR, the polymer is the **substrate**.
- ✅ **Common Polymer Support:** Crosslinked polystyrene with 1–2% divinylbenzene (gel-type or macroporous).
- ✅ **Merrifield Resin:** Chloromethylated PS-DVB; used as a solid support in peptide synthesis.
- ✅ **Wang Resin:** 4-Hydroxymethylphenoxymethyl PS-DVB; acid-labile linker; used in Fmoc-SPPS.
- ✅ **Site Density (Loading):** Measured in **mmol/g**; typical range 0.5–3.0 mmol/g.
- ✅ **Site Isolation:** Active sites on an insoluble polymer are physically separated, suppressing side reactions.
- ✅ **Swelling Ratio Q:** $V_{\text{swollen}}/V_{\text{dry}}$; Q ≈ 1.5–6 for gel-type PS-DVB; affects accessibility.
- ✅ **Kinetics in PAR:** First-order $\ln(1 - \alpha) = -kt$ for isolated groups; bi-exponential for first-neighbor effect.
- ✅ **PARs of PVAc → PVA:** Classic textbook example; DP unchanged, $M_n$ drops from 86,000 → 44,000 g/mol.
- ✅ **PAN Cyclization:** Thermal ladder polymer formation; precursor for carbon fibers.
- ✅ **Green Chemistry Hook:** PBRs reduce solvent waste, enable reagent recycling, and minimize exposure to toxic reagents.
- ✅ **Nobel Connection:** Merrifield (1984) for solid-phase peptide synthesis.
- ✅ **Application Domains:** Pharmaceuticals (SPPS), water purification (chelating PBRs), catalysis (phase-transfer, hydrogenation), sequencing (DNA/RNA synthesizers), analytical scavengers.
- ✅ **Advantages of PBRs:** Simple filtration, recyclability, automation, low toxicity, selectivity.
- ✅ **Disadvantages of PBRs:** High cost of supports, diffusion limitations, sometimes lower reactivity than solution-phase analogs, loading capacity limits.
- ✅ **Spacer Arm:** Required when the active site is sterically inaccessible on a rigid crosslinked bead; common spacers: $-\left(CH_2\right)_n-$, PEG-based.
- ✅ **Reactor Types:** Batch stirred tank (slurry), column flow reactor, microwave-assisted, continuous-flow.
- ✅ **Kerala KTU Board Tip:** When a question asks for "PAR or PBR with example," *always* write the equation showing DP invariance or the recycling arrow. Do not just write a definition.

> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Polymer Analogous Reactions: Polymer Bound Reagents** 🎯

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chain End and Random Degradation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chain End and Random Degradation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Polymer Degradation: The Foundational Concept

**Polymer Degradation** is defined as the irreversible change in the chemical structure, molecular weight, mechanical, or physical properties of a polymer under the influence of one or more environmental agents such as heat, light, oxygen, radiation, mechanical stress, or chemical reactants. In the context of thermal and chemical degradation mechanisms, the two most studied pathways are **Chain-End Degradation (Depolymerisation/Unzippering)** and **Random Degradation (Random Chain Scission)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Degradation is the process of breakdown of polymer chains into smaller fragments, accompanied by a reduction in molecular weight and deterioration of useful mechanical properties.*

### 1.2 Chain-End Degradation (Depolymerisation)

**Formal Definition:** Chain-end degradation, also known as **depolymerisation** or **unzippering**, is a degradation mechanism in which the polymer chain breaks down by successively cleaving the terminal monomer units starting from an active chain end. The monomer units are released one after another in a sequential manner, resembling the unzipping of a jacket.

**The Classical Reversal Argument:** Depolymerisation is considered to be the exact reverse of chain polymerisation. In chain polymerisation, monomer units add sequentially to a growing radical (or ionic) chain end. In depolymerisation, monomer units leave one by one from the active chain end in the reverse order.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In chain-end degradation, the **molecular weight decreases very slowly** because most chains still remain long while a few monomers "unzip" from the ends. However, the **number of polymer molecules does not change rapidly** for true unzipping.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Bead String Metaphor:**
Imagine a long necklace (the polymer chain) made of identical coloured beads (monomers). Now imagine the necklace catches fire at one end. The flame travels slowly, consuming one bead at a time. The beads pop off individually (as monomers), while the rest of the necklace remains intact. This is exactly what happens in chain-end degradation — only the *end* beads are released sequentially.

### 1.3 Random Degradation (Random Chain Scission)

**Formal Definition:** Random degradation is a degradation mechanism in which main-chain bonds are broken at *random positions* along the polymer backbone, producing smaller chain fragments of varying lengths. The scission events follow a purely statistical distribution with no preference for chain ends.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Brittle Twig Metaphor:**
Take a long bamboo stick (the polymer chain) and start tapping it randomly along its length. Splinters (chain fragments) will break off from *random positions* — sometimes from the middle, sometimes near the middle, sometimes close to the ends. The stick becomes many smaller pieces of varied sizes. This represents random chain scission — break points have *no positional preference*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In random degradation, the **molecular weight decreases rapidly** (because each scission halves a chain, etc.), and the **number of polymer molecules increases significantly** because every scission creates one additional chain.

### 1.4 Comparison Snapshot — Chain-End vs. Random Degradation

| Property | Chain-End Degradation | Random Degradation |
|---|---|---|
| **Site of scission** | Exclusively at chain ends | Anywhere along the backbone (statistical) |
| **Product** | Mostly monomer | Mixture of fragments of all lengths |
| **Molecular weight drop** | Very slow | Very rapid |
| **Activation energy** | ≈ Polymerisation Eₐ | ≈ Bond dissociation energy (~ 80–250 kJ/mol) |
| **Depropagation length (zip length)** | Large (50–10,000 units) | Small (~ 1) |
| **Ceiling temperature role** | Strong dependence | Negligible |
| **Classical example** | PMMA at 300 °C | Polyethylene, polyisobutylene |
| **Mechanism class** | Reverse of chain polymerisation | Reversible/non-reversible chain scission |

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Activation energy of polymerisation (E_p):** Typically **80–120 kJ/mol** for radical systems.
- **Bond dissociation energy (C–C backbone):** ~ **350 kJ/mol**.
- **Ceiling temperature (T_c):** Critical temperature above which depolymerisation dominates.
- **Zip length (Z):** Average number of monomer units released per radical initiation event.
- **Degree of polymerisation (X_n):** Average number of monomer units per polymer chain.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Chain-End Degradation — Mechanism in Depth

#### 2.1.1 The Five Sequential Steps

Chain-end degradation proceeds through a radical chain mechanism that is the *mirror image* of chain polymerisation:

**Step 1 — Initiation (Chain-End Activation)**
A terminal bond breaks to form an active radical (or ionic) centre at the chain end.

$$M_n^{\bullet} \longrightarrow M^{\bullet} + M_{n-1}$$

Here, a long polymer radical $M_n^{\bullet}$ releases a single monomer radical or a stable monomer unit, leaving behind a shorter active chain $M_{n-1}^{\bullet}$.

**Step 2 — Depropagation (Zipper Unzipping)**
The active chain end sequentially releases monomers by a chain reaction:

$$M_{n-1}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow M^{\bullet} + M_{n-2}$$

This continues rapidly (the "zipper effect"). The number of monomers released per initiation event is the **zip length (Z)**.

**Step 3 — Propagation (in reverse)**
Each monomer released may re-add to another chain radical. Whether it escapes as free monomer depends on the **ceiling temperature**.

**Step 4 — Chain Transfer (occasional)**
Sometimes the active centre attacks an internal bond, transferring radical activity inward — slowing the degradation rate.

**Step 5 — Termination**
Two radicals combine, disproportionating or coupling — halting degradation.

#### 2.1.2 The Concept of Zip Length (Z)

**Zip Length** is the *most important* KTU parameter for chain-end degradation:

$$Z = \frac{\text{Rate of depropagation}}{\text{Rate of termination}} = \frac{R_{dp}}{R_t}$$

- **Large Z (≥ 100):** Pure unzipping behaviour → mostly monomer recovered (e.g., poly(methyl methacrylate) ≈ 10,000).
- **Small Z (~ 1):** No unzipping — chain-end degradation effectively ceases.
- **Moderate Z (10–100):** Mixed mechanism producing oligomers + monomer.

#### 2.1.3 Real-World Utility of Chain-End Degradation

- **Recycling of polymers:** Thermal depolymerisation of PMMA gives back pure MMA monomer — the *cleanest recycling pathway*.
- **Kinetic studies:** Provides Eₐ and termination rate constants because Eₐ(depolymerisation) = Eₐ(polymerisation).
- **Ceiling temperature determination:** Critical for evaluating polymerisation feasibility of a monomer.

### 2.2 Random Degradation — Mechanism in Depth

#### 2.2.1 The Probabilistic Foundation

Random degradation assumes that *every bond* along the backbone has an equal probability of being broken in a given time interval. This leads to a **statistical (Poisson) distribution** of scission events.

Let:
- $N_0$ = Initial number of polymer molecules
- $p$ = Probability of any single bond being broken
- $n$ = Number of bonds per chain (initially $n_0$)

After random scission with $p$ broken bonds per chain on average:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{X_{n,0}}{1 + p}$$

where $X_{n,0}$ is the initial number-average degree of polymerisation.

#### 2.2.2 The Three Sub-Categories of Random Degradation

1. **Chain Scission (true random):** Each bond break produces two fragments.
2. **Cross-linking (occasionally grouped with random degradation):** Bonds form *between* chains — increasing molecular weight.
3. **Chain stripping:** Side groups are removed randomly, leaving a modified backbone (e.g., PVC dehydrochlorination).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Random chain scission **decreases** molecular weight but **increases** the *number* of molecules. Cross-linking **increases** molecular weight and **decreases** the number of molecules. Students frequently confuse these trends.

#### 2.2.3 Step-by-Step Behaviour of Random Degradation

The molecular weight drops sharply with only a small fraction of bonds broken:

| Bonds broken per chain (p) | $\overline{X}_n$ as fraction of $X_{n,0}$ |
|---|---|
| 0.01 | 1/1.01 ≈ 99 % |
| 0.10 | 1/1.10 ≈ 91 % |
| 0.50 | 1/1.50 ≈ 67 % |
| 1.00 | 1/2.00 = 50 % |
| 2.00 | 1/3.00 ≈ 33 % |
| 9.00 | 1/10.00 = 10 % |

This is the *hallmark signature* of random degradation — even at very low bond-break probability, $\overline{X}_n$ drops substantially.

#### 2.2.4 Real-World Utility of Random Degradation

- **Determining original molecular weight distribution:** Studying degradation kinetics extrapolated back to zero time.
- **Predicting polymer service life:** Used in accelerated aging studies.
- **Waste management & incineration:** Establishing safe decomposition temperatures.
- **Controlled drug release:** Designing degradable polymers for biomedical scaffolds.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Typical Range / Unit | Applicable Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Zip length | $Z = R_{dp} / R_t$ | 1 → 10,000 | Chain-end |
| 2 | Rate of depropagation | $R_{dp} = k_{dp} [M^{\bullet}]$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ | Chain-end |
| 3 | Activation energy equality | $E_a(\text{depolym.}) \approx E_a(\text{polym.})$ | 80–120 kJ/mol | Chain-end |
| 4 | Ceiling temperature | $T_c = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{\Delta S^{\circ}}$ | K | Chain-end |
| 5 | Number-average DP after random scission | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{X_{n,0}}{1 + p}$ | dimensionless | Random |
| 6 | Number of molecules after scission | $N = N_0(1 + p)$ | dimensionless | Random |
| 7 | Mass fraction of monomer released | $w_1 = 1 - e^{-p}$ | dimensionless | Random |
| 8 | First-order rate constant | $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | s⁻¹ | Both |
| 9 | Half-life of original bonds | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | s | Random |
| 10 | Initial DP for incipient gelation (cross-link) | $p_c = 1/(X_{n,0} - 1)$ | dimensionless | Random |

> [!WARNING]
> **Note on markdown rendering:** All absolute-value and bar notations are written using `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe `|` character to avoid breaking the markdown table grid in your renderer.

### 2.4 Conceptual 'Why & How' Summary

| Question | Chain-End | Random |
|---|---|---|
| **Why** does it start at the end? | Active centre is generated at the terminal bond; reverse propagation is energetically favourable at high T. | Every backbone bond is equally weak; scission is purely statistical. |
| **How** does molecular weight change? | Slowly — only one bond (the end bond) is broken at a time per chain. | Rapidly — each internal break splits a chain in two. |
| **How** is monomer recovered? | Monomers are the *dominant* product. | Monomers are a *minor* product; oligomers dominate. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Number-Average Degree of Polymerisation After Random Scission

This is the **central KTU derivation** for random degradation.

#### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

- Consider $N_0$ polymer molecules, each containing $n_0$ backbone bonds (so each chain has $n_0 + 1$ monomer units, but bonds ≈ monomers for long chains).
- Each bond has an **equal and independent probability** $p$ of being broken during the experiment.
- Each scission event converts one molecule into two — the *number* of molecules increases by one per scission.

#### 3.1.2 Counting Molecules After Scission

**Initial state:**
$$N_0 \text{ molecules}, \quad N_0 \cdot n_0 \text{ total bonds}$$

**After scission:**
- Total number of bonds broken = $p \cdot N_0 \cdot n_0$
- Each scission adds **one** new molecule.

Therefore, the final number of molecules is:

$$N = N_0 + p \cdot N_0 \cdot n_0$$

We can simplify by introducing the **fractional scission parameter** $\alpha = p \cdot n_0$ (average bonds broken per initial chain):

$$N = N_0 (1 + \alpha)$$

#### 3.1.3 Counting Total Monomer Units

Total monomer units are *conserved* (degradation does not destroy monomers, only rearranges them):

$$M_{total} = N_0 \cdot (n_0 + 1) \approx N_0 \cdot n_0$$

#### 3.1.4 Deriving the Number-Average DP

By definition:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{\text{Total monomer units}}{\text{Number of molecules}}$$

Substituting:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{N_0 \cdot n_0}{N_0(1 + \alpha)}$$

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{n_0}{1 + \alpha} = \frac{n_0}{1 + p \cdot n_0}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $n_0$:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1/n_0 + p}$$

For very long chains ($1/n_0 \approx 0$):

$$\overline{X}_n \approx \frac{n_0}{1 + p \cdot n_0} = \frac{X_{n,0}}{1 + p \cdot X_{n,0}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Compact Form:**
> $$\boxed{\;\overline{X}_n \;=\; \dfrac{X_{n,0}}{1 + p\,X_{n,0}}\;}$$
> where $p$ is the *fraction of bonds broken* (not bonds broken per chain — careful!).

> [!WARNING]
> **Most common student error:** Some textbooks define $p$ as bonds broken per chain. In that case, $p$ in the equation above is replaced by $p/(n_0)$. KTU 2024 follow the standard form where $p$ = fraction of total bonds broken. **Always read the definition from the question stem.**

#### 3.1.5 Limiting Cases (Sanity Check)

**Case 1 — No degradation** ($p = 0$):
$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{X_{n,0}}{1 + 0} = X_{n,0} \quad \checkmark$$

**Case 2 — One bond broken in every chain on average** ($p \cdot X_{n,0} = 1$):
$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{X_{n,0}}{1 + 1} = \frac{X_{n,0}}{2} \quad \checkmark$$
(Number of molecules doubles; chain length halves.)

**Case 3 — Many bonds broken** ($p \cdot X_{n,0} \gg 1$):
$$\overline{X}_n \approx \frac{X_{n,0}}{p \cdot X_{n,0}} = \frac{1}{p} \quad \checkmark$$
(Molecular weight is set by the *spacing between scission events*, not the original chain length.)

### 3.2 Derivation of Zip Length (Z)

#### 3.2.1 Definition by Rate Ratios

Depropagation and termination are competitive processes:

$$\text{Zip length:}\quad Z = \frac{\text{Rate of monomer release}}{\text{Rate of radical termination}}$$

$$\boxed{\;Z \;=\; \dfrac{R_{dp}}{R_t} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{dp}\,[M_n^{\bullet}]}{2 k_t\,[M_n^{\bullet}]^2} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{dp}}{2 k_t\,[M_n^{\bullet}]\;}\;}$$

#### 3.2.2 Interpretation

- High $k_{dp}$ (easy unzipping) → large Z
- High $k_t$ or high $[M_n^{\bullet}]$ → small Z (radicals terminate fast)

### 3.3 Derivation of Ceiling Temperature (T_c)

At equilibrium between polymerisation and depolymerisation:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T_c \Delta S^{\circ} = 0$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\;T_c \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{\text{polymerisation}}}{\Delta S^{\circ}_{\text{polymerisation}}}\;}$$

- If operating $T > T_c$ → depolymerisation is thermodynamically favoured.
- If operating $T < T_c$ → polymerisation is favoured.

**Example:** α-methyl styrene has $T_c \approx 61\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ — so even at room temperature it slowly depolymerises.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour Comparison

| Quantity being tracked | Chain-End (Depolymerisation) | Random Scission |
|---|---|---|
| $X_n$ vs. time | Drops very slowly (linear in $t$) | Drops sharply (exponential-like) |
| Number of molecules vs. time | Roughly constant (only ends activated) | Increases linearly with $p$ |
| Monomer yield | Very high (> 90 %) | Low (mostly oligomers) |
| Rate law (typical) | First-order in active ends | First-order in bonds present |
| Effect of inhibitors | Strong effect | Weak effect |
| Volume change during reaction | Decreases (gas monomer escapes) | None usually |
| Catalyst sensitivity | Highly sensitive | Less sensitive |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A polymer sample has initial $X_{n,0} = 10{,}000$. After random degradation, $5\,\%$ of the total backbone bonds are broken. Calculate the final number-average DP and the new number of molecules (per 1 mole of initial polymer).

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**
$$X_{n,0} = 10{,}000,\quad p = 0.05$$

**Step 2 — Apply the random degradation equation.**

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{X_{n,0}}{1 + p\,X_{n,0}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $p \cdot X_{n,0}$.**

$$p \cdot X_{n,0} = 0.05 \times 10{,}000 = 500$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\overline{X}_n$.**

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{10{,}000}{1 + 500} = \frac{10{,}000}{501} \approx 19.96$$

**Step 5 — Compute the number of molecules.**
Initial: $N_0 = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ chains per mole.
Scissions per chain = $p \cdot X_{n,0} = 500$.
New chains per initial chain = $1 + 500 = 501$.

$$N_{final} = 501 \times N_0 = 501 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.017 \times 10^{26}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check.**
The molecular weight dropped from $10{,}000$ units per chain to ~ $20$ units per chain — a dramatic 500-fold reduction — confirming random degradation's hallmark behaviour.

$$\boxed{\;\overline{X}_n \approx 19.96 \quad ; \quad N_{final} \approx 3.02 \times 10^{26}\ \text{chains/mol}\;}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic 1 — Degradation Pathway Comparison (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Chain M_n] --> B{Degradation Mechanism}
    B --> C[Chain-End Degradation]
    B --> D[Random Degradation]
    C --> C1[Initiation at chain end]
    C1 --> C2[Active radical formed]
    C2 --> C3[Sequential monomer release]
    C3 --> C4[Depropagation continues]
    C4 --> C5{Zip length Z}
    C5 --> C6[Large Z: pure monomer]
    C5 --> C7[Small Z: oligomers]
    D --> D1[Statistical bond scission]
    D1 --> D2[Bonds break at any position]
    D2 --> D3[Two fragments per scission]
    D3 --> D4[Chain length distribution widens]
    D4 --> D5[Number of molecules rises]
    D5 --> D6[Molecular weight drops sharply]
```

### 4.2 Schematic 2 — Mechanism Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INIT[Initiation Stage]
        I1[Thermal/Photochemical trigger] --> I2[Radical at chain end OR internal]
    end
    subgraph PROP[Propagation Stage]
        P1[Chain-end: monomer unzipping]
        P2[Random: internal bond cleavage]
    end
    subgraph TERM[Termination Stage]
        T1[Radical coupling]
        T2[Radical disproportionation]
        T3[Chain transfer to backbone]
    end
    INIT --> PROP --> TERM
    P1 --> Out1[Product: monomer dominant]
    P2 --> Out2[Product: oligomers + fragments]
    Out1 --> APP1[Application: polymer recycling]
    Out2 --> APP2[Application: mechanical recycling / feedstock]
```

### 4.3 Schematic 3 — Molecular Weight vs. Degradation Time

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Initial polymer DP Xn0] --> Q1{Mechanism?}
    Q1 --> R1[Chain-end degradation]
    Q1 --> R2[Random degradation]
    R1 --> S1[Xn decreases slowly and linearly with time]
    R2 --> S2[Xn decreases rapidly and asymptotically]
    S1 --> End1[Monomer-rich product stream]
    S2 --> End2[Mixed oligomer stream]
```

### 4.4 Schematic 4 — Zip Length Concept (Visual Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Long polymer chain] --> Z[Active radical at one end]
    Z --> M1[Release monomer 1]
    M1 --> M2[Release monomer 2]
    M2 --> Mn[Release monomer n]
    Mn --> T{Termination event}
    T --> Stop[Chain activity stops]
```

### 4.5 Schematic 5 — Random Bond Scission Event

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Chain[Initial polymer chain] --> B{Bond position broken}
    B --> P1[Near end produces short + long fragment]
    B --> P2[Middle produces two nearly equal fragments]
    B --> P3[Random position chosen by statistics]
    P3 --> Result[Polydisperse fragment distribution]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Tip:** In all five diagrams, the *left-to-right* flow mirrors the *cause-to-effect* direction of degradation. Memorise this orientation for quick recall during exam writing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define chain-end degradation. What is meant by zip length?**

**Model Answer:**

Chain-end degradation (depolymerisation) is a degradation mechanism in which the polymer chain progressively breaks down from one of its active ends, releasing monomer units one after another in a sequential manner, analogous to the unzipping of a zipper.

Zip length (Z) is defined as the average number of monomer units released per radical initiation event before termination occurs:

$$Z = \frac{\text{Rate of depropagation } (R_{dp})}{\text{Rate of termination } (R_t)} = \frac{k_{dp}}{2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]}$$

A large zip length (Z > 100) characterises a polymer that almost completely reverts to monomer (e.g., PMMA), whereas a small zip length (Z ≈ 1) means the chain essentially does not undergo unzipping.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Distinguish between random degradation and chain-end degradation with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Chain-End Degradation | Random Degradation |
|---|---|---|
| Site of scission | Only at chain ends | Any backbone bond (statistical) |
| Major product | Monomer | Mixture of fragments (oligomers) |
| Molecular weight | Drops slowly | Drops rapidly |
| Example 1 | Poly(methyl methacrylate) at 300 °C | Polyethylene (thermal) |
| Example 2 | Polystyrene at 300 °C | Polyisobutylene (thermal) |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries 14 marks with mandatory internal choice. Students answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. Both choices are provided below with full sub-part (a) [7 marks] and sub-part (b) [7 marks] solutions.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of chain-end degradation of polymers in detail. Discuss the role of zip length and ceiling temperature with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the number-average degree of polymerisation ($\overline{X}_n$) as a function of the fraction of bonds broken ($p$) during random degradation. Discuss the limiting cases. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Mechanism of Chain-End Degradation:**

Chain-end degradation proceeds through a radical chain mechanism in five steps, which mirror the five steps of chain polymerisation but in reverse order.

**Step 1 — Initiation:** A terminal C–C bond of the polymer chain is broken (thermally, photochemically, or catalytically) to generate a terminal radical. For example:

$$M_n \xrightarrow{\Delta} M^{\bullet} + M_{n-1}^{\bullet}$$

**Step 2 — Depropagation (unzipping):** The newly formed chain-end radical undergoes successive $\beta$-scissions, releasing one monomer unit per step:

$$M_{n-1}^{\bullet} \rightarrow M_{n-2}^{\bullet} + M$$
$$M_{n-2}^{\bullet} \rightarrow M_{n-3}^{\bullet} + M$$
$$\vdots$$
$$\rightarrow \text{many monomer units}$$

The number of monomer units released in this fashion before termination is the **zip length Z**.

**Step 3 — Chain transfer (occasionally):** The active radical may attack an internal bond of another chain, transferring activity inward — slowing or halting the unzipping.

**Step 4 — Re-propagation (reverse of Step 2):** Some released monomers may re-add to active centres; whether they escape permanently depends on the ceiling temperature.

**Step 5 — Termination:** Two radicals couple or disproportionate, halting the process.

**Role of Zip Length (Z):** Already defined above. **PMMA** at 300 °C has Z ≈ 10,000, making it the *ideal* chain-end degradation polymer. **Polystyrene** at 300 °C has Z ≈ 200 — significant unzipping but less complete. **Polyethylene** has Z ≈ 0.01 — no chain-end degradation.

**Role of Ceiling Temperature (T_c):** Above $T_c$, the polymer is thermodynamically unstable and depolymerisation is spontaneous. Below $T_c$, the monomer preferentially polymerises. $\alpha$-methyl styrene has $T_c \approx 61\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ — it cannot be stored at room temperature for long without degradation.

---

**Model Solution to Question A (b):**

Already derived in Section 3.1 above. The student should reproduce:

$$\boxed{\;\overline{X}_n = \frac{X_{n,0}}{1 + p\,X_{n,0}}\;}$$

with **three** limiting cases:

- $p = 0$: $\overline{X}_n = X_{n,0}$
- $p \cdot X_{n,0} = 1$: $\overline{X}_n = X_{n,0}/2$
- $p \cdot X_{n,0} \gg 1$: $\overline{X}_n \approx 1/p$

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of neat diagrams, describe the random chain scission mechanism of polymer degradation. Explain why the molecular weight drops sharply even when only a small fraction of bonds are broken. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polymer sample with initial number-average molecular weight $\overline{M}_{n,0} = 200{,}000\ \text{g/mol}$ and monomer unit mass $M_0 = 100\ \text{g/mol}$ undergoes random degradation such that $0.2\,\%$ of the bonds are broken. Calculate the new $\overline{M}_n$ and the number of new chains formed per mole of original polymer. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**Random Chain Scission Mechanism:**

In random degradation, scission events are not localised at any preferred site but occur at any of the $(n-1)$ backbone bonds with equal probability. The mechanism is best described as:

1. **Activation** (thermal/photochemical) of a backbone C–C bond, producing two radical fragments.
2. **$\beta$-scission** propagation — radicals undergo further internal cleavages.
3. **Termination** by radical combination or disproportionation.

The mathematical model assumes that **each bond has an independent probability $p$ of being broken** over the experimental timescale. After $N_0$ chains of $n_0$ bonds each undergo scission, the new number of chains is:

$$N = N_0(1 + p \cdot n_0)$$

and the new number-average DP is:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{X_{n,0}}{1 + p \cdot X_{n,0}}$$

**Why molecular weight drops sharply:**

Even when $p$ is very small, the denominator $1 + p X_{n,0}$ becomes large because $X_{n,0}$ is huge (typically $10^3$–$10^5$). A value of $p = 0.01$ in a chain of $X_{n,0} = 10{,}000$ halves the molecular weight. This explains the *sharp drop* hallmark of random degradation.

Diagrams to include: schematic of bonds at three positions (near end, middle, near end) all being broken with equal probability; a graph of $\overline{X}_n$ vs. $p$ showing the rapid decline.

---

**Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Given:**
$\overline{M}_{n,0} = 200{,}000\ \text{g/mol}$
$M_0 = 100\ \text{g/mol}$
$p = 0.2\,\% = 0.002$

**Step 1 — Compute initial DP.**
$$X_{n,0} = \frac{\overline{M}_{n,0}}{M_0} = \frac{200{,}000}{100} = 2000$$

**Step 2 — Compute $p \cdot X_{n,0}$.**
$$p \cdot X_{n,0} = 0.002 \times 2000 = 4$$

**Step 3 — Compute new DP.**
$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{X_{n,0}}{1 + p \cdot X_{n,0}} = \frac{2000}{1 + 4} = \frac{2000}{5} = 400$$

**Step 4 — Compute new $\overline{M}_n$.**
$$\overline{M}_n = \overline{X}_n \times M_0 = 400 \times 100 = 40{,}000\ \text{g/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Compute new number of chains.**
Initial chains per mole: $N_0 = N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$.
Scissions per original chain: $p \cdot X_{n,0} = 4$.
New chains per original chain: $1 + 4 = 5$.

$$N_{final} = 5 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.011 \times 10^{24}\ \text{chains/mol}$$

**Step 6 — Number of new chains formed:**
$$N_{new} = N_{final} - N_0 = 4 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 2.409 \times 10^{24}\ \text{new chains/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\;\overline{M}_n = 40{,}000\ \text{g/mol} \quad ; \quad N_{new} \approx 2.41 \times 10^{24}\ \text{chains/mol}\;}$$

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Chain-End & Random Degradation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $p$ definitions.** In some texts, $p$ = bonds broken per chain; in others, $p$ = fraction of bonds broken. KTU questions typically use $p$ = *fraction of bonds broken*. Wrong interpretation can cost **3–4 marks** out of 7.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $\overline{M}_n$ to $X_n$.** Always divide by $M_0$ before applying the random degradation formula.
> 3. **Mixing chain-end and random scission products.** Examiners *instantly* deduct marks if a "random degradation" answer claims "mostly monomer recovered."
> 4. **Skipping the limiting-case analysis.** Always show $p = 0$ and $p \cdot X_{n,0} = 1$ cases — these are **must-write** marks.
> 5. **Not drawing labelled diagrams.** A neat mechanism diagram earns **2 extra marks** in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 6. **Writing zip length as dimensionless but forgetting to define $R_{dp}$ and $R_t$ explicitly.** Always define each term once before using.
> 7. **Forgetting the ceiling temperature relation** $T_c = \Delta H^{\circ} / \Delta S^{\circ}$ in chain-end answers — a favourite 2-mark follow-up question.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Two principal modes of degradation:** Chain-end (depolymerisation/unzipping) and Random chain scission.
- [x] **Chain-end degradation** releases monomers sequentially from an active chain end — the reverse of chain polymerisation.
- [x] **Random degradation** breaks bonds at any backbone position with equal probability — produces polydisperse fragments.
- [x] **Zip length $Z = R_{dp} / R_t$** — large Z → mostly monomer; small Z → oligomers.
- [x] **Ceiling temperature $T_c = \Delta H^{\circ} / \Delta S^{\circ}$** — operating above $T_c$ favours depolymerisation.
- [x] **Random degradation formula:** $\overline{X}_n = X_{n,0} / (1 + p X_{n,0})$.
- [x] **Limiting cases:** $p = 0 \Rightarrow X_n = X_{n,0}$; $p X_{n,0} = 1 \Rightarrow X_n$ halved; $p X_{n,0} \gg 1 \Rightarrow X_n \approx 1/p$.
- [x] **Number of molecules** after random scission: $N = N_0(1 + p X_{n,0})$.
- [x] **Eₐ** for chain-end degradation ≈ Eₐ of polymerisation; for random degradation ≈ C–C bond dissociation energy.
- [x] **PMMA, polystyrene, α-methyl styrene** → classical chain-end examples.
- [x] **Polyethylene, polyisobutylene, polypropylene** → classical random degradation examples.
- [x] **Hallmark behaviour:** Chain-end → slow DP drop, mostly monomer; Random → sharp DP drop, polydisperse oligomers.
- [x] **Industrial significance:** Chain-end = clean monomer recovery / recycling; Random = service-life prediction, accelerated aging.
- [x] **Always convert** $\overline{M}_n$ to $X_n$ before applying the random scission formula in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degradation by Oxygen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Degradation by Oxygen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oxidative Degradation of Polymers** is defined as the irreversible deterioration of the physical, mechanical, optical, and chemical properties of a polymeric material resulting from the chemical reaction of atmospheric (molecular) oxygen — and its activated allotropes (singlet oxygen, ozone) — with the polymer backbone, side groups, or chain ends, typically proceeding via a **free-radical chain mechanism** that leads to chain scission, crosslinking, and the formation of oxygenated functional groups (hydroperoxides, carbonyls, hydroxyls).

In strict KTU terminology, this process is classified under the broader domain of **chemical ageing of polymers** and is one of the most commercially significant degradation pathways for both natural and synthetic macromolecules.

### Standard Classification of Oxygen-Mediated Degradation

| Sub-Type | Activating Agent | Conditions | Primary Outcome |
|----------|------------------|------------|-----------------|
| **Thermal Oxidation** | Heat + O₂ | Elevated T (≥ 100 °C) | Hydroperoxide → scission |
| **Photo-oxidation** | UV light + O₂ | Ambient sunlight | Carbonyl formation, yellowing |
| **Ozonolysis** | Ozone (O₃) | Stresses + atmospheric O₃ | Chain scission at double bonds |
| **Biodegradative Oxidation** | O₂ + enzymes | Biological environments | CO₂ + H₂O |
| **Thermo-oxidative** | Heat + UV + O₂ | Outdoor exposure | Combined mechanisms |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Rusting Polymer

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Rusting Plastic"**
> Imagine a long, freshly painted steel bridge. As rain and humid air attack it, oxygen slowly converts shiny iron (Fe) into flaky, brittle rust (Fe₂O₃). The metal did not "disappear" instantly — its strong Fe–Fe bonds were broken one-by-one by oxygen. Polymers degrade in **exactly the same way**: a long, strong polyethylene chain (the equivalent of "polished steel") is attacked by atmospheric O₂ molecule-by-molecule, breaking the strong C–C and C–H bonds into weaker C=O, –OOH, and –OH groups (the equivalent of "rust spots"). The result is the same: loss of strength, embrittlement, discoloration, and eventual failure.

The "rust" of polymers is visible as:
- **Yellowing** (e.g., old PVC pipes, white PVC turning brown)
- **Crazing and cracking** (e.g., tyre sidewalls, rubber seals)
- **Embrittlement** (e.g., aged plastic buckets snapping)
- **Loss of tensile strength** (e.g., brittle PP straps)

---

## 1.3 Thermodynamic Driving Force — Why Oxygen Attacks Polymers

The reaction between C–H/C–C bonds and O₂ is **thermodynamically spontaneous** but **kinetically hindered** at room temperature. This is the single most important concept in oxidative degradation.

### Standard Bond Dissociation Energies (BDE)

| Bond | BDE (kJ mol⁻¹) | Bond | BDE (kJ mol⁻¹) |
|------|----------------|------|----------------|
| C–H (primary) | **410** | O=O | **498** |
| C–H (tertiary) | **380** | O–H | **460** |
| C–C | **346** | C–O | **358** |
| C=C | **611** | C=O | **745** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although the O=O bond (**498 kJ mol⁻¹**) is *stronger* than the C–H bond (**410 kJ mol⁻¹**), the products of oxidation (C=O at **745 kJ mol⁻¹**, O–H at **460 kJ mol⁻¹**) are far more stable. The **net enthalpy change is highly exothermic (ΔH ≈ −400 kJ mol⁻¹ per mole of O₂)** — which is why oxidation is irreversible once initiated.

The kinetic barrier exists because O₂ in its ground state is a **triplet diradical** (•O–O•), while most organic C–H bonds are singlet. Spin restriction prevents the direct reaction until:
- Heat supplies the activation energy (thermal oxidation)
- UV light excites O₂ to singlet state or generates radicals (photo-oxidation)
- Mechanical stress creates radical sites (mechano-oxidation)

---

## 1.4 The Three Critical Oxygen Species

| Species | Formula | Electronic State | Reactivity |
|---------|---------|------------------|------------|
| Ground-state (triplet) oxygen | ³O₂ (•O–O•) | Diradical | Moderate; kinetically hindered |
| Singlet oxygen | ¹O₂ (¹Δg) | Excited singlet | Highly electrophilic; reacts with C=C |
| Ozone | O₃ | Bent, polar | Extremely reactive; ozonolysis |

---

## 1.5 KTU-Style Learning Outcomes Aligned with NEP 2020

After studying this topic, the B.Tech student must be able to:

- **CO4.1**: Define oxidative degradation with examples from commodity plastics.
- **CO4.2**: Draw and explain the free-radical chain mechanism (Bolland–Gee cycle).
- **CO4.3**: Identify the role of hydroperoxides as the primary initiators.
- **CO4.4**: Distinguish between thermal, photo-, and ozone-driven oxidation.
- **CO4.5**: Recommend suitable antioxidants and stabilizers for a given polymer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Bolland–Gee Free-Radical Chain Mechanism (1946)

This is the **canonical mechanism** universally accepted for polymer oxidation and is a *guaranteed KTU exam topic*. The mechanism was first proposed by J. L. Bolland and G. Gee and is sometimes called the **B–G cycle**.

The mechanism is divided into four stages:

### Stage 1: Chain Initiation (Rate = Rᵢ)

$$ R_i = \frac{d[\text{R}^\bullet]}{dt} = 2 k_d [\text{ROOH}] $$

A C–H bond (especially tertiary, allylic, or benzylic) is broken homolytically, generating the first carbon-centred macroradical **R•**.

$$ \text{RH} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{R}^\bullet + {^\bullet}\text{OOH} \quad \text{(slow)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> In real systems, initiation is rarely from RH + O₂ directly. It is mostly from the **decomposition of pre-existing hydroperoxides (ROOH)**, residual catalyst, or UV photons.

### Stage 2: Chain Propagation (Rate = Rₚ)

$$ \frac{-d[\text{O}_2]}{dt} = \frac{-d[\text{RH}]}{dt} = k_p [\text{R}^\bullet][\text{O}_2] $$

The alkyl radical reacts rapidly with oxygen to form a peroxy radical:

$$ \text{R}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{k_2} \text{ROO}^\bullet $$

The peroxy radical then abstracts a hydrogen from another polymer chain:

$$ \text{ROO}^\bullet + \text{RH} \xrightarrow{k_3} \text{ROOH} + \text{R}^\bullet $$

This second step **regenerates R•**, completing the autocatalytic cycle. The new **R•** re-enters Stage 2 → produces **ROOH** → which then decomposes to give **two new radicals** → exponential acceleration.

### Stage 3: Chain Branching (Hydroperoxide Decomposition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **key autocatalytic step** that makes oxidation "runaway".

$$ \text{ROOH} \xrightarrow{k_d} \text{RO}^\bullet + {^\bullet}\text{OH} $$

A single hydroperoxide produces **two radicals**, each of which can propagate a new chain. The rate of oxidation therefore accelerates with time — the classical **induction-period curve** seen experimentally (sigmoidal oxygen uptake vs. time).

At higher temperatures:

$$ 2\,\text{ROOH} \xrightarrow{k_d'} \text{ROO}^\bullet + \text{RO}^\bullet + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

### Stage 4: Chain Termination (Rate = Rₜ)

Two radicals combine or disproportionate:

$$ \text{R}^\bullet + \text{R}^\bullet \xrightarrow{k_t} \text{R–R} \quad \text{(recombination)} $$

$$ \text{ROO}^\bullet + \text{R}^\bullet \xrightarrow{k_t'} \text{ROOR} \quad \text{(peroxide crosslink)} $$

$$ \text{ROO}^\bullet + \text{ROO}^\bullet \xrightarrow{k_t''} \text{ROOR} + \text{O}_2 \quad \text{(Russell termination)} $$

---

## 2.2 Bolland–Gee Rate Equation (Master Equation for O₂ Uptake)

For steady-state oxidation (d[R•]/dt = 0), Bolland and Gee derived the rate of oxygen absorption:

$$ -\frac{d[\text{O}_2]}{dt} = \frac{k_2 \, [\text{O}_2] \sqrt{k_d [\text{ROOH}]_0}}{\sqrt{k_t}} $$

Where:

- $k_2$ = rate constant for R• + O₂
- $k_d$ = rate constant for ROOH decomposition
- $k_t$ = rate constant for termination
- $[\text{ROOH}]_0$ = initial hydroperoxide concentration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The square-root dependence on $[\text{ROOH}]$ and inverse square-root dependence on $k_t$ are characteristic fingerprints of radical oxidation kinetics. Questions asking "why does oxidation accelerate with time" are answered by this relationship.

---

## 2.3 Kinetic Chain Length (ν)

$$ \nu = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{k_p [\text{RH}]}{2 \sqrt{k_t \cdot R_i}} $$

A large ν means **one initiation event produces many propagation cycles** — typical values for polyolefin oxidation are ν ≈ 100–1000.

---

## 2.4 Oxidation Induction Time (OIT) — The Industrial Standard

> [!NOTE]
> This is the experimental parameter used in DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry) per **ASTM D3895** to rank polymer stability.

$$ \text{OIT} = \frac{[\text{ROOH}]_{\text{crit}}}{R_i} $$

A polymer with **higher OIT** is more oxidatively stable. Commercial PP grades have OIT values from a few minutes (unstabilized) to > 100 min (well-stabilized).

---

## 2.5 Activation Energy of Thermo-oxidative Degradation

Using the Arrhenius form for each step:

$$ k_i = A_i \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_i}{RT}\right) $$

For overall oxidation, the apparent activation energy is typically:

$$ E_a^{\text{oxid}} = \mathbf{80\text{–}120 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}} $$

| Polymer | $E_a$ (kJ mol⁻¹) | Half-life at 100 °C |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| Polyethylene (LDPE) | 95 | ~ 5 days |
| Polypropylene (PP) | 85 | ~ 2 days |
| Polystyrene (PS) | 105 | ~ 30 days |
| PVC | 110 | > 60 days |
| PTFE | 165 | Years |

---

## 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Cheat Sheet for Exam Day — Memorize This Block**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $R_i = 2 k_d [\text{ROOH}]$ | Initiation rate | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $R_p = k_2 [\text{R}^\bullet][\text{O}_2]$ | Propagation rate | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| 3 | $R_t = 2 k_t [\text{R}^\bullet]^2$ | Termination rate | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| 4 | $-\dfrac{d[\text{O}_2]}{dt} = \dfrac{k_2[\text{O}_2]\sqrt{k_d[\text{ROOH}]}}{\sqrt{k_t}}$ | B–G overall rate | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i}$ | Kinetic chain length | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{OIT} = \dfrac{[\text{ROOH}]_{\text{crit}}}{R_i}$ | Induction time | seconds |
| 7 | $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | Arrhenius | s⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\tau_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Half-life of polymer | s |
| 9 | $[\text{ROOH}] = [\text{ROOH}]_0 \, e^{k_d t}$ | Autocatalytic growth | mol L⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\Phi_{\text{carbonyl}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{1715}}{\ell \cdot \rho}$ | Carbonyl index (FTIR) | cm⁻¹ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Do **not** confuse **rate of oxidation** with **rate of oxygen uptake**. In volatile products, they differ by the stoichiometric factor. In solid-state polymer oxidation, they are usually equal because products stay in the matrix.

---

## 2.7 Structure–Reactivity Relationships (Why Some Polymers Oxidize Faster)

The C–H bond dissociation energy determines ease of oxidation:

| C–H Type | BDE (kJ mol⁻¹) | Polymer Example | Oxidation Susceptibility |
|----------|----------------|------------------|---------------------------|
| Primary (–CH₃) | 410 | PE | Low |
| Secondary (–CH₂–) | 395 | PE, PVC | Moderate |
| Tertiary (–CH<) | 380 | PP, PIB | **Very High** |
| Allylic (C=C–CH–) | 350 | Natural rubber | **Extreme** |
| Benzylic (Ph–CH–) | 355 | PS, PP with styrene | **High** |

> [!NOTE]
> **This is why polypropylene degrades far faster than polyethylene** — every repeat unit has a tertiary C–H (BDE = **380 kJ mol⁻¹**), making initiation facile. PP without stabilizer will be brittle within months of sun exposure.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Oxidation Concern | Stabilization Strategy |
|----------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Tyres (rubber) | Ozone cracking on sidewalls | Antiozonant waxes + 6-PPD |
| Food packaging | PP oxidation → off-flavour | Irganox 1010 + Irgafos 168 |
| Aerospace composites | High-temp epoxy oxidation | HALS + phenol antioxidants |
| Geomembranes (HDPE) | UV + soil oxidation | Carbon black + hindered phenols |
| Wire & cable insulation | XLPE thermal oxidation | Sulphur antioxidants + Cu deactivators |
| Medical implants (PEEK, UHMWPE) | Long-term oxidation in body | Vitamin E (α-tocopherol) blending |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bolland–Gee Master Rate Equation

This derivation is the **single most frequently asked analytical question** in KTU Polymer Science examinations for the Module-4 oxidative degradation section. We proceed in full detail.

### Step 1 — Write the Elementary Steps

We consider the simplified B–G scheme:

$$ \text{ROOH} \xrightarrow{k_d} \text{RO}^\bullet + {^\bullet}\text{OH} \quad (\text{initiation}) $$

$$ \text{R}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{k_2} \text{ROO}^\bullet \quad (\text{propagation 1}) $$

$$ \text{ROO}^\bullet + \text{RH} \xrightarrow{k_3} \text{ROOH} + \text{R}^\bullet \quad (\text{propagation 2}) $$

$$ \text{ROO}^\bullet + \text{ROO}^\bullet \xrightarrow{k_t} \text{Products} \quad (\text{termination}) $$

### Step 2 — Apply the Steady-State Approximation (SSA)

For the alkyl radical R• and peroxy radical ROO•, we assume d[R•]/dt = 0 and d[ROO•]/dt = 0.

**For R•:**
$$ \frac{d[\text{R}^\bullet]}{dt} = -k_2 [\text{R}^\bullet][\text{O}_2] + k_3 [\text{ROO}^\bullet][\text{RH}] = 0 $$

Solving for [R•]:

$$ [\text{R}^\bullet] = \frac{k_3 [\text{ROO}^\bullet][\text{RH}]}{k_2 [\text{O}_2]} $$

**For ROO•:**
$$ \frac{d[\text{ROO}^\bullet]}{dt} = k_2 [\text{R}^\bullet][\text{O}_2] - k_3 [\text{ROO}^\bullet][\text{RH}] - 2 k_t [\text{ROO}^\bullet]^2 = 0 $$

Substituting the [R•] from above, the first two terms cancel identically, leaving:

$$ 2 k_t [\text{ROO}^\bullet]^2 = R_i = 2 k_d [\text{ROOH}] $$

Solving for [ROO•]:

$$ [\text{ROO}^\bullet] = \sqrt{\frac{k_d [\text{ROOH}]}{k_t}} $$

### Step 3 — Write the Rate of Oxygen Uptake

Oxygen is consumed in the propagation step R• + O₂ → ROO•:

$$ -\frac{d[\text{O}_2]}{dt} = k_2 [\text{R}^\bullet][\text{O}_2] $$

From Step 2, $k_2 [\text{R}^\bullet][\text{O}_2] = k_3 [\text{ROO}^\bullet][\text{RH}]$ (this is exactly the SSA equality).

So:

$$ -\frac{d[\text{O}_2]}{dt} = k_3 [\text{ROO}^\bullet][\text{RH}] $$

Substitute [ROO•] from Step 2:

$$ -\frac{d[\text{O}_2]}{dt} = k_3 [\text{RH}] \sqrt{\frac{k_d [\text{ROOH}]}{k_t}} $$

### Step 4 — Final Form (Bolland–Gee Master Equation)

$$ \boxed{\,-\frac{d[\text{O}_2]}{dt} = k_3 [\text{RH}] \sqrt{\frac{k_d [\text{ROOH}]}{k_t}} \,} $$

This is the standard textbook form (often written with $k_2 [\text{O}_2]$ in place of $k_3[\text{RH}]$ when [O₂] is constant in solution).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the rate ACCELERATES with time:** [ROOH] is not constant — it *grows* autocatalytically. Differentiating [ROOH]:

$$ \frac{d[\text{ROOH}]}{dt} = k_3 [\text{ROO}^\bullet][\text{RH}] = \text{(rate of O}_2\text{ uptake)} $$

Therefore:

$$ \frac{d[\text{ROOH}]}{dt} = k_3 [\text{RH}] \sqrt{\frac{k_d [\text{ROOH}]}{k_t}} $$

This is an **autocatalytic ODE** of the form $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = A\sqrt{y}$, whose solution is a parabola:

$$ [\text{ROOH}](t) = \left(\frac{A t}{2}\right)^2 + [\text{ROOH}]_0 \;\;\text{(approx.)} $$

Hence the classical **sigmoidal oxygen-uptake curve** observed in every polymer ageing experiment.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Activation Energy from OIT Measurements

The Oxidation Induction Time (OIT) measured at several temperatures follows:

$$ \ln(\text{OIT}) = \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \text{const} $$

### Sample KTU Problem (Worked)

**Problem (KTU 2019 Model):** The OIT of an antioxidant-stabilized polypropylene sample is found to be:
- At 180 °C: OIT = 8.2 min
- At 200 °C: OIT = 1.6 min

Determine the activation energy of oxidation.

### Solution

Using $\ln(\text{OIT}) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T} + C$:

$$ \ln(8.2) = \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{453} + C \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2.104 = \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{453} + C $$

$$ \ln(1.6) = \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{473} + C \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.470 = \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{473} + C $$

Subtracting:

$$ 2.104 - 0.470 = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{453} - \frac{1}{473}\right) $$

$$ 1.634 = \frac{E_a}{8.314}\left(\frac{473 - 453}{453 \times 473}\right) $$

$$ 1.634 = \frac{E_a}{8.314}\cdot\frac{20}{214{,}269} $$

$$ 1.634 = \frac{E_a}{8.314}\cdot 9.334 \times 10^{-5} $$

$$ E_a = \frac{1.634 \times 8.314}{9.334 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{13.585}{9.334 \times 10^{-5}} $$

$$ \boxed{E_a \approx \mathbf{1.455 \times 10^{5} \text{ J mol}^{-1}} = \mathbf{145.5 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}}} $$

This is consistent with literature values for stabilized PP ($E_a$ ≈ 100–150 kJ mol⁻¹).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Carbonyl Index Calculation from FTIR

> [!NOTE]
> **The carbonyl index (CI)** is the experimental metric used in **ASTM D7252** for ranking oxidation levels of polyolefins. It is the ratio of the absorbance of the carbonyl peak (≈ **1715 cm⁻¹**) to that of a reference internal peak (e.g., the **1465 cm⁻¹** CH₂ scissoring band in PE).

$$ \text{CI} = \frac{A_{1715}}{A_{1465}} $$

| Sample | $A_{1715}$ | $A_{1465}$ | CI | Visual Appearance |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-----|--------------------|
| Virgin PE | 0.02 | 1.00 | **0.020** | White, glossy |
| Aged 30 days | 0.18 | 0.96 | **0.188** | Yellow |
| Aged 90 days | 0.55 | 0.91 | **0.604** | Cracked |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A CI > 0.1 typically signals *significant* oxidation and loss of mechanical properties. CI > 0.5 means the polymer has reached end-of-life service.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comprehensive Mechanism-to-Property Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix connects every mechanistic event to its **measurable** consequence — this is the framework for full-mark KTU answers.

| Stage | Chemical Event | Functional Group Formed | Analytical Signature | Property Change |
|-------|----------------|--------------------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Initiation | R–H → R• | Alkyl radical | EPR (spin trap) | — (silent) |
| Propagation 1 | R• + O₂ → ROO• | Peroxy radical | EPR (PBN spin adduct) | — (silent) |
| Propagation 2 | ROO• + RH → ROOH | Hydroperoxide | FTIR: **3410 cm⁻¹** (broad) | Slight discoloration |
| β-scission | ROO• → R′• + R″C=O | Carbonyl | FTIR: **1715 cm⁻¹** | **Yellowing** |
| Termination | 2 ROO• → ROOR | Peroxide crosslink | FTIR: **1100 cm⁻¹** | Hardening |
| Termination | R• + R• → R–R | C–C crosslink | DSC: ↑ Tg | **Embrittlement** |
| Norrish I | RC(=O)R → R• + R′C•O | Carbonyl + radicals | FTIR + EPR | Chalking |
| Norrish II | RC(=O)CR'H → RCH=C(OH)R′ | Vinyl + enol | FTIR: **1640, 910 cm⁻¹** | Cracking |

---

## 3.5 Detailed Worked Example — Kinetic Chain Length Calculation

**Problem:** In the oxidation of natural rubber (cis-1,4-polyisoprene) at 60 °C, the following data are obtained:

- $R_i = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹
- $k_p = 1.2 \times 10^3$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹
- $k_t = 5 \times 10^7$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹
- [RH] = 12 mol L⁻¹

Calculate the kinetic chain length $\nu$.

### Solution

$$ \nu = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{k_p [\text{RH}]}{2\sqrt{k_t \cdot R_i}} $$

First, compute the denominator:

$$ 2\sqrt{k_t \cdot R_i} = 2 \sqrt{5 \times 10^{7} \times 5 \times 10^{-8}} $$

$$ = 2 \sqrt{2.5} = 2 \times 1.581 = 3.162 \text{ s}^{-1} $$

Numerator:

$$ k_p [\text{RH}] = 1.2 \times 10^{3} \times 12 = 1.44 \times 10^{4} \text{ s}^{-1} $$

Therefore:

$$ \nu = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{4}}{3.162} \approx \mathbf{4.55 \times 10^{3}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each initiating radical produces ~ **4550 propagation events** before termination — i.e., one initiation event leads to ~ 4550 oxygen molecules being consumed. This is why natural rubber ages catastrophically without antioxidant (e.g., aged tyres crack on sidewalls).

---

## 3.6 Photo-oxidation — Quantum Yield and Beer–Lambert Coupling

For photo-oxidation, the initiation rate depends on **UV absorption**:

$$ R_i = 2.303 \, \Phi \, \varepsilon \, \ell \, I_0 \, [\text{ROOH}] $$

Where:
- $\Phi$ = quantum yield for radical formation (typical: **0.01–0.5**)
- $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity at the wavelength of interest
- $\ell$ = film thickness
- $I_0$ = incident UV intensity
- [ROOH] = local hydroperoxide concentration

This couples the **B–G kinetic scheme** to the **Beer–Lambert optical law** — explaining why thin films oxidize faster than thick sections and why pigments (TiO₂, carbon black) protect polymers by absorbing/scattering UV.

---

## 3.7 Ozonolysis Mechanism — Step-by-Step

Ozone (O₃) is highly electrophilic and reacts *only* with **C=C double bonds**:

**Step 1 — 1,3-Dipolar Cycloaddition:**

$$ \text{R}_2\text{C}=\text{CR}_2 + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{[molozonide]} $$

**Step 2 — Retro-[3+2] Cycloaddition:**

$$ \text{[molozonide]} \longrightarrow \text{[carbonyl oxide]} + \text{[carbonyl]} $$

**Step 3 — Recombination to Stable Ozonide:**

$$ \text{[carbonyl oxide]} + \text{[carbonyl]} \longrightarrow \text{[ozonide]} $$

The **ozonide** then hydrolyses to carboxylic acids, aldehydes, and hydrogen peroxide — leading to chain scission and surface cracking in unsaturated rubbers (NR, SBR, BR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Saturated polymers (PE, PP, PVC) are *immune* to ozonolysis because they have no C=C. Unsaturated rubbers must be protected with **antiozonants** such as N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N′-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine (6-PPD) and paraffin wax.
