# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q:** Define oxidative degradation of polymers. List the three principal stages of the Bolland–Gee free-radical mechanism.

**Model Answer:**

> **Oxidative degradation** is the irreversible chemical breakdown of a polymer caused by its reaction with atmospheric oxygen (³O₂), proceeding through a free-radical chain mechanism. It is one of the most commercially important ageing pathways for polyolefins and diene rubbers.

The three principal stages are:

1. **Initiation** — generation of the first alkyl macroradical R• by homolytic cleavage of a C–H bond (or by decomposition of pre-existing hydroperoxides).
2. **Propagation** — reaction of R• with O₂ to give ROO•, which then abstracts a hydrogen from another polymer chain, regenerating R• and forming a new hydroperoxide (ROOH).
3. **Termination** — combination or disproportionation of two radicals (R• + R•, ROO• + ROO•, or ROO• + R•) yielding stable but inactive products.

> [!NOTE]
> A fourth stage, **branching**, is sometimes included because the hydroperoxide (ROOH) decomposes to give two radicals, thereby accelerating the cycle autocatalytically.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q:** What are hydroperoxides? Why are they considered the "key intermediate" in polymer oxidation?

**Model Answer:**

**Hydroperoxides (ROOH)** are oxygen-containing functional groups in which an –OOH moiety is attached to a polymer backbone or side chain carbon. They are formed during the propagation stage of polymer oxidation.

They are the key intermediate because:

- They **decompose homolytically** under heat, UV, or metal catalysis to produce **two new free radicals** (RO• and •OH), each of which can start a new propagation chain.
- This makes the oxidation **autocatalytic**: the more hydroperoxide forms, the faster more radicals form, which produce more hydroperoxide — leading to the characteristic **induction-period** (sigmoidal) curve in oxygen-uptake experiments.
- The O–O bond is weak (**≈ 150 kJ mol⁻¹**), making it the "weak link" that initiates runaway oxidation.
- They can be detected quantitatively by **FTIR (broad –OH band at ~ 3410 cm⁻¹)** or by **iodometric titration**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q (a)** With the help of a neat mechanism, explain the free-radical oxidative degradation of polyethylene. Discuss the role of hydroperoxides. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Explain the classification of antioxidants with suitable examples. How do hindered amine light stabilizers (HALS) function? **(7 marks)**

---

### Detailed Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Free-Radical Mechanism for PE Oxidation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Initiation**

$$ \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–} \xrightarrow{\Delta,\,h\nu} \text{–CH}_2\text{–}\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}\text{–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{H}^\bullet $$

A C–H bond in the polyethylene backbone is cleaved homolytically, generating a secondary alkyl macroradical.

**Step 2 — Propagation (Reaction with O₂)**

$$ \text{–CH}_2\text{–}\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}\text{–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH(O}\overset{\bullet}{\text{O}}\text{)–CH}_2\text{–} $$

The alkyl radical adds O₂ (rate constant $k_2 \approx 10^9$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹ — diffusion-controlled).

**Step 3 — Hydrogen Abstraction**

$$ \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH(O}\overset{\bullet}{\text{O}}\text{)–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–} \longrightarrow \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH(OOH)–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{–CH}_2\text{–}\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}\text{–CH}_2\text{–} $$

A new hydroperoxide and a new alkyl radical are formed — the cycle propagates.

**Step 4 — Branching (Hydroperoxide Decomposition)**

$$ \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH(OOH)–CH}_2\text{–} \xrightarrow{k_d} \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH(O}\overset{\bullet}{\text{O}}\text{)–CH}_2\text{–} + \overset{\bullet}{\text{OH}} $$

The hydroxyl radical •OH is extremely reactive and immediately abstracts another hydrogen from the polymer, generating a second macroradical. Thus **one hydroperoxide → two new chains**.

**Step 5 — β-Scission (Chain Breaking)**

$$ \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH(O}\overset{\bullet}{\text{O}}\text{)–CH}_2\text{–} \longrightarrow \text{–CH}_2\text{–CHO} + \text{–CH}_2\text{–}\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}\text{–} $$

The alkoxy radical cleaves the C–C bond β to the radical, producing a **carbonyl group** (aldehyde/ketone, FTIR at **~ 1715 cm⁻¹**) and a new chain-end radical. This causes **chain scission** and **molecular weight reduction**.

**Step 6 — Termination**

$$ \text{–R}^\bullet + \text{–R}^\bullet \longrightarrow \text{–R–R–} \quad (\text{crosslink}) $$

$$ \text{–ROO}^\bullet + \text{–ROO}^\bullet \longrightarrow \text{–ROOR–} + \text{O}_2 \quad (\text{Russell termination}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Role of Hydroperoxides (asked explicitly):**
> 1. They are the **propagation product** that **drives autocatalysis**.
> 2. Their decomposition generates radicals that **accelerate** oxidation exponentially.
> 3. They are the **rate-limiting intermediate** — controlling [ROOH] controls the entire oxidation rate (per the Bolland–Gee equation: $R \propto \sqrt{[\text{ROOH}]}$).
> 4. They are the **target** of secondary antioxidants (phosphites, thioesters) which reduce them to inert alcohols.

---

#### Part (b) — Antioxidant Classification & HALS (7 marks)

**Classification of Antioxidants:**

| Class | Function | Mechanism | Examples |
|-------|----------|-----------|----------|
| **Primary (chain-breaking)** | Donate H• to ROO• | Hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) | Irganox 1010, BHT, 6-PPD |
| **Secondary (preventive)** | Decompose ROOH | Reduction / peroxide decomposition | Irgafos 168, DSTDP |
| **HALS** | Scavenge radicals catalytically | Denisov cycle (nitroxyl regeneration) | Tinuvin 770, Chimassorb 944 |
| **UV absorbers** | Filter harmful UV | Absorption + thermal dissipation | Tinuvin P, benzotriazoles |
| **Metal deactivators** | Chelate trace metals | Complexation | Salicylaldehyde oximes |

**How HALS Work — The Denisov Cycle:**

HALS do **not** donate a hydrogen atom. They function via a **catalytic radical-scavenging cycle**:

**Step 1:** The HALS amine (R₂N–R′) is oxidized by ROO• to form a stable **nitroxyl radical (R₂N–O•)**:

$$ \text{R}_2\text{N–R}' + \text{ROO}^\bullet \longrightarrow \text{R}_2\text{N–O}^\bullet + \text{ROOR} $$

**Step 2:** The nitroxyl scavenges an alkyl radical:

$$ \text{R}_2\text{N–O}^\bullet + \text{R}^\bullet \longrightarrow \text{R}_2\text{N–OR} $$

**Step 3:** The alkoxyamine reacts with a peroxy radical, regenerating the nitroxyl:

$$ \text{R}_2\text{N–OR} + \text{ROO}^\bullet \longrightarrow \text{R}_2\text{N–O}^\bullet + \text{ROOR} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The remarkable feature of HALS is that the nitroxyl radical is regenerated — it acts catalytically.** A single HALS molecule can scavenge **~ 50–100 radicals** before being consumed. This is why HALS are used at very low loadings (0.1–0.5 wt %) yet provide exceptional UV protection for polypropylene fibres and automotive coatings.

---

### ⭐ Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q (a)** Explain the mechanism of ozonolysis of natural rubber. Why are saturated polymers immune to ozone attack? **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Write a detailed note on the photo-oxidative degradation of polymers, including the role of chromophores and the Beer–Lambert coupling. **(7 marks)**

---

### Detailed Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Ozonolysis of Natural Rubber (7 marks)

Natural rubber (cis-1,4-polyisoprene) contains a C=C double bond in every repeat unit, making it extremely susceptible to ozone attack.

**Step 1 — 1,3-Dipolar Cycloaddition (Criegee Intermediate)**

$$ \text{–CH}_2\text{–C(CH}_3\text{)=CH–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{[Primary molozonide]} $$

The ozone adds across the double bond to form a 5-membered molozonide (1,2,3-trioxolane).

**Step 2 — Retro [3+2] Cycloaddition**

$$ \text{[Primary molozonide]} \longrightarrow \text{[Carbonyl oxide]} + \text{[Carbonyl fragment]} $$

The molozonide is unstable and cleaves into a carbonyl (e.g., levulinaldehyde) and a carbonyl oxide (Criegee intermediate).

**Step 3 — Recombination**

$$ \text{[Carbonyl oxide]} + \text{[Carbonyl]} \longrightarrow \text{[Secondary ozonide]} $$

A more stable 1,2,4-trioxolane is formed.

**Step 4 — Hydrolysis / Decomposition**

$$ \text{[Ozonide]} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Carboxylic acids} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{Aldehydes} $$

The chain is **cleaved** at the original C=C position, generating two fragments terminated by –COOH and –CHO groups. The molecular weight drops sharply and **stress-cracking** occurs (especially in stretched rubber).

**Why Saturated Polymers Are Immune:**

Saturated polymers (PE, PP, PVC, PTFE) have **no C=C double bonds** in the backbone. Ozone is an electrophile that **only** attacks π-bonds via the Criegee cycloaddition. Without a π-bond, there is no reaction pathway. Saturated polyolefins are therefore inherently ozone-resistant.

**Protection of Rubber:**

- **Antiozonant waxes** (paraffin waxes) bloom to the surface and form a protective physical film.
- **Chemical antiozonants** like **6-PPD** react with ozone at the surface, sacrificing themselves to protect the rubber (a "scavenger" mechanism).

---

#### Part (b) — Photo-oxidative Degradation of Polymers (7 marks)

**Mechanism Overview:**

Photo-oxidation is the combined action of UV light and atmospheric oxygen. The photons (typically **λ < 400 nm**, i.e., UV-A and UV-B) supply the activation energy for both radical generation and oxygen activation.

**Step 1 — Light Absorption by Chromophores:**

Polymers do not absorb UV directly (PE, PP are transparent in UV). However, **impurities** act as chromophores:

- Carbonyl groups (C=O from prior oxidation) — absorb at **~ 280 nm**
- Hydroperoxides (ROOH) — absorb at **~ 210 nm** and dissociate on absorption
- Catalyst residues (Ti, Zr from Ziegler–Natta synthesis)
- Aromatic rings (in PS, PET) — intrinsic chromophores
- Conjugated unsaturations

**Step 2 — Norrish Type I and II Reactions:**

Carbonyl groups, once excited (C=O*), undergo:

- **Norrish I:** $\text{C=O}^* \longrightarrow \text{R}^\bullet + \text{R}'\text{C}^\bullet\text{O}$ (α-cleavage)
- **Norrish II:** $\text{C=O}^* \longrightarrow \text{ene} + \text{enol}$ (intramolecular H-transfer)

Both generate radicals that feed the B–G cycle.

**Step 3 — Initiation from Photolysis of ROOH:**

$$ \text{ROOH} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{RO}^\bullet + {^\bullet}\text{OH} \quad (\Phi = 0.1 \text{ to } 0.5) $$

The quantum yield $\Phi$ is the number of radicals produced per photon absorbed.

**Step 4 — Beer–Lambert Coupling — Why Thin Films Degrade Faster:**

Because UV intensity decays exponentially with depth:

$$ I(z) = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon c z} $$

Where $\varepsilon$ is the molar absorptivity, $c$ is the chromophore concentration, and $z$ is the depth. The **rate of initiation** is highest at the **surface** and lowest in the bulk. Therefore:

- Thin films (< 50 μm) oxidize *throughout* and fail quickly.
- Thick sections (> 1 mm) develop a **surface-eroded** "chalky" layer with a relatively unaffected core.

**Step 5 — Acceleration of B–G Cycle:**

The photogenerated radicals enter the Bolland–Gee cycle, accelerating hydroperoxide accumulation, carbonyl formation, and chain scission. This is the reason **yellowing** appears first at the surface of white plastic articles.

**Step 6 — Stabilization:**

- **UV absorbers** (benzotriazoles, benzophenones) compete for photons.
- **HALS** (Tinuvin 770) scavenge the photogenerated radicals.
- **Pigments** (carbon black, rutile TiO₂) absorb/scatter UV photons.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic**
>
> 1. **Confusing "oxidation" with "ozonolysis"** — These are two *different* mechanisms with different initiators. Oxidation requires elevated temperature or UV; ozonolysis attacks only C=C bonds at room temperature. Examiners **deduct 2 marks** if you do not distinguish.
> 2. **Writing the propagation as a single step** — Always show R• + O₂ → ROO• as a *separate* step from ROO• + RH → ROOH. Merging them is the most common mechanistic error.
> 3. **Forgetting the role of H atom in propagation 2** — The peroxy radical abstracts an H atom, not the O atom. Drawing it correctly is worth **1 mark**.
> 4. **Confusing primary and secondary antioxidants** — Primary (e.g., hindered phenols) scavenge radicals; secondary (e.g., phosphites) decompose peroxides. Many students mix them up.
> 5. **Omitting the "autocatalytic" feature** — The hydroperoxide accumulation is what makes oxidation *accelerate*. If you skip this, you lose **2 marks**.
> 6. **Not mentioning the Denisov cycle** for HALS — Examiners expect the catalytic regeneration of the nitroxyl radical as the key feature.
> 7. **Neglecting units in rate equations** — Always state mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹. Students frequently write only "M/s" which is ambiguous.
> 8. **Forgetting that saturated polymers resist ozone** — It is a classic 2-mark question to test this understanding.
> 9. **Mixing photolysis and thermolysis mechanisms** — Photolysis involves $h\nu$ (photon) and proceeds via excited states; thermolysis proceeds via ground-state bond homolysis.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Exam Day — Must Know**

- [ ] **Definition:** Oxidative degradation = reaction of polymer with O₂ (or O₃, ¹O₂) via a free-radical chain mechanism.
- [ ] **Mechanism:** Bolland–Gee (1946) — Initiation → Propagation (×2) → Branching → Termination.
- [ ] **Hydroperoxide (ROOH)** is the *key* intermediate; its decomposition autocatalyses the cycle.
- [ ] **B–G Rate Equation:** $\;-\dfrac{d[\text{O}_2]}{dt} = k_3[\text{RH}]\sqrt{\dfrac{k_d[\text{ROOH}]}{k_t}}\;$
- [ ] **OIT (Oxidation Induction Time):** measured by DSC per ASTM D3895; higher = more stable.
- [ ] **Arrhenius:** $\;E_a \approx$ **80–120 kJ mol⁻¹** for typical polyolefins; PP < PE due to tertiary C–H.
- [ ] **B–G rate accelerates** with time because [ROOH] grows autocatalytically.
- [ ] **Primary antioxidants** (hindered phenols, secondary amines) donate H• to ROO•.
- [ ] **Secondary antioxidants** (phosphites, thioesters) reduce ROOH to ROH.
- [ ] **HALS** (hindered amine light stabilizers) act catalytically via the **Denisov cycle** — nitroxyl radical regenerated.
- [ ] **Ozonolysis** attacks *only* C=C bonds → saturated polymers (PE, PP, PVC) are immune; unsaturated rubbers (NR, SBR) require **6-PPD** antiozonant.
- [ ] **Photo-oxidation** is initiated by chromophores (impurities, carbonyls, catalyst residues); coupled to Beer–Lambert depth profile.
- [ ] **Visible consequences:** yellowing, embrittlement, surface cracking, loss of tensile strength, Tg shift.
- [ ] **Analytical signatures:** FTIR carbonyl peak at **1715 cm⁻¹**, hydroperoxide at **3410 cm⁻¹**, vinyl at **910 cm⁻¹**; EPR for radicals; DSC for OIT.
- [ ] **Carbonyl Index (CI):** $\;\text{CI} = A_{1715}/A_{1465}\;$ — CI > 0.1 → significant oxidation; CI > 0.5 → end-of-life.
- [ ] **Kinetic Chain Length** $\;\nu = R_p/R_i = k_p[\text{RH}]/2\sqrt{k_t R_i}\;$ — large ν ⇒ each initiation produces many propagation events.
- [ ] **Industrial additives:** Irganox 1010 (primary), Irgafos 168 (secondary), Tinuvin 770 (HALS), 6-PPD (antiozonant), carbon black (UV shield).

---

### ⭐ FINAL EXAM-WEIGHTED CONCEPTS (High Frequency in KTU 2024)

| # | Concept | Frequency in Past Papers |
|---|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Bolland–Gee mechanism (full diagram) | Every year |
| 2 | Role of hydroperoxides | Every year |
| 3 | Antioxidant classification | Every year |
| 4 | HALS Denisov cycle | 3 out of 4 years |
| 5 | Ozonolysis of unsaturated rubber | Alternate years |
| 6 | Photo-oxidation & Beer–Lambert coupling | Recent emphasis |
| 7 | OIT and activation energy calculations | Alternate years |
| 8 | Carbonyl index | Recent emphasis |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ozone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ozone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ozone (O₃)** is an allotrope of oxygen consisting of three oxygen atoms arranged in a bent (V-shaped) molecular geometry, with a bond angle of approximately **116.8°** and a bond length of about **127.8 pm**. In polymer science, ozone refers to its role as a highly reactive atmospheric oxidant that initiates and propagates degradation reactions in polymeric materials — particularly those containing unsaturation (C=C double bonds) such as natural rubber, polybutadiene, SBR, NBR, and other diene-based elastomers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Ozone degradation is a specialized form of **oxidative degradation** that occurs even at ambient temperatures and at very low ozone concentrations (as low as **1–10 pphm** — parts per hundred million). Unlike thermal or photo-oxidation, ozone attack is a **surface-initiated, chain-scission-dominated** mechanism producing characteristic **anisotropic cracks** oriented perpendicular to the strain direction.

## 1.2 The Ozone Molecule — Bonding Picture

Ozone possesses a **resonance-stabilized structure** that can be drawn as two equivalent canonical forms:

$$\text{O}_3 \longleftrightarrow \text{O}^{-}\!-\!\text{O}^{+}\!=\!\text{O} \longleftrightarrow \text{O}\!=\!\text{O}^{+}\!-\!\text{O}^{-}$$

The central oxygen atom is **sp² hybridized** with one lone pair, giving the molecule its bent geometry. The O–O bond order is approximately **1.5**, and the molecule is diamagnetic with a singlet ground state.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Silent Scissor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a stretched rubber band left near an electrical motor or a photocopier for a few weeks. You will observe fine, sharp cracks appearing across its surface — always running *across* the stretch direction, never along it. This is ozone at work.
>
> **Why does this happen?** Ozone molecules act like invisible, ultra-sharp chemical scissors. They selectively snip the carbon–carbon double bonds (C=C) in the rubber, but only on the surface where they first land. Because the rubber is under tension, the surface cuts grow inward as the material tries to relieve the stress — producing the classic "cracks-perpendicular-to-strain" fingerprint of ozone damage.

## 1.4 Sources of Ozone Relevant to Polymer Service Life

| Source | Typical O₃ Concentration | Polymer-Service Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Stratospheric ozone layer** | ~300 DU (Dobson Units) | Indirect — UV photolysis creates reactive species |
| **Urban/industrial atmosphere** | **20–100 pphm** | Major cause of rubber component failure |
| **Indoor environments** | 5–30 pphm | Office equipment, copier rooms |
| **Electrical discharge (motors, transformers)** | Localized >1000 pphm | Severe cracking of cable insulation |
| **UV-light / corona discharge** | Generated in situ | Aging of cables, weather strips |

---

> [!TIP]
> **For First-Time Learners:** The key mental model is — **ozone does not need heat or light to attack a polymer**. It is reactive even at room temperature, which is why rubber seals in aircraft, automobiles, and high-voltage equipment require explicit antiozonant protection. This is a *unique* property compared to thermal oxidation (needs heat) and photo-oxidation (needs UV).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Polymers Degrade Under Ozone — The Mechanistic Foundation

The ozone molecule is a powerful **1,3-dipole** and a strong electrophile. It preferentially attacks electron-rich π-bonds. Polymers containing **C=C unsaturation** (diene rubbers) are therefore primary targets.

### Mechanism — Step-by-Step (Criegee Ozonolysis of an Olefin)

**Step 1 — Primary Ozonide (Molozonide) Formation [1,3-Dipolar Cycloaddition]**

The ozone molecule adds across the C=C double bond in a concerted **1,3-dipolar cycloaddition**, forming an unstable **1,2,3-trioxolane** (primary ozonide / molozonide):

$$\text{R-CH=CH-R'} + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \underset{\text{Molozonide}}{\text{R-CH}-\text{CH-R'}} \atop{\diagdown\,\,\,O-O-O\,\,\,\diagup}$$

**Step 2 — Retro-[3+2] Cycloreversion**

The molozonide rapidly fragments via a retro-1,3-dipolar cycloaddition into a carbonyl compound and a carbonyl oxide (Criegee intermediate):

$$\text{Molozonide} \longrightarrow \text{R-CHO} \;+\; \underset{\text{Criegee Intermediate}}{\text{R'}\!\!-\!\text{CH}=\text{O}^{+}\!-\!\text{O}^{-}}$$

**Step 3 — Secondary Ozonide Formation**

The Criegee intermediate recombines with the carbonyl partner in another 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition, yielding the **1,2,4-trioxolane** (secondary ozonide / final ozonide):

$$\text{R-CHO} + \text{R'HC=O^{+}\!-\!O^{-}} \longrightarrow \underset{\text{Secondary Ozonide}}{\text{1,2,4-trioxolane}}$$

**Step 4 — Hydroperoxide Generation & Chain Scission**

In the polymeric matrix, the Criegee intermediate reacts with proton donors (RH) to form **hydroperoxides (ROOH)**, which subsequently undergo homolytic cleavage to alkoxy (RO·) and hydroxyl (·OH) radicals:

$$\text{Criegee Intermediate} + \text{RH} \longrightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{other products}$$

$$\text{ROOH} \xrightarrow{h\nu \,or\, \Delta} \text{RO}^{\cdot} + {}^{\cdot}\text{OH}$$

**Step 5 — Radical Chain Scission**

These radicals abstract allylic hydrogens and propagate the chain-scission process, leading to molecular weight reduction and embrittlement.

## 2.2 Macroscopic Manifestation — Ozone Cracking

Under static or dynamic strain, ozone-induced chain scission at the surface produces **characteristic cracks**:

- **Orientation:** Perpendicular to the maximum principal stress direction
- **Depth:** Initially surface-limited (a few μm to ~100 μm)
- **Growth law:** Linear with time under constant ozone exposure
- **Critical strain threshold:** Most rubbers crack only when strained above **ε_critical ≈ 5–10%** (unstrained rubber is largely immune)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas and parameters** that the KTU examiner expects students to recognize and apply.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Crack Growth Rate (Andrews Model)** | $\dfrac{dc}{dt} = k \, [\text{O}_3]^n$ | c = crack depth (m), t = time (s), k = rate constant, n ≈ 1 (1st order in O₃) |
| 2 | **Arrhenius Temperature Dependence** | $k = A \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | A = pre-exponential factor, Eₐ = activation energy (~50–80 kJ/mol), R = 8.314 J/mol·K |
| 3 | **Critical Strain for Cracking** | $\varepsilon_c = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}$ (empirical) | Below εc, no cracks form; σy = yield, E = modulus |
| 4 | **Henry's Law for O₃ Solubility** | $C_{\text{O}_3,\text{polymer}} = S \cdot p_{\text{O}_3}$ | S = solubility coefficient; pO₃ = partial pressure |
| 5 | **Ozone Concentration Unit Conversion** | $1\;\text{ppm} = 100\;\text{pphm} = 10^3\;\text{ppb}$ | pphm = parts per hundred million (commonly used in rubber testing) |
| 6 | **Average Bond Energy of O–O in O₃** | $D_{\text{O-O}} \approx 146\;\text{kJ/mol}$ (weak) | Explains high reactivity |
| 7 | **Oxidation Induction Time (OIT)** | $t_{\text{OIT}} \propto [\text{Antiozonant}]^m$ | m ≈ 0.5–1 depending on additive chemistry |
| 8 | **Diffusion-Limited Crack Depth** | $c(t) = \sqrt{2 D_{\text{O}_3} \, t}$ | D = diffusivity of O₃ in rubber (~10⁻¹¹ m²/s) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where This Knowledge Is Applied:**
> 1. **Automotive Industry** — Tire sidewalls, weather-strip seals, fuel hoses (must pass OEM ozone tests like ASTM D1149, ISO 1431)
> 2. **High-Voltage Insulation** — EPDM and silicone insulators in substations exposed to corona-generated ozone
> 3. **Aerospace** — Aircraft window seals, de-icing boots (ozone at 30,000 ft ≈ 200–500 pphm)
> 4. **Polymer Stabilizer Industry** — Designing antiozonants (PPDs, waxes) for commercial rubber compounds
> 5. **Failure Analysis** — Forensic identification of ozone cracking vs. other degradation modes

## 2.5 Protection Strategies — The Engineer's Toolkit

| Strategy | Mechanism | Example Additives |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Barrier Waxes** | Migrate to surface, form protective film | Paraffin wax, microcrystalline wax |
| **Chemical Antiozonants** | Sacrificial scavengers; react with O₃ preferentially | **N-isopropyl-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine (IPPD)**, **6PPD**, **DPD** |
| **Polymer Backbone Saturation** | Eliminate the C=C target site | EPDM, butyl rubber, HNBR (hydrogenated NBR) |
| **Surface Coatings** | Block O₃ diffusion into bulk | Polyurethane, chloroprene coatings |
| **Strain Reduction** | Keep operating strain below εc | Mechanical design considerations |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam — "WS-SSB":** **W**axes, **S**cavengers (chemical antiozonants), **S**aturated backbone, **B**arrier coatings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Crack Growth Rate vs. Ozone Concentration

We now derive the **Andrews–Lingstead kinetic model** for ozone-induced crack growth, which is a frequent analytical question in KTU Part B papers.

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumptions

1. Crack growth is **reaction-rate-controlled** (not diffusion-limited), valid for shallow cracks and moderate O₃ concentrations.
2. The reaction of O₃ with the strained C=C bond follows **first-order kinetics** in ozone concentration.
3. The polymer surface contains a constant density of reactive allylic sites.

### 3.1.2 Derivation

Let:
- $r$ = crack growth rate (m/s)
- $[\text{O}_3]$ = ozone concentration at the crack tip (mol/m³)
- $k$ = intrinsic rate constant (m/s per unit concentration order n)
- $n$ = reaction order in ozone

**Step 1 — Write the empirical rate law:**

$$
r = \frac{dc}{dt} = k \, [\text{O}_3]^n
$$

**Step 2 — Take natural logarithm of both sides to linearize:**

$$
\ln r = \ln k + n \ln [\text{O}_3]
$$

**Step 3 — This is a straight line equation $y = mx + C$ in $\ln r$ vs $\ln [\text{O}_3]$ space:**
- Slope $m = n$ (reaction order)
- Intercept $C = \ln k$

**Step 4 — Apply Arrhenius temperature dependence to k:**

$$
k(T) = A \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain the full kinetic expression:**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{dc}{dt} = A \, [\text{O}_3]^n \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)\;}
$$

This is the master equation that links crack growth rate to ozone concentration and service temperature.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- $[\text{O}_3] = 50$ pphm $= 50 \times 10^{-8}$ (volume fraction) $\approx 2.0 \times 10^{-6}$ mol/m³ at STP
- $T = 298$ K
- $A = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s·(mol/m³)⁻¹
- $E_a = 65$ kJ/mol $= 6.5 \times 10^4$ J/mol
- $n = 1$

**Step 1 — Compute the exponential term:**

$$
\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{6.5 \times 10^4}{8.314 \times 298}\right)
$$

$$
= \exp(-26.23) = 4.05 \times 10^{-12}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by A:**

$$
A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.05 \times 10^{-12} = 4.86 \times 10^{-15}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute rate:**

$$
\frac{dc}{dt} = 4.86 \times 10^{-15} \times (2.0 \times 10^{-6})^1 = 9.72 \times 10^{-21}\;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to more useful units (μm/day):**

$$
\frac{dc}{dt} = 9.72 \times 10^{-21} \times 86400 \times 10^6 \approx 8.4 \times 10^{-7}\;\mu\text{m/day}
$$

This is consistent with the experimentally observed **extremely slow** crack growth rates in pure elastomers under mild ozone conditions.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Rubbers vs. Ozone Resistance

| Polymer | C=C Content | Ozone Resistance | Reason | Service Life Under O₃ Stress |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Rubber (NR)** | High | **Poor** | Direct C=C target | Cracks in days at 50 pphm |
| **Polybutadiene (BR)** | Very high | **Very Poor** | Every repeat unit has C=C | Cracks in hours |
| **SBR (Styrene–Butadiene)** | Moderate | **Poor** | Butadiene units vulnerable | Cracks in days |
| **NBR (Nitrile Rubber)** | Moderate | Poor–Fair | Butadiene units | Limited outdoor use |
| **CR (Neoprene / Chloroprene)** | Low (Cl-stabilized) | **Good** | Electron-withdrawing Cl deactivates C=C | Months–years |
| **EPDM** | **None (saturated)** | **Excellent** | No C=C target site | Decades |
| **Butyl Rubber (IIR)** | **None (saturated)** | **Excellent** | Saturated backbone | Decades |
| **HNBR** | Partially saturated | **Very Good** | Residual C=C minimal | Years |
| **Silicone Rubber (VMQ)** | None | **Excellent** | Inorganic Si–O–Si backbone | Decades |
| **Fluorocarbon (FKM, Viton)** | None | **Outstanding** | C–F bond inertness | Decades |

## 3.3 KTU Conceptual Flow — From Molecular Event to Engineering Failure

The logical sequence that an examiner expects a student to articulate:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{O}_3 \text{ Diffusion} \rightarrow \text{Surface Adsorption} \rightarrow \text{C=C Cleavage} \rightarrow \text{Hydroperoxide} \rightarrow \text{Radical Chain Scission} \rightarrow \text{Crack Initiation} \rightarrow \text{Crack Propagation} \rightarrow \text{Macro Failure}\;}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Mechanism of Ozone Attack on a Diene Polymer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["O3 diffuses to polymer surface"]:::step1
    B["1,3-Dipolar Cycloaddition at C=C bond"]:::step2
    C["Molozonide 1,2,3-Trioxolane formed"]:::step3
    D["Retro Cycloaddition fragments molozonide"]:::step4
    E["Carbonyl Oxide Criegee Intermediate + Aldehyde"]:::step5
    F["Reaction with allylic RH forms Hydroperoxide ROOH"]:::step6
    G["ROOH homolysis yields RO radical and OH radical"]:::step7
    H["Radical chain scission reduces MW"]:::step8
    I["Surface microcrack nucleation"]:::step9
    J["Crack propagation perpendicular to strain"]:::step10
    K["Macroscopic ozone cracking failure"]:::step11

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J --> K

    classDef step1 fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef step2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef step3 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef step4 fill:#FFC0CB,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef step5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef step6 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef step7 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    classDef step8 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    classDef step9 fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    classDef step10 fill:#DC143C,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    classDef step11 fill:#B22222,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Antiozonant Action in a Rubber Compound

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:polymer["Unsaturated Elastomer Matrix C=C Bonds Present"]:3
        A["Polymer Chain Segment"]:::pc
        B["Polymer Chain Segment"]:::pc
        C["Polymer Chain Segment"]:::pc
    end
    space
    block:protection["Ozone Defense Layer"]:3
        D["Antiozonant 6PPD Molecule Migrates to Surface"]:::ad
        E["Paraffin Wax Crystallites Form Physical Film"]:::wax
        F["Saturated Polymer Shell HDPE or EPDM Coating"]:::coat
    end
    space
    block:ozone["Ozone Environment 50 pphm"]:3
        G["O3 Molecule Approaches"]:::ozone1
        H["O3 Scavenged by Antiozonant"]:::ozone2
        I["O3 Blocked by Wax Film"]:::ozone3
    end

    classDef pc fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef ad fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef wax fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000
    classDef coat fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,color:#000
    classDef ozone1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef ozone2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef ozone3 fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Diagram — Selecting an Ozone-Resistant Polymer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Select Polymer for Ozone Service"]:::start
    Q1{"Operating Strain?"}:::dnode
    Q2{"Temperature?"}:::dnode
    Q3{"Cost Constraint?"}:::dnode
    R1["Use EPDM or Butyl Rubber"]:::rec
    R2["Use HNBR or CR Neoprene"]:::rec
    R3["Use Natural Rubber + 6PPD Antiozonant + Wax"]:::rec
    R4["Use FKM Viton or Silicone"]:::rec

    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -->|Low Under 5 percent| Q2
    Q1 -->|High Above 10 percent| Q4
    Q4{"Temperature Above 150 C?"}
    Q4 -->|Yes| R4
    Q4 -->|No| R2
    Q2 -->|Below 100 C| Q3
    Q2 -->|Above 100 C| R4
    Q3 -->|Tight| R3
    Q3 -->|Flexible| R2

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef dnode fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef rec fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic — Ozone Crack Morphology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Applied Tensile Strain Direction"]
        direction LR
        X1["Stress Axis"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Polymer Surface Micrograph"]
        direction TB
        C1["Crack 1 vertical line"]
        C2["Crack 2 vertical line"]
        C3["Crack 3 vertical line"]
    end
    S1 --- S2
    X1 -.perpendicular to.-> C1
    X1 -.perpendicular to.-> C2
    X1 -.perpendicular to.-> C3
```
