# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define ozone cracking. Why is natural rubber particularly vulnerable to ozone attack?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Ozone cracking is a **surface-initiated degradation phenomenon** in which ozone molecules (O₃) attack the C=C double bonds of unsaturated elastomers, leading to chain scission and the formation of characteristic anisotropic cracks oriented **perpendicular to the applied strain direction**.

Natural rubber (polyisoprene) is particularly vulnerable because its repeat unit contains a **reactive C=C double bond** in every monomer unit, providing an abundant target site for the electrophilic 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of ozone. Under even mild strain (>5%) and ambient ozone levels (20–100 pphm), NR cracks within days. *(3 marks — full mechanism expected: 1 mark definition + 1 mark mechanism + 1 mark strain-orientation explanation.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three antiozonants used in rubber compounding. State their mechanism of action.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Antiozonant | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **6PPD** (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) | Sacrificial scavenger — reacts preferentially with O₃, diffuses to surface |
| **IPPD** (N-isopropyl-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) | Sacrificial scavenger (similar PPD chemistry) |
| **Paraffin / Microcrystalline Wax** | Physical barrier — blooms to surface, blocks O₃ diffusion |

*(3 marks — 1 mark for correct list + 1 mark mechanism + 1 mark for any two additional examples or examples of antiozonants.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mechanism Steps:** When asked to write the ozonolysis mechanism, students frequently **skip the Criegee intermediate** or jump directly from molozonide to products. The 3-step Criegee pathway (molonide → Criegee + carbonyl → secondary ozonide) is the **single most-valuated pathway** in KTU papers. Skipping it costs **2–3 marks minimum**.
> 2. **Strain Orientation:** Many students incorrectly state ozone cracks run *parallel* to the strain. The correct answer is **perpendicular** — this is a one-mark trap question.
> 3. **EPDM Exception:** Students often confuse "saturated rubber" with "ozone-proof rubber." EPDM is ozone-resistant **only because it lacks C=C bonds**, not because of any chemical antiozonant mechanism.
> 4. **Units Confusion:** Mixing pphm, ppm, and ppb. Always convert to a consistent unit before solving numerical problems.
> 5. **Drawing Mistakes:** In mechanism diagrams, students forget the *1,2,3-trioxolane* (5-membered ring with three oxygens) vs *1,2,4-trioxolane* (oxygen positions) — these are distinct intermediates.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 4 Choice A]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat mechanism, explain the **Criegee ozonolysis** of an alkene/polymeric C=C bond. Discuss the formation of the molozonide, Criegee intermediate, and secondary ozonide. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **factors affecting ozone degradation** of polymers. How does the applied strain influence crack formation? Compare the ozone resistance of natural rubber, SBR, and EPDM. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Criegee Ozonolysis Mechanism (7 marks)

The Criegee mechanism of ozonolysis proceeds through **three discrete steps** when applied to a generic olefin R–CH=CH–R':

**Step 1: Primary Ozonide (Molozonide) Formation — 1,3-Dipolar Cycloaddition [2 marks]**

Ozone acts as a 1,3-dipole and adds across the C=C π-bond in a concerted, stereospecific manner:

$$
\text{R–CH=CH–R'} + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \underset{\text{Molozonide (1,2,3-trioxolane)}}{\begin{array}{c}\text{R–CH–CH–R'}\\ \diagdown\,\,O\!-\!O\!-\!O\,\,\diagup\end{array}}
$$

The 1,2,3-trioxolane ring is unstable and is *not* isolated.

**Step 2: Retro-[3+2] Cycloreversion — Criegee Intermediate [2 marks]**

The molozonide spontaneously fragments via retro-1,3-dipolar cycloaddition into two fragments — a carbonyl compound and a carbonyl oxide (the **Criegee intermediate**):

$$
\text{Molozonide} \longrightarrow \text{R–CHO} + \text{R'}\!\!-\!\text{HC=O}^{+}\!-\!\text{O}^{-}
$$

**Step 3: Secondary Ozonide Formation — Final Ozonide [2 marks]**

The Criegee intermediate recombines with the carbonyl partner in another 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition, giving the stable 1,2,4-trioxolane (secondary ozonide):

$$
\text{R–CHO} + \text{R'}\!\!-\!\text{HC=O}^{+}\!-\!\text{O}^{-} \longrightarrow \underset{\text{Secondary Ozonide}}{\text{1,2,4-trioxolane}}
$$

**Conclusion [1 mark]:**

In polymeric systems, the secondary ozonide is rarely formed because the Criegee intermediate preferentially reacts with **proton donors (RH)** in the matrix, yielding **hydroperoxides (ROOH)** that subsequently drive radical chain scission.

---

#### Part (b) — Factors Affecting Ozone Degradation (7 marks)

**(i) Effect of Polymer Structure [2 marks]:** Polymers with high C=C unsaturation (NR, BR, SBR) are highly vulnerable; saturated polymers (EPDM, IIR, silicone) are inherently resistant.

**(ii) Effect of Applied Strain [2 marks]:** The critical strain threshold is **ε_c ≈ 5–10%**. Below this, ozone attack produces only a hazy "frosting" with no visible cracks. Above the threshold, characteristic **anisotropic cracks** form perpendicular to the principal strain axis. This is because ozone scission at the surface creates stress-concentrating notches; under tension, these notches propagate in the direction normal to the maximum tensile stress.

**(iii) Effect of Ozone Concentration [1 mark]:** Crack growth rate follows $dc/dt \propto [\text{O}_3]^n$ with $n \approx 1$ at low concentrations, deviating at higher concentrations where diffusion becomes limiting.

**(iv) Effect of Temperature [1 mark]:** Higher temperatures accelerate both the chemical reaction (Arrhenius) and O₃ diffusion, but thermal decomposition of O₃ above 100 °C slightly reduces effective concentration — a complex trade-off.

**(v) Comparative Table — Ozone Resistance [1 mark]:**

| Polymer | Resistance | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| NR | Poor | Every unit has C=C |
| SBR | Poor | Butadiene segments vulnerable |
| EPDM | Excellent | Saturated backbone (no C=C) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 4 Choice B]`

**(a)** Explain the **role of antiozonants** in rubber compounding. Discuss the chemical action of **p-phenylenediamine (PPD) class antiozonants** with a suitable reaction scheme. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **test methods** used to evaluate ozone resistance of polymers (ASTM D1149 / ISO 1431). What is the **critical strain** concept, and how does it affect the design of rubber components for outdoor service? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Antiozonants and Their Chemical Action (7 marks)

**Definition [1 mark]:** Antiozonants are specialty additives incorporated into rubber compounds (typically at 1–3 phr) to prevent or retard ozone-induced degradation.

**Classification [1 mark]:**
- **Chemical antiozonants** (PPDs, dihydroquinolines)
- **Physical barriers** (waxes, surface coatings)

**Mechanism of PPD-type Antiozonants (e.g., 6PPD) [3 marks]:**

The N–H bond of the secondary amine group reacts with ozone in a **charge-transfer / radical-type** sacrificial scavenging mechanism:

$$
\text{Ar–NH–R'} + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{Ar–N}^{\cdot}\!-\!\text{R'} + \text{HO}_2^{\cdot} + \text{O}_2
$$

The PPD is consumed (sacrificed) but the polymer is protected. Subsequent regeneration reactions involving the amine radical and additional O₃ molecules produce nitroxide and nitro intermediates that further scavenge radicals.

**Migration Mechanism (Self-Healing) [2 marks]:** PPDs are mobile in the rubber matrix. As the surface additive is consumed by ozone attack, fresh antiozonant diffuses from the bulk to the surface — providing continuous protection. This is why PPDs are described as **"self-replenishing sacrificial scavengers."**

---

#### Part (b) — Testing Methods & Critical Strain (7 marks)

**Standard Test Methods [3 marks]:**

| Standard | Description |
|---|---|
| **ASTM D1149** | Standard Test Method for Rubber Deterioration — Surface Ozone Cracking in a Chamber |
| **ISO 1431-1** | Static strain exposure (constant elongation) |
| **ISO 1431-2** | Dynamic strain exposure (cyclic elongation) |
| **ASTM D3395** | Dynamic ozone testing |

In these tests, **dumbbell or strip specimens** are strained to a predetermined elongation (typically 20% or 50%), placed in an ozone chamber with controlled concentration (e.g., 50 pphm at 40 °C), and inspected at intervals for crack initiation and growth.

**Critical Strain Concept [2 marks]:** The **critical strain (ε_c)** is the threshold elongation below which ozone cracking does not occur for a given rubber compound, regardless of exposure time. For natural rubber, ε_c ≈ 5–20%. Engineers designing rubber components (e.g., window seals) ensure that the maximum in-service strain stays **below ε_c** to achieve long service life.

**Design Implications [2 marks]:**
- Specify EPDM or silicone for components with unavoidable strain.
- For NR/NBR, ensure operating strain ≤ 0.5 × ε_c.
- Specify antiozonant packages in the compound.
- Use protective wax blooms for static applications.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Ozone in 60 Seconds":**

- **Ozone (O₃)** is a bent, electrophilic, 1,3-dipolar molecule — a powerful ambient-temperature oxidant.
- **Targets:** C=C double bonds in unsaturated polymers (NR, BR, SBR, NBR).
- **Mechanism:** Criegee ozonolysis — *Molozonide → Criegee intermediate + Carbonyl → Secondary ozonide* (or Hydroperoxide in polymers).
- **Cracks:** Form perpendicular to applied strain; require strain > ε_c (~5–10%) to initiate.
- **Rate Law:** $dc/dt = k[\text{O}_3]^n$, $n \approx 1$, Arrhenius temperature dependence with $E_a \approx 50\text{–}80$ kJ/mol.
- **Saturated polymers are immune:** EPDM, IIR, silicone, FKM have no C=C targets.
- **Antiozonants:** PPDs (chemical scavengers) + waxes (physical barriers) — 1–3 phr typical loading.
- **Test Standards:** ASTM D1149, ISO 1431-1 (static), ISO 1431-2 (dynamic).
- **Atmospheric levels:** Urban 20–100 pphm; indoor 5–30 pphm; high altitude >200 pphm.
- **Units:** $1$ ppm $= 100$ pphm $= 1000$ ppb — convert before any numerical problem.
- **Engineering applications:** Tire sidewalls, weather seals, HV insulators, aerospace seals.
- **Common trap:** Cracks run *perpendicular*, not parallel, to strain direction.
- **Common trap:** Saturated ≠ always resistant — FKM (Viton) is best; silicone is also excellent.
- **Mnemonic:** **"C-M-C-H-S"** = **C**riegee, **M**olozonide, **C**arbonyl, **H**ydroperoxide, **S**cission.
- **Henry's Law** governs O₃ dissolution in polymer; diffusion coefficient ~10⁻¹¹ m²/s in elastomers.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Topic: Ozone | PCPOT302 — Polymer Science | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Heat # HEAT — Thermal Behaviour & Thermal Properties of Polymers ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition In the context of **PCPOT302 – Polymer Science (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the term **"Heat"** in Module 4 refers to the study of **thermal response and thermal transitions of polymeric materials** when subjected to a controlled thermal environment. It encompasses the *thermal, mechanical, and morphological changes* that a polymer undergoes as a function of temperature, time, and applied stress. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** > *Thermal properties of polymers — Heat Distortion Temperature (HDT), Vicat Softening Point (VST), Glass Transition Temperature (Tg), Melt Flow Index (MFI), Thermal conductivity, Specific heat capacity, Coefficient of thermal expansion, and Thermal stability/degradation.* The study of "Heat" in polymers is **not just about raising the temperature** — it is a *thermo-mechanical investigation* that maps how a polymer's free volume, segmental mobility, crystalline order, and viscoelastic response evolve with temperature. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of a polymer chain as a bowl of cooked spaghetti.** - At **very low temperatures**, the spaghetti strands are *frozen rigid* in a tangled ball — brittle, hard, and they snap if bent. This is the **glassy state** (below Tg). - As you **heat** the bowl slightly, the strands start *wiggling* and *slipping past one another*. The mass becomes flexible and rubbery. This is the **rubbery state** (above Tg for amorphous polymers). - Heat it more, and the strands *flow freely* like a liquid — the polymer now **melts** or **flows**. This is the **viscous flow state** (above Tm for semi-crystalline, or above Tf for amorphous). **Key insight:** Polymers do not have a single "melting point" like water. They undergo a **spectrum of transitions** — each tied to a specific type of molecular motion being "unlocked" by thermal energy. --- ## 1.3 The Three Critical Temperature Markers | Marker | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Test | |---|---|---|---| | **Glass Transition Temperature** | **Tg** | Onset of long-range segmental motion | DSC, DMA | | **Heat Deflection Temperature** | **HDT** (or HDUL) | Temperature at which a polymer bar deflects 0.25 mm under load (ASTM D648) | Heat deflection tester | | **Vicat Softening Point** | **VST** | Temperature at which a needle penetrates 1 mm into polymer under load (ASTM D1525) | Vicat apparatus | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Remember this hierarchy:** > **Tg < VST < HDT < Tm (or flow temperature)** > This ordering is a *direct consequence* of the increasingly severe conditions (load + temperature) required to observe each transition. --- ## 1.4 Why "Heat" Matters in Polymer Engineering The thermal profile of a polymer governs almost every real-world engineering decision: - **Service temperature limits** for plastic gears, pipes, and enclosures. - **Processing windows** during injection moulding, extrusion, and blow moulding. - **Sterilization compatibility** for medical polymers (autoclaving at 121 °C requires HDT > 130 °C). - **Fire safety ratings** (UL 94) depend on heat release and ignition behaviour. - **Dimensional stability** under thermal cycling — governed by **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE / α)**. # DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — Heat in Polymers ## 2.1 Hierarchical Thermal Transitions in Polymers When a polymer is heated, transitions occur in a specific order governed by **activation energy barriers** to molecular motion: ### 2.1.1 Sub-Tg Transitions (β, γ relaxations) - Localized motions: side-group rotations, bond bending, methyl group rotations. - Observed below Tg, contribute to **toughness** and **ductility** even in the glassy state. - Example: γ-relaxation in PVC at ~ −70 °C. ### 2.1.2 Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) - **Definition:** The temperature at which **cooperative segmental motion** of 10–20 monomer units becomes active, marked by a sudden change in slope of specific volume, enthalpy, and modulus. - **Theoretical framework:** Free Volume Theory (Fox & Flory, 1950). - **Effect:** Modulus drops by **3–4 orders of magnitude** (from ~10⁹ Pa glassy to ~10⁶ Pa rubbery). - **Heat capacity jump:** Cp changes by ~ **0.3–0.5 J/g·K** at Tg (DSC signature). ### 2.1.3 Crystalline Melting (Tm) - Applicable to **semi-crystalline polymers** only (PE, PP, PA, PET). - Tm is *always* greater than Tg (typically **1.4–2.5 × Tg** in Kelvin — the **Boyer-Beaman rule**). ### 2.1.4 Decomposition (Td) - The temperature at which **chemical bond scission** (chain breaking, crosslink cleavage) becomes dominant. - Characterized by TGA (Thermogravimetric Analysis). --- ## 2.2 Heat Distortion Temperature (HDT) > [!IMPORTANT] > **ASTM D648 / ISO 75 — Standard Test Method for Deflection Temperature of Plastics Under Load.** A standard test bar (127 × 13 × 3 mm) is loaded in **three-point bending** to a fibre stress of either: - **0.45 MPa** (HDT at low stress — typical service load). - **1.82 MPa** (HDT at high stress — structural load). The temperature is raised at **2 °C/min**, and the bar's deflection is monitored. The **HDT is the temperature at which the bar deflects by 0.25 mm**. **Governing equation (beam theory under load):** $$ \delta = \frac{F L^{3}}{48 E(T) I} $$ Where: - $\delta$ = mid-span deflection (m) - $F$ = applied load (N) - $L$ = span length (m) - $E(T)$ = temperature-dependent flexural modulus (Pa) - $I$ = second moment of area (m⁴) > [!NOTE] > **Crucial point:** HDT is *not* a true material property — it is a **loading-condition-dependent empirical value**. The same polymer gives different HDT values at 0.45 MPa vs 1.82 MPa. --- ## 2.3 Vicat Softening Point (VST) **ASTM D1525 / ISO 306:** A flat-ended needle (1 mm² cross-section) loaded with either **10 N (Vicat A)** or **50 N (Vicat B)** is pressed into the polymer surface as temperature rises at **50 °C/h** or **120 °C/h**. **VST = temperature at which the needle penetrates 1 mm.** > [!NOTE] > VST is *less dependent on the modulus drop* and more sensitive to *the onset of plastic flow*. Hence **VST > HDT** for the same polymer under similar conditions. --- ## 2.4 Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) — Theory ### 2.4.1 Free Volume Theory (Fox & Flory) The total specific volume of a polymer is the sum of the **occupied volume** (van der Waals volume of molecules) and the **free volume** (voids allowing segmental motion). $$ V_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{occupied}} + V_{\text{free}} $$ At Tg, the **free volume fraction (f)** reaches a critical value of approximately: $$ f_{\text{Tg}} \approx 0.025 \text{ to } 0.030 $$ **Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) equation** describes time–temperature superposition above Tg: $$ \log a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)} $$ Universal constants (when Tg is the reference): $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ °C. ### 2.4.2 Fox–Flory Equation (Molecular Weight Dependence) $$ T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{M_n} $$ Where: - $T_{g,\infty}$ = Tg at infinite molecular weight - $K$ = Fox–Flory constant (≈ 2 × 10⁵ for polystyrene) - $M_n$ = number-average molecular weight > [!IMPORTANT] > This shows that **Tg increases with molecular weight** but plateaus at high Mn. Low-MW oligomers are *plasticizers of themselves* — the chain ends create excess free volume, lowering Tg. ### 2.4.3 Effect of Plasticizers (Kelley–Bueche Equation) $$ T_g = \frac{\alpha_p V_p T_{gp} + \alpha_d V_d T_{gd}}{\alpha_p V_p + \alpha_d V_d} $$ Where subscripts $p$ = polymer, $d$ = diluent (plasticizer); $\alpha$ = thermal expansion coefficient; $V$ = volume fraction. --- ## 2.5 Thermal Conductivity (k) Polymers are generally **thermal insulators** because: - Low atomic packing density. - Absence of free electrons (unlike metals). - Phonon scattering at disordered chain boundaries. **Typical thermal conductivity values:** | Material | k (W/m·K) | |---|---| | Steel | ~45 | | Aluminium | ~205 | | HDPE | 0.45–0.52 | | PVC (rigid) | 0.16–0.20 | | Polystyrene | 0.10–0.14 | | Air (for reference) | 0.026 | **Mechanism:** Heat conduction in polymers occurs via **lattice vibrations (phonons)** travelling along and between covalent chains. The **inter-chain coupling** is weak (van der Waals), so phonons scatter at every chain interface — hence low k. **Eiermann model (semi-crystalline polymers):** $$ \frac{1}{k} = \frac{1-X_c}{k_a} + \frac{X_c}{k_c} $$ Where $X_c$ = degree of crystallinity, $k_a$ = amorphous-phase conductivity, $k_c$ = crystalline-phase conductivity. --- ## 2.6 Specific Heat Capacity (Cp) **Definition:** The energy required to raise 1 gram of polymer by 1 K at constant pressure. - Solid polymers: **Cp ≈ 1.0–2.0 J/g·K** - At Tg: **abrupt jump in Cp** (ΔCp ≈ 0.3–0.5 J/g·K) - Above Tg, Cp increases linearly with T (Dulong–Petit limit approached at very high T). **Rule of approximation (per-atom contribution — Wunderlich):** $$ C_p \approx \sum_i n_i \cdot C_{p,i} $$ Each atom (C, H, O, N) contributes roughly 8–25 J/mol·K to the molar heat capacity. --- ## 2.7 Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE / α) $$ \alpha = \frac{1}{V} \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P $$ - **Below Tg:** $\alpha_g \approx (1-3) \times 10^{-4}$ /K - **Above Tg:** $\alpha_\ell \approx (4-8) \times 10^{-4}$ /K — typically **2–3× larger**. This jump at Tg is a **defining signature** used in dilatometry to locate Tg. --- ## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **frequently tested** in KTU Module 4 problems. Memorize the units. | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---| | $\delta = FL^3 / (48EI)$ | Beam deflection (HDT test) | m | | $\log a_T = -C_1(T-T_g)/[C_2+(T-T_g)]$ | WLF shift factor | dimensionless | | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K/M_n$ | Fox–Flory (MW dependence) | K | | $1/T_g = w_1/T_{g1} + w_2/T_{g2}$ | Fox equation (blends/copolymers) | K⁻¹ | | $T_m/T_g \approx 1.4 - 2.5$ | Boyer–Beaman rule (ratio in Kelvin) | dimensionless | | $q = -k \, dT/dx$ | Fourier's law (thermal conduction) | W/m² | | $k = \alpha \rho C_p$ | Thermal diffusivity relation | m²/s | | $\alpha = (1/V)(\partial V/\partial T)_P$ | Coefficient of thermal expansion | /K | | $C_p \approx \sum n_i C_{p,i}$ | Additive heat capacity (Wunderlich) | J/g·K | | $H_f = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat | J | | $\eta_0 = A \exp(E_a / RT)$ | Arrhenius viscosity flow model | Pa·s | | $\eta = K M_w^{3.4}$ | Zero-shear viscosity vs MW | Pa·s | **Fox equation for random copolymers / miscible blends:** $$ \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g2}} $$ Where $w_i$ = weight fraction of component $i$, $T_{gi}$ = Tg in Kelvin. > [!WARNING] > **Units trap:** Fox equation requires Tg in **Kelvin**, not °C. Many KTU students lose marks by using Celsius. --- ## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility - **HDT dictates service temperature** of structural plastics in hot environments (under-the-hood automotive parts: PA66 HDT at 1.82 MPa ≈ 100 °C, vs PC ≈ 130 °C). - **VST guides thermoforming temperature selection.** - **Tg governs rubber elasticity window** — a tyre's winter/summer performance. - **Thermal conductivity** is critical for **heat sinks** (filled polymers, e.g., AlN-filled PPS) and **thermal insulators** (PU foam). - **CTE mismatch** between polymer and metal is a major design constraint for **PCB substrates** and **composite laminates**. # STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Fox–Flory Equation for Tg vs Molecular Weight ### Starting Point — Free Volume Concept The **free volume at the chain ends** is higher than in the interior of a chain because chain ends have fewer constraints and more configurational freedom. Let: - $V_{f,\text{end}}$ = free volume per chain end - $V_{f,\text{int}}$ = free volume per repeat unit (interior) - $n$ = number of repeat units - $M_0$ = molar mass of repeat unit Total free volume per gram: $$ V_f = \frac{2 V_{f,\text{end}} + n V_{f,\text{int}}}{n M_0} $$ As $n \to \infty$ (high MW): $V_f \to V_{f,\text{int}} / M_0 = V_{f,\infty}$. The glass transition occurs at a **critical free volume fraction** $V_{f,\text{crit}}$: $$ V_{f,\text{crit}} = \frac{2 V_{f,\text{end}} + n V_{f,\text{int}}}{n M_0} $$ ### Step 1 — Solve for n Multiplying both sides by $n M_0$: $$ V_{f,\text{crit}} \cdot n M_0 = 2 V_{f,\text{end}} + n V_{f,\text{int}} $$ ### Step 2 — Isolate terms with n $$ n (V_{f,\text{crit}} M_0 - V_{f,\text{int}}) = 2 V_{f,\text{end}} $$ ### Step 3 — Express in terms of Mn Since $M_n = n M_0$, we have $n = M_n / M_0$. Substitute: $$ \frac{M_n}{M_0}(V_{f,\text{crit}} M_0 - V_{f,\text{int}}) = 2 V_{f,\text{end}} $$ $$ M_n (V_{f,\text{crit}} - \frac{V_{f,\text{int}}}{M_0}) = \frac{2 V_{f,\text{end}}}{M_0} $$ ### Step 4 — Define constants Let: $$ V_{f,\infty} = V_{f,\text{crit}} - \frac{V_{f,\text{int}}}{M_0} $$ $$ K' = \frac{2 V_{f,\text{end}}}{M_0 \cdot V_{f,\infty}} $$ Then $M_n V_{f,\infty} = 2V_{f,\text{end}}/M_0$ gives: $$ M_n = \frac{2 V_{f,\text{end}}}{M_0 \cdot V_{f,\infty}} = K' $$ ### Step 5 — Convert to Tg Fox and Flory observed that $V_{f,\text{crit}}$ is proportional to $(T_g - T_{g,\infty})$. After algebraic rearrangement: $$ T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{M_n} $$ Where $K = 2 V_{f,\text{end}} / (\alpha \cdot M_0)$ and $\alpha$ is the thermal expansion coefficient difference. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** As $M_n \to \infty$, $T_g \to T_{g,\infty}$. For low $M_n$, Tg drops *significantly*. Practical implication: **oligomers flow like waxes** even below room temperature. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — HDT Calculation from Modulus Data **Problem (KTU-style):** A polymer test bar (L = 100 mm, width = 13 mm, thickness = 3 mm) is loaded to 0.45 MPa fibre stress in a 3-point bending HDT test. At what temperature will the bar deflect 0.25 mm? **Given:** $E(T)$ data: | T (°C) | E (MPa) | |---|---| | 25 | 3000 | | 80 | 2500 | | 120 | 500 | | 150 | 50 | ### Step 1 — Geometry Cross-section: $b = 13$ mm, $h = 3$ mm Span: $L = 100$ mm = 0.1 m Allowed deflection: $\delta = 0.25$ mm = $2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m Stress: $\sigma = 0.45$ MPa = $4.5 \times 10^{5}$ Pa ### Step 2 — Second moment of area $$ I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12} = \frac{(13 \times 10^{-3})(3 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}{12} $$ $$ I = \frac{(13 \times 10^{-3})(27 \times 10^{-9})}{12} $$ $$ I = \frac{351 \times 10^{-12}}{12} = 29.25 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^{4} $$ ### Step 3 — Required load F For 3-point bending with stress at outer fibre $\sigma = 3FL / (2bh^2)$: $$ F = \frac{2 \sigma b h^{2}}{3 L} = \frac{2 (4.5 \times 10^{5})(13 \times 10^{-3})(3 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{3 (0.1)} $$ $$ F = \frac{2 (4.5 \times 10^{5})(13 \times 10^{-3})(9 \times 10^{-6})}{0.3} $$ $$ F = \frac{2 \times 4.5 \times 10^{5} \times 1.17 \times 10^{-7}}{0.3} $$ $$ F = \frac{1.053 \times 10^{-1}}{0.3} = 0.351 \text{ N} $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for E(T) at δ = 0.25 mm Using $\delta = F L^{3} / (48 E I)$: $$ E(T) = \frac{F L^{3}}{48 \delta I} $$ $$ E(T) = \frac{(0.351)(0.1)^{3}}{48 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times (29.25 \times 10^{-12})} $$ $$ E(T) = \frac{(0.351)(10^{-3})}{48 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times (29.25 \times 10^{-12})} $$ $$ E(T) = \frac{3.51 \times 10^{-4}}{3.51 \times 10^{-13}} $$ $$ E(T) \approx 1.0 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa} = 1000 \text{ MPa} $$ ### Step 5 — Interpolate from table At 80 °C: E = 2500 MPa → too high At 120 °C: E = 500 MPa → too low Linear interpolation: $$ T_{HDT} = 80 + (120 - 80) \times \frac{2500 - 1000}{2500 - 500} $$ $$ T_{HDT} = 80 + 40 \times \frac{1500}{2000} $$ $$ T_{HDT} = 80 + 40 \times 0.75 = 80 + 30 = 110 \text{ °C} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU tip:** Whenever deflection-based questions appear, always check whether modulus is *increasing or decreasing* with temperature, then interpolate carefully. Polymers soften → modulus *decreases* with T. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Fox Equation for a Miscible Blend **Problem:** A miscible blend of 40 wt% PS (Tg₁ = 100 °C) and 60 wt% PMMA (Tg₂ = 105 °C). Calculate the blend Tg. ### Step 1 — Convert Tg to Kelvin $$ T_{g1} = 100 + 273 = 373 \text{ K} $$ $$ T_{g2} = 105 + 273 = 378 \text{ K} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply Fox equation $$ \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g2}} $$ $$ \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.40}{373} + \frac{0.60}{378} $$ $$ \frac{1}{T_g} = 1.0724 \times 10^{-3} + 1.5873 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ \frac{1}{T_g} = 2.6597 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1} $$ ### Step 3 — Invert $$ T_g = \frac{1}{2.6597 \times 10^{-3}} = 376.0 \text{ K} $$ ### Step 4 — Convert back to °C $$ T_g = 376.0 - 273 = 103.0 \text{ °C} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** The blend Tg (103 °C) lies *between* the two pure-component Tg values (100 and 105 °C) but is **closer to PMMA** because PMMA has higher weight fraction. This is physically correct. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Boyer–Beaman Rule Verification **Problem:** A semi-crystalline polymer has Tg = 60 °C. Predict its Tm. ### Step 1 — Convert Tg to Kelvin $$ T_g = 60 + 273 = 333 \text{ K} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply ratio range (1.4 to 2.5) $$ T_m = 1.4 \times T_g = 1.4 \times 333 = 466.2 \text{ K} = 193 \text{ °C} $$ $$ T_m = 2.5 \times T_g = 2.5 \times 333 = 832.5 \text{ K} = 559.5 \text{ °C} $$ ### Step 3 — Typical central value For symmetric chains (e.g., PE): $T_m / T_g \approx 2$ → $T_m \approx 393 \text{ K} = 120 \text{ °C}$. For aromatic polymers (PET): ratio ≈ 1.4–1.5. > [!WARNING] > **Always specify the ratio you are using** — the rule is a *range*, not a fixed value. Examiners expect you to justify your choice. # STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Thermal Transition Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Cryogenic T] --> B[Beta and Gamma Sub-Tg Relaxations
Localized bond rotations] B --> C[Glass Transition Tg
Cooperative segmental motion begins] C --> D{Is the polymer semi-crystalline?} D -->|Yes| E[Crystalline Melting Tm
Long-range order collapses] D -->|No| F[Rubbery Plateau] F --> G[Viscous Flow Tf
Entire chains slip past each other] E --> H[Thermal Decomposition Td
Bond scission] G --> H H --> I[Char and Volatile Products] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid:** Every node is a *physical state*; arrows represent the **temperature progression** as thermal energy is added. Note the **branch at Tg** — semi-crystalline and amorphous polymers follow *different paths* above Tg. --- ## 4.2 HDT vs VST Test Apparatus Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HDT[HDT Test - ASTM D648] H1[Heated oil bath] --> H2[Test bar 127 x 13 x 3 mm] H2 --> H3[Three point bending load
0.45 or 1.82 MPa fibre stress] H3 --> H4[Record T at deflection = 0.25 mm] end subgraph VST[VST Test - ASTM D1525] V1[Heated bath] --> V2[Flat polymer specimen] V2 --> V3[Needle 1 mm square tip
10 N or 50 N load] V3 --> V4[Record T at penetration = 1 mm] end HDT -.Reports.-> HDTresult[HDT Value] VST -.Reports.-> VSTresult[VST Value] HDTresult --> Compare{VST greater than HDT?} VSTresult --> Compare Compare -->|Yes| Answer[Correct - VST is typically higher because
penetration requires more energy than bending] ``` --- ## 4.3 Effect of Molecular Weight on Tg (Fox-Flory Curve) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Polymer sample] --> MW{Number average
molecular weight} MW -->|Low Mn, 1000 g/mol| L[Tg drops significantly
Chain ends act as internal plasticizer] MW -->|Medium Mn, 10000 g/mol| M[Tg rises with Mn
Fox Flory equation applies] MW -->|High Mn, 100000 g/mol| H[Tg plateaus at Tg infinity
Chain ends negligible] L --> Curve[Plot Tg vs 1/Mn] M --> Curve H --> Curve Curve --> Result[Linear relationship
Slope = minus K
Y intercept = Tg infinity] ``` --- ## 4.4 Thermal Conductivity Pathways in a Semi-Crystalline Polymer ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SC[Semi Crystalline Polymer Microstructure] direction LR A[Heat source side] --> B[Amorphous region
k low, ~0.2 W per m K] B --> C[Crystalline lamella
k higher, ~0.5 W per m K] C --> D[Amorphous region again] D --> E[Heat sink side] B -. Phonon scattering at interface .-> C C -. Crystalline phonon path .-> D end Note[Net conductivity governed by
Eiermann series-parallel model
1 slash k equals 1 minus Xc over ka plus Xc over kc] -.applies to.-> SC ``` # KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Heat Distortion Temperature (HDT). Mention the standard and the test geometry. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** > **Heat Distortion Temperature (HDT)** is defined as the temperature at which a standard polymer test bar (127 mm × 13 mm × 3 mm) deflects by **0.25 mm** under a specified fibre stress of **0.45 MPa or 1.82 MPa**, when heated at a uniform rate of **2 °C/min** in a three-point bending fixture. > > **Standard:** ASTM D648 / ISO 75. > > **Test geometry:** Three-point bending with span length = 100 mm (for 3 mm thick specimen). > > HDT is **not** a fundamental material property — it is an *empirical measure* of short-term heat resistance under load, often used as a proxy for the maximum service temperature of a plastic. *(3 marks — definition 1.5, standard & geometry 1.5)* --- ### Question 2: Distinguish between Vicat Softening Point and Glass Transition Temperature. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Feature | Vicat Softening Point (VST) | Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) | |---|---|---| | **Standard test** | ASTM D1525 | DSC / DMA (ASTM E1356, D7028) | | **Physical change observed** | Needle penetration of 1 mm | Change in Cp, modulus, slope of expansion | | **Loading condition** | Yes — 10 N or 50 N load | No — unloaded thermal measurement | | **What it detects** | Onset of *plastic flow* (mechanical softening) | Onset of *segmental motion* (molecular mobility) | | **Effect of crystallinity** | Affected | Tg exists only for amorphous phase | | **Typical order** | VST > Tg (for same polymer) | Lowest of the three thermal markers | *(3 marks — table with 3 distinct differences)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks Explain the **Free Volume Theory** of glass transition. Derive the **Fox–Flory equation** and discuss how molecular weight, branching, and crosslinking affect Tg. **Model Solution:** **Free Volume Theory:** The specific volume of a polymer consists of two parts: $$ V_{\text{specific}} = V_{\text{occupied}} + V_{\text{free}} $$ - $V_{\text{occupied}}$ = space actually occupied by molecules (van der Waals volume). - $V_{\text{free}}$ = voids and holes where segmental motion can occur. As temperature rises, $V_{\text{free}}$ increases due to thermal expansion. **At Tg, the free volume reaches a critical value (≈ 2.5–3% of total volume)** beyond which cooperative segmental motion becomes possible. **Derivation of Fox–Flory:** Following the derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes, we obtain: $$ \boxed{T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{M_n}} $$ Where $T_{g,\infty}$ is Tg at infinite molecular weight, $K$ is the Fox–Flory constant (≈ 2 × 10⁵ for PS), and $M_n$ is the number-average molecular weight. **Effects on Tg:** | Structural Factor | Effect on Tg | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Increasing molecular weight** | Tg ↑ (approaches plateau) | Fewer chain ends → less free volume | | **Branching** | Tg ↓ (for short branches) | Branches increase free volume | | **Long-chain branching** | Tg ≈ same as linear (slight increase) | Branches reduce chain-end density | | **Crosslinking** | Tg ↑ | Restricts segmental mobility | | **Steric hindrance (bulky side groups)** | Tg ↑ | Reduces chain mobility | *(7 marks — Free Volume 2, derivation 3, effects table 2)* #### Part (b) — 7 Marks With a neat sketch, describe the **DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimeter)** method for determining Tg. What information does the Cp jump at Tg provide? **Model Solution:** **DSC Principle:** Two pans — one containing the polymer sample, the other empty (reference) — are heated at a controlled rate (typically 10 °C/min). The instrument measures the **differential heat flow (dH/dt)** required to keep both pans at the same temperature. **Sketch (Mermaid-equivalent schematic):** ``` [Heater Block] | +--[Reference Pan (empty)]--> T_ref | +--[Sample Pan (polymer)]----> T_sample | [Heat Flow Sensor measures dH/dt = (T_sample - T_ref) * K] ``` **Typical DSC trace for an amorphous polymer:** ``` Heat flow (W/g) | |***** | ***** ← Cp jump at Tg | ***** |____________*****__________ |______________________________ T_low T_high (→ T) ↑ Tg (midpoint of step) ``` **Information from Cp jump:** - **Magnitude (ΔCp):** Indicates the change in degrees of freedom activated at Tg. Strong polymer chain rigidity → smaller ΔCp; flexible chains → larger ΔCp. - **Width of transition (ΔTg):** Narrow for pure amorphous polymers; broad for blends and plasticized systems. - **Position of Tg (midpoint):** The glass transition temperature by convention. *(7 marks — principle 2, sketch 2, Cp interpretation 3)* --- ### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks Explain the **Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) equation** and its significance in polymer processing. How does it differ from the Arrhenius equation? **Model Solution:** **WLF Equation:** $$ \boxed{\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_s)}{C_2 + (T - T_s)}} $$ Where: - $a_T$ = shift factor (ratio of relaxation time at temperature T to that at reference T_s) - $C_1, C_2$ = empirical constants - $T_s$ = reference temperature (often Tg) **Universal constants when T_s = Tg:** - $C_1 = 17.44$ - $C_2 = 51.6$ K **Significance in processing:** - Predicts **viscosity/relaxation time** changes with temperature above Tg. - Used in **time–temperature superposition (TTS)** to construct master curves from limited experimental data. - Critical for **injection moulding, extrusion, blow moulding** design — tells the processor how much pressure/time is needed at a given melt temperature. **Difference from Arrhenius:** | Feature | WLF | Arrhenius | |---|---|---| | **Temperature range** | Tg to Tg + 100 °C | Above Tg + 100 °C or chemical reactions | | **Constants** | $C_1 = 17.44, C_2 = 51.6$ (universal near Tg) | $E_a$ depends on material | | **Underlying physics** | Cooperative free-volume motion | Single-barrier thermally activated jumps | | **Applicability** | Polymer segmental motion | Viscosity of simple liquids, chemical kinetics | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For temperatures within ~100 °C of Tg**, always use WLF. **For temperatures far above Tg**, both equations converge to Arrhenius behaviour. *(7 marks — equation 2, significance 2, comparison table 3)* #### Part (b) — 7 Marks A polymer rod of **circular cross-section (diameter 5 mm, length 100 mm)** has its ends maintained at **100 °C and 30 °C** respectively. If the thermal conductivity is **0.25 W/m·K**, calculate: 1. The steady-state heat flow rate through the rod. 2. The temperature at the midpoint of the rod. **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Diameter $d = 5$ mm → radius $r = 2.5$ mm = $2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m - Length $L = 100$ mm = 0.1 m - $T_1 = 100$ °C, $T_2 = 30$ °C - $k = 0.25$ W/m·K **Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:** $$ A = \pi r^{2} = \pi (2.5 \times 10^{-3})^{2} $$ $$ A = \pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ A = 1.963 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Fourier's law of heat conduction:** $$ q = \frac{k A \Delta T}{L} $$ Where $q$ = heat flow rate (W), $\Delta T = T_1 - T_2$. $$ q = \frac{(0.25)(1.963 \times 10^{-5})(100 - 30)}{0.1} $$ $$ q = \frac{(0.25)(1.963 \times 10^{-5})(70)}{0.1} $$ $$ q = \frac{3.435 \times 10^{-4}}{0.1} $$ $$ q = 3.435 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} \approx 3.44 \text{ mW} $$ **Step 3 — Temperature distribution (linear under steady state):** For 1-D conduction with no internal heat generation: $$ T(x) = T_1 - \frac{q x}{k A} $$ At midpoint, $x = L/2 = 0.05$ m: $$ T(0.05) = 100 - \frac{(3.435 \times 10^{-3})(0.05)}{(0.25)(1.963 \times 10^{-5})} $$ $$ T(0.05) = 100 - \frac{1.7175 \times 10^{-4}}{4.9075 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ T(0.05) = 100 - 35.0 = 65.0 \text{ °C} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** Midpoint of linear thermal gradient = (100 + 30)/2 = 65 °C ✓ *(7 marks — Fourier's law setup 2, numerical calculation 3, midpoint logic 2)* --- ## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students lose marks on Module 4 Heat questions:** > > 1. **Units trap in Fox equation:** Using Tg in °C instead of K — leads to a wrong answer by ~50–80% error. > 2. **HDT vs Tg confusion:** HDT is a *mechanical loading* property; Tg is a *thermal transition*. Many students interchange the two in definitions. > 3. **Missing linear interpolation step:** HDT problems often require *interpolation* between tabulated modulus values — students leave this step out. > 4. **VST vs HDT inverted:** Stating VST < HDT is **wrong** for the same polymer at the same load rate. > 5. **Forgetting Cp jump at Tg:** When asked about DSC, simply stating "Tg is measured" is incomplete — must mention the **ΔCp signature**. > 6. **Boyer–Beaman ratio range:** Never write $T_m = 1.4 \times T_g$ as a fixed equation; specify the *range* and justify. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Heat in Polymers (Module 4)** > > ☐ **Hierarchy of thermal markers:** Tg < VST < HDT < Tm < Td — memorize the order. > > ☐ **ASTM standards:** HDT = D648, VST = D1525, Tg = DSC E1356 / DMA D7028. > > ☐ **Fox–Flory equation:** $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K/M_n$ — Tg increases with Mn, plateaus at high MW. > > ☐ **Fox blend equation:** $1/T_g = w_1/T_{g1} + w_2/T_{g2}$ — always use **Kelvin**. > > ☐ **WLF equation:** $\log a_T = -17.44(T-T_g)/[51.6 + (T-T_g)]$ — valid for Tg to Tg + 100 °C. > > ☐ **Boyer–Beaman rule:** $T_m / T_g = 1.4$ to $2.5$ (in Kelvin) — symmetric chains closer to 2.0; aromatic closer to 1.4–1.5. > > ☐ **HDT fibre stresses:** 0.45 MPa (low) and 1.82 MPa (high) — HDT at 0.45 MPa > HDT at 1.82 MPa. > > ☐ **Tg detection methods:** DSC (Cp jump), DMA (tan δ peak or E' drop), dilatometry (volume vs T). > > ☐ **Crosslinking raises Tg;** plasticizers lower Tg. > > ☐ **Polymers are thermal insulators:** typical $k$ = 0.1–0.5 W/m·K; ~1000× lower than metals. > > ☐ **Fourier's law:** $q = -k A \, dT/dx$ — heat flow is proportional to conductivity, area, and temperature gradient. > > ☐ **Specific heat of polymers:** solid ≈ 1–2 J/g·K; ΔCp at Tg ≈ 0.3–0.5 J/g·K. > > ☐ **CTE jump at Tg:** $\alpha_\ell \approx 2\alpha_g$ to $3\alpha_g$ above Tg. > > ☐ **Eiermann model:** $1/k = (1-X_c)/k_a + X_c/k_c$ — for semi-crystalline thermal conductivity. > > ☐ **Heat capacity additivity (Wunderlich):** Per-atom contributions can be summed for estimation.

topics.title: UV Light
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** UV Light

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ultraviolet (UV) Light** is a form of **non-ionizing electromagnetic radiation** occupying the spectral region between visible light and X-rays, formally defined by the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) as electromagnetic waves with wavelengths spanning **10 nm to 400 nm**. In the context of **Polymer Science (PCPOT302)**, UV light is treated as a high-energy photophysical stimulus that interacts with the chromophoric groups of polymer chains, initiating a cascade of photochemical events including **photodegradation, photo-oxidation, chain scission, crosslinking, and chromophoric discoloration**.

In photochemistry, the energy carried by a single photon of UV radiation is given by Planck's relation:

$$E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

where $h$ is **Planck's constant (6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s)**, $c$ is the speed of light (**3 × 10⁸ m/s**), and $\lambda$ is the wavelength of incident radiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PCPOT302 explicitly covers UV light as a degradation agent and the corresponding stabilization strategies (UV absorbers, HALS, quenchers). Mastery of the Beer-Lambert Law and the Jablonski diagram is mandatory for board examinations.

## 1.2 Classification of UV Spectrum (CIE Standard)

The UV region is sub-divided into four bands based on wavelength, each possessing distinct photochemical penetration and energy characteristics:

| Band | Wavelength Range | Energy Range (kJ/mol) | Photobiological Effect |
|------|------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **UV-A** (Near UV) | 315 – 400 nm | 299 – 380 kJ/mol | Long-range atmospheric penetration; causes yellowing of polymers |
| **UV-B** (Middle UV) | 280 – 315 nm | 380 – 427 kJ/mol | Most damaging to polymers; initiates Norrish-type reactions |
| **UV-C** (Far UV) | 100 – 280 nm | 427 – 1196 kJ/mol | Absorbed by ozone layer; germicidal; rapid polymer cleavage |
| **Vacuum UV (VUV)** | 10 – 200 nm | > 600 kJ/mol | Requires vacuum; causes ionization and bond breaking |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Sunburn of Plastics"**
> Imagine a polymer chain as a long, organized string of pearls (monomer units) hanging in a museum. When UV light strikes this chain, it acts like an invisible hammer delivering precise, targeted blows at the weakest links of the chain. Each blow (photon) carries enough energy to break a specific covalent bond (C–C ≈ 347 kJ/mol, C–H ≈ 414 kJ/mol, C=O ≈ 715 kJ/mol).
>
> When UV-A light (the gentler hammer) hits the polymer, it slowly discolours the chain (yellowing — like skin tanning). UV-B (a stronger hammer) snaps the chain into fragments (chain scission — like brittle hair breaking). UV-C (a sledgehammer) would shatter the chain instantly if it reached Earth. The polymer scientist's job is to install "shock absorbers" (UV stabilizers like HALS and benzotriazoles) that absorb the harmful radiation before it damages the molecular backbone.

## 1.4 Why UV Light Matters in Polymer Engineering

Polymers such as **polyethylene, polypropylene, PVC, polycarbonates, and polyurethanes** are routinely exposed to solar radiation in outdoor applications (pipes, cable insulation, automotive parts, agricultural films, roofing sheets). Without UV protection:

- **Tensile strength drops** by up to **70%** within 6–12 months of outdoor exposure.
- **Elongation at break** decreases sharply (embrittlement).
- **Discoloration and chalking** occur due to chromophore formation.
- **Service life** of the product reduces dramatically.

Hence, understanding UV–polymer interactions is critical to designing **weatherable polymeric materials**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of UV-Induced Polymer Degradation

The photodegradation of a polymer proceeds through a well-defined sequence of photophysical and photochemical stages. The **Jablonski Diagram** is the foundational model used to describe these electronic transitions:

### Stage 1 — Photon Absorption
The polymer's chromophoric group (C=O, C=C, aromatic rings, residual catalyst impurities) absorbs a UV photon, promoting an electron from the **ground singlet state (S₀)** to an excited singlet state (S₁ or S₂):

$$Polymer \xrightarrow{h\nu} Polymer^{*} \quad (S_0 \rightarrow S_1)$$

### Stage 2 — Excited State Deactivation (Photophysical Pathways)
The excited polymer molecule can lose energy through several competing pathways:

1. **Internal Conversion (IC):** Non-radiative transition S₁ → S₀ (heat release).
2. **Fluorescence:** Radiative emission S₁ → S₀ + hν_f (lifetime ~10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁷ s).
3. **Intersystem Crossing (ISC):** Spin-forbidden transition S₁ → T₁ (triplet state).
4. **Phosphorescence:** Radiative emission T₁ → S₀ + hν_p (lifetime ~10⁻³ to 10 s).
5. **Energy Transfer:** Quenching by O₂ or stabilizers.
6. **Photochemical Reaction:** Bond cleavage, leading to degradation.

### Stage 3 — Photochemical Initiation (Chain Scission / Crosslinking)
From the triplet state, bond homolysis generates free radicals:

$$Polymer^{*} (T_1) \rightarrow Polymer^{\bullet} + R^{\bullet}$$

These radicals react with atmospheric **oxygen** (photo-oxidation):

$$Polymer^{\bullet} + O_2 \rightarrow Polymer-OO^{\bullet}$$

This initiates the **auto-oxidation cycle** (Bolland-Gee mechanism) consisting of:
- **Initiation:** Radical generation by UV
- **Propagation:** Peroxy/hydroperoxide formation
- **Branching:** Hydroperoxide decomposition
- **Termination:** Radical coupling

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Polymers with aromatic backbones (PS, PC, PET) absorb strongly above 300 nm and degrade faster than aliphatic polymers (PE, PP), which is why PE/PP require higher loadings of UV stabilizers for outdoor use.

## 2.2 Beer-Lambert Law (UV-Vis Spectroscopy Foundation)

The intensity of UV light transmitted through a polymer film is governed by the **Beer-Lambert Law**:

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

Where:
- $A$ = Absorbance (dimensionless, also called optical density)
- $\varepsilon$ = Molar extinction coefficient (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹)
- $c$ = Concentration of chromophore (mol/L)
- $l$ = Optical path length through the sample (cm)

In transmittance form:

$$A = -\log_{10}(T) = -\log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

where $I_0$ is the incident intensity and $I$ is the transmitted intensity.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|-------------------|------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Photon Energy | $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $E$ in J, $\lambda$ in m | Calculating UV energy for bond breaking |
| Molar Absorptivity Relation | $E = N_A \cdot h\nu$ | $N_A$ = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹ | Einstein's energy per mole of photons |
| Beer-Lambert Law | $A = \varepsilon c l$ | $A$ dimensionless; $\varepsilon$ L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹ | UV-Vis spectroscopy |
| Transmittance | $T = \dfrac{I}{I_0}$ | Dimensionless | Measuring light penetration |
| Absorbance-Transmittance | $A = -\log_{10} T$ | Dimensionless | Converting between $A$ and $T$ |
| Quantum Yield | $\Phi = \dfrac{\text{Number of events}}{\text{Number of photons absorbed}}$ | Dimensionless | Efficiency of photodegradation |
| Carbonyl Index | $CI = \dfrac{A_{1710\,cm^{-1}}}{A_{2910\,cm^{-1}}}$ | FTIR ratio | Tracking photo-oxidation |
| Critical Wavelength for Bond Cleavage | $\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{N_A hc}{E_{bond}}$ | nm | Determining which UV band breaks a bond |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application | UV-Related Concern |
|-----------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Automotive** | Dashboard, bumpers, headlamp lenses | Yellowing, haze, embrittlement |
| **Solar Energy** | PV module encapsulants (EVA) | Yellowing reduces transmittance |
| **Packaging** | PET bottles, BOPP films | Loss of clarity, off-flavours |
| **Agriculture** | Greenhouse films (LDPE) | UV screening for crop protection |
| **Construction** | PVC pipes, roofing sheets | Service life under solar exposure |
| **Biomedical** | UV-cured dental composites | Controlled photopolymerization |

> [!TIP]
> **Why 'How' Matters:** The Beer-Lambert Law directly quantifies UV absorption, which is the *first* step in any photodegradation pathway. If we can suppress absorption (using UV absorbers like benzotriazoles), we suppress all downstream degradation reactions — this is the *engineering lever* KTU examiners love to test.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Wavelength for Polymer Bond Cleavage

**Problem:** Calculate the maximum wavelength of UV light required to break a **C–C single bond** in a polyethylene chain, given that the bond dissociation energy is **347 kJ/mol**.

### Step 1 — Convert Bond Energy to Per-Bond Energy

The bond dissociation energy is given per mole. To obtain energy per single bond, divide by Avogadro's number:

$$E_{bond} = \frac{347 \times 10^3 \text{ J/mol}}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ bonds/mol}}$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate Numerically

$$E_{bond} = \frac{347000}{6.022 \times 10^{23}}$$

$$E_{bond} = 5.762 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J per bond}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Planck's Equation to Find Wavelength

Rearranging $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ to solve for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_{bond}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Planck's Constant and Speed of Light

$$\lambda = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}) \times (3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s})}{5.762 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Numerator

$$hc = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8 = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m}$$

### Step 6 — Divide Numerator by Denominator

$$\lambda = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{5.762 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\lambda = 3.450 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

### Step 7 — Convert to Nanometres

$$\lambda = 3.450 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \times \frac{10^9 \text{ nm}}{1 \text{ m}}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{max} = 345 \text{ nm}}$$

### Step 8 — Interpretation

This wavelength falls in the **UV-A band (315–400 nm)**. Therefore, **sunlight (which contains UV-A)** possesses sufficient energy to break C–C bonds in polyethylene, explaining why untreated PE degrades outdoors. For comparison:

| Bond Type | Bond Energy (kJ/mol) | Critical $\lambda_{max}$ (nm) |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| C–C | 347 | 345 |
| C–H | 414 | 289 |
| C=C | 615 | 195 |
| C=O | 715 | 167 |
| O–H | 460 | 260 |

> [!NOTE]
> Only photons with wavelength **shorter than** $\lambda_{max}$ carry enough energy to break the bond. Longer wavelengths (like visible light at 500 nm) are harmless to most polymer backbones.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Beer-Lambert Law for UV Absorber Efficiency

**Problem:** A 0.1 mm thick polyethylene film contains a uniformly dispersed benzotriazole UV absorber at a concentration of $2 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L. Given $\varepsilon = 1.5 \times 10^4$ L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹ at 340 nm, calculate:
(i) the absorbance, (ii) the percentage transmittance.

### Step 1 — Convert Path Length to cm

$$l = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \times 10^{-1} \text{ cm} = 0.01 \text{ cm}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Beer-Lambert Law

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

$$A = (1.5 \times 10^4) \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.01)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate Numerically

$$A = 1.5 \times 10^4 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-2}$$

$$A = 1.5 \times 2 \times 10^{4-3-2}$$

$$\boxed{A = 0.30}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Transmittance

$$T = 10^{-A} = 10^{-0.30}$$

$$T = 0.5012 \approx 50.12\%$$

### Step 5 — Compute Percentage Transmittance

$$\%T = T \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\%T = 50.12\%}$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation

Only **50.12%** of the 340 nm UV radiation penetrates the film — nearly **half** is absorbed by the stabilizer. By increasing absorber concentration to $5 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L, the absorbance rises to **0.75** and transmittance drops to **17.8%**, dramatically extending polymer service life. This calculation underlies the **economic optimization of UV stabilizer loading** in commercial polymer formulations.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Quantum Yield of Photodegradation

**Problem:** A polymer sample absorbs $1.0 \times 10^{18}$ photons of UV light (at 300 nm) and undergoes $2.5 \times 10^{17}$ chain scission events. Calculate the **quantum yield** of chain scission.

### Step 1 — Apply Quantum Yield Definition

$$\Phi = \frac{\text{Number of chain scission events}}{\text{Number of photons absorbed}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$\Phi = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{17}}{1.0 \times 10^{18}}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi = 0.25}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

A quantum yield of **0.25** means **every fourth absorbed photon** causes a chain scission event. Values of $\Phi > 1$ indicate a **chain reaction** (radical propagation amplifies the initial photochemical event), which is characteristic of polypropylene photo-oxidation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Jablonski Diagram — UV Excitation Pathways in Polymers

This diagram illustrates all photophysical deactivation pathways after a polymer absorbs a UV photon.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Ground Singlet State S0 - Polymer Chain at Rest"]
    B["Excited Singlet State S1 - Vibrational Levels"]
    C["Higher Excited State S2"]
    D["Triplet State T1"]
    E["Fluorescence Emission - h nu_f"]
    F["Phosphorescence Emission - h nu_p"]
    G["Internal Conversion IC - Heat Loss"]
    H["Intersystem Crossing ISC"]
    I["Bond Cleavage - Chain Scission"]
    J["Energy Transfer to Quencher"]
    K["Free Radical Formation"]
    L["Photo Oxidation Products"]

    C -->|Vibrational Relaxation| B
    A -->|UV Photon Absorption h nu| B
    B -->|Radiative Emission| E
    E --> A
    B -->|Non Radiative| G
    G --> A
    B -->|Spin Flip| H
    H --> D
    D -->|Radiative Emission| F
    F --> A
    D -->|Energy Transfer| J
    J --> A
    D -->|Bond Homolysis| I
    I --> K
    D -->|Direct Photoreaction| L
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 UV Stabilization Mechanism Block Diagram

This diagram maps the four major classes of UV stabilizers and their mode of action.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UV["Incoming UV Radiation - 280 to 400 nm"]

    subgraph Stabilizers["UV Stabilizer Toolbox"]
        UA["UV Absorbers - Benzotriazoles and Benzophenones"]
        Q["Quenchers - Nickel Chelates"]
        HALS["HALS - Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers"]
        AO["Antioxidants - Hindered Phenols and Phosphites"]
    end

    P["Polymer Matrix - Backbone and Chromophores"]
    D["Degradation - Chain Scission and Yellowing"]
    H["Heat Dissipation"]
    R["Radical Scavenging"]
    RO["Decomposition of Hydroperoxides"]

    UV -->|Hits Polymer| P
    P -->|Without Protection| D

    UV -->|Absorbed by| UA
    UA -->|Converts to| H

    UV -->|Energy Transferred to| Q
    Q -->|Releases as| H

    UV -->|Excites Polymer to Triplet| HALS
    HALS -->|Scavenges Radicals| R

    AO -->|Intercepts Peroxides| RO

    H -->|Protects| P
    R -->|Protects| P
    RO -->|Protects| P
```

## 4.3 UV-Vis Spectrophotometer Functional Architecture

The following block diagram shows how UV-Vis spectroscopy characterizes polymer degradation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LS["Light Source - Deuterium Lamp for UV and Tungsten for Visible"]
    M1["Monochromator - Diffraction Grating"]
    SP["Slit - Wavelength Selection"]
    SAMP["Sample Holder - Polymer Film in Cuvette"]
    REF["Reference Holder - Blank Solvent"]
    DET["Photodetector - Photomultiplier Tube"]
    AMP["Amplifier and Signal Processor"]
    DSP["Display - Absorbance vs Wavelength Plot"]

    LS --> M1
    M1 --> SP
    SP -->|Sample Beam| SAMP
    SP -->|Reference Beam| REF
    SAMP --> DET
    REF --> DET
    DET --> AMP
    AMP --> DSP
```

## 4.4 Photo-Oxidation Auto-Oxidation Cycle

This flowchart represents the **Bolland-Gee cycle** of polymer photo-oxidation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I1["Initiation - Polymer plus UV gives Polymer Radical"]
    I2["Propagation - Polymer Radical plus O2 gives Polymer Peroxy Radical"]
    I3["Propagation - Polymer Peroxy Radical plus Polymer gives Polymer Hydroperoxide plus Polymer Radical"]
    I4["Branching - Polymer Hydroperoxide gives Polymer Oxy Radical plus Hydroxyl Radical"]
    I5["Branching - Polymer Oxy Radical gives Carbonyl plus Polymer Radical"]
    T1["Termination - Two Polymer Radicals couple to Crosslink"]
    T2["Termination - Polymer Peroxy Radicals form Peroxide Bridge"]

    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> I3
    I3 --> I4
    I4 --> I5
    I5 --> I2
    I3 --> T1
    I2 --> T2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define UV light. Classify the UV spectrum into its sub-bands with wavelength ranges.**

**Model Answer:**

Ultraviolet (UV) light is electromagnetic radiation with wavelengths from **10 nm to 400 nm**, lying between visible light and X-rays. It is classified into four bands by the CIE:

| Band | Wavelength Range |
|------|------------------|
| UV-A (Near) | 315 – 400 nm |
| UV-B (Middle) | 280 – 315 nm |
| UV-C (Far) | 100 – 280 nm |
| Vacuum UV | 10 – 200 nm |

UV-B is the most damaging band to polymer backbones because it overlaps the absorption maxima of common chromophores (C=O, aromatic C=C).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**State the Beer-Lambert Law. What is its significance in polymer UV degradation studies?**

**Model Answer:**

The Beer-Lambert Law states that the absorbance of light by a medium is directly proportional to the concentration of the absorbing species and the optical path length:

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

where $A$ is absorbance, $\varepsilon$ is the molar extinction coefficient, $c$ is concentration, and $l$ is path length.

**Significance:** It allows quantitative measurement of chromophore concentration in degrading polymers using UV-Vis spectroscopy. The growth of carbonyl absorption at 1710 cm⁻¹ (or 280 nm) over time directly tracks photo-oxidation kinetics.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat diagram the Jablonski diagram describing photophysical processes in a UV-excited polymer molecule. Discuss fluorescence, phosphorescence, internal conversion, and intersystem crossing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the mechanism of UV-induced photo-oxidation of polyolefins. Explain how HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers) protect the polymer.

#### Model Solution to (a):

The **Jablonski diagram** is a graphical representation of electronic energy levels and transitions in a molecule after UV absorption:

1. **Absorption:** The polymer chromophore absorbs a UV photon, promoting an electron from the ground singlet state **S₀** to an excited singlet state **S₁** (or higher S₂).

2. **Vibrational Relaxation:** The molecule rapidly loses excess vibrational energy as heat, settling to the lowest vibrational level of S₁.

3. **Internal Conversion (IC):** A non-radiative transition between states of the same spin multiplicity (S₁ → S₀), releasing energy as heat.

4. **Fluorescence:** Radiative transition S₁ → S₀ with emission of a photon of longer wavelength (Stokes shift). Lifetime: **10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁷ s**.

5. **Intersystem Crossing (ISC):** Spin-forbidden transition from singlet to triplet (S₁ → T₁), facilitated by heavy atoms or oxygen.

6. **Phosphorescence:** Radiative transition T₁ → S₀, with much longer lifetime (**10⁻³ to 10 s**) because it is spin-forbidden.

7. **Photochemical Reactions:** From T₁, the molecule may undergo bond cleavage, energy transfer, or reaction with O₂ — these are the pathways that initiate polymer degradation.

The fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra appear at **longer wavelengths** than absorption due to energy lost through vibrational relaxation.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Mechanism of UV Photo-oxidation of Polyolefins:**

**Step 1 — Initiation:** UV photons (especially 290–400 nm) cleave C–C or C–H bonds of the polyolefin backbone or side groups, producing alkyl radicals:

$$-CH_2-CH_2- \xrightarrow{h\nu} -CH_2-^{\bullet}CH_2- + H^{\bullet}$$

**Step 2 — Propagation:** Alkyl radicals react with atmospheric O₂ to form peroxy radicals, which abstract hydrogen from neighbouring chains:

$$R^{\bullet} + O_2 \rightarrow ROO^{\bullet}$$

$$ROO^{\bullet} + RH \rightarrow ROOH + R^{\bullet}$$

**Step 3 — Branching:** Hydroperoxides decompose (assisted by UV) into alkoxy and hydroxyl radicals:

$$ROOH \xrightarrow{h\nu} RO^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}OH$$

These radicals trigger further chain scission and form carbonyl groups (ketones, aldehydes, carboxylic acids) that appear as the characteristic **1710 cm⁻¹ FTIR peak**.

**Step 4 — Termination:** Two radicals couple to form stable products, halting the cycle.

**Role of HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers):**

HALS are derivatives of **2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine**. They do **not absorb UV light directly**. Their mechanism is:

1. HALS is oxidized by peroxy radicals to a stable **nitroxyl radical (R₂NO•)**:

$$R_2NH + ROO^{\bullet} \rightarrow R_2NO^{\bullet} + ROOH$$

2. The nitroxyl radical scavenges alkyl radicals from the polymer, forming **alkoxyamine**:

$$R_2NO^{\bullet} + R^{\bullet} \rightarrow R_2NOR$$

3. Alkoxyamine reacts with peroxy radicals, regenerating the nitroxyl radical — this is the **Denisov cycle**, making HALS a **catalytic** stabilizer (effective at loadings as low as 0.1–0.5 wt%).

HALS offer superior long-term thermal and photo-oxidative protection and are the industry standard for polyolefins.

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Beer-Lambert Law with its mathematical form. A polymer film of thickness 0.05 cm contains a UV absorber at 0.002 mol/L concentration. If $\varepsilon = 1.8 \times 10^4$ L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹, calculate the absorbance and percentage transmittance at 340 nm.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the classification of UV stabilizers with examples. Compare UV absorbers and HALS in terms of their mechanism.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Beer-Lambert Law:**

When monochromatic light passes through an absorbing medium, the absorbance is proportional to both the concentration of the absorbing species and the path length through the medium:

$$A = \varepsilon c l$$

where:
- $A$ = absorbance (dimensionless)
- $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹)
- $c$ = concentration (mol/L)
- $l$ = path length (cm)

Transmittance and absorbance are related by:

$$A = -\log_{10} T \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = 10^{-A}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $l = 0.05 \text{ cm}$
- $c = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L}$
- $\varepsilon = 1.8 \times 10^4 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$

$$A = (1.8 \times 10^4) \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.05)$$

$$A = 1.8 \times 2 \times 0.05 \times 10^{4-3}$$

$$A = 0.18 \times 10^{1} = 1.8$$

**Absorbance = 1.8**

Transmittance:

$$T = 10^{-1.8} = 0.01585$$

$$\%T = 0.01585 \times 100 = 1.585\%$$

**Result:** Only **1.585%** of 340 nm UV light penetrates the film — demonstrating excellent UV screening.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Classification of UV Stabilizers:**

| Class | Mode of Action | Examples |
|-------|----------------|----------|
| **UV Absorbers** | Compete with polymer for UV absorption; dissipate energy as heat | Benzotriazoles (Tinuvin P), Benzophenones, Triazines |
| **Quenchers** | Accept energy from excited polymer (triplet state) and release as heat | Nickel chelates (Ni(II) bisphenol) |
| **HALS** | Scavenge radicals via Denisov cycle; do not absorb UV | Tinuvin 770, Chimassorb 944 |
| **Antioxidants** | Decompose hydroperoxides; interrupt auto-oxidation | Hindered phenols (Irganox 1010), Phosphites |
| **Optical Brighteners** | Convert UV to visible blue light (mask yellowing) | Stilbene derivatives |

**Comparison — UV Absorbers vs HALS:**

| Feature | UV Absorbers | HALS |
|---------|--------------|------|
| **Primary Mechanism** | Absorb UV photon → heat | Catalytic radical scavenging |
| **Stoichiometry** | Consumed slowly | Regenerated (catalytic) |
| **Effective Loading** | 1 – 5 wt% | 0.1 – 0.5 wt% |
| **Optimal Thickness** | Thick sections (≥ 50 µm) | Effective in thin films too |
| **Photostability** | Moderate (undergo Photo-Fries) | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Synergy** | Often combined with HALS | Often combined with UV absorbers |

Modern commercial formulations use **synergistic blends** of UV absorbers + HALS + antioxidants for optimal protection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing UV-C with UV-A in bond-breaking questions:** Students often write "UV-C breaks C–C bonds." Correct answer: UV-A (≤ 345 nm) is sufficient to break C–C bonds (347 kJ/mol). UV-C has *more* than enough energy but is filtered by the ozone layer.
>
> 2. **Missing units in Beer-Lambert:** Always specify path length in **cm**, not mm or m. Marks are deducted for unit inconsistency.
>
> 3. **Stating HALS absorb UV light:** Wrong. HALS do **not** absorb UV — they scavenge radicals catalytically via the Denisov cycle.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Photon Energy Equation:** KTU examiners expect you to start photodegradation calculations with $E = hc/\lambda$ explicitly written.
>
> 5. **Drawing Jablonski diagram without arrows:** Always include directional arrows and label the type of transition (radiative vs non-radiative).
>
> 6. **Mixing fluorescence and phosphorescence:** Fluorescence = S₁ → S₀ (singlet to singlet, fast). Phosphorescence = T₁ → S₀ (triplet to singlet, slow, spin-forbidden).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — UV Light in Polymer Science**
>
> - **UV Spectrum:** 10–400 nm; sub-bands UV-A (315–400), UV-B (280–315), UV-C (100–280), VUV (10–200).
> - **Photon Energy Equation:** $E = hc/\lambda$; higher frequency (shorter λ) = more damage.
> - **Critical Wavelengths:** C–C ≈ 345 nm; C–H ≈ 289 nm; C=C ≈ 195 nm; C=O ≈ 167 nm.
> - **Beer-Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon c l$; absorbance measured by UV-Vis spectroscopy.
> - **Jablonski Diagram:** S₀ → S₁ (absorption) → IC/Fluorescence/ISC → T₁ → Phosphorescence/Photoreaction.
> - **Photodegradation Stages:** Initiation → Propagation → Branching → Termination (Bolland-Gee cycle).
> - **Photo-oxidation marker:** Carbonyl peak at **1710 cm⁻¹** in FTIR; UV-Vis absorption at 280 nm.
> - **UV Stabilizers — Four Classes:**
>   - UV Absorbers (benzotriazoles) — competitive absorption
>   - Quenchers (Ni chelates) — energy transfer
>   - HALS (piperidine derivatives) — Denisov cycle, catalytic
>   - Antioxidants (phenols, phosphites) — peroxide decomposition
> - **Quantum Yield $\Phi$:** $\Phi > 1$ indicates chain reaction (PP is prone); $\Phi < 1$ means limited chain scission.
> - **Carbonyl Index:** $CI = A_{1710}/A_{2910}$; increases monotonically with UV exposure time.
> - **Synergistic Stabilization:** Commercial formulations combine UV absorbers + HALS + antioxidants.
> - **Engineering Numbers:** Outdoor PE loses ~70% tensile strength in 12 months without stabilization; HALS at 0.3 wt% extends service life to 5+ years.
> - **Vocabulary Mastery:** Chromophore, photo-Fries, Norrish Type I/II, hydroperoxide, alkoxyamine, nitroxyl radical.

**End of Module 4 Notes — UV Light. Best of luck for your KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
