<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — MICROORGANISMS IN POLYMER SCIENCE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Polymer Science (PCPOT302)**, *microorganisms* are microscopic living entities — primarily **bacteria, fungi (moulds and yeasts), actinomycetes, and microalgae** — that interact with polymeric materials in two principal, industrially relevant ways:

1. **Biosynthesis (Microbial Polymer Production):** Certain microorganisms synthesize intracellular or extracellular polymers as part of their metabolic activity (e.g., storage granules, exopolysaccharides). These are termed **biopolymers** or **microbial polymers**.
2. **Biodegradation:** Microorganisms secrete enzymes (hydrolases, oxidoreductases, lyases) that cleave the polymer backbone or side chains, converting high-molecular-weight macromolecules into oligomers, monomers, CO₂, H₂O, and biomass — a process formally called **microbial/enzymatic polymer degradation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> *Microorganisms are nature's smallest polymer chemists — they can both **build** polymers (via fermentation) and **break** polymers (via enzymatic hydrolysis/oxidation), making them the cornerstone of sustainable polymer technology.*

### 1.2 Classes of Microorganisms Relevant to Polymers

| Class | Example Genera | Polymer-Related Role | Typical Polymer Targets |
|-------|---------------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| **Bacteria** | *Pseudomonas, Bacillus, Ralstonia, Cupriavidus, Alcaligenes* | PHA biosynthesis; depolymerase secretion | PHB, PHBV, PHBHHx, PLA |
| **Fungi (Moulds)** | *Aspergillus, Penicillium, Fusarium, Cladosporium* | Cutinase/laccase/Mn-peroxidase secretion | Polyester, lignin-model polymers, PE (oxidised) |
| **Yeasts** | *Saccharomyces, Yarrowia* | Extracellular polysaccharide production | Pullulan-like polysaccharides |
| **Actinomycetes** | *Streptomyces, Nocardia* | Complex hydrolytic enzyme cocktails | Chitin, polyesters |
| **Microalgae** | *Chlorella, Spirulina* | PHB accumulation under nutrient stress | PHB, polysaccharides |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recycling Crew"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Municipal Recycling Plant**
> Think of microorganisms as a **biological recycling plant** operating at the molecular level.
> - **Collection trucks** → Microbial cells transport enzymes to the polymer surface.
> - **Sorting facility** → Surface adhesion (biofilm formation) identifies the polymer type.
> - **Shredders** → Extracellular depolymerases cut long polymer chains into small oligomers/monomers.
> - **Energy recovery** → The monomers are metabolised (TCA cycle, β-oxidation) to yield **CO₂ + H₂O + biomass + energy (ATP)**.
> - **Specialty products** → Some microbes "rebuild" monomers into **new, useful biopolymers** (e.g., PHAs from sugars/fatty acids).
>
> Just as a recycling plant cannot shred *every* type of plastic at once, microbes can only degrade polymers whose **chemical bonds match the active site geometry** of their enzymes — this is why PET (aromatic, highly crystalline) resists most microbes, whereas PLA (ester-linked, amorphous) degrades readily.

### 1.4 Why Study Microorganisms in Polymer Science?

- **Environmental sustainability** — Microbial degradation offers an end-of-life solution for plastic waste.
- **Renewable feedstocks** — Bio-based monomers (lactic acid, succinic acid, 1,3-propanediol) are produced via fermentation.
- **Biomedical applications** — Microbial polyesters (PHA, PLA) are biocompatible and used in sutures, scaffolds, drug-delivery.
- **Industrial biopolymers** — Xanthan gum, gellan, pullulan, curdlan are microbially produced thickeners/stabilizers with multi-billion-dollar markets.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 Microbial Synthesis of Polymers (Biopolymer Production)

Microbial polymer biosynthesis follows one of two metabolic pathways:

#### (a) **Intracellular accumulation under nutrient imbalance**
- *Carbon source present, but N, P, O₂, or Mg limited* → Excess carbon is channelled into storage polymers.
- Classic example: **Polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs)** accumulated as granular inclusions (0.2–0.5 µm diameter) inside the cytoplasm.
- Once nutrient stress is relieved, the granules are mobilised as carbon/energy reserve.

#### (b) **Extracellular secretion**
- Microbes secrete polysaccharides into the medium as part of biofilm/extracellular polymeric substance (EPS) matrix.
- Examples: **Xanthan gum** (*Xanthomonas campestris*), **pullulan** (*Aureobasidium pullulans*), **gellan** (*Sphingomonas paucimobilis*), **curdlan** (*Agrobacterium*).

### 2.2 Microbial Degradation of Polymers — The Four-Step Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** *Any biodegradable polymer, before being mineralised, must pass through these four sequential stages. Examiners frequently award marks for naming them in order.*

**Step 1 — Biodeterioration:** Microbial colonisation and biofilm formation on the polymer surface. Surface erosion, pitting, and release of additives.

**Step 2 — Biofragmentation:** Extracellular depolymerases (hydrolases, oxidases) cleave the polymer into low-MW oligomers and monomers. The polymer's M̅n and M̅w decrease dramatically.

**Step 3 — Assimilation:** Oligomers/monomers cross the microbial cell membrane (passive diffusion or active transport) and enter primary metabolism.

**Step 4 — Mineralisation:** Complete oxidation to CO₂, H₂O, CH₄ (anaerobic), and new biomass. Mineralisation is the only step that genuinely "closes the carbon loop."

### 2.3 Key Enzymes Involved

| Enzyme Class | EC Number | Action on Polymer | Example |
|--------------|-----------|--------------------|---------|
| **Lipase / Cutinase** | EC 3.1.1.x | Hydrolyses ester bonds in polyesters | PHB depolymerase |
| **Protease** | EC 3.4.x.x | Hydrolyses peptide bonds in polyamides/ proteins | Serine protease on silk/collagen |
| **Cellulase / Hemicellulase** | EC 3.2.1.x | Hydrolyses glycosidic bonds in polysaccharides | Cellulase on cellulose |
| **Laccase / Peroxidase** | EC 1.10.3.x / 1.11.1.x | Oxidative radical formation on aromatics | Laccase on lignin-like structures |
| **PHB Depolymerase** | EC 3.1.1.75 | Specific hydrolysis of PHB/PHA chains | *Pseudomonas* depolymerase |

### 2.4 Factors Governing Microbial Degradation

1. **Polymer chemistry** — Backbone bond type, presence of hydrolysable linkages.
2. **Crystallinity (χ_c)** — Higher χ_c → slower degradation (enzymes attack amorphous regions first).
3. **Molecular weight (M̅w)** — Low M̅w → faster degradation (more chain ends, higher solubility of fragments).
4. **Glass transition temperature (T_g)** — Polymer must be above its T_g (rubbery) for efficient enzymatic attack.
5. **Surface area / morphology** — Porosity, hydrophilicity, surface energy.
6. **Environmental factors** — Temperature, pH, humidity, oxygen availability, nutrient supply, microbial consortium diversity.
7. **Additives / plasticisers** — May accelerate or inhibit degradation.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for KTU University Exam calculations.**

| # | Equation / Concept | LaTeX Form | Meaning / Units |
|---|--------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | First-order biodegradation kinetics | $C_t = C_0 \, e^{-k t}$ | $C_t$ = polymer conc. at time $t$; $C_0$ = initial conc.; $k$ = biodegradation rate constant (day⁻¹) |
| 2 | Half-life of polymer | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Time for 50% degradation |
| 3 | Mineralisation (CO₂ evolution) | $\text{Degree of mineralisation (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2^{\text{sample}}}{\text{CO}_2^{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | ASTM D5988 / D6691 test parameter |
| 4 | Biochemical Oxygen Demand | $\text{BOD} = \dfrac{D_1 - D_2}{P}$ | $D_1, D_2$ = dissolved O₂; $P$ = sample vol. fraction |
| 5 | M̅w reduction rate | $-\dfrac{dM_w}{dt} = k_d \, M_w$ | Random-chain scission by enzymes |
| 6 | PHA accumulation yield | $Y_{PHA/X} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{PHA}}{\Delta X}$ | g PHA per g dry cell weight |
| 7 | Degree of biodegradation | $D_b = \dfrac{M_0 - M_t}{M_0} \times 100$ | Mass loss percentage at time $t$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Table Notation:** All absolute-value signs and conditionals in formulas are written with `\vert` to keep the markdown table parser safe.

### 2.6 Engineering / Industrial Utility

- **Bioplastics Industry** — Manufacturing PHB, PHBV, PLA via fermentation; product value > $5 billion globally.
- **Waste Management** — Composting infrastructure depends on microbial consortia degrading PLA, PHA, starch blends.
- **Medical Devices** — Suture materials, bone scaffolds, controlled drug-release matrices all rely on predictable microbial/enzymatic breakdown *in vivo*.
- **Bioremediation** — Engineered *Pseudomonas* strains degrade recalcitrant polymers (PET-like) via evolved PETases.
- **Food Industry** — Xanthan, gellan, pullulan as thickeners, gelling agents, edible films.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — First-Order Biodegradation Kinetics (Mineralisation Model)

Microbial degradation in well-aerated, nutrient-rich compost typically follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to the remaining polymer concentration.

**Starting assumption:**
The rate at which the polymer mass disappears is proportional to the remaining polymer mass.

$$
-\frac{dC}{dt} = k \cdot C
$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables:**
Bring all concentration terms to one side and time terms to the other.

$$
\frac{dC}{C} = -k \, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides from $C_0$ at $t=0$ to $C_t$ at time $t$:**

$$
\int_{C_0}^{C_t} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$
\ln(C_t) - \ln(C_0) = -k \, t
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -k \, t
$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate both sides to obtain the time-evolution of polymer concentration:**

$$
\frac{C_t}{C_0} = e^{-k t}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_t = C_0 \, e^{-k t}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the half-life (time for 50% degradation, $C_t = C_0/2$):**

$$
\frac{C_0}{2} = C_0 \, e^{-k \, t_{1/2}}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k \, t_{1/2}}
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -k \, t_{1/2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Favourite:** A PHB film in activated sludge has $k = 0.04$ day⁻¹. Find the time for 90% degradation.
>
> Solution: $C_t/C_0 = 0.10 = e^{-0.04 t} \Rightarrow t = \frac{\ln 10}{0.04} = \frac{2.303}{0.04} = 57.6$ days.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Random-Chain Scission and Molecular Weight Reduction

For an enzyme that cleaves the polymer backbone randomly (e.g., PHB depolymerase), the rate of decrease in number-average molecular weight (M̅n) follows:

$$
\frac{1}{M_{n,t}} - \frac{1}{M_{n,0}} = \frac{k_d \, t}{\overline{M_0^{\,0}}}
$$

where $\overline{M_0^{\,0}}$ is the average molecular weight of the monomer repeat unit.

**Step 1 — For random scission, the number of chain breaks per unit mass at time $t$ is:**

$$
N_{\text{breaks}} = k_d \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Each break converts one chain into two. Using Saito's relation for random scission:**

$$
\frac{1}{M_{n,t}} = \frac{1}{M_{n,0}} + k_d \, t
$$

**Step 3 — Integrating over time (assuming constant enzyme activity):**

$$
\boxed{\,M_{n,t} = \frac{M_{n,0}}{1 + k_d \, M_{n,0} \, t}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Limiting cases:**
- When $k_d \, M_{n,0} \, t \ll 1$: $M_{n,t} \approx M_{n,0}$ (early degradation, few breaks).
- When $k_d \, M_{n,0} \, t \gg 1$: $M_{n,t} \approx \dfrac{1}{k_d t}$ (chains become oligomers).

This equation is widely used to fit GPC/SEC data during enzymatic degradation studies.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Degree of Mineralisation from CO₂ Evolution

The **theoretical CO₂ yield** from complete oxidation of a polymer of empirical formula $C_a H_b O_c$ with mass $m$:

$$
\text{CO}_2^{\text{theor}} = m \cdot \frac{a \cdot 44}{12a + b + 16c}
$$

**Step 1 — Write the stoichiometric oxidation equation:**

$$
\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{O}_c + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow a\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{b}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 2 — From the equation, 1 mol of polymer (mass $12a+b+16c$) produces $a$ mol CO₂ (mass $44a$).**

**Step 3 — Therefore, the theoretical CO₂ per gram of polymer is:**

$$
\text{CO}_2^{\text{theor}} = \frac{44 \, a}{12a + b + 16c} \quad \text{(g CO}_2 \text{ per g polymer)}
$$

**Step 4 — The experimental degree of mineralisation at time $t$ is:**

$$
\boxed{\,D_{\min}(t) = \frac{\text{CO}_2^{\text{exp}}(t)}{\text{CO}_2^{\text{theor}}} \times 100\,}
$$

**Worked Example (PHB, empirical formula C₄H₆O₂, M₀⁰ = 86 g/mol):**

$$
\text{CO}_2^{\text{theor}} = \frac{44 \times 4}{12 \times 4 + 6 + 16 \times 2} = \frac{176}{86} = 2.047 \,\text{g CO}_2 \text{ per g PHB}
$$

So 1 g of PHB, fully mineralised, releases **2.047 g CO₂**. If a compost experiment measures 1.5 g CO₂ in 30 days:

$$
D_{\min} = \frac{1.5}{2.047} \times 100 = 73.3\% \text{ in 30 days}
$$

---

### 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Polymer-Degradable Microbe Cross-Reference

| Polymer | Backbone Bond | Degradable Microbe | Key Enzyme | Rate (typical) |
|---------|---------------|--------------------|------------|---------------|
| **PHB / PHA** | Ester | *Pseudomonas lemoignei, Alcaligenes faecalis* | PHB depolymerase (EC 3.1.1.75) | Fast (weeks–months) |
| **PLA** | Ester | *Amycolatopsis*, *Saccharothrix* | Proteinase K-like serine protease | Moderate (months in compost) |
| **Starch** | Glycosidic | *Aspergillus niger, Bacillus subtilis* | Amylase / glucoamylase | Very fast (days) |
| **Cellulose** | β-1,4-glycosidic | *Trichoderma reesei, Cellulomonas* | Cellulase (endoglucanase + cellobiohydrolase) | Fast (days–weeks) |
| **Chitosan** | β-1,4-glycosidic (N-acetyl) | *Penicillium*, *Aspergillus* | Chitinase / chitosanase | Fast (days–weeks) |
| **PCL (polycaprolactone)** | Ester | *Pseudomonas* sp. | Lipase / esterase | Moderate (months) |
| **PE (polyethylene)** | C–C (no heteroatom) | *Rhodococcus ruber* (after oxidation) | Alkane hydroxylase (Laccase on pre-oxidised) | Very slow (years–decades) |
| **PET** | Aromatic ester | *Ideonella sakaiensis* | PETase + MHETase | Slow (years ambient; weeks at 70 °C with engineered enzyme) |
| **Nylon-6** | Amide | *Pseudomonas* sp. (NK87) | 6-aminohexanoate-oligomer hydrolase | Moderate |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define biodegradation of polymers. List any four classes of microorganisms involved.**

**Model Answer:**
Biodegradation of polymers is the process by which microbial enzymatic action converts high-molecular-weight polymeric materials into low-molecular-weight fragments (oligomers and monomers) and ultimately mineralises them into CO₂, H₂O, CH₄, and biomass under aerobic or anaerobic conditions.

Four classes of microorganisms involved:
1. **Bacteria** (e.g., *Pseudomonas, Bacillus*) — degrade PHB, PLA, PCL via esterases/lipases.
2. **Fungi** (e.g., *Aspergillus, Penicillium*) — secrete cutinase, laccase, peroxidase.
3. **Actinomycetes** (e.g., *Streptomyces*) — versatile hydrolytic enzyme producers.
4. **Yeasts** (e.g., *Saccharomyces*) — degrade polysaccharides and some polyesters.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What are polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs)? Mention two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**
PHAs are a family of **intracellular, biodegradable, biocompatible polyesters** synthesised and accumulated as energy/carbon storage granules by various bacteria (e.g., *Cupriavidus necator, Alcaligenes latus*) under conditions of nutrient limitation (N or P) with excess carbon. The general repeat unit is $\text{--O--CHR--CH}_2\text{--CO--}$ where R varies (CH₃ in PHB, C₂H₅ in PHV, etc.).

**Two industrial applications:**
1. **Medical implants** — biodegradable sutures, bone scaffolds, and tissue-engineering matrices (FDA-approved).
2. **Packaging films and containers** — fully compostable replacement for PE/PP in single-use items.

*(Additional valid applications: drug-delivery microspheres, agricultural mulch films.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the four sequential stages of microbial polymer degradation with a neat schematic. Discuss how polymer structure (crystallinity, molecular weight, T_g) controls the rate.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the first-order biodegradation equation $C_t = C_0 e^{-kt}$ and obtain an expression for the half-life. A PLA cup (initial mass 5.0 g) is composted. After 90 days its mass is 1.2 g. Assuming first-order kinetics, calculate the rate constant *k* and the time required for 95% degradation.

---

**Model Solution to Q(a) — 7 Marks:**

*(Schematic: see Diagram A in Section 4.1; draw equivalent block diagram in exam.)*

**Stage 1 — Biodeterioration:** Microbes (bacteria, fungi) colonise the polymer surface using pili, adhesins, and EPS (extracellular polymeric substances). Biofilm forms; surface roughness increases; additives leach out.

**Stage 2 — Biofragmentation:** Cells secrete extracellular depolymerases (lipases, proteases, cellulases, PHB depolymerases, etc.) that hydrolyse backbone bonds, reducing M̅w. Water-soluble oligomers/monomers are released.

**Stage 3 — Assimilation:** Monomers cross the cytoplasmic membrane via passive diffusion (small MW) or active transporters. They enter catabolic pathways (β-oxidation, glycolysis, TCA cycle).

**Stage 4 — Mineralisation:** Complete oxidation yields CO₂ + H₂O (aerobic) or CO₂ + CH₄ (anaerobic) + new microbial biomass. The carbon is fully returned to the atmosphere.

**Effect of polymer structure on rate:**

| Factor | Effect on Degradation Rate | Reason |
|--------|---------------------------|--------|
| ↑ Crystallinity (χ_c) | Slower | Tight chain packing blocks enzyme access |
| ↑ Molecular weight (M̅w) | Slower | More chain scissions needed; lower solubility of fragments |
| T_g above test T | Slower | Polymer in glassy state, low chain mobility |
| T_g below test T | Faster | Rubbery state, greater free volume for enzyme diffusion |
| ↑ Hydrophilicity | Faster | Better water sorption, enzyme activity requires water |
| ↑ Surface area/porosity | Faster | Greater enzyme-substrate contact |

*(Award 1 mark for schematic, 4 marks for four stages in order, 2 marks for the structure-property table.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Derivation** (already shown in Section 3.1; reproduce concisely):

$$
-\frac{dC}{dt} = k C \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{dC}{C} = -k\,dt
$$

Integrating from $C_0$ to $C_t$ and 0 to $t$:

$$
\int_{C_0}^{C_t}\frac{dC}{C} = -k\int_0^t dt \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ln\!\left(\frac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -k t
$$

$$
\boxed{C_t = C_0 \, e^{-k t}}
$$

For half-life ($C_t = C_0/2$):

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k}
$$

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: $C_0 = 5.0$ g, $C_t = 1.2$ g at $t = 90$ days.

$$
\frac{C_t}{C_0} = \frac{1.2}{5.0} = 0.240
$$

$$
\ln(0.240) = -k \times 90
$$

$$
-1.427 = -90\,k \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{k = 0.01586\;\text{day}^{-1}}
$$

Half-life:

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{0.01586} \approx 43.7\;\text{days}
$$

Time for 95% degradation ($C_t/C_0 = 0.05$):

$$
\ln(0.05) = -k t \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t = \frac{\ln(1/0.05)}{0.01586} = \frac{2.996}{0.01586}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{95\%} \approx 188.9\;\text{days}}
$$

*(Award 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for rate-constant calculation, 2 marks for time calculation.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the biosynthesis of polyhydroxybutyrate (PHB) by *Cupriavidus necator*. Discuss the role of nutrient limitation, the PHA polymerase enzyme, and granule formation. List three commercial PHB copolymers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PHB film of initial mass 200 mg is placed in compost. After 60 days, 70 mg remains. After 120 days, 18 mg remains. Verify whether the degradation follows first-order kinetics. If yes, determine the rate constant and the time for 99% degradation.

---

**Model Solution to Q(a) — 7 Marks:**

*Cupriavidus necator* (formerly *Ralstonia eutropha / Wautersia eutropha*) is the model organism for industrial PHA production.

**Biosynthesis Pathway:**
1. **Growth phase** — Cells multiply on a balanced medium (glucose + ammonium chloride + mineral salts). No PHA accumulates.
2. **Production phase** — Once cell density is high, the nitrogen (or phosphorus, or magnesium) source is **limited**, while the carbon source (glucose, fructose, propionic acid, fatty acids) remains in excess.
3. Under this nutrient imbalance, acetyl-CoA accumulates. Two molecules of acetyl-CoA condense via **β-ketothiolase (PhaA)** → acetoacetyl-CoA → reduced by **acetoacetyl-CoA reductase (PhaB)** (NADPH-dependent) → **3-hydroxybutyryl-CoA (3-HB-CoA)**.
4. **PHA polymerase (PhaC)** polymerises 3-HB-CoA monomers into PHB chains of MW ~ 10⁵–10⁶ Da, which crystallise into amorphous-proteins-coated granules (0.2–0.5 µm).
5. The granules contain the polymer, plus proteins PhaP (phasin), PhaR (regulator), and depolymerases for later mobilisation.

**Three commercial PHB copolymers:**

1. **PHBV** — Poly(3-hydroxybutyrate-co-3-hydroxyvalerate); flexible, used in packaging.
2. **PHBHHx** — Poly(3-hydroxybutyrate-co-3-hydroxyhexanoate); low Tg, used in biomedical devices.
3. **P(3HB-co-4HB)** — Poly(3-hydroxybutyrate-co-4-hydroxybutyrate); elastic, used in drug delivery.

*(Award 3 marks for pathway, 2 marks for enzyme roles + granule formation, 2 marks for copolymer list.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q(b) — 7 Marks:**

For first-order kinetics, the ratio $C_t / C_0$ must obey:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -k\,t
$$

**At $t = 60$ days, $C_t = 70$ mg:**
$$
\ln(70/200) = \ln(0.350) = -1.050
$$
$$
k_1 = \frac{1.050}{60} = 0.0175\;\text{day}^{-1}
$$

**At $t = 120$ days, $C_t = 18$ mg:**
$$
\ln(18/200) = \ln(0.090) = -2.408
$$
$$
k_2 = \frac{2.408}{120} = 0.02007\;\text{day}^{-1}
$$

**Verification:** Both $k$ values are close (~14% deviation, attributable to mass-transfer lag and crystallinity changes as amorphous regions degrade first). Within experimental error, **first-order kinetics is confirmed**.

Average rate constant:

$$
\boxed{k_{\text{avg}} \approx 0.0188\;\text{day}^{-1}}
$$

**Time for 99% degradation** ($C_t/C_0 = 0.01$):

$$
t_{99\%} = \frac{\ln(100)}{k_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{4.605}{0.0188}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{99\%} \approx 245\;\text{days}}
$$

*(Award 3 marks for verifying linearity, 2 marks for k calculation, 2 marks for t₉₉%.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — Read Before Writing Your Exam:**
> 1. **Order of degradation stages:** Many students write "Biofragmentation → Biodeterioration → Assimilation → Mineralisation" — wrong. The KTU-correct order is **Biodeterioration → Biofragmentation → Assimilation → Mineralisation** (B-B-A-M).
> 2. **Confusing PHB with PLA:** PHB is **biosynthesised** by microbes; PLA is **chemically synthesised** from lactic acid (which itself can be fermentation-derived). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting units of *k*:** First-order biodegradation *k* is in **day⁻¹** or **h⁻¹** — not "per day⁻¹" or "1/day" (which are both acceptable, but be consistent).
> 4. **Crystallinity effect:** Students often write "more crystalline → faster degradation" — **opposite is true**. Enzymes attack the amorphous regions first because they have looser chain packing.
> 5. **Polymer–microbe specificity:** Do **not** state that *all* microbes degrade *all* polymers. Specificity arises from enzyme-substrate active-site geometry (lock-and-key / induced-fit).
> 6. **Mineralisation ≠ Fragmentation:** Mineralisation requires complete conversion to CO₂ + H₂O + biomass. Fragmentation is only M̅w reduction. Examiners award partial credit — be precise.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Microorganisms** relevant to polymers = bacteria, fungi, actinomycetes, yeasts, microalgae.
- [x] Two opposite roles: **biosynthesis** (PHAs, exopolysaccharides) and **biodegradation** (mineralisation).
- [x] The **four stages** of microbial degradation are: **Biodeterioration → Biofragmentation → Assimilation → Mineralisation** (B-B-A-M).
- [x] **Key enzymes:** PHB depolymerase (EC 3.1.1.75), PETase (EC 3.1.1.101), lipase, protease, cellulase, laccase.
- [x] **First-order kinetics:** $C_t = C_0 e^{-kt}$ ; half-life $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$.
- [x] **Random scission model:** $M_{n,t} = \dfrac{M_{n,0}}{1 + k_d M_{n,0} t}$.
- [x] **Mineralisation %** = (CO₂ sample / CO₂ theoretical) × 100.
- [x] **PHA producers:** *Cupriavidus necator, Alcaligenes latus, Pseudomonas putida* under N/P limitation.
- [x] **Commercial PHAs:** PHB, PHBV, PHBHHx, P(3HB-co-4HB).
- [x] **Polymer-structure effect on rate:** ↑ χ_c, ↑ M̅w, ↑ T_g → slower degradation.
- [x] **Biodegradable ≠ Bio-based:** Bio-based PET is partially renewable but is **not biodegradable**; PLA is bio-based **and** biodegradable.
- [x] **Degradability hierarchy** (fast → slow): Starch > Cellulose > PHB > PLA > PCL > PET > PE > PTFE.
- [x] **Recalcitrant polymers** (PE, PP, PTFE, PVC) resist microbial attack due to lack of hydrolysable bonds and high crystallinity.
- [x] **Aerobic degradation** → CO₂ + H₂O; **Anaerobic degradation** → CO₂ + CH₄ (biogas).
- [x] **PHB empirical formula** C₄H₆O₂ (M₀⁰ = 86 g/mol); theoretical CO₂ yield ≈ **2.047 g CO₂/g PHB**.
- [x] **ASTM standards** for biodegradability testing: **ASTM D5988** (soil), **D6691** (marine), **D5338** (compost).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT302 - POLYMER SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Crazing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crazing** is defined as a localized, non-catastrophic plastic deformation phenomenon occurring in glassy and semi-crystalline polymers under tensile stress, in which thin, planar, crack-like regions termed **crazes** develop perpendicular to the maximum principal stress direction. Each craze is a sponge-like microstructure comprising highly drawn **polymer fibrils** (typically 5–30 nm in diameter) interconnected across **microvoids**, and critically remains **load-bearing** until the fibrils rupture or disentangle.

This is the textbook definition expected in KTU board examinations for **PCPOT302 – Polymer Science**, Module 4 (Mechanical Behavior and Failure of Polymers).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a stretched rubber band that develops tiny, whitish, stress-whitened bands across its surface just before it breaks. These whitish bands are *not* cracks — if you carefully slice one open under a microscope, you will see thousands of hair-thin polymer strands bridging the two faces like a miniature fibrous bridge. The polymer has "pulled itself apart internally" but is still holding on by those tiny fibrils. This is precisely what a **craze** is.

The whitened appearance is caused by light scattering from the microvoids, while the fibrils maintain structural integrity — distinguishing crazes from true cracks.

## 1.3 Distinguishing Crazes from Cracks (Critical KTU Distinction)

| Feature | Craze | Crack |
|---|---|---|
| **Load-bearing capacity** | Yes (carries load via fibrils) | No (stress-free faces) |
| **Internal structure** | Fibrils + voids | Empty (atomically sharp) |
| **Refractive index** | Lower than bulk polymer (scatters light) | Same as air gap |
| **Reversibility** | Partially recoverable above Tg | Permanent |
| **Visual appearance** | Whitish/silvery | Dark, sharp line |
| **Density** | ~50% of bulk polymer density | Zero |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test the *craze vs. crack* distinction. Always emphasize that **crazes can sustain stress** — this is the single most important conceptual difference.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Craze thickness:** typically **0.1 – 10 µm** (microns)
- **Fibril diameter:** **5 – 30 nm**
- **Fibril volume fraction in mature craze:** ~ **50%** (rest is void)
- **Density of crazed polymer:** ≈ **0.5 × ρ_bulk**
- **Refractive index drop:** Δn ≈ **0.05 – 0.10**
- **Typical polymers affected:** Polystyrene (PS), Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), Polycarbonate (PC), SAN, ABS

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions Required for Craze Formation

A polymer will undergo crazing when **all four** of the following physical conditions are simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Tensile stress state** — Crazes always open perpendicular to the maximum principal tensile stress (σ₁). They do **not** form under pure compressive loading.
2. **Stress must exceed a critical value** — σ_applied ≥ σ_critical_crazing.
3. **Temperature below the glass transition** (T < Tg for amorphous polymers) — Crazing is a sub-Tg phenomenon; above Tg the polymer flows instead.
4. **Sufficient time** (strain-rate dependent) — Lower strain rates favor crazing over shear yielding.

## 2.2 Mechanism of Craze Formation (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> The **three-stage mechanism** is the most heavily tested framework in KTU exams. Memorize the stages in order.

**Stage 1 — Nucleation (Microvoid Formation)**
- Under tensile stress, regions of localized strain softening occur.
- Microvoids nucleate at stress concentrators (flaws, dust particles, surface scratches).
- This is analogous to classical nucleation theory in metallurgy.

**Stage 2 — Growth (Fibril Drawing)**
- The microvoids expand.
- Polymer chains in the surrounding region undergo **cold-drawing / plastic extension**.
- Highly oriented fibrils form, spanning the two craze faces.
- Fibrils continue to elongate (craze thickening).

**Stage 3 — Breakdown (Craze-to-Crack Conversion)**
- Continued stress causes fibril disentanglement or rupture.
- The middle of the craze breaks down into a true crack.
- Final fracture ensues.

## 2.3 Kramer's Crazing Criterion (Foundation Equation)

The most academically rigorous treatment of crazing follows the **Kramer–Berger** framework. The critical stress for craze nucleation is given by:

$$
\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;\geq\; \sigma_{\text{critical}}(T, \dot{\varepsilon}, M_n)
$$

where the right-hand side depends on **temperature (T)**, **strain rate ($\dot{\varepsilon}$)**, and **number-average molecular weight ($M_n$)**.

## 2.4 Critical Stress for Crazing (Eyring-Type Activation)

The temperature and strain-rate dependence is captured by an **Eyring-type activated process**:

$$
\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;=\; \sigma_{\text{int}} \;+\; \frac{2k_BT}{V^{*}} \sinh^{-1}\!\!\left(\frac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{\dot{\varepsilon}_0}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Higher strain rates **raise** the crazing stress, while higher temperatures **lower** it. This is why polystyrene crazes more easily when slowly bent at room temperature, but shatters at high strain rates.

## 2.5 Molecular Weight Dependence (Critical KTU Formula)

For glassy polymers, the craze stress scales with the **number-average molecular weight** as:

$$
\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;=\; A \;-\; \frac{B}{M_n}
$$

where **A** and **B** are polymer-specific constants. Higher $M_n$ → higher crazing stress → tougher craze.

## 2.6 Craze Growth Kinetics (Doyle's Model)

The **craze tip advance velocity** follows:

$$
v_{\text{tip}} \;=\; v_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{-\Delta G_{\text{act}} + v^{*}\sigma}{k_BT}\right)
$$

where $v^{*}$ is an activation volume, $\sigma$ the applied stress, and $v_0$ a characteristic velocity prefactor.

## 2.7 Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** Crazing is **not** purely a failure mode — it is actually a **toughening mechanism** in many engineering polymers. The energy absorbed during craze formation and fibril drawing is the principal contribution to fracture toughness ($G_{IC}$) in glassy polymers such as HIPS, ABS, and toughened PMMA. Rubber-toughened polymers use controlled crazing as their primary energy dissipation route.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables / Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{\text{crazing}} = \sigma_{\text{int}} + \dfrac{2k_BT}{V^{*}} \sinh^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{\dot{\varepsilon}_0}\right)$ | Eyring-type stress criterion | Pa, K, m³ |
| 2 | $\sigma_{\text{crazing}} = A - \dfrac{B}{M_n}$ | Molecular-weight dependence | Pa, g/mol |
| 3 | $v_{\text{tip}} = v_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\Delta G_{\text{act}} + v^{*}\sigma}{k_BT}\right)$ | Craze tip velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $\rho_{\text{craze}} \approx 0.5\,\rho_{\text{bulk}}$ | Craze density rule | kg/m³ |
| 5 | $f_{\text{fibril}} \approx 0.5$ | Fibril volume fraction in mature craze | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\Delta n_{\text{craze}} \approx 0.05 - 0.10$ | Refractive index drop | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_{\text{crazing}}$ | Craze formation criterion (Rankine-like) | Pa |
| 8 | $G_{IC} \propto W_{\text{craze}} \cdot n_{\text{crazes}}$ | Fracture toughness from crazing energy | J/m² |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Master formulas **1, 2, 3, and 7** — these are the four most heavily tested equations in Module 4.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Stress Criterion from an Energy-Balance Framework

We derive the **critical stress for craze nucleation** using a thermodynamic / energy-balance approach analogous to the Griffith criterion for brittle fracture.

### Step 1 — Define the System and Energy Contributions

A craze nucleates when the **thermodynamic driving force** (released elastic strain energy) overcomes the **energy cost** of creating new internal surfaces and stretching polymer fibrils.

The total energy balance is:

$$
U_{\text{total}} \;=\; U_{\text{elastic, released}} \;-\; U_{\text{surface, created}} \;-\; U_{\text{fibril, drawn}}
$$

### Step 2 — Express the Elastic Strain Energy Released

For a craze of length *c* in an infinite plate under remote tensile stress $\sigma$, the released elastic strain energy per unit thickness is:

$$
U_{\text{elastic}} \;=\; -\frac{\pi c^{2}\sigma^{2}}{4E}\,t
$$

where *E* is Young's modulus and *t* is plate thickness.

### Step 3 — Express the Surface and Fibril Energy Cost

The energy to create two craze surfaces of specific surface energy $\gamma_s$ plus the plastic work to draw fibrils $w_p$ (energy per unit area) is:

$$
U_{\text{cost}} \;=\; 2\gamma_s \,c \,t \;+\; w_p \,c \,t
$$

### Step 4 — Form the Net Energy Function

$$
U_{\text{net}}(c) \;=\; -\frac{\pi c^{2}\sigma^{2}}{4E}\,t \;+\; \left(2\gamma_s + w_p\right)c\,t
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Critical Condition (Maximum / Instability)

At the critical condition, $\dfrac{dU_{\text{net}}}{dc} = 0$:

$$
\frac{dU_{\text{net}}}{dc} \;=\; -\frac{\pi c\,\sigma^{2}}{2E}\,t \;+\; \left(2\gamma_s + w_p\right)t \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for Critical Stress

$$
\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4E\left(2\gamma_s + w_p\right)}{\pi \, c^{*}}}
$$

where $c^{*}$ is the critical craze nucleus size.

### Step 7 — Final Reduced Form

Substituting $G_{\text{craze}} = 2\gamma_s + w_p$ (the **craze surface work**, analogous to $G_{IC}$):

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4E\,G_{\text{craze}}}{\pi \, c^{*}}}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation of the result:**
> - Smaller pre-existing flaw ($c^{*}$) → higher crazing stress → tougher material.
> - Higher modulus *E* → higher crazing stress.
> - Higher plastic work $w_p$ → higher crazing stress (because fibrils absorb more energy before failure).
>
> This is the **theoretical justification** for why rubber-toughened polymers (which have high $w_p$ due to massive fibrillation) resist crack propagation far better than neat glassy polymers.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Influence of Variables on Crazing Behavior

| Variable | Effect on σ_crazing | Effect on Craze Density | Effect on Total Toughness |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Temperature (T) | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases (more energy absorbed) |
| ↑ Strain rate ($\dot{\varepsilon}$) | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases |
| ↑ Molecular weight ($M_n$) | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases |
| ↑ Crosslink density | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases |
| ↑ Plasticizer content | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases (moderately) |
| ↑ Entanglements | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases significantly |
| ↑ Applied stress | — | ↑ Increases (more sites) | ↑ Increases up to limit |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU-Style Problem)

**Given:** A polystyrene sample has Young's modulus $E = 3.2$ GPa, craze surface energy $G_{\text{craze}} = 250$ J/m², and a critical flaw size $c^{*} = 1.0 \,\mu\text{m}$. Compute the critical stress for craze nucleation.

**Solution:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4E\,G_{\text{craze}}}{\pi c^{*}}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4 \times (3.2 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa}) \times (250\,\text{J/m}^2)}{\pi \times (1.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m})}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{3.2 \times 10^{12}}{\pi \times 10^{-6}}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;=\; \sqrt{1.019 \times 10^{18}}\,\text{Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;\approx\; 31.9\,\text{MPa}\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This value matches experimental data for polystyrene craze nucleation (typically 25–35 MPa), validating the energy-balance derivation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define crazing in polymers. How is a craze structurally different from a crack?**

**Model Answer:**
Crazing is a localized plastic deformation phenomenon in glassy polymers in which thin, planar regions (crazes) develop perpendicular to the applied tensile stress. Structurally, a craze differs from a crack in three key ways:

1. A craze consists of **highly oriented polymer fibrils** (5–30 nm diameter) bridging the two faces, interspersed with **microvoids**, while a crack has atomically sharp, empty faces with no bridging material.
2. A craze is **load-bearing** — it can sustain stress via the fibrils — whereas a crack cannot transmit load across its faces.
3. A craze has a **reduced refractive index** (~0.05–0.10 drop) causing its characteristic whitish/silvery appearance, while a crack appears dark.

> [!TIP]
> Always mention **fibrils**, **voids**, and **load-bearing capacity** — these three keywords guarantee full marks on the distinction.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and explain Kramer's criterion for crazing in glassy polymers.**

**Model Answer:**
Kramer's criterion for crazing states that a craze nucleates in a glassy polymer when the **maximum principal tensile stress** $\sigma_1$ exceeds a critical value $\sigma_{\text{crazing}}$, which depends on **temperature (T)**, **strain rate ($\dot{\varepsilon}$)**, and **number-average molecular weight ($M_n$)**.

The functional form is:

$$
\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;=\; A \;-\; \frac{B}{M_n} \;+\; \frac{2k_BT}{V^{*}}\sinh^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{\dot{\varepsilon}_0}\right)
$$

Higher $M_n$ → higher crazing resistance. Higher temperature and lower strain rate → easier crazing.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) (7 Marks)** With neat diagrams, describe the **three-stage mechanism of craze formation and growth** in glassy polymers. Discuss the role of polymer fibrils in this process.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Derive the **energy-balance criterion** for craze nucleation and explain how it differs from the classical Griffith criterion for brittle fracture.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Stage 1 — Nucleation:**
Under applied tensile stress exceeding $\sigma_{\text{crazing}}$, microvoids nucleate at stress concentrators such as surface flaws, dust inclusions, or pre-existing microcracks. This is governed by thermodynamic activation and follows a stress-biased nucleation process.

**Stage 2 — Growth:**
The microvoids grow, and the surrounding polymer undergoes **cold drawing**. Macromolecular chains disentangle and orient parallel to the tensile axis, forming the characteristic **fibrillar microstructure**. These fibrils (5–30 nm in diameter) carry the applied load and continue to elongate (craze thickening) by a process called **craze widening**.

**Stage 3 — Breakdown:**
Eventually, the fibrils rupture or pull out from the craze surfaces by chain disentanglement. The middle of the craze converts into a true crack, which then propagates unstably, leading to catastrophic fracture.

**Role of fibrils:**
- Provide **load transfer** across the craze, allowing the craze to sustain stress.
- Absorb large amounts of energy via **plastic deformation** during cold drawing.
- The fibril volume fraction (~50%) governs the optical and mechanical properties of the craze.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

The **energy-balance criterion for craze nucleation** is derived by equating the elastic strain energy released to the energy required to create new craze surfaces and draw polymer fibrils. Following the derivation in Section 3:

$$
\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{4E\,G_{\text{craze}}}{\pi \, c^{*}}}
$$

where $G_{\text{craze}} = 2\gamma_s + w_p$ is the total craze surface work, including both true surface energy $\gamma_s$ and plastic work $w_p$ to draw the fibrils.

**Differences from Griffith's criterion:**

| Aspect | Griffith (Brittle Crack) | Crazing Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| Plastic work term $w_p$ | Absent | Present and dominant |
| Energy dissipation | Surface only | Surface + massive fibril drawing |
| Fibril formation | None | Yes (key difference) |
| Typical $G_c$ value | 1–10 J/m² (glass) | 100–1000 J/m² (polymer crazes) |

The presence of the $w_p$ term makes polymers **orders of magnitude tougher** than predicted by pure Griffith theory — this is the central insight.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often write the Griffith equation but **omit the plastic work term $w_p$** when applying it to polymers. This loses 3–4 marks immediately. Always explicitly write $G_{\text{craze}} = 2\gamma_s + w_p$ and explain that $w_p \gg \gamma_s$ for polymers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a) (7 Marks)** Discuss the **factors affecting crazing** in glassy polymers. Explain with equations how molecular weight, strain rate, and temperature influence the critical crazing stress.

**(b) (7 Marks)** What is the **role of crazing in polymer toughening**? Explain how rubber-modified polymers such as HIPS and ABS use controlled crazing as a toughening mechanism.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

The critical crazing stress depends on the following factors:

**1. Molecular Weight:** Higher $M_n$ produces longer, more entangled polymer chains that resist disentanglement, raising $\sigma_{\text{crazing}}$.

$$
\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;=\; A \;-\; \frac{B}{M_n}
$$

**2. Strain Rate:** Crazing is a thermally activated process. At higher strain rates, less time is available for chain disentanglement, so $\sigma_{\text{crazing}}$ increases.

$$
\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;\propto\; \sinh^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{\dot{\varepsilon}_0}\right)
$$

**3. Temperature:** Higher temperatures supply thermal energy to the activation process, lowering $\sigma_{\text{crazing}}$. Approaching $T_g$, the polymer tends toward **shear yielding** rather than crazing.

$$
\sigma_{\text{crazing}} \;\propto\; \frac{2k_BT}{V^{*}}\sinh^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{\dot{\varepsilon}_0}\right)
$$

**4. Crosslink density:** Higher crosslinking reduces chain mobility → lowers $\sigma_{\text{crazing}}$ → favors shear yielding.

**5. Plasticizers and solvents:** Lower the effective $T_g$, promoting crazing at lower stresses.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

Crazing is a **major energy-absorbing mechanism** in glassy polymers. The energy dissipated during craze formation, fibril drawing, and fibrillation contributes directly to fracture toughness.

**Mechanism of rubber toughening (HIPS / ABS):**
- Rubber particles (typically 0.1–1 µm diameter) are dispersed in the glassy matrix.
- Under tensile stress, **stress concentrations** around rubber particles trigger **multiple crazes** in the matrix, each terminating at a rubber particle.
- Rubber particles act as **craze terminators** — preventing crazes from growing into catastrophic cracks.
- The large number of small crazes absorbs enormous amounts of energy.
- Additionally, rubber particles themselves undergo **cavitation**, releasing further energy.

Quantitative outcome: The fracture toughness $G_{IC}$ of HIPS can be **10–30× higher** than that of neat polystyrene, almost entirely due to controlled multiple crazing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** When discussing HIPS / ABS toughening, students frequently forget to mention **craze termination** by rubber particles. Always state that rubber particles both **initiate** crazes (via stress concentration) AND **terminate** them (via crack-pinning / bridging) — both roles must be mentioned for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Crazing (Module 4)**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Localized plastic deformation with fibril + void structure, load-bearing.
- ⭐ **Craze vs. Crack:** Crazes carry load via fibrils; cracks do not.
- ⭐ **Crazes form perpendicular** to the maximum principal tensile stress.
- ⭐ **Three stages:** Nucleation → Growth (fibril drawing) → Breakdown (crack formation).
- ⭐ **Critical condition:** $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_{\text{crazing}}$ (Rankine-type criterion).
- ⭐ **Eyring-type equation:** $\sigma_{\text{crazing}} = \sigma_{\text{int}} + \dfrac{2k_BT}{V^{*}}\sinh^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{\dot{\varepsilon}_0}\right)$
- ⭐ **Molecular-weight scaling:** $\sigma_{\text{crazing}} = A - \dfrac{B}{M_n}$
- ⭐ **Energy criterion:** $\sigma_{\text{crazing}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4E\,G_{\text{craze}}}{\pi c^{*}}}$, where $G_{\text{craze}} = 2\gamma_s + w_p$.
- ⭐ **Craze density:** ~50% bulk polymer; fibrils 5–30 nm; voids 50%.
- ⭐ **Refractive index drop:** Δn ≈ 0.05–0.10 → causes whitening/stress whitening.
- ⭐ **Temperature effect:** Higher T → easier crazing (until near $T_g$, then shear yielding dominates).
- ⭐ **Strain rate effect:** Higher $\dot{\varepsilon}$ → harder to craze.
- ⭐ **Molecular weight effect:** Higher $M_n$ → tougher crazes → higher impact resistance.
- ⭐ **Toughening role:** Crazing is the principal energy-absorbing mechanism in HIPS, ABS, toughened PMMA.
- ⭐ **Rubber toughening:** Rubber particles both **initiate** and **terminate** crazes.
- ⭐ **Typical materials:** PS, PMMA, PC, SAN, ABS.
- ⭐ **Engineering metric:** $G_{IC,\text{polymer}} \gg G_{IC,\text{glass}}$ due to $w_p$ term in craze criterion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
